## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECET752]**
*Define the term "Daylight Factor". State the standard sky condition assumed for its evaluation and mention the minimum recommended value for an office space as per ECBC 2017.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The Daylight Factor ($DF$) is defined as the ratio of the indoor illuminance at a reference point on the horizontal working plane (usually 0.85 m above floor) to the simultaneous outdoor horizontal illuminance on an unobstructed surface, expressed as a percentage.

$$DF = \frac{E_{\text{indoor}}}{E_{\text{outdoor}}} \times 100 \, \%$$

**Standard sky (1 mark):** The standard CIE (Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage) Overcast Sky is assumed, with a design outdoor horizontal illuminance of approximately **$10{,}000 \text{ lux}$** and a luminance distribution where the zenith is three times the horizon.

**ECBC recommendation (0.5 mark):** For general office spaces, a minimum daylight factor of **$2\,\%$** is prescribed.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PECET752]**
*List the three components of the Daylight Factor and briefly explain the "Internally Reflected Component (IRC)".*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three components are:

1. **Sky Component ($SC$):** Direct sky luminance reaching the reference point through the window.
2. **Externally Reflected Component ($ERC$):** Light from the sky reflected off the ground or external opposing surfaces before entering the room.
3. **Internally Reflected Component ($IRC$):** (1 mark each for listing)

**IRC Explanation (1.5 marks):** The $IRC$ is the portion of the daylight that enters the room through the window, falls on the internal surfaces (walls, ceiling, floor, furniture), undergoes **multiple inter-reflections**, and is eventually redistributed to the reference point on the working plane. The magnitude of $IRC$ is strongly dependent on the mean interior surface reflectance ($\rho_{\text{int}}$), which is why ECBC 2017 prescribes **$\rho_{\text{ceiling}} \ge 0.7$, $\rho_{\text{walls}} \ge 0.5$, $\rho_{\text{floor}} \ge 0.2$**. A high $IRC$ is the key reason why light-coloured interior finishes dramatically improve daylight distribution in deep rooms.

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — READ CAREFULLY:**
> - Marks are deducted (typically **1 to 2 marks**) if you forget to specify the **sky condition** assumed (must mention CIE Overcast).
> - Marks are deducted (typically **1 mark**) if you do not state the **units** of the final answer (% for $DF$, lx for $E$).
> - Marks are deducted (typically **1 to 2 marks**) if the **arithmetic in $SC$** uses degrees instead of radians in the $\sin$ term (always use **degrees** in KTU; mention "$\theta$ in degrees" once for clarity).
> - Marks are deducted (typically **1 mark**) if you omit the **"compliance comment"** at the end of the numerical (e.g., "Since $DF \ge 2\,\%$, the design satisfies ECBC 2017"). The compliance remark is **mandatory** for full marks.

---

#### QUESTION A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**Q(A). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, PECET752, Module 1]**

**(a) Explain the concept of the "Window-to-Wall Ratio (WWR)" and its influence on daylighting. With the help of a neat sketch, describe the placement of an internal light shelf and explain how it improves daylight penetration in a deep office room. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of WWR (2 marks):**

$$WWR = \frac{A_{\text{window}}}{A_{\text{gross wall}}} \times 100 \, \%$$

The WWR is a fundamental architectural metric expressing the proportion of a wall occupied by fenestration. Typical ECBC-recommended values are:
- North façade: **40 to 60 %** (to harvest soft, glare-free skylight)
- South façade: **25 to 40 %** (with overhangs to control summer sun)
- East/West façades: **15 to 25 %** (low WWR to avoid low-angle glare and heat gain)

A higher WWR generally increases $SC$ and $DF$, but it also increases heat gain (SHGC) and glare risk. The optimum is a balance that satisfies $DF \ge 2\,\%$ without exceeding the cooling load budget.

**Influence of WWR on daylighting (1.5 marks):**
- **Increases $SC$ linearly** (since $A_{\text{window}}$ appears directly in the $SC$ formula).
- **Increases $ERC$** as more external ground-reflected light enters.
- **Increases $IRC$** (more light enters, more bounces inside, but the denominator $\frac{1}{\rho_{\text{int}}} - \frac{A_{\text{open}}}{A_{\text{total}}}$ shrinks as the opening fraction rises, slightly *reducing* the per-unit $IRC$).
- The net $DF$ rises with WWR, but the **law of diminishing returns** sets in beyond a WWR of about 60 %, as glare and overheating dominate.

**Light Shelf — Concept, Sketch, and Function (3.5 marks):**

A **light shelf** is a horizontal, highly reflective (typically $\rho \ge 0.9$, white painted aluminium or polished anodised) element placed inside a window, usually at the height of the window head (or slightly below). It has two parts:
- **Exterior shelf (optional):** outside the glass, deflects high summer sun.
- **Interior shelf:** inside the glass, bounces deep-penetrating light upward onto the ceiling, which then redistributes it as diffused $IRC$ to the rear of the room.

The lower portion of the window (below the shelf) provides view-out and task light; the upper portion (above the shelf) provides "bounced" top light that can penetrate **up to 4.5 times** the window-head height (compared to **2.5 times** for a conventional side window).

A textual schematic of the working:

> Sky light → upper window pane → strikes interior light shelf (white, $\rho = 0.9$) → bounces upward onto ceiling → ceiling reflects diffusely to the entire room depth. Simultaneously, lower window admits direct task light near the window and visual connection to outdoors. The shelf physically cuts glare from the bright sky, raising visual comfort.

**Summary (0.5 mark):** Light shelves can raise $DF$ at the rear of a 6 m deep office from below $1\,\%$ to above $2.5\,\%$, satisfying ECBC without enlarging the window.

---

**(b) A classroom 8 m × 6 m × 3.2 m (height) has a single window of size 3.0 m × 1.5 m on the north wall, located 0.9 m above the floor. The clear glass has $\tau_v = 0.82$. The design outdoor illuminance is $9{,}500 \text{ lx}$. The mean internal surface reflectance is 0.55, and the window opening ratio $A_{\text{open}} / A_{\text{total}} = 0.04$. The angle $\theta$ subtended at the reference point (on a desk 4 m from the window) is $25^{\circ}$. Compute the Sky Component, ERC, IRC, total Daylight Factor, and the working-plane illuminance, and comment on compliance with ECBC 2017. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- $A_w = 3.0 \times 1.5 = 4.5 \text{ m}^{2}$
- $\tau_v = 0.82$, $E_{\text{out}} = 9{,}500 \text{ lx}$
- $\rho_{\text{int}} = 0.55$, $A_{\text{open}} / A_{\text{total}} = 0.04$
- $\theta = 25^{\circ}$, $d = 4 \text{ m}$ (reference point distance from window)

**Step 1 — Sky Component (2 marks):**

$$SC = \frac{\tau_v \cdot A_w \cdot (1 + 2 \sin \theta)}{3 \cdot d^{2}} \times 100$$

Compute the trigonometric term: $1 + 2 \sin 25^{\circ} = 1 + 2(0.4226) = 1 + 0.8452 = 1.8452$.

$$SC = \frac{0.82 \times 4.5 \times 1.8452}{3 \times 4^{2}} \times 100 = \frac{6.808}{48.0} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{SC = 14.18 \, \%}$$

**Step 2 — Externally Reflected Component (1 mark):** With $\rho_g = 0.2$ (typical grey concrete):

$$ERC = 0.05 \times SC \times \rho_g = 0.05 \times 14.18 \times 0.2 = 0.142 \, \%$$

**Step 3 — Internally Reflected Component (1.5 marks):**

$$IRC = \frac{SC + ERC}{\dfrac{1}{\rho_{\text{int}}} - \dfrac{A_{\text{open}}}{A_{\text{total}}}} = \frac{14.18 + 0.142}{\dfrac{1}{0.55} - 0.04}$$

Compute the denominator: $\frac{1}{0.55} - 0.04 = 1.8182 - 0.04 = 1.7782$.

$$IRC = \frac{14.322}{1.7782} = 8.05 \, \%$$

**Step 4 — Total Daylight Factor (1 mark):**

$$DF = SC + ERC + IRC = 14.18 + 0.142 + 8.05 = 22.37 \, \%$$

**Step 5 — Working Plane Illuminance (0.5 mark):**

$$E_{\text{indoor}} = \frac{DF \times E_{\text{out}}}{100} = \frac{22.37 \times 9{,}500}{100} = 2{,}125 \text{ lux}$$

**Compliance comment (1 mark):** ECBC 2017 requires a minimum $DF$ of $2\,\%$ and an illuminance of at least **$300 \text{ lx}$** for classrooms (NBC 2016 specifies $500 \text{ lx}$ for classrooms). Since $DF = 22.37\,\% \gg 2\,\%$ and $E_{\text{in}} = 2{,}125 \text{ lx} \gg 500 \text{ lx}$, the design **comfortably complies**. However, the high $DF$ indicates a risk of **over-illumination and glare** near the window — adding an internal light shelf or translucent roller blind is recommended.

---

#### QUESTION B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

**Q(B). [KTU University Exam – June 2023, PECET752, Module 1]**

**(a) Differentiate between "Illuminance" and "Luminance". Why is luminance, rather than illuminance, the metric that the human eye directly perceives? State the units of each. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Illuminance (1.5 marks):**

Illuminance ($E$) is the **incident** luminous flux per unit area on a surface.

$$E = \frac{d\Phi}{dA} \quad \text{(units: lux, lx = lm/m}^{2}\text{)}$$

It is a **scalar quantity** that quantifies "how much light is falling on a surface." It is measured using a lux-meter (cosine-corrected sensor) placed on the surface. Typical design values: corridor $100 \text{ lx}$, office $500 \text{ lx}$, operation theatre $1{,}000 \text{ lx}$, fine bench work $1{,}500 \text{ lx}$.

**Definition of Luminance (1.5 marks):**

Luminance ($L$) is the **luminous intensity emitted (or reflected) per unit projected area** of a source (or surface) in a given direction.

$$L = \frac{dI}{dA \cos \theta} \quad \text{(units: candela per square metre, cd/m}^{2}\text{)}$$

It is a **directional (vector) quantity** that quantifies "how bright a surface *appears* to the eye when viewed from a particular direction." It is measured using a luminance meter (calibrated imaging photometer) pointed at the surface from the observer's position.

**Why the human eye perceives luminance, not illuminance (3 marks):**

1. **Retinal physiology:** The retina has rod and cone photoreceptors that respond to light flux *entering the eye* per unit solid angle per unit retinal area. The relevant physical quantity for this is the **luminance** of the surface as projected onto the retina — not the illuminance on the surface.
2. **Visual adaptation:** The eye adapts over a luminance range of $10^{-6}$ to $10^{6} \text{ cd/m}^{2}$ (a trillion-fold range). This adaptation is governed by luminance, not illuminance.
3. **Glare perception:** Discomfort and disability glare are *defined* in terms of luminance contrast between the source and the surround. The Hopkinson and Petherbridge glare index uses luminance ratios.
4. **Practical implication in daylighting:** Two surfaces can have identical illuminance (say $500 \text{ lx}$) but vastly different perceived brightness if their reflectances differ (a white wall at $\rho = 0.8$ has $L = 127 \text{ cd/m}^{2}$ while a black wall at $\rho = 0.05$ has $L = 8 \text{ cd/m}^{2}$). Hence, the daylighting designer must check both illuminance (for task performance) and luminance (for visual comfort and glare).

**Units Summary Table (1 mark):**

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Defining Equation** | **SI Unit** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Luminous flux | $\Phi$ | — | Lumen (lm) |
| Luminous intensity | $I$ | $I = d\Phi / d\omega$ | Candela (cd) |
| Illuminance | $E$ | $E = d\Phi / dA$ | Lux (lx) |
| Luminance | $L$ | $L = dI / (dA \cos \theta)$ | Candela per square metre (cd/m²) |

---

**(b) A reading room 10 m × 7 m × 3.5 m is illuminated by a south-facing window of 4.0 m × 1.8 m with a tinted double-glazed unit ($\tau_v = 0.70$). The reference point on a table is 5 m from the window. The internal mean reflectance is 0.6 and the external ground reflectance is 0.15. The angle subtended by the upper edge of the window at the reference point is $20^{\circ}$. Design outdoor illuminance is $11{,}000 \text{ lx}$. Calculate $SC$, $ERC$, $IRC$, total $DF$ and the working-plane illuminance. If the recommended value is $DF \ge 2.5\,\%$ for reading rooms, what is your design verdict? Suggest two modifications if non-compliant. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- $A_w = 4.0 \times 1.8 = 7.2 \text{ m}^{2}$
- $\tau_v = 0.70$, $E_{\text{out}} = 11{,}000 \text{ lx}$
- $\rho_{\text{int}} = 0.6$, $\rho_g = 0.15$
- $\theta = 20^{\circ}$, $d = 5 \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — Sky Component (1.5 marks):**

$$SC = \frac{\tau_v \cdot A_w \cdot (1 + 2 \sin \theta)}{3 \cdot d^{2}} \times 100$$

$1 + 2 \sin 20^{\circ} = 1 + 2(0.3420) = 1.6840$.

$$SC = \frac{0.70 \times 7.2 \times 1.6840}{3 \times 5^{2}} \times 100 = \frac{8.485}{75.0} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{SC = 11.31 \, \%}$$

**Step 2 — Externally Reflected Component (1 mark):**

$$ERC = 0.05 \times SC \times \rho_g = 0.05 \times 11.31 \times 0.15 = 0.085 \, \%$$

**Step 3 — Internally Reflected Component (1.5 marks):**

Assuming $A_{\text{open}} / A_{\text{total}} \approx 0.04$ (typical for a single large window in a perimeter wall):

$$IRC = \frac{SC + ERC}{\dfrac{1}{\rho_{\text{int}}} - 0.04} = \frac{11.31 + 0.085}{\dfrac{1}{0.6} - 0.04} = \frac{11.395}{1.6667 - 0.04} = \frac{11.395}{1.6267}$$

$$\boxed{IRC = 7.00 \, \%}$$

**Step 4 — Total Daylight Factor (1 mark):**

$$DF = 11.31 + 0.085 + 7.00 = 18.40 \, \%$$

**Step 5 — Working-Plane Illuminance (0.5 mark):**

$$E_{\text{in}} = \frac{18.40 \times 11{,}000}{100} = 2{,}024 \text{ lx}$$

**Design Verdict (1 mark):** The calculated $DF = 18.40\,\%$ is **vastly greater** than the required $2.5\,\%$. The room is **over-daylit** at the reference point, which means excessive solar heat gain, possible glare near the window, and visual discomfort. This is a case of **non-compliance in the direction of "excess daylight"** (which ECBC treats as a deviation just as serious as deficiency, since it leads to cooling penalties and glare).

**Suggested Modifications (1 mark — 0.5 each):**
1. **Add an internal light shelf** at window-head height to redirect light deep into the room, reducing the harsh bright zone near the window and balancing the luminance distribution.
2. **Add an external horizontal overhang or vertical fins** on the south façade to limit summer sun penetration by 40–60 %, while preserving winter sun. Alternatively, use **spectrally selective glazing** (e.g., $\tau_v = 0.6$, SHGC $= 0.25$) to decouple light from heat, thereby reducing cooling load without sacrificing daylight.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pin this checklist to your study wall the night before the exam.**

- **Definition:** Daylight Factor $DF = (E_{\text{in}} / E_{\text{out}}) \times 100\,\%$, evaluated under a **CIE Standard Overcast Sky** ($E_{\text{out}} \approx 10{,}000$ lx, zenith luminance 3× horizon).
- **Three components, in order of magnitude:** $DF = SC + ERC + IRC$. Typical magnitudes: $SC \approx 10$–$15\,\%$, $ERC \approx 0.1$–$0.5\,\%$, $IRC \approx 5$–$10\,\%$. **$SC$ is the largest; $ERC$ is often negligible.**
- **Reference plane height:** Working plane is at **$0.85$ m** above finished floor (desk height).
- **ECBC 2017 minimum $DF$ for offices:** **$2\,\%$** (Indian Green Building Council recommends $2.5\,\%$ for schools).
- **Interior reflectance targets (ECBC 2017):** Ceiling $\ge 0.7$, Walls $\ge 0.5$, Floor $\ge 0.2$.
- **WWR rule of thumb:** North $40$–$60\,\%$; South $25$–$40\,\%$; East/West $15$–$25\,\%$.
- **Side window penetration depth:** Up to **$2.5 \times$** window-head height; light shelf extends to **$4.5 \times$**.
- **$SC$ formula to memorise:** $SC = [\tau_v \cdot A_w \cdot (1 + 2\sin\theta)] / [3 d^2] \times 100 \, \%$.
- **$IRC$ formula to memorise:** $IRC = (SC + ERC) / [(1/\rho_{\text{int}}) - (A_{\text{open}}/A_{\text{total}})]$.
- **Units in every answer:** $\% \cdot$ for $DF$, $SC$, $ERC$, $IRC$; **lux (lx)** for $E$; **cd/m²** for $L$.
- **Glare is luminance-driven**, not illuminance-driven. Two walls at the same illuminance look very different if reflectances differ.
- **Modern evaluation metric:** Prefer **$sDA_{300/50} \ge 55\,\%$** and **$DA \ge 60\,\%$** for IGBC Platinum / LEED v4 certification.
- **Compliance remark is mandatory** at the end of every numerical — failure to add it costs 1 mark.
- **Daylighting = energy + health + sustainability** trinity — always mention all three in 7-mark and 14-mark descriptive answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Ventilation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET752 - SUSTAINABLE CONSTRUCTION PRACTICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Air Ventilation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Air Ventilation** in the context of sustainable construction is the deliberate, controlled movement of outdoor air into and out of an enclosed building space to maintain acceptable **Indoor Air Quality (IAQ)**, regulate thermal comfort, control moisture, and dilute airborne contaminants such as CO₂, VOCs (Volatile Organic Compounds), particulate matter (PM2.5/PM10), and bioaerosols.

According to **ASHRAE Standard 62.1 (Ventilation for Acceptable Indoor Air Quality)**, ventilation is defined as the process of supplying air to, or removing air from, a space for the purpose of controlling air contaminant levels, humidity, or temperature. For KTU 2024 syllabus, ventilation is classified into three primary categories:

1. **Natural Ventilation** – Air movement driven by natural forces (wind pressure and buoyancy/stack effect).
2. **Mechanical Ventilation** – Air movement driven by fans, blowers, or HVAC systems.
3. **Hybrid (Mixed-Mode) Ventilation** – A combination of natural and mechanical systems controlled by sensors and automation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For PECET752, ventilation is treated as a *passive design strategy* that reduces operational energy demand (especially fan power in HVAC systems), which directly supports **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC)** and **GRIHA/IGBC green rating** objectives.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a sealed water bottle left in the sun. The air inside heats up, becomes lighter, and if you punch a small hole near the top, the hot air rushes out while cooler outside air enters from the bottom hole. This is **stack effect ventilation** in its simplest form.

Now, imagine standing between two open windows of a classroom on a windy day. You feel a strong breeze flowing in one window and out the other. This is **cross ventilation** powered by wind pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Building as a Lung"**
> A building "breathes" just like a human lung. Inhaled air is fresh outdoor air (oxygen), and exhaled air is stale indoor air (carrying CO₂ and pollutants). Good ventilation design ensures the building "inhales" enough clean air and "exhales" stale air efficiently — minimizing the "breathing effort" (fan energy) needed.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (in Bold)

- **Air Changes per Hour (ACH):** Volume of air supplied to a space per hour divided by the volume of the space. Typical residential: **0.5 to 1.0 ACH**; Offices: **4 to 8 ACH**; Hospitals/Operation Theatres: **15 to 25 ACH**.
- **Ventilation Effectiveness (εᵥ):** Ratio of actual contaminant removal to perfect mixing removal. Range: **0 to 1.0** (1.0 = perfect displacement).
- **Infiltration Rate:** Uncontrolled air leakage through cracks, gaps, and openings, measured in **ACH₅₀** (air changes per hour at 50 Pa pressure differential).
- **CO₂ Threshold:** Indoor CO₂ concentration should not exceed **1000 ppm** above outdoor levels (per ASHRAE 62.1).

> [!TIP]
> **Why Ventilation Matters in Sustainable Construction:**
> 1. Reduces dependence on mechanical air-conditioning (saves 20–40% HVAC energy).
> 2. Improves occupant health and productivity (sick building syndrome prevention).
> 3. Controls humidity to prevent mold growth and structural degradation.
> 4. Qualifies for green building credit points (LEED, GRIHA, IGBC, BREEAM).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework

The science of ventilation rests on three fundamental driving forces:

### 2.1.1 Wind Pressure (Cross Ventilation)
When wind strikes a building façade, it creates a **positive pressure** on the windward side and a **negative pressure (suction)** on the leeward side. This pressure differential drives air through openings.

**Wind Pressure Equation (Bernoulli's Principle):**
$$P_w = \frac{1}{2} \rho \cdot v^2 \cdot C_p$$

Where:
- $P_w$ = Wind pressure (Pa)
- $\rho$ = Air density (≈ **1.2 kg/m³** at standard conditions)
- $v$ = Wind velocity (m/s)
- $C_p$ = Wind pressure coefficient (dimensionless, ranges from **−0.8 to +0.8** depending on façade location)

### 2.1.2 Buoyancy / Stack Effect (Vertical Ventilation)
Warm air is lighter than cool air. In a building with vertical height, warm indoor air rises and escapes through high openings while cool outdoor air enters through low openings.

**Stack Pressure Equation:**
$$\Delta P_s = \rho_o \cdot g \cdot H \cdot \left(\frac{T_i - T_o}{T_i}\right)$$

Where:
- $\Delta P_s$ = Stack pressure differential (Pa)
- $\rho_o$ = Outdoor air density (kg/m³)
- $g$ = Gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $H$ = Vertical distance between inlet and outlet openings (m)
- $T_i$ = Indoor absolute temperature (K)
- $T_o$ = Outdoor absolute temperature (K)

### 2.1.3 Mechanical Pressure (Fan-Driven)
When natural forces are insufficient, fans create the necessary pressure differential. Fan power is given by:
$$P_{fan} = \frac{\dot{V} \cdot \Delta P_{total}}{\eta_{fan}}$$

Where:
- $\dot{V}$ = Volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $\Delta P_{total}$ = Total pressure drop across the system (Pa)
- $\eta_{fan}$ = Fan efficiency (typically **0.5 to 0.75**)

## 2.2 Air Change Calculation Methodology

The most fundamental ventilation equation:
$$ACH = \frac{Q \times 60}{V}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Fresh air supply rate (m³/s)
- $V$ = Volume of the ventilated space (m³)
- The factor **60** converts seconds to hours.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always convert units carefully. Flow rate is often given in L/s or m³/hr, while volume is in m³. Use **1 m³/s = 1000 L/s = 3600 m³/hr**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $ACH = \dfrac{Q \times 60}{V}$ | Air Changes per Hour | dimensionless |
| 2 | $Q = \dfrac{ACH \times V}{3600}$ | Required flow rate from ACH | m³/s |
| 3 | $\Delta P_s = \rho_o g H \left(\dfrac{T_i - T_o}{T_i}\right)$ | Stack effect pressure | Pa |
| 4 | $P_w = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v^2 C_p$ | Wind pressure on façade | Pa |
| 5 | $v_{ref} = v_{met} \left(\dfrac{h}{10}\right)^{0.15}$ | Wind velocity at building height $h$ | m/s |
| 6 | $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Orifice flow equation | m³/s |
| 7 | $\dot{m}_{CO_2} = \dfrac{G \times 10^{-6}}{V \times \rho_{air}}$ | CO₂ generation per person | kg/s |
| 8 | $C_{ss} = C_o + \dfrac{\dot{N}}{Q}$ | Steady-state contaminant concentration | ppm |
| 9 | $P_{fan} = \dfrac{\dot{V} \cdot \Delta P_{total}}{\eta_{fan}}$ | Fan shaft power | W |
| 10 | $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{C_{e,ideal} - \bar{C}}{C_{e,ideal} - C_s}$ | Ventilation effectiveness index | dimensionless |

**Where:**
- $C_d$ = Discharge coefficient (≈ **0.6 for sharp-edged orifice, 0.95 for rounded**)
- $A$ = Open area of opening (m²)
- $G$ = CO₂ generation rate per person (≈ **0.018 m³/hr per person**)
- $C_{e,ideal}$ = Exhaust concentration at ideal (displacement) flow
- $\bar{C}$ = Average room concentration
- $C_s$ = Supply air concentration

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

These equations are used to:
- **Size ventilation ducts and fans** in HVAC design (Carrier, Trane, Daikin load sheets).
- **Design passive cooling** for tropical climates (typical Kerala requirement: maximize **cross ventilation** in summer).
- **Verify code compliance** with NBC 2016 (India), ASHRAE 62.1, and ECBC+.
- **Compute energy savings** in green building certification (LEED EQ Credit: Indoor Air Quality).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Air Changes per Hour (ACH) and Required Flow Rate

### Step 1 — Conceptual Setup
Let us consider a room of dimensions $L \times B \times H$ (length × breadth × height in meters). The volume of the room is:
$$V = L \times B \times H \quad [\text{m}^3]$$

### Step 2 — Define Ventilation Rate
The fresh air supply rate $Q$ is defined as the volume of outdoor air entering the room per unit time. If $Q$ is in **m³/s**, then in one hour, the total volume of air entering is:
$$Q_{hourly} = Q \times 3600 \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{hr}]$$

### Step 3 — Define Air Changes per Hour
The number of times the entire room's air volume is replaced in one hour is:
$$ACH = \frac{Q_{hourly}}{V} = \frac{Q \times 3600}{V}$$

> *Logic:* If a room holds 100 m³ and the supply rate is 50 m³/hr, then in 1 hour, half the room's air is replaced → ACH = 0.5.

### Step 4 — Derive Required Flow Rate from ACH
Rearranging the ACH equation to solve for $Q$:
$$Q = \frac{ACH \times V}{3600} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{s}]$$

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example
A classroom has dimensions **10 m × 8 m × 3.5 m**. The required ACH (per ASHRAE 62.1 for classrooms) is **6 ACH**. Calculate the required fresh air supply.

**Solution:**

Step 5.1 — Calculate room volume:
$$V = 10 \times 8 \times 3.5 = 280 \text{ m}^3$$

Step 5.2 — Apply the formula:
$$Q = \frac{6 \times 280}{3600} = \frac{1680}{3600}$$

$$Q = 0.4667 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 466.67 \text{ L/s}$$

> *Interpretation:* The HVAC system must deliver approximately **467 liters of fresh air per second** to maintain 6 air changes per hour.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Stack Effect Pressure

### Step 1 — Physical Principle
The stack effect arises from the density difference between warm indoor air and cool outdoor air. Per the ideal gas law:
$$\rho = \frac{P}{R \cdot T}$$

Where $R$ is the specific gas constant for air (**287 J/kg·K**) and $T$ is absolute temperature (K).

### Step 2 — Pressure Balance
Consider a building with a neutral pressure level (NPL) somewhere mid-height. Above the NPL, indoor air is at a higher pressure than outdoor air. The pressure differential at any height $h$ is:
$$\Delta P(h) = (\rho_o - \rho_i) \cdot g \cdot h$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Density Expressions
Using $\rho = P/(R \cdot T)$ and assuming the absolute pressure $P$ is approximately equal indoor and outdoor:
$$\Delta P(h) = \frac{P \cdot g \cdot h}{R} \left(\frac{1}{T_o} - \frac{1}{T_i}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Simplify Using Temperature Difference
$$\Delta P(h) = \rho_o \cdot g \cdot h \cdot \frac{T_i - T_o}{T_i}$$

For total stack pressure between inlet and outlet separated by height $H$:
$$\Delta P_s = \rho_o \cdot g \cdot H \cdot \frac{T_i - T_o}{T_i}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Verification
Given: $H = 10$ m, $T_i = 30°C = 303$ K, $T_o = 25°C = 298$ K, $\rho_o = 1.184$ kg/m³.

$$\Delta P_s = 1.184 \times 9.81 \times 10 \times \frac{303 - 298}{303}$$

$$\Delta P_s = 1.184 \times 9.81 \times 10 \times 0.01650$$

$$\Delta P_s \approx 1.917 \text{ Pa}$$

> *Interpretation:* A temperature difference of just 5°C across a 10 m height generates nearly **2 Pa** of pressure differential — sufficient to drive ventilation through large openings.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Ventilation Strategies

| Parameter | Natural Ventilation | Mechanical Ventilation | Hybrid (Mixed-Mode) |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------------|---------------------|
| **Driving Force** | Wind + Stack effect | Fan/blower power | Both, with sensor control |
| **Energy Consumption** | Very low (passive) | High (fan electricity) | Moderate (optimized) |
| **Capital Cost** | Low (windows, vents) | High (AHU, ducts, fans) | Medium-High |
| **Operational Cost** | Negligible | Significant | Lower than full mechanical |
| **Control Precision** | Low (weather-dependent) | High (precise ACH) | High (adaptive) |
| **Suitability — Kerala Climate** | Excellent (tropical) | Required for sealed AC spaces | Optimal for energy efficiency |
| **CO₂ Control** | Variable | Reliable | Highly reliable |
| **Maintenance** | Minimal | High (filters, belts, motors) | Moderate |
| **Green Rating Points (GRIHA/LEED)** | High | Low–Medium | Very High |
| **Acoustic Comfort** | May allow noise ingress | Excellent (with attenuation) | Configurable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Natural Ventilation System

The following diagram maps the functional architecture of a passive cross-ventilation system, illustrating how external climatic inputs drive air movement through a building envelope.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Outdoor Climate Inputs] --> B[Wind Pressure on Windward Facade]
    A --> C[Solar Radiation Heating Roof]
    B --> D[Positive Pressure Cp positive]
    C --> E[Stack Effect Buoyancy Force]
    D --> F[Air Inlet Window Low Level]
    E --> G[Air Outlet Vent High Level]
    F --> H[Room Airflow Path]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Indoor Space Occupants and Heat Sources]
    I --> J[Stale Air Exhaust]
    J --> K[Contaminant Removal CO2 VOCs PM]
    K --> L[Ventilation Effectiveness Achieved]
    L --> M{Sensor Monitoring}
    M --> N[CO2 Sensor]
    M --> O[Temperature Sensor]
    M --> P[Humidity Sensor]
    N --> Q[Control Logic]
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Operable Window Actuator]
    Q --> S[Auxiliary Fan Activation]
    R --> L
    S --> L
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Stack Effect Pressure Distribution in a Building

The following diagram illustrates the vertical pressure profile of a building experiencing the stack effect, where warm indoor air rises and creates pressure differentials relative to the cooler outdoor air.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cold Outdoor Air Density rho_o] --> B[Lower Opening Inlet]
    B --> C[Neutral Pressure Level NPL]
    C --> D[Warm Indoor Air Density rho_i lower than rho_o]
    D --> E[Upper Opening Outlet]
    E --> F[Hot Air Exhaust to Atmosphere]
    B -.Delta P negative.-> C
    C -.Delta P positive.-> E
    G[Height H between openings] --> C
    H[Temperature Difference T_i minus T_o] --> G
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow: Mechanical Ventilation with Heat Recovery (MVHR)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Outdoor Fresh Air Intake] --> B[Filter Stage G4 and F7]
    B --> C[Heat Exchanger Plate Type]
    C --> D[Supply Fan to Room]
    D --> E[Conditioned Indoor Space]
    E --> F[Extract Fan to Heat Exchanger]
    F --> G[Heat Recovery Efficiency 70 to 90 percent]
    G --> C
    F --> H[Stale Air Exhaust to Outside]
    I[CO2 and Humidity Sensors] --> J[BMS Control]
    J --> D
    J --> F
    J --> K[Variable Speed Fan Drive VFD]
```

## 4.4 Cross Ventilation Layout Plan View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wind Direction] --> B[Windward Window Inlet]
    B --> C[Room Interior Airflow Streamline]
    C --> D[Internal Partitions with Vents Below]
    C --> E[Leeward Window Outlet]
    E --> F[Negative Pressure Zone Wake]
    D --> G[Adjacent Room Receives Air]
    G --> H[Cross Flow Path Maintained]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Practical Insight:** In Kerala's tropical monsoon climate, optimal cross-ventilation design requires:
> 1. Window-to-wall ratio of **20–40%** on both windward and leeward sides.
> 2. Internal openings (vents in doors, transfer grilles) at **floor level** for unimpeded cross flow.
> 3. Building orientation along the **prevailing southwest/northeast wind axis** during monsoon.
> 4. Use of **ventilated skylights (monkey coolies)** in sloped roofs for stack-driven exhaust.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Air Changes per Hour (ACH)" and state its significance in ventilation design. Mention the recommended ACH values for a residential bedroom and a hospital operation theatre.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Air Changes per Hour (ACH) is the ratio of the volumetric flow rate of fresh air supplied to a space per hour to the volume of the space. Mathematically, $ACH = (Q \times 3600) / V$, where $Q$ is the flow rate in m³/s and $V$ is the room volume in m³.

**Significance (1 mark):** ACH quantifies ventilation intensity. It determines the rate at which stale air, CO₂, odors, and contaminants are flushed out, directly impacting indoor air quality, thermal comfort, and health.

**Recommended Values (1 mark):**
- Residential bedroom: **0.5 to 1.0 ACH**
- Hospital operation theatre: **15 to 25 ACH** (with HEPA filtration and positive pressure)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the "Stack Effect" with a neat sketch. Write the formula for stack pressure and identify the factors on which it depends.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Explanation (1.5 marks):** The stack effect is the natural upward movement of warm, less dense indoor air through vertical openings, replaced by cooler, denser outdoor air entering from below. It occurs due to the density difference between indoor and outdoor air and is most pronounced in tall buildings or buildings with high ceilings.

**Formula (1 mark):**
$$\Delta P_s = \rho_o \cdot g \cdot H \cdot \frac{T_i - T_o}{T_i}$$

**Factors (0.5 mark):** It depends on (i) the vertical height $H$ between openings, (ii) the temperature difference $(T_i - T_o)$, and (iii) the outdoor air density $\rho_o$.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECET752, Module 1]**

**(a)** A conference hall has dimensions **20 m × 15 m × 4.5 m (clear height)**. It accommodates **120 occupants**. The space is air-conditioned and requires mechanical ventilation. Compute the following:
- (i) The required fresh air supply rate in m³/s assuming **6 ACH**.
- (ii) The total fresh air supply in **liters per second per person (L/s·person)**, and verify if it satisfies ASHRAE 62.1 minimum of **8 L/s·person** for conference rooms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the difference between **natural, mechanical, and hybrid (mixed-mode) ventilation** strategies. For a residential building in **Kerala's tropical climate**, recommend the most appropriate strategy with at least three justifying points. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Given Data:**
- $L = 20$ m, $B = 15$ m, $H = 4.5$ m
- $N = 120$ occupants
- $ACH = 6$
- ASHRAE requirement: 8 L/s·person

**Step 1 — Volume calculation:**
$$V = 20 \times 15 \times 4.5 = 1350 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Required flow rate for 6 ACH:**
$$Q = \frac{ACH \times V}{3600} = \frac{6 \times 1350}{3600} = 2.25 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to L/s:**
$$Q = 2.25 \times 1000 = 2250 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 4 — Per-person ventilation rate:**
$$Q_{per\ person} = \frac{2250}{120} = 18.75 \text{ L/s·person}$$

**Step 5 — ASHRAE verification:**
Since 18.75 L/s·person **> 8 L/s·person**, the design **satisfies** ASHRAE 62.1 minimum ventilation rate requirement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The fresh air supply rate is **2.25 m³/s (2250 L/s)**, providing **18.75 L/s·person**, comfortably exceeding the ASHRAE minimum standard.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Strategy | Driver | Best Use |
|----------|--------|----------|
| Natural | Wind + Stack | Mild weather, low-rise |
| Mechanical | Fan power | Sealed/AC buildings |
| Hybrid | Both + controls | Variable climate zones |

**Recommendation: Hybrid (Mixed-Mode) Ventilation (4 marks):**

1. **Climate Suitability:** Kerala has high humidity (70–95%) and temperatures (24–34°C). Pure natural ventilation is comfortable only during dry months; pure mechanical is energy-intensive year-round. Hybrid adapts automatically.

2. **Energy Efficiency:** Saves 30–50% fan energy compared to full mechanical system, supporting ECBC compliance and GRIHA credit points.

3. **Comfort and IAQ Reliability:** Sensors (CO₂, temperature, RH) automatically modulate window actuators and fans, ensuring consistent indoor air quality regardless of outdoor conditions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert m³/s to L/s in ventilation problems (1 m³/s = 1000 L/s). Many students lose 1–2 marks here.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using 1.2 kg/m³ for air density without noting it assumes 20°C standard conditions; in tropical climates, use **1.184 kg/m³ at 25°C** for higher accuracy.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing ACH with air velocity (m/s). ACH is a volumetric metric; air velocity is measured by anemometer.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECET752, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the **stack pressure equation** in detail. A factory shed has a **ridge vent at 8 m height** and **low-level louvers at 1.5 m height**. If the indoor air temperature is **35°C (308 K)** and outdoor temperature is **28°C (301 K)**, calculate the stack pressure differential. Take outdoor air density = **1.16 kg/m³**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a labeled diagram, describe the principles of **cross ventilation**. List **four design guidelines** for maximizing cross ventilation in a tropical building. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Given:**
- $H_{ridge} = 8$ m, $H_{low} = 1.5$ m → $H = 6.5$ m
- $T_i = 308$ K, $T_o = 301$ K
- $\rho_o = 1.16$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Stack Pressure Formula:**
$$\Delta P_s = \rho_o \cdot g \cdot H \cdot \frac{T_i - T_o}{T_i}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**
$$\Delta P_s = 1.16 \times 9.81 \times 6.5 \times \frac{308 - 301}{308}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify temperature ratio:**
$$\frac{7}{308} = 0.02273$$

**Step 4 — Multiply all terms:**
$$\Delta P_s = 1.16 \times 9.81 \times 6.5 \times 0.02273$$

$$\Delta P_s = 1.6816 \text{ Pa} \approx 1.68 \text{ Pa}$$

> **Conclusion:** The stack pressure differential is **1.68 Pa**, sufficient to drive approximately **0.3–0.5 m³/s** of airflow per square meter of opening.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Principle of Cross Ventilation (3 marks):**
Cross ventilation occurs when wind enters through an inlet opening on the windward façade and exits through an outlet on the leeward façade. The pressure differential (positive on windward, negative on leeward) creates a horizontal air stream that sweeps across the room, providing uniform ventilation and cooling.

**Four Design Guidelines for Tropical Buildings (4 marks):**

1. **Building Orientation:** Align the long axis **perpendicular to the prevailing wind direction** to maximize pressure differential.
2. **Window-to-Wall Ratio:** Provide **20–40% opening area** on both windward and leeward walls.
3. **Internal Layout:** Use **low partitions (1.2 m height)** or door undercuts (100 mm gap) to allow air to flow between rooms; avoid solid partitions.
4. **External Shading:** Use **chajjas, louvers, or jaalis** that block direct solar radiation but allow unobstructed airflow, preventing back-pressure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q.B):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Using $\rho_i$ instead of $\rho_o$ in the stack pressure formula. The standard form uses **outdoor density as the reference**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert Celsius to **Kelvin** (add 273.15). The formula requires **absolute temperature**; using Celsius directly gives the wrong answer.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not specifying whether wind speed is at 10 m (meteorological standard) or at building height. For KTU problems, always use $v_{ref} = v_{met} (h/10)^{0.15}$ for correction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Air Ventilation (KTU Module 1):**

- **Ventilation =** Controlled air movement for IAQ, comfort, and moisture control.
- **Three types:** Natural (wind + stack), Mechanical (fan-driven), Hybrid (mixed-mode).
- **ACH formula:** $ACH = (Q \times 3600) / V$ — most frequently asked equation.
- **Stack pressure:** $\Delta P_s = \rho_o g H (T_i - T_o) / T_i$ — **always use Kelvin**.
- **Wind pressure:** $P_w = 0.5 \rho v^2 C_p$ — Bernoulli's principle applied to buildings.
- **Standard air density:** **1.2 kg/m³** (at 20°C, 101.325 kPa); **1.184 kg/m³** at 25°C.
- **ASHRAE 62.1 IAQ Standard** — primary reference for ventilation rates.
- **Typical ACH values:** Residential **0.5–1**, Office **4–8**, Classroom **6**, Hospital OT **15–25**.
- **CO₂ threshold:** **1000 ppm** above outdoor baseline (~420 ppm currently).
- **Cross ventilation** needs openings on **opposite façades**; **stack ventilation** needs openings at **different heights**.
- **Sustainable design integration:** ECBC compliance, GRIHA/LEED credits, reduced fan power.
- **Kerala-specific:** Tropical climate favors **cross + stack hybrid** with monsoon-resistant operable louver systems.
- **Key design ratios:** Window-to-Wall Ratio (**WWR**) of 20–40% for cross ventilation.
- **Common conversion:** 1 m³/s = **1000 L/s = 3600 m³/hr**.
- **Discharge coefficient $C_d$:** 0.6 (sharp orifice) to 0.95 (rounded inlet).
- **Wind velocity correction** for height: $v_h = v_{10} (h/10)^{0.15}$ (power law exponent 0.15 for open terrain).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy for KTU Exam:**
> 1. Memorize the **ACH and stack pressure equations** with their units.
> 2. Practice at least **3 numerical problems** on ACH calculation.
> 3. Be ready to **sketch** cross ventilation, stack effect, and a ventilation layout.
> 4. Connect your answers to **Kerala climate** and **green building codes** — examiners reward contextual application.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exhaust Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET752 - SUSTAINABLE CONSTRUCTION PRACTICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Exhaust Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Exhaust System** in the context of sustainable building construction is a network of ducts, fans, terminals, and associated control devices designed to remove contaminated, stale, or excess air from specific zones of a building (such as kitchens, toilets, laboratories, basements, parking areas, and industrial process rooms) while maintaining acceptable **Indoor Air Quality (IAQ)**, energy efficiency, and occupant comfort. As per KTU 2024 sustainable design pedagogy, an exhaust system is not merely a "smoke-removal" utility — it is an engineered environmental control system that contributes to **ventilation effectiveness**, **thermal management**, **pollutant dilution**, and **energy recovery**, forming a critical subset of the building's mechanical (HVAC) services.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Highlight:** The focus of "Exhaust Systems" in *Sustainable Construction Practices* is on the **sustainable design, energy recovery potential, low-flow optimization, and green-building compliance** of exhaust systems, rather than purely on industrial pollution control. Concepts align with **IGBC, LEED v4, GRIHA, and ASHRAE 62.1/90.1** standards.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine a building as a living, breathing organism. The HVAC system is its **lungs**:
- The **supply system** is the **inhalation** (bringing in fresh, conditioned air).
- The **exhaust system** is the **exhalation** (expelling stale, contaminated, or moisture-laden air).

Without proper exhalation, the organism suffocates — stale air accumulates, CO₂ rises, humidity soars, odors persist, and harmful VOCs (Volatile Organic Compounds) build up. But in a *sustainable* building, even the exhaled breath is not wasted — it passes through a **Heat Recovery Ventilator (HRV)** or **Energy Recovery Ventilator (ERV)** that "reclaims" the energy before the air is finally released to the atmosphere, just like a smart human reuses body heat through nasal counter-flow exchange.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Standards)

- **ACH** (Air Changes per Hour) — number of times the total air volume of a room is replaced in one hour. **ASHRAE 62.1** minimums typically range from **0.35 to 1.0 ACH** for offices, **15–25 ACH** for hospital isolation rooms, and **10–15 ACH** for commercial kitchens.
- **CFM** (Cubic Feet per Minute) or **L/s** (Litres per second) — the volumetric flow rate. **1 CFM = 0.4719 L/s**.
- **Face Velocity** at exhaust grilles — typically **0.5 to 1.0 m/s** for general exhaust, **1.5 to 2.5 m/s** for kitchen exhaust hoods.
- **Duct Velocity** — main ducts: **5 to 10 m/s**; branch ducts: **3 to 5 m/s**; terminal devices: **2 to 4 m/s** (for acoustic comfort).
- **Specific Fan Power (SFP)** — fan power (W) per unit airflow (m³/s). **SFP < 1.6 kW/(m³/s)** is a typical green-building benchmark.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — SFP:** Specific Fan Power quantifies the *electrical energy efficiency* of the exhaust system. Lower SFP = greener exhaust system. It is a direct KTU-relevant metric in energy audit calculations.

### 1.4 Why Exhaust Systems Matter in Sustainable Construction

In conventional (non-sustainable) construction, exhaust air is simply dumped outside — a 100% energy loss. In a **sustainable building**:
1. **Energy Recovery** — exhaust heat is captured via HRV/ERV to pre-condition incoming fresh air, reducing HVAC load by **30–80%**.
2. **Pollution Dispersion Modeling** — exhaust outlets are placed to avoid re-entrainment of contaminants back into intakes (minimum **3 m to 10 m** separation or per ASHRAE 62.1 Table 5.5.1).
3. **Variable Air Volume (VAV) exhaust** — fan speed modulates with demand instead of running at full capacity, cutting fan energy by **30–60%**.
4. **Demand-Controlled Ventilation (DCV)** — CO₂ or VOC sensors adjust exhaust rates in real time.
5. **Source Capture & Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** — captures pollutants at the source, drastically reducing overall airflow needs.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Exhaust Systems

Exhaust systems in sustainable construction are classified along multiple axes:

- **By Purpose:**
  - *General exhaust* — toilet/bathroom exhaust, basement ventilation.
  - *Process exhaust* — kitchen exhaust, laboratory fume hoods, welding bays.
  - *Smoke exhaust* — fire/smoke management per NBC 2016 Part 4 (Clause 4.6).
  - *Pollution-control exhaust* — parking garages, diesel generator rooms, transformer rooms.

- **By Operating Mode:**
  - *Intermittent exhaust* — on/off operation, common in toilets and storerooms.
  - *Continuous exhaust* — 24×7 operation, e.g., hospital isolation rooms, server rooms.
  - *On-demand exhaust* — sensor-triggered, e.g., CO/CO₂ triggered parking exhaust.

- **By Pressure Strategy:**
  - *Negative-pressure exhaust* — most common, the space is held at slight negative pressure relative to surroundings.
  - *Neutral-pressure balanced exhaust* — paired with supply air, common in offices (HRV/ERV).
  - *Positive-pressure relief exhaust* — used in cleanrooms to prevent contaminant ingress.

- **By Sustainability Tier:**
  - *Tier 1 — Basic exhaust* (dumped, no recovery).
  - *Tier 2 — Exhaust with VFD* (variable frequency drives for fan speed control).
  - *Tier 3 — Heat/Energy Recovery exhaust* (HRV/ERV integrated).
  - *Tier 4 — Smart demand-controlled exhaust* (sensor-based, AI-driven).

### 2.2 The Core 'Why' and 'How' of Sustainable Exhaust Design

**Why is sustainable exhaust design non-negotiable in modern KTU curriculum?**
- Buildings account for ~**30% of global final energy consumption** (UN Environment Programme, IEA 2023).
- Exhaust fans are typically among the **top 5 energy end-uses** in commercial buildings.
- Improper exhaust design causes **Sick Building Syndrome (SBS)** — a recognized occupational health issue.

**How does the system achieve sustainability?**
1. **Reduce** — minimize the volume of air exhausted (use local/LEV, demand-control).
2. **Recover** — capture sensible and latent heat in the exhaust stream (HRV/ERV).
3. **Reuse** — redirect recovered energy to pre-heat/cool incoming fresh air.
4. **Recommission** — periodic testing, balancing, and controls retrofits.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units | Boundary Conditions / Notes |
|---|--------------------|-------------|-------|------------------------------|
| 1 | $Q = A \times V$ | Volumetric flow rate from duct cross-section | m³/s | $A$ = duct area (m²), $V$ = air velocity (m/s) |
| 2 | $Q_{room} = \dfrac{V_{room} \times ACH}{3600}$ | Room exhaust flow rate | m³/s | $V_{room}$ in m³, ACH in h⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\Delta P_{friction} = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^{2}}{2}$ | Frictional pressure drop in duct (Darcy-Weisbach) | Pa | $f$ ≈ 0.02 (smooth ducts), $\rho_{air} \approx 1.2$ kg/m³ |
| 4 | $P_{fan} = \dfrac{Q \times \Delta P_{total}}{\eta_{fan}}$ | Required fan shaft power | W (or kW) | $\eta_{fan}$ = 0.5–0.85; SFP = $P/Q$ |
| 5 | $SFP = \dfrac{P_{fan}}{Q}$ | Specific Fan Power | W/(m³/s) or kW/(m³/s) | Green benchmark $\leq 1.6$ kW/(m³/s) (ECBC+) |
| 6 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{T_{sup,in} - T_{fresh,in}}{T_{exh,in} - T_{fresh,in}}$ | Heat Recovery Efficiency (sensible HRV) | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Typical HRV: 0.60–0.80; ERV: 0.50–0.75 |
| 7 | $E_{saved} = \rho \cdot c_{p} \cdot Q \cdot \Delta T \cdot \varepsilon \cdot t$ | Energy recovered per unit time | J (or kWh) | $c_{p,air} \approx 1005$ J/(kg·K) |
| 8 | $N_{CO_2} = \dfrac{G}{Q \times (C_{s} - C_{e})}$ | CO₂ concentration in steady-state (mass balance) | ppm | $G$ = CO₂ generation rate (L/s per person ≈ 0.005 L/s) |
| 9 | $v_{face} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{grille}}$ | Grille face velocity | m/s | General exhaust: 0.5–1.0 m/s; kitchen hood: 0.5–1.0 m/s; fume hood: 0.4–0.6 m/s |
| 10 | $A_{opening} = \dfrac{Q}{K \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P}}$ | Free area of an opening (Orifice equation) | m² | $K$ ≈ 0.65 for sharp-edged orifice |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Board Exam Tip (KTU):** Always quote $\rho_{air} = 1.2$ kg/m³ and $c_{p} = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K) unless the question explicitly states otherwise. Marks are lost on units!

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of Exhaust System Equations

- **Equation 1–2** → used by MEP design engineers to size ducts and fans.
- **Equation 3–4** → used to estimate electricity consumption for fan selection and energy audit reports.
- **Equation 6–7** → used to justify HRV/ERV in IGBC/LEED documentation and compute payback period.
- **Equation 8** → used to design **Demand-Controlled Ventilation (DCV)** based on CO₂ setpoint (typically 800–1000 ppm above outdoor).
- **Equation 9** → used for acoustic comfort and draft avoidance.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Room Exhaust Flow Rate from ACH

**Statement:** Derive the volumetric exhaust flow rate $Q$ required to achieve a target Air Changes per Hour (ACH) for a room of given volume.

**Given:** Room volume $V_{room}$ (m³), target ACH ($h^{-1}$), time interval $t = 1$ hour = 3600 seconds.

**Step 1 — Define ACH.**
Air Changes per Hour is defined as the number of times the entire air volume of a space is replaced per hour.

$$ACH = \dfrac{Q \times t}{V_{room}}$$

where $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate in m³/s, and $t$ is in seconds.

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for Q.**

$$Q = \dfrac{ACH \times V_{room}}{t}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $t = 3600$ s (one hour).**

$$Q = \dfrac{ACH \times V_{room}}{3600} \quad \text{[m}^3/\text{s]}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to L/s if required (multiply by 1000).**

$$Q_{L/s} = \dfrac{ACH \times V_{room} \times 1000}{3600} = \dfrac{ACH \times V_{room}}{3.6}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-type):**
A $4$ m × $5$ m × $3$ m kitchen requires **15 ACH** as per ASHRAE 62.1. Find the exhaust flow rate in m³/s and L/s.

Step 1 — Room volume:
$$V_{room} = 4 \times 5 \times 3 = 60 \text{ m}^3$$

Step 2 — Apply the formula:
$$Q = \dfrac{15 \times 60}{3600} = \dfrac{900}{3600} = 0.25 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Step 3 — Convert:
$$Q = 0.25 \times 1000 = 250 \text{ L/s}$$

> **Conversion Logic Note:** The factor 3600 converts hours → seconds. Always carry units through every step in the KTU answer sheet — full-mark answers invariably show unit cancellation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Fan Power and Specific Fan Power (SFP)

**Statement:** Derive the Specific Fan Power (SFP) of an exhaust system and the electrical energy it consumes annually.

**Given:** Exhaust flow rate $Q$ (m³/s), total system pressure drop $\Delta P_{total}$ (Pa), fan efficiency $\eta_{fan}$ (decimal), hours of operation $t_{annual}$ (h).

**Step 1 — Fan shaft (output) power from first principles.**

By definition, fluid power delivered by the fan equals pressure rise times volumetric flow rate:

$$P_{shaft} = Q \times \Delta P_{total} \quad [\text{W}]$$

**Step 2 — Account for fan efficiency to obtain electrical input power.**

The fan efficiency is the ratio of output (air) power to input (electrical) power:

$$\eta_{fan} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{P_{electrical}}$$

Rearranging:

$$P_{electrical} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{fan}} = \dfrac{Q \times \Delta P_{total}}{\eta_{fan}} \quad [\text{W}]$$

**Step 3 — Define Specific Fan Power (SFP).**

$$SFP = \dfrac{P_{electrical}}{Q} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{total}}{\eta_{fan}} \quad [\text{W/(m}^3/\text{s)}]$$

In green-building practice, SFP is reported in **W·s/m³ = J/m³**, but the more common unit is **kW/(m³/s)** by dividing the above by 1000.

**Step 4 — Annual energy consumption.**

$$E_{annual} = P_{electrical} \times t_{annual} \quad [\text{Wh}]$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-type):**
A toilet exhaust fan handles $Q = 0.1$ m³/s against a total pressure of $\Delta P = 250$ Pa. Fan efficiency = 0.6. Operating 24×7, find (a) fan input power, (b) SFP, (c) annual energy in kWh, (d) whether SFP meets the ECBC+ benchmark of 1.6 kW/(m³/s).

Step (a) — Input power:
$$P_{electrical} = \dfrac{0.1 \times 250}{0.6} = \dfrac{25}{0.6} = 41.67 \text{ W}$$

Step (b) — SFP:
$$SFP = \dfrac{41.67}{0.1} = 416.7 \text{ W/(m}^3/\text{s)} = 0.417 \text{ kW/(m}^3/\text{s)}$$

Step (c) — Annual energy (t = 8760 h):
$$E_{annual} = 41.67 \times 8760 = 365{,}030 \text{ Wh} \approx 365 \text{ kWh}$$

Step (d) — Compare to benchmark:
$$0.417 \text{ kW/(m}^3/\text{s)} \ll 1.6 \text{ kW/(m}^3/\text{s)}$$

Result: **Comfortably meets the green-building benchmark** (with >70% margin). ✅

> **Conversion Logic Note:** $\Delta P \times Q$ gives Watts because $1$ Pa = $1$ N/m² and $1$ N·m/s = $1$ W. This is the single most important dimensional check in the entire HVAC syllabus.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Heat Recovery Effectiveness and Energy Saved

**Statement:** Derive the energy recovered per hour by a sensible Heat Recovery Ventilator (HRV) installed on an exhaust system.

**Given:** Exhaust flow rate $Q$ (m³/s), outdoor temperature $T_{out}$ (°C or K), exhaust temperature $T_{exh}$ (°C or K), HRV sensible effectiveness $\varepsilon_{s}$ (decimal), operating hours $t$ (s).

**Step 1 — Sensible heat content in exhaust air per unit time (above outdoor baseline).**

The mass flow rate of exhaust air:
$$\dot{m} = \rho \times Q \quad [\text{kg/s}]$$

Sensible heat carried away per second:
$$\dot{Q}_{heat} = \dot{m} \times c_{p} \times (T_{exh} - T_{out}) \quad [\text{W}]$$

**Step 2 — Apply HRV effectiveness.**

The HRV transfers a fraction $\varepsilon_{s}$ of this heat to the incoming fresh air:

$$\dot{Q}_{recovered} = \varepsilon_{s} \times \dot{m} \times c_{p} \times (T_{exh} - T_{out}) \quad [\text{W}]$$

**Step 3 — Energy saved over time $t$:**

$$E_{saved} = \dot{Q}_{recovered} \times t \quad [\text{J}]$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent in kWh (for energy-bill savings):**

$$E_{saved,kWh} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{recovered} \times t}{3.6 \times 10^{6}}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-type):**
A commercial building exhausts $Q = 1.0$ m³/s at $T_{exh} = 24$°C. Outdoor air is at $T_{out} = 10$°C (winter). HRV sensible effectiveness = 0.70. Operating 12 hours/day for 150 heating days. Find annual recovered heat in kWh.

Step 1 — Mass flow:
$$\dot{m} = 1.2 \times 1.0 = 1.2 \text{ kg/s}$$

Step 2 — Instantaneous recovered heat:
$$\dot{Q}_{recovered} = 0.70 \times 1.2 \times 1005 \times (24 - 10) = 0.70 \times 1.2 \times 1005 \times 14$$
$$\dot{Q}_{recovered} = 11{,}822 \text{ W} \approx 11.82 \text{ kW}$$

Step 3 — Total operating hours:
$$t = 12 \times 150 = 1800 \text{ h} = 6.48 \times 10^{6} \text{ s}$$

Step 4 — Annual recovered energy:
$$E_{saved} = 11{,}822 \times 6.48 \times 10^{6} = 7.66 \times 10^{10} \text{ J}$$

$$E_{saved,kWh} = \dfrac{7.66 \times 10^{10}}{3.6 \times 10^{6}} \approx 21{,}280 \text{ kWh/year}$$

Assuming electricity cost of ₹8/kWh, the **annual monetary saving ≈ ₹1,70,240**, often justifying the HRV payback in 2–4 years.

> **Conversion Logic Note:** $1$ kWh = $3.6 \times 10^6$ J exactly. This is the second most important dimensional check in KTU energy-recovery problems.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Exhaust System Sustainability Tiers

| Sustainability Tier | Energy Recovery | Fan Control | Sensor Integration | Typical SFP (kW/(m³/s)) | LEED/IGBC Credit | Payback |
|---------------------|-----------------|-------------|--------------------|--------------------------|------------------|---------|
| Tier 1 — Basic Dump | None | Single-speed | None | 2.0 – 3.0 | None | — |
| Tier 2 — VFD Retrofit | None | Variable | None | 1.0 – 1.6 | Possible (1 pt) | 1–2 yr |
| Tier 3 — HRV/ERV | 60–80% | Variable | Partial | 1.2 – 1.8 | Strong (2–3 pts) | 2–5 yr |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Sustainable Exhaust System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *functional architecture* of a typical sustainable exhaust system in a commercial building — the interactions between supply, exhaust, energy recovery, and control loops.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Indoor Occupied Zone] --> B1[Contaminated Air Plenum]
    B1 --> C1[Exhaust Air Terminal Grille]
    C1 --> D1[Exhaust Duct Network]
    D1 --> E1[Filter Bank G4 plus F7]
    E1 --> F1[Heat Recovery Wheel HRV or ERV]
    F1 --> G1[Exhaust Fan with VFD Control]
    G1 --> H1[Exhaust Stack with Rain Cap]
    H1 --> I1[Atmospheric Discharge]
    F1 -. Heat Recovery Loop .-> J1[Fresh Air Intake Louver]
    J1 --> K1[Supply Filter Bank]
    K1 --> L1[Pre-Conditioned Fresh Air Plenum]
    L1 --> M1[AHU or DOAS Unit]
    M1 --> N1[Supply Fan with EC Motor]
    N1 --> O1[Supplied Air Diffusers]
    O1 --> A1
    P1[CO2 Sensor Indoor] -. DCV Feedback .-> G1
    P2[CO Sensor Parking Zone] -. On-Demand Trigger .-> G1
    P3[EMS Building Management System] -. Modbus or BACnet .-> G1
    P3 -. Modbus or BACnet .-> N1
    P3 -. Modbus or BACnet .-> F1
    P4[Outdoor Air Temp and RH Sensor] -. Adaptive Setpoint .-> P3
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **solid black arrows** show the *physical airflow path* of the contaminated exhaust stream (left side: building → discharge).
- The **dashed arrows** depict the *energy-recovery feedback loop* (heat from exhaust pre-warms/colds the incoming fresh air).
- The **dotted control arrows** represent the *sensor and BMS (Building Management System) signals* that modulate fan speed, damper position, and wheel rotation.

### 4.2 Tiered Sustainability Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start New Exhaust System Design] --> S1{Building Type and Use}
    S1 -->|Residential| S2[Basic Intermittent Exhaust Tier 1]

```
