# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** Define the Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC). State the eligibility criteria for a building to come under ECBC compliance.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

ECBC, developed by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** in **2007**, is a regulatory code that prescribes **minimum energy performance standards** for the design and construction of **commercial buildings** in India.

**Eligibility Criteria:** A building must have a **connected load of 100 kW or more** OR a **contract demand of 120 kVA or more** to be subject to ECBC compliance. The code covers building envelope, HVAC, lighting, electrical systems, and renewable energy integration.

*(Examiner's Note: Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for eligibility threshold, 1 mark for scope.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** List any **three** compliance approaches prescribed by ECBC.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The three compliance approaches under ECBC are:

1. **Prescriptive Method:** Each component (wall, roof, window, HVAC, lighting) is individually designed to meet the prescribed performance values.
2. **Trade-off Method:** Under-performing components are compensated by over-performing ones, ensuring the overall Envelop Performance Factor (EPF) does not exceed the reference value.
3. **Whole Building Performance Method:** Annual energy simulation is performed, and the proposed building's Energy Cost Index (ECI) must be $\leq$ the ECBC baseline ECI.

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT**
> - **Do NOT confuse ECBC (commercial) with ENS (residential)** — they have separate prescriptive tables.
> - **Always state units** (W/m²K, Rs/m²/year, kW/ton) — failure to do so costs 1–2 marks per sub-question.
> - **Mention BEE + 2007** in the first sentence of any ECBC definition question — a common omission costing 0.5 mark.
> - **Numerical questions on U-value require FULL layer-by-layer derivation** — skipping the surface film resistances ($R_{\text{si}}$, $R_{\text{se}}$) loses 1 mark.
> - **Trade-off method ≠ Performance method** — students frequently swap them; the former uses EPF, the latter uses ECI.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified for Module 1 ECBC]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three compliance approaches** prescribed by ECBC. For each approach, state the governing compliance parameter and one advantage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A multi-storey office building in **Ahmedabad (Hot & Dry climate)** has the following envelope composition:
- **East Wall (Opaque):** Area = 250 m², U-value = 0.65 W/m²K
- **East Window (Glazing):** Area = 80 m², SHGC = 0.45, Shading Factor = 0.85
- **Roof (Opaque):** Area = 600 m², U-value = 0.55 W/m²K

**ECBC Reference Values (Ahmedabad):**
- Wall U$_{\text{max}}$ = 0.440 W/m²K
- Roof U$_{\text{max}}$ = 0.409 W/m²K
- Window SHGC$_{\text{max}}$ = 0.25
- Shading Factor = 1.0 (no overhangs)

Calculate the **Envelop Performance Factor (EPF)** of the proposed building. Check whether the building is **EPF-compliant** with ECBC for the Hot & Dry climate. If not, suggest **two remedies**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Solution to Part (a): Three ECBC Compliance Approaches (7 Marks)

**1. Prescriptive Method (Component-Based) – 2 Marks**

In this approach, every individual building component must independently satisfy the maximum permissible U-value, SHGC, VLT, COP, and LPD values specified in ECBC prescriptive tables for the applicable climate zone.

- **Governing Parameter:** Component-level U-value, SHGC, LPD, COP.
- **Advantage:** Simple, no simulation required; suitable for conventional designs.

**2. Trade-off Method (Envelop Trade-Off) – 2 Marks**

This approach allows flexibility by permitting under-performing components in some areas, provided the **overall Envelop Performance Factor (EPF)** of the proposed building is less than or equal to the EPF calculated using prescriptive reference values.

- **Governing Parameter:** $\text{EPF} = \sum U_i A_i + \sum \text{SHGC}_j A_j \cdot \text{SF}$ (W/K equivalent).
- **Advantage:** Architectural flexibility without sacrificing overall energy performance.

**3. Whole Building Performance Method – 2 Marks**

This approach uses **hourly energy simulation** (EnergyPlus, eQuest, DesignBuilder) to calculate the **annual Energy Cost Index (ECI)**. The building is compliant if $\text{ECI}_{\text{proposed}} \leq \text{ECI}_{\text{baseline}}$.

- **Governing Parameter:** $\text{ECI} = \dfrac{C_{\text{annual}}}{A_{\text{conditioned}}}$ (Rs/m²/year).
- **Advantage:** Allows innovative design freedom; accounts for occupancy and operational diversity. **(1 Mark for conclusion)**

#### Solution to Part (b): EPF Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Calculate Opaque Component Heat Flow Contribution (2 Marks)**

East Wall contribution:
$$U_{\text{wall}} \times A_{\text{wall}} = 0.65 \times 250 = 162.5 \text{ W/K}$$

Roof contribution:
$$U_{\text{roof}} \times A_{\text{roof}} = 0.55 \times 600 = 330.0 \text{ W/K}$$

Total opaque contribution:
$$\sum U_i A_i = 162.5 + 330.0 = 492.5 \text{ W/K}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Fenestration Contribution (2 Marks)**

East Window contribution (using proposed SHGC = 0.45 and SF = 0.85):
$$\text{SHGC}_{\text{prop}} \times A_{\text{window}} \times \text{SF} = 0.45 \times 80 \times 0.85 = 30.6 \text{ W/K}$$

**Step 3: Total EPF of Proposed Building (1 Mark)**

$$\text{EPF}_{\text{proposed}} = 492.5 + 30.6 = 523.1 \text{ W/K}$$

**Step 4: Calculate EPF of Reference Building (1 Mark)**

Using reference values: Wall U = 0.440, Roof U = 0.409, Window SHGC = 0.25, SF = 1.0 (no overhangs).

Wall (ref): $0.440 \times 250 = 110.0$ W/K
Roof (ref): $0.409 \times 600 = 245.4$ W/K
Window (ref): $0.25 \times 80 \times 1.0 = 20.0$ W/K

$$\text{EPF}_{\text{reference}} = 110.0 + 245.4 + 20.0 = 375.4 \text{ W/K}$$

**Step 5: Compliance Check (0.5 Mark)**

$$\text{EPF}_{\text{proposed}} = 523.1 \text{ W/K} > \text{EPF}_{\text{reference}} = 375.4 \text{ W/K}$$

$$\therefore \textbf{NOT ECBC-COMPLIANT}$$

**Step 6: Two Remedies (0.5 Mark)**

1. **Wall Insulation:** Add external insulation (e.g., 50 mm EPS) to reduce wall U-value from 0.65 to $\leq$ 0.40 W/m²K.
2. **Window Glazing Upgrade:** Replace single-glazed windows with **double-glazed low-E glazing** having SHGC $\leq$ 0.25 to bring window contribution to 20.0 W/K.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Discuss the **ECBC tiered classification system** (ECBC, ECBC+, SuperECBC). Include the ECI thresholds and the rationale for introducing the three-tier system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **composite wall** of a commercial building consists of the following layers (from outside to inside):
- 20 mm cement plaster ($\lambda$ = 0.91 W/mK)
- 230 mm brick masonry ($\lambda$ = 0.81 W/mK)
- 50 mm EPS insulation ($\lambda$ = 0.038 W/mK)
- 12 mm gypsum plaster ($\lambda$ = 0.42 W/mK)

Surface resistances: $R_{\text{si}}$ = 0.130 m²K/W, $R_{\text{se}}$ = 0.040 m²K/W.

**Calculate** the overall thermal transmittance (U-value) of the wall. If ECBC prescribes a **maximum U-value of 0.440 W/m²K** for this climate zone, determine whether the wall is ECBC-compliant. If not, calculate the **minimum additional EPS thickness** required. **(7 Marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### Solution to Part (a): ECBC Tiered Classification (7 Marks)

**1. ECBC (Tier 1) – 2 Marks**

- **ECI Threshold:** $\text{ECI}_{\text{proposed}} \leq \text{ECI}_{\text{baseline}}$ (i.e., 100% of baseline).
- This is the **minimum mandatory standard**, introduced to ensure new commercial buildings achieve at least the prescriptive energy performance benchmark.

**2. ECBC+ (Tier 2) – 2 Marks**

- **ECI Threshold:** $\text{ECI}_{\text{proposed}} \leq 0.75 \times \text{ECI}_{\text{baseline}}$.
- Represents **25% energy savings** over standard ECBC. Recognized for buildings that go beyond mandatory compliance, often through superior HVAC efficiency and high-performance glazing.

**3. SuperECBC (Tier 3) – 2 Marks**

- **ECI Threshold:** $\text{ECI}_{\text{proposed}} \leq 0.50 \times \text{ECI}_{\text{baseline}}$.
- Represents **50% energy savings**, paving the way for **net-zero energy buildings**. Often achieved by integrating renewable energy, daylight harvesting, and ground-source heat pumps.

**4. Rationale for Three-Tier System (1 Mark)**

The three-tier system was introduced to:
- **Reward energy performance** beyond compliance, encouraging voluntary innovation.
- Provide a **graduated pathway** for designers, allowing incremental improvements.
- **Align with international benchmarks** such as ASHRAE 90.1 and LEED.
- Drive India's commitments under the **Paris Agreement** to reduce building-sector emissions.

#### Solution to Part (b): U-value Calculation and Compliance (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Layer-by-Layer Resistance Calculation (3 Marks)**

$$R_1 = \frac{0.020}{0.91} = 0.0220 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_2 = \frac{0.230}{0.81} = 0.2840 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_3 = \frac{0.050}{0.038} = 1.3158 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_4 = \frac{0.012}{0.42} = 0.0286 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 2: Total Resistance (1 Mark)**

$$R_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{si}} + R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_4 + R_{\text{se}}$$

$$R_{\text{total}} = 0.130 + 0.0220 + 0.2840 + 1.3158 + 0.0286 + 0.040$$

$$R_{\text{total}} = 1.8204 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 3: Calculate U-value (1 Mark)**

$$U = \frac{1}{R_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{1.8204} = 0.5493 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 4: Compliance Check (1 Mark)**

$$U_{\text{calculated}} = 0.5493 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K} > U_{\text{max}} = 0.440 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

$$\therefore \textbf{NOT ECBC-COMPLIANT}$$

**Step 5: Required Additional EPS Thickness (1 Mark)**

For compliance, $U \leq 0.440$, so $R_{\text{total}} \geq \dfrac{1}{0.440} = 2.2727$ m²K/W.

Required $R_3^{\text{new}}$:
$$R_3^{\text{new}} = 2.2727 - (0.130 + 0.0220 + 0.2840 + 0.0286 + 0.040) = 1.7681 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

New required EPS thickness:
$$L_3^{\text{new}} = R_3^{\text{new}} \times \lambda_3 = 1.7681 \times 0.038 = 0.0672 \text{ m} = 67.2 \text{ mm}$$

**Additional thickness required:**
$$\Delta L = 67.2 - 50 = 17.2 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Add at least 18 mm of extra EPS insulation to achieve ECBC compliance.}}$$

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — ECBC**

- ✅ **ECBC** = Energy Conservation Building Code, developed by **BEE, 2007**.
- ✅ **Applies to:** Commercial buildings with **connected load $\geq$ 100 kW** or **contract demand $\geq$ 120 kVA**.
- ✅ **Three Compliance Approaches:** Prescriptive, Trade-off (EPF), Whole Building Performance (ECI).
- ✅ **Three Tiers:** ECBC (100%), ECBC+ (75%), SuperECBC (50%) — all based on ECI.
- ✅ **Building Envelope Key Parameters:** U-value, SHGC, VLT, WWR.
- ✅ **Climate Zones (India):** Composite, Hot & Dry, Warm & Humid, Temperate, Cold.
- ✅ **U-value Formula:** $U = \dfrac{1}{R_{\text{total}}}$ where $R_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{si}} + \sum \dfrac{L_k}{\lambda_k} + R_{\text{se}}$.
- ✅ **ECI Formula:** $\text{ECI} = \dfrac{C_{\text{annual}}}{A_{\text{conditioned}}}$ in **Rs/m²/year**.
- ✅ **EPF Formula:** $\sum U_i A_i + \sum \text{SHGC}_j A_j \cdot \text{SF}$ in **W/K**.
- ✅ **COP Formula:** $\text{COP} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{cooling}}}{W_{\text{input}}}$ (dimensionless).
- ✅ **LPD Formula:** $\text{LPD} = \dfrac{P_{\text{total,lighting}}}{A_{\text{illuminated}}}$ in **W/m²**.
- ✅ **Recommended WWR $\leq$ 40%.**
- ✅ **Required surface film resistances:** $R_{\text{si}} = 0.130$, $R_{\text{se}} = 0.040$ m²K/W.
- ✅ **Renewable Energy:** Minimum **1% of total energy** to be from on-site RE sources.
- ✅ **ECBC ≠ ENS** (Eco Niwas Samhita is for residential buildings).
- ✅ **Always carry units** in every numerical step — **a major mark-grabber in ESE**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ASHRAE
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET752 - SUSTAINABLE CONSTRUCTION PRACTICES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** ASHRAE # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of ASHRAE ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **ASHRAE (American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers)** is a global professional society founded in **1959** (formed by the merger of ASHVE and ASRE) that develops standards, guidelines, and research publications focused on **building systems, indoor environmental quality, refrigeration, and energy efficiency in the built environment**. In the context of **Sustainable Construction Practices (PECET752)**, ASHRAE is positioned as a **technical standard-setting body** whose codes form the **engineering backbone** of high-performance green buildings. For KTU 2024 Scheme students, the most relevant ASHRAE documents are: | Standard Code | Full Title | Domain | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **ASHRAE 90.1** | Energy Standard for Buildings Except Low-Rise Residential Buildings | Energy Efficiency | | **ASHRAE 62.1** | Ventilation for Acceptable Indoor Air Quality | Indoor Air Quality | | **ASHRAE 55** | Thermal Environmental Conditions for Human Occupancy | Thermal Comfort | | **ASHRAE 189.1** | Standard for the Design of High-Performance Green Buildings | Holistic Sustainability | | **ASHRAE 169** | Climatic Data for Building Design Standards | Climate Classification | | **ASHRAE 100** | Energy Conservation in Existing Buildings | Retrofit / Existing Stock | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Building Doctor" Intuition) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Think of ASHRAE as the "Specialist Doctor" for a building**, while **LEED/GRIHA act as the "Fitness Trainer"**. - A **doctor** (ASHRAE) checks vital parameters — energy consumption, oxygen (ventilation), body temperature (thermal comfort) — and prescribes **minimum technical thresholds** to keep the patient alive and healthy. - A **trainer** (LEED/GRIHA) pushes the building beyond health into a state of **high performance** using points-based certifications. In Indian/KTU exam language: **ASHRAE sets the engineering "pass marks" (mandatory minimums), and rating systems award the "distinction grades" (certification points).** ## 1.3 The Role of ASHRAE in Sustainable Construction ASHRAE contributes to the **three pillars of sustainability** as applied to buildings: 1. **Environmental Pillar** → Reducing HVAC energy (≈ 40–60% of building energy) via **ASHRAE 90.1**. 2. **Social Pillar** → Safeguarding occupant health via **ASHRAE 62.1** and **ASHRAE 55**. 3. **Economic Pillar** → Setting measurable efficiency benchmarks (EUI in **kBtu/ft²·yr** or **kWh/m²·yr**) that quantify lifecycle cost savings. > [!NOTE] > **Historical Highlight for KTU Board Exam**: ASHRAE Standard **90.1-1989** became the **reference standard** for the U.S. Energy Policy Act of 1992 and is the technical basis for the **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC) 2017** adopted by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), Government of India. This is a **favourite 3-mark question** in KTU papers. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of the Key ASHRAE Standards ### 2.1.1 ASHRAE 90.1 — The Energy Bible This is the most widely adopted energy code in the world. It regulates: - **Building Envelope** (U-factor, SHGC, VT, Air Leakage — per climate zone) - **HVAC Systems** (Chiller COP, Fan efficiency, Economizers) - **Service Water Heating** (Insulation, efficiency) - **Lighting** (LPD — Lighting Power Density in W/ft²) - **Electrical Power** (Motors, transformers) > **Why this matters in KTU exams:** ASHRAE 90.1 uses a **"Prescriptive"** OR **"Energy Cost Budget (ECB)"** compliance path — both are examinable. ### 2.1.2 ASHRAE 62.1 — Ventilation Standard Establishes minimum **outdoor air intake** rates (in **cfm/person** and **cfm/ft²**) to maintain acceptable **CO₂ ≤ 800 ppm** and **TVOC limits**. The minimum outdoor airflow equation is: $$V_{bz} = R_p \cdot P_z + R_a \cdot A_z$$ Where each term is defined in the formula sheet below. ### 2.1.3 ASHRAE 55 — Thermal Comfort Defines the **comfort zone** on a psychrometric chart using: - **Operative Temperature** $T_o$ - **Mean Radiant Temperature** $\overline{T}_r$ - **Air Temperature** $T_a$ - **Relative Humidity** $\phi$ Operative temperature is the **uniform temperature** of an imaginary enclosure in which the heat transfer from the human body equals the heat transfer in the actual non-uniform environment: $$T_o = h \cdot T_a + h_r \cdot \overline{T}_r$$ ### 2.1.4 ASHRAE 189.1 — Green Building Standard A **mandatory + credit-based** document combining **90.1 + 62.1 + 55** with site, water, materials, and indoor environmental quality requirements. It is the **technical twin** of LEED. ### 2.1.5 ASHRAE 169 — Climate Zones Divides the world into **8 primary climate zones (1–8)** and **3 moisture regimes (A, B, C)**. India maps primarily to: | Indian Region | ASHRAE Climate Zone | | :--- | :--- | | Coastal Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Goa, Mumbai | **1A** (Very Hot – Humid) | | Hot-Dry Interior (Rajasthan, Gujarat) | **1B** (Very Hot – Dry) | | Warm-Humid (Bengal, Odisha) | **2A** | | Temperate (Bangalore, Pune plateau) | **2B** / **3A** | | Cold (Shimla, Leh, Northeast hills) | **6A / 7 / 8** | ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master the table below — questions appear almost every KTU cycle on these formulas.** | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Zone Outdoor Airflow (Breathing Zone) | $V_{bz} = R_p P_z + R_a A_z$ | **cfm** | ASHRAE 62.1 ventilation design | | 2 | System Outdoor Airflow | $V_{ot} = V_{ou} / (E_v \times E_c)$ | **cfm** | Accounts for ventilation efficiency | | 3 | Energy Use Intensity (EUI) | $EUI = E_{total} / A_{floor}$ | **kBtu/ft²·yr** or **kWh/m²·yr** | Building energy benchmarking | | 4 | Operative Temperature | $T_o = h T_a + h_r \overline{T}_r$ | **°C or °F** | ASHRAE 55 thermal comfort | | 5 | PMV (Predicted Mean Vote) | $PMV = [0.303 e^{-0.036 M} + 0.028] \, L$ | dimensionless | Fanger's thermal comfort model | | 6 | Energy Cost Budget (ECB) Compliance | $\sum_{i} C_i^{prop} \leq \sum_{i} C_i^{base}$ | **Currency/yr** | ASHRAE 90.1 performance path | | 7 | U-factor (Thermal Transmittance) | $U = 1/R_{total}$ | **W/m²·K** or **Btu/hr·ft²·°F** | Envelope heat transfer | | 8 | COP of Refrigeration Cycle | $COP = Q_{cooling} / W_{input}$ | dimensionless | HVAC efficiency metric | | 9 | Lighting Power Density | $LPD = P_{lighting} / A_{floor}$ | **W/ft²** or **W/m²** | ASHRAE 90.1 lighting compliance | | 10 | Ventilation Effectiveness | $E_v = C_{avg} / C_{return}$ | dimensionless | ASHRAE 62.1 system performance | > **Notation used in table:** > $R_p$ = outdoor air rate per person (cfm/person); $P_z$ = zone population; $R_a$ = outdoor air rate per area (cfm/ft²); $A_z$ = zone floor area; $E_v$ = ventilation effectiveness; $E_c$ = enthalpy recovery effectiveness; $L$ = thermal load on body; $M$ = metabolic rate. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Code Compliance:** ASHRAE 90.1-2016 is the **parent code** from which India's **ECBC+ (SuperECBC)** was derived. 2. **Energy Audits:** Auditors use ASHRAE Level 1, 2, 3 protocols to grade energy performance. 3. **Net-Zero Buildings:** The **ASHRAE 90.1-2019** Appendix G is the **de facto** baseline for net-zero energy certification. 4. **Indoor Health:** Post-COVID, **ASHRAE 62.1-2019** with elevated ventilation (≥ 0.06 cfm/ft²) is now a global reference for pandemic-resilient design. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: ASHRAE 62.1 — Zone Outdoor Airflow Calculation ### Problem Statement A **KTU Board-style numerical** is shown below to derive $V_{bz}$ for a typical classroom. > **Given:** > - Classroom floor area $A_z = 1000 \; ft^2$ > - Number of occupants $P_z = 40$ (students + 1 teacher) > - From ASHRAE 62.1 Table 6.2.2.1, for "Classrooms (age 9+)" the rates are: > - $R_p = 10 \; cfm/person$ > - $R_a = 0.18 \; cfm/ft^2$ ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1** — Identify the governing equation from ASHRAE 62.1. $$V_{bz} = R_p \cdot P_z + R_a \cdot A_z$$ This equation has two components: the **people-related outdoor air** (proportional to occupancy) and the **area-related outdoor air** (proportional to floor area to dilute area-based pollutants like formaldehyde from furnishings). **Step 2** — Substitute the people-related term. $$V_{bz,people} = R_p \cdot P_z = 10 \;\tfrac{cfm}{person} \times 41 \;persons = 410 \;cfm$$ The 41 persons is used (40 students + 1 teacher). Students should always include the **maximum design occupancy**, not the average. **Step 3** — Substitute the area-related term. $$V_{bz,area} = R_a \cdot A_z = 0.18 \;\tfrac{cfm}{ft^2} \times 1000 \;ft^2 = 180 \;cfm$$ **Step 4** — Add both components to get the breathing zone outdoor airflow. $$V_{bz} = 410 + 180 = 590 \;cfm$$ **Step 5** — Convert to SI units for a KTU-style answer (1 cfm = 0.0004719 m³/s). $$V_{bz} = 590 \times 0.0004719 \approx 0.2784 \;m^3/s$$ > **Final Answer:** $V_{bz} = 590 \;cfm \approx 0.278 \;m^3/s$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Use Intensity (EUI) Calculation ### Problem Statement Compute the EUI for a 50,000 ft² commercial office building in Trivandrum (ASHRAE Zone 1A) with the following annual energy consumption: - Grid electricity: 1,20,000 kWh - Diesel generator: 4,000 litres (assume calorific value = 35.8 MJ/L; generator efficiency = 30%) - LPG for canteen: 200 kg (calorific value = 46 MJ/kg; stove efficiency = 60%) ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1** — Convert all energy sources to a common unit: **MJ**. $$ \begin{aligned} E_{electricity} &= 1{,}20{,}000 \;kWh \times 3.6 \;MJ/kWh \\ &= 4{,}32{,}000 \;MJ \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} E_{diesel,delivered} &= 4{,}000 \;L \times 35.8 \;MJ/L \times 0.30 \\ &= 42{,}960 \;MJ \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} E_{LPG,delivered} &= 200 \;kg \times 46 \;MJ/kg \times 0.60 \\ &= 5{,}520 \;MJ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2** — Sum total site energy delivered. $$ E_{total} = 4{,}32{,}000 + 42{,}960 + 5{,}520 = 4{,}80{,}480 \;MJ $$ **Step 3** — Convert MJ to kBtu (1 MJ = 0.9478 kBtu). $$ E_{total} = 4{,}80{,}480 \times 0.9478 \approx 4{,}55{,}319 \;kBtu $$ **Step 4** — Calculate EUI in kBtu/ft²·yr. $$ EUI = \frac{E_{total}}{A_{floor}} = \frac{4{,}55{,}319}{50{,}000} \approx 9.11 \;kBtu/ft^2 \cdot yr $$ **Step 5** — Convert to kWh/m²·yr for Indian benchmarking (1 kBtu/ft² = 3.1546 kWh/m²). $$ EUI_{SI} = 9.11 \times 3.1546 \approx 28.74 \;kWh/m^2 \cdot yr $$ > **Final Answer:** $EUI = 9.11 \;kBtu/ft^2 \cdot yr \equiv 28.74 \;kWh/m^2 \cdot yr$ > > **Benchmarking:** ASHRAE 90.1-2016 baseline for a Trivandrum office = ~45 kWh/m²·yr ⇒ this building is **~36% more efficient** than baseline. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: ASHRAE Standard Cross-Reference | ASHRAE Standard | Compliance Path | Indian Parallel | Mandatory / Voluntary | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 90.1 (Energy) | Prescriptive OR ECB | ECBC 2017 | Mandatory for commercial > 100 kW | | 62.1 (Ventilation) | Procedure 1 to 5 | NBC 2016 Part 8 | Mandatory in National Building Code | | 55 (Thermal Comfort) | Comfort Zone Chart | IS 3362 | Voluntary | | 189.1 (Green Buildings) | Mandatory + Credits | GRIHA / LEED-India | Voluntary certification | | 169 (Climate Data) | 8 zones, 3 moisture | IS 3792 | Reference only | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 ASHRAE Standards Hierarchy in Sustainable Construction ```mermaid flowchart TD A[ASHRAE FRAMEWORK FOR SUSTAINABLE CONSTRUCTION] --> B[ENERGY DOMAIN] A --> C[INDOOR ENVIRONMENTAL QUALITY] A --> D[GREEN BUILDING INTEGRATION] A --> E[CLIMATE & SITE] B --> B1[ASHRAE 90.1
Energy Standard] B --> B2[ASHRAE 100
Existing Buildings] B1 --> B1a[Prescriptive Path] B1 --> B1b[Energy Cost Budget] C --> C1[ASHRAE 62.1
Ventilation] C --> C2[ASHRAE 55
Thermal Comfort] C1 --> C1a[Breathing Zone Equation] C2 --> C2a[PMV PPD Model] D --> D1[ASHRAE 189.1
High Performance Green] D1 --> D1a[Mandatory Provisions] D1 --> D1b[Credit Provisions] E --> E1[ASHRAE 169
Climate Zones] E1 --> E1a[8 Zones plus 3 Moisture Regimes] ``` ## 4.2 ASHRAE 169 — India Climate Zone Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph IN[INDIA CLIMATE MAP ASHRAE 169] Z1A[1A VERY HOT HUMID
Kerala, Goa, Mumbai, Chennai] Z1B[1B VERY HOT DRY
Western Rajasthan, Kutch] Z2A[2A HOT HUMID
Kolkata, Bhubaneswar] Z2B[2B HOT DRY
Pune, Bangalore outskirts] Z3A[3A WARM HUMID
Hyderabad edges] Z6A[6A COLD
Shimla, Mussoorie] Z7[7 VERY COLD
Leh, Manali high altitude] Z8[8 SUBARCTIC
Siachen sector] end IMP[DESIGN IMPACT] --> Z1A IMP --> Z1B IMP --> Z2A Z1A --> R1[High SHGC films, low U factor, dehumidification, cross ventilation] Z1B --> R2[High SHGC rejected, radiant cooling, low U, night purging] Z2A --> R3[Moderate insulation, balanced ventilation] Z6A --> R4[High insulation, sealed envelope, mechanical heating] Z7 --> R5[Very high insulation, triple glazing, robust HVAC] Z8 --> R6[Extreme insulation, compact form, heat recovery] ``` ## 4.3 ASHRAE 62.1 Ventilation Calculation Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD START[START ZONE DESIGN] --> OCP[Determine Design Occupancy Pz] OCP --> AREA[Determine Zone Floor Area Az] AREA --> RATES[Lookup Rp and Ra from Table 6.2.2.1] RATES --> VBZ[Compute Breathing Zone Airflow Vbz] VBZ --> EV[Determine Ventilation Effectiveness Ev] EV --> VOU[Compute Uncorrected Outdoor Airflow Vou] VOU --> EC[Apply Enthalpy Recovery Factor Ec] EC --> VOT[Final System Outdoor Airflow Vot] VOT --> DUCT[Duct Sizing and Fan Selection] DUCT --> END[END HVAC DESIGN] ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Architecture: ASHRAE vs. LEED vs. GRIHA ```mermaid flowchart LR A[ASHRAE STANDARDS] -->|Mandatory minimums| A1[Technical Compliance] L[LEED USGBC] -->|Points based certification| L1[Performance Certification] G[GRIHA INDIA] -->|Points plus national context| G1[Sustainable Rating] A1 -->|Adopted as code| X[ECBC 2017] L1 -->|Voluntary adoption| Y[IGBC LEED India] G1 -->|Mandatory for Govt projects| Z[TERI GRIHA] X --> COMMON[NET ZERO READY BUILDINGS] Y --> COMMON Z --> COMMON ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **Q1. Define ASHRAE and list any four of its standards relevant to sustainable construction.** **Model Answer:** ASHRAE stands for **American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers**. It is an international technical society that develops standards for building energy efficiency, indoor air quality, thermal comfort, and refrigeration. Four key standards are: 1. **ASHRAE 90.1** – Energy Standard for Buildings 2. **ASHRAE 62.1** – Ventilation for Acceptable Indoor Air Quality 3. **ASHRAE 55** – Thermal Environmental Conditions for Human Occupancy 4. **ASHRAE 189.1** – Standard for the Design of High-Performance Green Buildings *(Other valid: ASHRAE 100, ASHRAE 169.)* --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **Q2. What is Energy Use Intensity (EUI)? Why is it a key metric in ASHRAE 90.1 compliance?** **Model Answer:** EUI is the **annual energy consumed per unit floor area** of a building, expressed in **kBtu/ft²·yr** (IP units) or **kWh/m²·yr** (SI units). $$EUI = \frac{\text{Total Annual Energy Consumption}}{\text{Total Floor Area}}$$ It is a key metric in ASHRAE 90.1 because it normalizes energy performance across buildings of different sizes, allowing **direct comparison** against baseline values prescribed by climate zone. Lower EUI indicates higher energy efficiency and lower operational carbon footprint. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 1 | CO1 | Bloom: Apply/Analyse]** ### ⭐ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — ASHRAE 90.1 & Energy Cost Budget **(a)** Explain the **Energy Cost Budget (ECB) method** of ASHRAE 90.1 compliance. Describe the procedure for establishing the baseline building and the proposed design. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A proposed 30,000 ft² office building in Trivandrum (ASHRAE Climate Zone 1A) consumes the following annually: - Electricity: 2,00,000 kWh at ₹8/unit - Diesel: 5,000 L at ₹95/L (Calorific value 38 MJ/L, generator efficiency 35%) - LPG: 100 kg at ₹90/kg (Calorific value 46 MJ/kg, stove efficiency 60%) Compute the **annual site EUI in kBtu/ft²·yr and kWh/m²·yr**. Comment on whether the building meets the ASHRAE 90.1-2016 baseline (assume baseline EUI = 40 kWh/m²·yr). **(7 marks)** --- ### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A #### Part (a) — ECB Method Explanation [7 Marks] The **Energy Cost Budget (ECB) method** is a **performance-based** compliance path in ASHRAE Standard 90.1. **Procedure:** 1. A **Baseline Building** is mathematically modelled with the *same geometry, occupancy, and climate zone* as the proposed design, but with **minimum prescriptive compliance** (i.e., envelope, HVAC, lighting, and service water heating meeting the bare-minimum 90.1 values). 2. The **Proposed Building** is modelled identically but with the actual designer-specified systems. 3. Annual energy costs of both buildings are calculated for each fuel type (electricity, gas, etc.) using the same energy prices. 4. **Compliance criterion:** $$\sum_{i} C_i^{proposed} \;\leq\; \sum_{i} C_i^{baseline}$$ If the proposed building's energy cost is less than or equal to the baseline, the design complies. This method allows the designer to **trade-off** high-efficiency chillers for less-efficient windows (or vice versa) as long as the overall energy cost is met. #### Part (b) — EUI Numerical [7 Marks] **Step 1: Convert each fuel to MJ.** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{elec} &= 2{,}00{,}000 \times 3.6 = 7{,}20{,}000 \;MJ \\ E_{diesel} &= 5{,}000 \times 38 \times 0.35 = 66{,}500 \;MJ \\ E_{LPG} &= 100 \times 46 \times 0.60 = 2{,}760 \;MJ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Total site energy in MJ.** $$ E_{total} = 7{,}20{,}000 + 66{,}500 + 2{,}760 = 7{,}89{,}260 \;MJ $$ **Step 3: Convert to kBtu (1 MJ = 0.9478 kBtu).** $$ E_{total} = 7{,}89{,}260 \times 0.9478 \approx 7{,}48{,}067 \;kBtu $$ **Step 4: EUI in kBtu/ft²·yr.** $$ EUI = \frac{7{,}48{,}067}{30{,}000} \approx 24.94 \;kBtu/ft^2 \cdot yr $$ **Step 5: Convert to kWh/m²·yr (1 kBtu/ft²·yr = 3.1546 kWh/m²·yr).** $$ EUI_{SI} = 24.94 \times 3.1546 \approx 78.68 \;kWh/m^2 \cdot yr $$ **Step 6: Comparison with ASHRAE 90.1-2016 baseline (40 kWh/m²·yr).** $$ \text{Difference} = 78.68 - 40 = 38.68 \;kWh/m^2 \cdot yr $$ > **Result:** The proposed building's EUI (78.68 kWh/m²·yr) **EXCEEDS** the baseline (40 kWh/m²·yr) by **97%** ⇒ **The design FAILS the ASHRAE 90.1 ECB compliance test.** The HVAC or envelope must be redesigned to reduce site EUI by at least 49%. --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 1 | CO1 | Bloom: Understand/Apply]** ### ⭐ **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — ASHRAE 62.1 Ventilation Design **(a)** Explain the **breathing zone outdoor airflow equation** of ASHRAE 62.1. What is the significance of the people-related and area-related components? **(7 marks)** **(b)** An IT office in Kochi (Zone 1A) measures 1,500 m². The design occupancy is 120 people. ASHRAE 62.1-2019 rates for "Office space" are $R_p = 5 \;cfm/person$ and $R_a = 0.06 \;cfm/ft^2$. Compute: (i) Breathing zone outdoor airflow $V_{bz}$ in cfm and m³/s. (ii) If ventilation effectiveness $E_v = 0.8$ and no enthalpy recovery ($E_c = 1.0$), the uncorrected system outdoor airflow $V_{ou} = 0.9 \times V_{bz}$. Compute the system outdoor airflow $V_{ot}$. **(7 marks)** --- ### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B #### Part (a) — Breathing Zone Equation Explanation [7 Marks] The **breathing zone outdoor airflow rate** $V_{bz}$ is the volume of outdoor air delivered to the occupied zone of a space to maintain acceptable indoor air quality. $$V_{bz} = R_p \cdot P_z + R_a \cdot A_z$$ - **$R_p \cdot P_z$ (People-related component):** Accounts for **bio-effluents (CO₂, body odor)** generated by occupants. Increases linearly with population density. - **$R_a \cdot A_z$ (Area-related component):** Accounts for **area-based contaminants** like formaldehyde from furnishings, paints, carpets, and adhesives. Increases linearly with floor area. Both components are summed because they represent **physically independent pollution sources** — adding them ensures that both human-emitted and material-emitted pollutants are adequately diluted to meet IAQ targets. #### Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks] **Step 1: Convert $A_z$ to ft² (1 m² = 10.7639 ft²).** $$ A_z = 1{,}500 \times 10.7639 = 16{,}145.85 \;ft^2 $$ **Step 2: Apply the breathing zone equation.** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{bz} &= R_p \cdot P_z + R_a \cdot A_z \\ &= 5 \times 120 + 0.06 \times 16{,}145.85 \\ &= 600 + 968.75 \\ &= 1{,}568.75 \;cfm \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Convert to m³/s (1 cfm = 0.0004719 m³/s).** $$ V_{bz} = 1{,}568.75 \times 0.0004719 \approx 0.7404 \;m^3/s $$ **Step 4: Compute $V_{ou}$.** $$ V_{ou} = 0.9 \times V_{bz} = 0.9 \times 1{,}568.75 = 1{,}411.88 \;cfm $$ **Step 5: Apply system-level correction $V_{ot} = V_{ou} / (E_v \times E_c)$.** $$ V_{ot} = \frac{1{,}411.88}{0.8 \times 1.0} = 1{,}764.85 \;cfm $$ > **Final Answers:** (i) $V_{bz} = 1{,}568.75 \;cfm \approx 0.740 \;m^3/s$; (ii) $V_{ot} = 1{,}764.85 \;cfm \approx 0.833 \;m^3/s$ --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes where KTU students LOSE marks on ASHRAE questions:** > 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Converting MJ to kWh using the wrong factor (must use **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ**; many students use 1.163 in reverse). > 2. **Forgetting Efficiency Factors:** Diesel/LPG energy must be multiplied by **device efficiency** (0.30, 0.60, etc.) — not raw calorific value. > 3. **Confusing $V_{bz}$ with $V_{ot}$:** Students often skip the system-level correction and report $V_{bz}$ as the final answer, losing 2-3 marks. > 4. **Climate Zone Ignorance:** Kerala = **1A (Very Hot-Humid)**, NOT 2A. Examiners will deduct marks for incorrect zone identification. > 5. **Sign Convention Errors:** In $T_o = hT_a + h_r \overline{T}_r$, both temperatures must be in the **same units** (°C or K) before adding. > 6. **ASHRAE vs. IS Code confusion:** ASHRAE 62.1 cfm values are **IP units**. Do not directly substitute liters/second without conversion. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist for ASHRAE (Module 1)** - **ASHRAE** = American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers (founded 1959). - The **5 must-know standards**: **90.1, 62.1, 55, 189.1, 169**. - **ASHRAE 90.1** is the global energy benchmark; its Indian parallel is **ECBC 2017** (BEE). - **ASHRAE 62.1** governs ventilation: $V_{bz} = R_p P_z + R_a A_z$. - **ASHRAE 55** governs thermal comfort: $T_o = hT_a + h_r \overline{T}_r$ + PMV/PPD model. - **ASHRAE 169** divides Earth into **8 climate zones × 3 moisture regimes** = 24 sub-zones; **Kerala = Zone 1A**. - **ASHRAE 189.1** is the **technical twin of LEED** (mandatory + credit-based). - **EUI** = Total annual energy ÷ Floor area, expressed in **kBtu/ft²·yr** (IP) or **kWh/m²·yr** (SI). - The **ECB method** allows trade-offs between envelope, HVAC, and lighting as long as total annual energy cost ≤ baseline. - **System airflow** $V_{ot}$ is always ≥ $V_{bz}$ (it accounts for system inefficiencies). - India is **NOT** an ASHRAE 169 adopter — it follows **IS 3792** and **NBC 2016**, but **ECBC 2017 is ASHRAE 90.1-aligned**. - **Key 3-mark favourites:** define ASHRAE, explain EUI, name standards, map Kerala's climate zone. - **Key 14-mark favourites:** ECB method, ventilation numerical, comfort zone on psychrometric chart, EUI numerical with multi-fuel conversion. > **🎯 Final Exam Tip:** Whenever you see "sustainable construction" + "energy" in a KTU question, **always default to ASHRAE 90.1 / ECBC** terminology. Whenever you see "indoor air" or "ventilation", default to **ASHRAE 62.1**. This automatic mental mapping will save you 30–60 seconds per question in the actual exam.

topics.title: Environmental Impact of Building Constructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET752 - SUSTAINABLE CONSTRUCTION PRACTICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Environmental Impact of Building Constructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Environmental Impact of Building Construction (EIBC)** refers to the totality of measurable alterations — both detrimental and beneficial — imposed upon the natural environment across the entire life cycle of a built facility, encompassing raw material extraction, manufacturing of construction products, transportation, on-site construction activities, operational use, maintenance, deconstruction, and end-of-life disposal or reuse.

In strict KTU 2024 / NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) terminology, the environmental impact of building construction is evaluated through the framework of **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** as codified in **ISO 14040** and **ISO 14044**, which mandates the four-phase procedure:

1. **Goal and Scope Definition**
2. **Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) Analysis**
3. **Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA)**
4. **Interpretation**

The principal impact categories tracked are:
- **Global Warming Potential (GWP)** measured in kg CO₂-equivalent (kgCO₂e)
- **Embodied Energy (EE)** measured in MJ/kg or GJ/m²
- **Embodied Carbon (EC)** measured in kgCO₂e/m²
- **Acidification Potential (AP)** in kg SO₂e
- **Eutrophication Potential (EP)** in kg PO₄e
- **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP)** in kg CFC-11e
- **Primary Energy Demand (PED)** in MJ

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome Mapping (PECET752):** This topic directly addresses **CO2 — "Assess the environmental impact of construction activities and recommend sustainable alternatives"** and **CO3 — "Apply life cycle thinking to building materials and processes."**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Bread-Baking" Analogy** 🍞
> Imagine a loaf of bread. The **flour, water, yeast, and energy used to bake it** is the *embodied cost* — you don't see it after the bread is made, but it took real resources to create. Then, every day you keep the bread on the counter, it goes stale — that's the *operational decay*. A building works the same way:
> - **Embodied impacts** = everything "baked in" before you move in (cement, steel, transport, water, labour energy).
> - **Operational impacts** = the energy the building "eats" daily (AC, lighting, lifts).
> - **End-of-life impacts** = what happens when the building is demolished (waste, dust, recycling).
>
> A *truly* sustainable building minimises **both** the baking cost and the daily eating cost.

In one sentence: **Every kilogram of cement, every litre of diesel burnt, every cubic metre of water pumped, and every cubic metre of land stripped leaves a permanent ecological footprint on the planet.**

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used Globally

- **Embodied Carbon Intensity (ECI)**: measured in **kgCO₂e/m²** of Gross Internal Floor Area (GIFA) — adopted by **RICS (2017)** and **IS 15884 (Part 1)**.
- **Operational Energy Intensity**: **kWh/m²/year** (ECBC India sets ≤ 90 kWh/m²/year for office buildings).
- **Construction & Demolition (C&D) Waste**: **kg/m²** of built-up area (Indian average ≈ **300–500 kg/m²**).
- **GWP of 1 kg OPC cement** ≈ **0.83–0.95 kgCO₂e**.
- **GWP of 1 kg structural steel** ≈ **1.99–2.50 kgCO₂e**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *Embodied Energy* (cumulative primary energy, MJ) with *Embodied Carbon* (CO₂ emissions, kgCO₂e). They are **not the same** — energy can be renewable (low carbon) but still high in embodied energy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Life Cycle Stages of a Building (Cradle-to-Grave)

A building's environmental footprint is conventionally divided into the following sequential stages, all of which contribute to the cumulative impact:

| Stage | Module | Activities | Major Impacts |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **A1–A3** | Product Stage | Raw material extraction, transport to factory, manufacturing | Quarrying, CO₂ from cement kiln, slag processing |
| **A4** | Transport to Site | Movement of materials to construction site | Diesel emissions, particulates |
| **A5** | Construction–Installation | On-site assembly, wastage, site energy | C&D waste (5–15 % of materials), noise, dust |
| **B1–B7** | Use Stage | Maintenance, repair, replacement, operational energy | Electricity consumption, water usage |
| **C1–C4** | End-of-Life | Deconstruction, transport, waste processing, disposal | Landfill emissions, recyclate recovery |
| **D** | Beyond System | Reuse, recovery, recycling potential | Avoided future burden |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EN 15978 / IS 16714 : 2018** is the European/Indian standard that codifies this modular LCA methodology. **KTU 2024 questions frequently ask for stage-wise identification of impacts.**

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the Major Environmental Burdens

### 2.2.1 Why is Cement the #1 Emitter?
- Calcination reaction: **CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂** releases ≈ **0.53 kgCO₂ per kg clinker** purely from chemistry.
- Combustion of kiln fuel (coal/petcoke) adds another **0.30–0.40 kgCO₂ per kg clinker**.
- The remaining **0.10–0.20 kgCO₂** comes from electricity and transport.
- Total ≈ **0.83–0.95 kgCO₂e/kg of OPC**, and global cement industry alone contributes ≈ **7–8 % of anthropogenic CO₂**.

### 2.2.2 Why is Steel Energy-Intensive?
- Integrated steel mill route (BF-BOF) consumes ≈ **20–25 GJ/t** of primary energy.
- Electric Arc Furnace (EAF) recycling route consumes only ≈ **9–11 GJ/t**.
- Therefore, **using recycled-content steel** is a high-leverage sustainability intervention.

### 2.2.3 Why Does Construction-Phase Waste Matter?
- **C&D waste in India ≈ 530 million tonnes/year** (TIFAC, 2021 estimate).
- Only ≈ **1 %** is formally recycled — the rest goes to low-lying land fills, blocking storm drains and polluting water.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Expression | Standard Unit | Boundary / Note |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | Embodied Energy | $EE$ | $EE = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (M_i \times e_i)$ | **MJ** | $M_i$ = mass of material $i$ (kg); $e_i$ = EE coefficient of material $i$ (MJ/kg) |
| 2 | Embodied Carbon | $EC$ | $EC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (M_i \times c_i)$ | **kgCO₂e** | $c_i$ = carbon factor of material $i$ (kgCO₂e/kg) |
| 3 | Embodied Carbon Intensity | $ECI$ | $ECI = \dfrac{EC_{total}}{GIFA}$ | **kgCO₂e/m²** | $GIFA$ = Gross Internal Floor Area |
| 4 | Operational Energy Intensity | $OEI$ | $OEI = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{A_{floor}}$ | **kWh/m²/yr** | $E_{annual}$ = annual operational energy |
| 5 | GWP Conversion (mass) | $GWP_m$ | $GWP_m = \sum_{g} m_g \times GWP_{100,g}$ | **kgCO₂e** | $g$ = greenhouse gas species |
| 6 | GWP Conversion (CO₂ from CaCO₃ calcination) | $m_{CO_2,chem}$ | $m_{CO_2,chem} = M_{clinker} \times \dfrac{44}{100}$ | **kgCO₂** | Stoichiometric ratio from 100 kg pure limestone |
| 7 | Site Waste Diversion Rate | $SWDR$ | $SWDR = \dfrac{W_{recycled} + W_{reused}}{W_{total}} \times 100$ | **%** | LEED / GRIHA benchmark |
| 8 | Recycled-Content Ratio | $RCR$ | $RCR = \dfrac{M_{recycled}}{M_{total}} \times 100$ | **%** | Sum of pre-consumer + post-consumer content |
| 9 | Cumulative Energy Demand | $CED$ | $CED = EE_{materials} + E_{transport} + E_{construction} + E_{operation} + E_{EOL}$ | **MJ** | Full cradle-to-grave |
| 10 | Acidification Potential | $AP$ | $AP = \sum_p m_p \times AP_f$ | **kgSO₂e** | $AP_f$ = characterisation factor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stoichiometric constants used in cement chemistry:** $M_{CaCO_3} = 100\ g/mol$, $M_{CO_2} = 44\ g/mol$. Therefore, every **100 g of pure limestone calcined** liberates **44 g of CO₂** by mass — a *thermodynamic certainty*, irrespective of fuel type.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Green Building Certification (GRIHA, LEED-India, IGBC)** use $ECI$ values for credit allocation under *Materials & Resources* and *Energy* categories.
- **Carbon-neutral building design** relies on offsetting the calculated $EC$ with renewable energy generation or purchased carbon credits.
- **Disaster-resilient retrofits** compare pre- and post-retrofit $EE$ and $EC$ to justify funding.
- **Circular economy implementation** in construction uses $RCR$ and $SWDR$ as key performance indicators (KPIs).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric CO₂ from Cement Calcination

The calcination of limestone is the single largest *chemical* source of CO₂ in the building industry. We will derive the exact emission factor from first principles.

**Given (assumptions for derivation):**
- Limestone is pure calcium carbonate: $\text{CaCO}_3$
- Molar mass of $\text{CaCO}_3$ = **100 g/mol** (Ca = 40, C = 12, O₃ = 48)
- Molar mass of $\text{CO}_2$ released = **44 g/mol** (C = 12, O₂ = 32)

**Step 1 — Write the balanced chemical equation**

The thermal decomposition of calcium carbonate is:

$$
\text{CaCO}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta \, (\approx 900^\circ C)} \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the conservation of mass (stoichiometry)**

One mole of $\text{CaCO}_3$ (100 g) produces one mole of $\text{CO}_2$ (44 g).

$$
\dfrac{m_{CO_2}}{m_{CaCO_3}} = \dfrac{44}{100} = 0.44
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mass of clinker basis**

Portland cement clinker is essentially CaO (plus minor phases). To produce **1 kg of CaO**, the stoichiometry requires:

$$
\text{CaCO}_3 : \text{CaO} = 100 : 56
$$

So, to make 1 kg of CaO, we need:

$$
\dfrac{100}{56} = 1.786 \, \text{kg of CaCO}_3
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the chemically-bound CO₂ per kg of CaO (clinker basis)**

$$
m_{CO_2,chem} = 1.786 \times 0.44 = 0.786 \, \text{kgCO}_2 \text{ per kg CaO}
$$

**Step 5 — Add the fuel-combustion CO₂ (typical value ≈ 0.30–0.40 kgCO₂/kg clinker)**

$$
m_{CO_2,total} = 0.786 + 0.35 = 1.136 \, \text{kgCO}_2/\text{kg clinker}
$$

> The above assumes 100 % pure limestone. In practice, with impurities, the *cement industry* average for **OPC** is reported as **0.83–0.95 kgCO₂e/kg**, lower than pure-clinker stoichiometry because the *finished cement* contains supplementary materials (fly ash, slag) that *do not* release calcination CO₂.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Embodied Carbon of a Reinforced Concrete Beam

**Problem (KTU-style):** A simply-supported RC beam has the following bill of quantities:
- **Concrete (M25 grade)** = 1.20 m³
- **HYSD steel (Fe-415)** = 180 kg
- Density of reinforced concrete = **2500 kg/m³**
- Density of steel = **7850 kg/m³**

Take the following emission factors (from ICE Database v3.0):
- Concrete M25: $c_{conc} = 0.10 \, \text{kgCO}_2\text{e/kg}$ (includes cement, aggregates, water)
- HYSD Rebar: $c_{steel} = 1.99 \, \text{kgCO}_2\text{e/kg}$

**Compute the Embodied Carbon (EC) of the beam.**

**Step 1 — Mass of concrete**

$$
M_{conc} = V_{conc} \times \rho_{conc} = 1.20 \times 2500 = 3000 \, \text{kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of steel**

$$
M_{steel} = 180 \, \text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 — EC of concrete component**

$$
EC_{conc} = M_{conc} \times c_{conc} = 3000 \times 0.10 = 300 \, \text{kgCO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Step 4 — EC of steel component**

$$
EC_{steel} = M_{steel} \times c_{steel} = 180 \times 1.99 = 358.2 \, \text{kgCO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Embodied Carbon**

$$
EC_{total} = EC_{conc} + EC_{steel} = 300 + 358.2 = 658.2 \, \text{kgCO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation**

> The steel, despite being only **6 %** of the beam's mass, contributes **≈ 54.4 %** of the beam's embodied carbon. This is *the* classic KTU teaching point: **mass-based prioritisation misleads; carbon-based prioritisation is the correct strategy.**

$$
\text{Steel share} = \dfrac{358.2}{658.2} \times 100 = 54.41 \, \%
$$

$$
\text{Concrete share} = \dfrac{300}{658.2} \times 100 = 45.59 \, \%
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Stage-Wise Impact Allocation (Cradle-to-Gate)

| Stage | Module | % of Total EC (typical residential, India) | Dominant Driver | Mitigation Lever |
|:---|:---|:--:|:---|:---|
| A1–A3 | Product | **60 – 75 %** | Cement & steel manufacture | SCMs, EAF steel, low-clinker cement |
| A4 | Transport | **3 – 8 %** | Diesel logistics | Local sourcing (< 100 km) |
| A5 | Construction | **2 – 5 %** | Site wastage, fuel | Modular construction, just-in-time delivery |
| B1–B7 | Use (60 yr) | **10 – 25 %** | Operational electricity | Passive design, PV, efficient HVAC |
| C1–C4 | End-of-Life | **1 – 4 %** | Landfill, transport | Design-for-disassembly, recycling |
| D | Beyond | **−5 to −15 %** *(credit)* | Recyclate substitution | High-recycled-content design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The negative values in Module **D** are *system credits* — they represent future emissions *avoided* by recycling today's materials.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Calculating Operational CO₂ from Electricity

**Given:**
- Annual electricity consumption of a building: $E_{ann} = 250{,}000 \, \text{kWh/yr}$
- Grid emission factor (India CEA, 2023 baseline): $EF_{grid} = 0.72 \, \text{kgCO}_2\text{e/kWh}$

**Step 1 — Apply the operational emissions equation**

$$
E_{op} = E_{ann} \times EF_{grid}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$
E_{op} = 250{,}000 \times 0.72 = 180{,}000 \, \text{kgCO}_2\text{e/yr}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to convenient units**

$$
E_{op} = \dfrac{180{,}000}{1000} = 180 \, \text{tCO}_2\text{e/yr}
$$

**Step 4 — Total over 60-year design life**

$$
E_{op,60} = 180 \times 60 = 10{,}800 \, \text{tCO}_2\text{e}
$$

> For a 5000 m² office, that is $E_{op,60} = 10{,}800{,}000 / 5000 = 2160 \, \text{kgCO}_2\text{e/m²}$ from operations alone — illustrating why **the next 30 years of energy policy** is inseparable from *building* policy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Life-Cycle Impact Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid

    E --> F[Deconstruction and Demolition]
    F --> G[Waste Disposal and Landfill]
    G --> H[Recycling and Reuse]
    H -. Avoided Burden .-> B

    A1[Quarrying]:::sub --> A
    A2[Deforestation]:::sub --> A
    A3[Water Withdrawal]:::sub --> A
```

