# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2024 Scheme | Module 2 | 3 Marks]**

**Define "Solar Heat Gain Coefficient (SHGC)" of a window. State its typical range and its significance in the design of energy-efficient building envelopes in Indian climates.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Solar Heat Gain Coefficient (SHGC) is the **ratio of solar heat gain through a glazing system to the total incident solar radiation** on that glazing, expressed as a dimensionless number between 0 and 1.

$$SHGC = \frac{\text{Solar heat admitted through window}}{\text{Total incident solar radiation on window}}$$

**Typical Range (1 mark):** SHGC values typically range from **0.10 (highly reflective spectrally selective low-e glass) to 0.80 (single clear glass)**. ECBC 2017 prescribes SHGC ≤ **0.25** for non-south façades and ≤ **0.40** for south-facing façades in Indian composite climates.

**Significance (1 mark):** In hot Indian climates (Trivandrum, Chennai, Kochi), solar heat gain through windows is the **largest single cooling load contributor** (often 30–40% of total AC load). Selecting a low SHGC glazing directly reduces chiller capacity and electricity consumption, making it one of the most cost-effective envelope interventions.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2024 Scheme | Module 2 | 3 Marks]**

**What is a "cool roof"? Explain two mechanisms by which it reduces the operational energy of a building.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** A cool roof is a roofing system engineered to reflect more sunlight and absorb less heat than a conventional roof, characterized by a high **Solar Reflectance Index (SRI)** ≥ 78 (per ECBC 2017) and high **thermal emittance** ≥ 0.75.

**Mechanism 1 — High Solar Reflectance (1 mark):** Cool roof coatings contain reflective pigments (TiO₂, acrylic white, ceramic microspheres) that reflect **60–85% of incident solar radiation** back to the sky instead of absorbing it. This reduces the surface temperature of the roof by **15–30 °C** compared to a black tar roof.

**Mechanism 2 — High Thermal Emittance + Reduced Convective Heat Gain (1 mark):** The roof re-radiates the small absorbed heat efficiently back to the sky (sky cooling effect), and the lower roof surface temperature reduces conductive heat flux into the room below. This shrinks the cooling load on the chiller by **15–25%** with a payback period of often **less than 1 year**, making it one of the most cost-effective EE envelope strategies.

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B questions in the End Semester Examination carry **14 marks** with an **internal choice** (either/or) at the module level. Below, two alternative full-mark questions are provided.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2024 Scheme | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the various **insulation materials** used in building envelopes. Classify them and discuss their typical thermal conductivities, advantages, and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An external wall of a building (Trivandrum climate) has the following layered construction from outside to inside:

| Layer | Material | Thickness (mm) | k (W/m·K) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cement plaster | 20 | 0.72 |
| 2 | Burnt clay brick | 200 | 0.81 |
| 3 | Expanded Polystyrene (EPS) | 40 | 0.038 |
| 4 | Air gap (ventilated) | 30 | $R = 0.16$ m²K/W |
| 5 | Gypsum plasterboard | 12 | 0.21 |

Take $h_i = 8.3$ W/m²K, $h_o = 23$ W/m²K. **(7 Marks)**

(i) Calculate the overall U-value of the wall.
(ii) If the indoor and outdoor design temperatures are 25 °C and 36 °C respectively, compute the heat gain per m² of wall area.
(iii) If the EPS thickness is increased to 60 mm, by what percentage does the heat gain reduce?

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Insulation Materials Classification (7 Marks)**

**1. Bulk Insulation (Resists Conductive Heat Transfer) (3 Marks):**

| Type | Material | k (W/m·K) | Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mineral Fibre** | Glass wool, Rock wool | 0.030–0.045 | Non-combustible, sound absorbing, cheap | Absorbs moisture, needs vapour barrier |
| **Cellulosic** | Recycled paper, cellulose fibre | 0.035–0.040 | Eco-friendly, low embodied energy | Fire-treated chemicals |
| **EPS (Expanded Polystyrene)** | Foamed polystyrene beads | 0.032–0.040 | Light, cheap, easy to install | Flammable, solvents degrade it |
| **XPS (Extruded Polystyrene)** | Extruded foam | 0.026–0.032 | High R per mm, moisture resistant | Costlier than EPS, HCFC-blown historically |
| **Polyurethane (PU) Foam** | Spray/injection foam | 0.020–0.028 | Highest R per mm, air-sealing | Cost, off-gassing, skilled labour |
| **Aerogel Blanket** | Silica aerogel composite | 0.013–0.020 | Premium, thinnest solution | Very expensive |
| **Natural Insulation** | Cork, hemp, sheep wool, wood fibre | 0.038–0.055 | Bio-based, low carbon, hygroscopic | Variable supply, pest risk |

**2. Reflective Insulation (Resists Radiative Heat Transfer) (2 Marks):**
- **Aluminium foil laminates**, multi-layer insulation (MLI), low-e films
- Operate by reflecting >95% of radiant heat
- Effective in hot climates with a still air gap
- k (effective) < 0.05 W/m·K

**3. Structural Insulated Panels (SIPs) (1 Mark):**
- Prefabricated sandwich panels: OSB/steel skins + EPS/XPS/PU core
- Factory quality, reduced thermal bridging, rapid construction
- Used in modern prefab and cold-storage buildings

**Selection Criteria (1 Mark):** k-value, fire rating, moisture behaviour, embodied carbon, cost, climatic compatibility, building code (ECBC) compliance.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)**

**(i) U-value Calculation (3 Marks):**

Surface film resistances:

$$R_{si} = \frac{1}{8.3} = 0.1205 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{so} = \frac{1}{23} = 0.0435 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

Layer resistances:

$$R_1 = \frac{0.020}{0.72} = 0.0278$$

$$R_2 = \frac{0.200}{0.81} = 0.2469$$

$$R_3 = \frac{0.040}{0.038} = 1.0526$$

$$R_4 = R_{air\,gap} = 0.16$$

$$R_5 = \frac{0.012}{0.21} = 0.0571$$

Summing all resistances in series:

$$R_{total} = 0.1205 + 0.0278 + 0.2469 + 1.0526 + 0.16 + 0.0571 + 0.0435$$

Step-by-step addition:

$$R_{total} = 0.1205 + 0.0278 = 0.1483$$

$$R_{total} = 0.1483 + 0.2469 = 0.3952$$

$$R_{total} = 0.3952 + 1.0526 = 1.4478$$

$$R_{total} = 1.4478 + 0.16 = 1.6078$$

$$R_{total} = 1.6078 + 0.0571 = 1.6649$$

$$R_{total} = 1.6649 + 0.0435 = 1.7084 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

Therefore:

$$U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{1.7084} = 0.5854 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**(ii) Heat Gain per m² (2 Marks):**

$$\Delta T = 36 - 25 = 11 \text{ K}$$

$$q = U \cdot \Delta T = 0.5854 \times 11 = 6.44 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**(iii) Percentage Reduction with 60 mm EPS (2 Marks):**

New R₃:

$$R_3^{new} = \frac{0.060}{0.038} = 1.5789$$

New R_total:

$$R_{total}^{new} = 0.1205 + 0.0278 + 0.2469 + 1.5789 + 0.16 + 0.0571 + 0.0435$$

$$R_{total}^{new} = 2.2347 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

New U-value:

$$U_{new} = \frac{1}{2.2347} = 0.4475 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

New heat flux:

$$q_{new} = 0.4475 \times 11 = 4.92 \text{ W/m}^2$$

Percentage reduction:

$$\%\text{Reduction} = \frac{6.44 - 4.92}{6.44} \times 100 = 23.6\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often forget to include **both surface film resistances** ($R_{si}$ and $R_{so}$). Forgetting $R_{so}$ alone changes U from 0.585 to 0.601 W/m²K — a 2.7% error that loses 1 full mark.
> **Pitfall #2:** Using **wrong sign convention for $\Delta T$** (inside–outside) — always use the **heat flow direction** to determine the correct sign.

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2024 Scheme | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain in detail **cool roofs and green roofs** as methods to reduce operational energy of buildings. Compare them on parameters of energy savings, cost, embodied energy, and storm-water management. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 12 mm clear glass window (U = 5.8 W/m²K, SHGC = 0.82) in a Trivandrum office faces west during peak afternoon. Window area = 40 m². Peak solar irradiance on west façade = 650 W/m². Indoor design temperature = 25 °C, outdoor = 36 °C. **(7 Marks)**

(i) Compute the peak conductive heat gain through the window.
(ii) Compute the peak solar heat gain through the window.
(iii) If replaced by a double-glazed low-e unit (U = 1.6 W/m²K, SHGC = 0.25), calculate the percentage reduction in total heat gain.

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Cool Roofs vs Green Roofs (7 Marks)**

**Cool Roofs (3 Marks):**

A cool roof is a roof system that delivers **high solar reflectance** (SRI ≥ 78) and **high thermal emittance** (≥ 0.75) to minimize heat absorption. Types include:

1. **Coated roof** — acrylic, silicone, or elastomeric white coatings applied over existing concrete/metal roofs
2. **Cool-coloured shingles/tiles** — pigmented tiles with high reflectance (SRI 40–60)
3. **Single-ply membranes** — TPO, PVC sheets with reflective surface

**Working principle:** Reflects 60–85% of solar radiation; reduces roof surface temperature by 15–30 °C; lower conductive heat flux into the room; reduces chiller load by 15–25%.

**Advantages:** Low cost (₹250–450/m²), < 1 year payback, easy retrofit, minimal maintenance.

**Limitations:** Coating degrades over 8–10 years (requires re-coating), soiling reduces reflectance (needs cleaning), less effective during monsoons.

**Green Roofs (3 Marks):**

A green roof is a vegetated roof system comprising waterproofing, root barrier, drainage layer, growing medium (substrate), and plants. Two main types:

1. **Extensive** — shallow substrate (50–150 mm), sedums, mosses; low maintenance; 60–150 kg/m² load
2. **Intensive** — deep substrate (>200 mm), shrubs, trees; garden-like; needs structural reinforcement; > 300 kg/m² load

**Working principle:** Three simultaneous effects — (a) **Shading** of the roof by plants reduces solar gain; (b) **Evapotranspiration** by plants cools the roof surface (latent heat of vaporization); (c) **Thermal mass and insulation** of the substrate layers.

**Advantages:** Reduces cooling load by 30–40%, excellent storm-water retention (50–80% annual rainfall), biodiversity, urban heat island mitigation, extends roof membrane life.

**Limitations:** Higher capital cost (₹2000–5000/m²), structural loading, irrigation need, skilled maintenance, leak risk.

**Comparison Table (1 Mark):**

| Parameter | Cool Roof | Green Roof (Extensive) |
|---|---|---|
| Energy savings on roof | 15–25% | 30–40% |
| Capital cost (₹/m²) | 250–450 | 2000–5000 |
| Embodied energy | Low | High |
| Payback | < 1 year | 8–10 years |
| Storm-water mgmt. | None | Excellent |
| Structural load | Negligible | 60–150 kg/m² |
| Maintenance | Low | Moderate |

---

**Part (b) — Window Heat Gain Numerical (7 Marks)**

**(i) Conductive Heat Gain (2 Marks):**

$$Q_{cond} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 5.8 \times 40 \times (36 - 25)$$

$$Q_{cond} = 5.8 \times 40 \times 11 = 2552 \text{ W} = 2.552 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) Solar Heat Gain (2 Marks):**

$$Q_{solar} = SHGC \cdot A \cdot I = 0.82 \times 40 \times 650$$

$$Q_{solar} = 21{,}320 \text{ W} = 21.32 \text{ kW}$$

Total heat gain (baseline):

$$Q_{total}^{old} = 2.552 + 21.32 = 23.872 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Reduction with Low-e Double Glazing (3 Marks):**

New conductive heat gain:

$$Q_{cond}^{new} = 1.6 \times 40 \times 11 = 704 \text{ W} = 0.704 \text{ kW}$$

New solar heat gain:

$$Q_{solar}^{new} = 0.25 \times 40 \times 650 = 6500 \text{ W} = 6.5 \text{ kW}$$

New total heat gain:

$$Q_{total}^{new} = 0.704 + 6.5 = 7.204 \text{ kW}$$

Percentage reduction:

$$\%\text{Reduction} = \frac{23.872 - 7.204}{23.872} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{16.668}{23.872} \times 100 = 69.83\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #3:** Students frequently compute only the **solar** heat gain reduction (which is 69.5%) and forget the conductive component. Since the problem explicitly states "total heat gain," both components **must** be included.
> **Pitfall #4:** Common error — using SHGC for solar heat gain but forgetting that **some absorbed solar radiation is also re-radiated inward** as secondary heat transfer. The simplified SHGC equation above is the **admitted solar gain** (ECBC/NFRC convention) and is acceptable at B.Tech level.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-Exam Quick-Fire Revision Points (Most Frequently Asked in KTU):**

- [x] **Building Envelope** = physical separator between indoor and outdoor; includes walls, roof, floor, openings, insulation, air barriers.
- [x] **Three modes of heat transfer** through envelope: **Conduction, Convection, Radiation** — all three must be addressed in design.
- [x] **U-value** (W/m²K) = rate of heat flow; **lower is better**. **R-value** (m²K/W) = insulating power; **higher is better**. They are reciprocals.
- [x] **Master formula** for multi-layer wall (1-D steady-state):
$$U = \frac{1}{R_{si} + \sum (x_i/k_i) + R_{so}}$$
- [x] **ECBC 2017 India Limits** (composite climate): Roof U ≤ 0.4, Wall U ≤ 0.4, Window U ≤ 3.3 W/m²K.
- [x] **SHGC** = solar fraction admitted through window (0 to 1); **lower for hot climates** (≤ 0.25 ECBC).
- [x] **Cool Roof** = SRI ≥ 78; reflects 60–85% solar; saves 15–25% roof energy; cheapest intervention.
- [x] **Green Roof** = 30–40% roof energy savings; storm-water retention 50–80%; costly; structural loading.
- [x] **Insulation rule of diminishing returns:** Doubling insulation thickness does NOT halve U. Always recompute $R_{total}$.
- [x] **Surface film coefficients (ECBC):** $h_i = 8.3$ W/m²K, $h_o = 23$ W/m²K — these are **never zero**, always include.
- [x] **Window-to-Wall Ratio (WWR):** Typical optimum 25–40%; high WWR increases solar gain and reduces wall area (trade-off).
- [x] **Thermal bridge** = localized area of high conductivity (e.g., concrete column in brick wall) — must be mitigated using insulation wraps.
- [x] **Air infiltration** (ACH₅₀) is responsible for **5–15% of total cooling load** in leaky buildings — air-sealing is as critical as insulation.
- [x] **Embodied vs Operational energy** trade-off: A 50 mm aerogel blanket (high embodied energy) takes 15+ years to pay back operationally — context matters.
- [x] **Kerala-specific note:** Use **high-mass walls with external insulation** to handle both monsoon humidity and summer heat. Cool roofs + external shading devices + cross ventilation = Kerala's envelope design mantra.
- [x] **For 14-mark numerical questions:** Always show the full resistance-sum with units, the final U-value to 2–3 decimal places, and the final Q in watts — the valuation key demands each step.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Heat Gain Coefficient
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET752 - SUSTAINABLE CONSTRUCTION PRACTICES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Solar Heat Gain Coefficient # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition The **Solar Heat Gain Coefficient (SHGC)**, also denoted as **g-value** (in European/ISO standards) or **Solar Factor (SF)**, is a dimensionless ratio that quantifies the fraction of incident total solar radiation (direct + diffuse) that passes through a fenestration system (window, skylight, curtain wall, or translucent building envelope component) and is admitted as heat into the interior of a building. It is the modern, ASHRAE 90.1 / ISO 15099 / ECBC-compliant replacement for the older **Shading Coefficient (SC)** metric and is mathematically defined as: $$\text{SHGC} = \frac{\text{Total solar heat gain through the fenestration element}}{\text{Total solar radiation incident on the fenestration element}}$$ The value ranges from **0 to 1**: - **SHGC = 0**: No solar heat is transmitted (perfectly opaque / reflective). - **SHGC = 1**: All incident solar radiation enters the building. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** > *"SHGC is the ratio of total solar heat gain through a glazing system to the incident solar radiation on that glazing system, including both directly transmitted radiation and radiation absorbed by the glazing and re-radiated, conducted, or convected inwards."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Building **Imagine a glass window as a "Heat Sieve."** - If you pour 100 buckets of sunlight energy (Watts) onto a window from the sun, and **40 buckets** of heat energy actually end up heating your room, the window has an SHGC of **0.40**. - A regular single-pane clear glass lets in roughly **86 buckets** → SHGC ≈ **0.86** (very hot room). - A high-performance **Low-E double-glazed window** with spectrally selective coating might only let **25 buckets** through → SHGC ≈ **0.25** (comfortable room). **Why does this matter in Kerala?** Kerala's tropical, hot-humid climate (Köppen: *Am*) means high solar intensity (~4.5–5.5 kWh/m²/day) strikes buildings almost year-round. **Reducing SHGC directly reduces air-conditioning (AC) load**, which is the single largest electricity consumer in commercial buildings of Kerala (often 50–60% of total building energy). > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction — SHGC vs. U-factor:** > - **SHGC** controls *solar heat* (radiant, sun-driven) — important in **cooling-dominant** climates like Kerala. > - **U-factor** controls *conductive heat* (temperature-driven, day & night) — important in **cold** climates. > - In Kerala, we prioritize **low SHGC** for east, west, and south-facing glazing; in cold climates, the priority flips to **low U-factor**. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Values | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit | |-----------|--------|----------------|------| | Reference glass for SC | g_ref | **0.87** | dimensionless | | Indoor film coefficient | h_i | **8.0** | W/m²·K | | Outdoor film coefficient (wind ~3 m/s) | h_o | **23.0** | W/m²·K | | Total solar irradiance (standard) | I_solar | **783** | W/m² | | Conversion: SC to SHGC | SHGC | **= 0.87 × SC** | dimensionless | > [!TIP] > The reference glass for Shading Coefficient is **1/8-inch (3 mm) clear double-strength float glass**, whose SHGC ≈ 0.87. This is why the conversion factor between SC and SHGC is **0.87** (not 1.0). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of SHGC The total solar heat gain is **not** just direct transmission. A fenestration system interacts with solar radiation in three distinct physical pathways: 1. **Direct Transmittance (τ_sol)**: Solar radiation that passes straight through the glass without being absorbed or reflected. 2. **Absorptance (α_sol)**: The portion of solar radiation absorbed by the glass body and frame. 3. **Reflectance (ρ_sol)**: The portion reflected back to the outside. Conservation of energy for the glazing system requires: $$\tau_{\text{sol}} + \alpha_{\text{sol}} + \rho_{\text{sol}} = 1.0$$ However, the **SHGC is greater than just τ_sol** because the absorbed portion (α_sol) is partially re-released **inward** into the room as heat (via re-radiation, conduction, and convection). Only a small fraction of absorbed heat is re-released outward. ## 2.2 The Two-Term SHGC Equation The SHGC is split into a **direct (transmitted) component** and an **indirect (re-radiated inward) component**: $$\text{SHGC} = \tau_{\text{sol}} + q_i$$ Where: - **τ_sol** = Direct solar transmittance of the glazing (0 to 1). - **q_i** = Indirect heat transfer factor, representing the inward-released fraction of absorbed solar radiation. ## 2.3 Derivation of the Indirect Heat Transfer Factor (q_i) The absorbed solar radiation (α_sol × I_solar) is split into **outward** and **inward** heat flows through the glass body. Using the simple **resistance-network analogy** for a single-pane glass exposed to indoor and outdoor film coefficients: $$q_i = \alpha_{\text{sol}} \times \frac{h_i}{h_i + h_o}$$ Where: - **h_i** = Indoor surface heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K). - **h_o** = Outdoor surface heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K). The intuition: heat absorbed by the glass "splits" between the two faces proportionally to the conductances (the h-values act as conductances to the two ambient air reservoirs). ## 2.4 SHGC — Shading Coefficient Conversion The older **Shading Coefficient (SC)** is referenced in many Indian building codes (e.g., ECBC 2007). The conversion is: $$\text{SC} = \frac{\text{SHGC}}{\text{SHGC}_{\text{ref}}} = \frac{\text{SHGC}}{0.87}$$ $$\boxed{\text{SHGC} = 0.87 \times \text{SC}}$$ ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formula Bank — Solar Heat Gain Coefficient** | # | Formula | Description | Unit | |---|---------|-------------|------| | 1 | SHGC = τ_sol + q_i | Fundamental definition (direct + indirect) | dimensionless | | 2 | q_i = α_sol × [h_i / (h_i + h_o)] | Indirect re-radiated fraction | dimensionless | | 3 | SHGC = 0.87 × SC | Conversion from Shading Coefficient | dimensionless | | 4 | SC = SHGC / 0.87 | Inverse conversion | dimensionless | | 5 | τ_sol + α_sol + ρ_sol = 1 | Energy balance on glazing | dimensionless | | 6 | Q_solar = SHGC × A × I_solar | Total solar heat gain into room | W | | 7 | Cooling Load Factor (CLF) | Q_cooling = CLF × Q_solar | W | | 8 | ECBC max SHGC (warm-humid) | 0.25 – 0.40 (orient. dependent) | dimensionless | | 9 | g-value (ISO) | = SHGC (synonymous in modern codes) | dimensionless | | 10 | u-value (ISO for U-factor) | NOT same as SHGC — conduction only | W/m²·K | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | How SHGC is Used | |-------------|------------------| | **Building Energy Simulation** | Direct input to EnergyPlus, eQUEST, IES, DesignBuilder for cooling load. | | **ECBC Compliance (India)** | ECBC 2017 + SuperECBC set maximum allowable SHGC for different orientations and Window-to-Wall Ratios (WWR). | | **Green Rating (GRIHA, IGBC)** | Optimized SHGC values earn credit points under envelope design categories. | | **Window Selection Specification** | Architects specify SHGC + U-factor + VT (Visible Transmittance) in window schedules. | | **Passive Design** | Overhangs, fins, louvers are designed to *modify effective SHGC* seasonally. | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: SHGC of a 3 mm Clear Float Glass **Given data** (standard optical properties of 3 mm clear float glass under normal incidence, total solar spectrum, weighted): - Solar transmittance, τ_sol = **0.83** - Solar absorptance, α_sol = **0.13** - Solar reflectance (front), ρ_sol = **0.04** - Indoor film coefficient, h_i = **8.0 W/m²·K** - Outdoor film coefficient, h_o = **23.0 W/m²·K** ### Step 1 — Verify energy conservation We must first confirm that the optical properties sum to 1.0 (no energy lost in the glass body): $$\tau_{\text{sol}} + \alpha_{\text{sol}} + \rho_{\text{sol}} = 0.83 + 0.13 + 0.04 = 1.00 \;\; \checkmark$$ > *Logic check: This proves no hidden optical absorption is unaccounted for. If the sum were not 1.00, we would know data is incomplete or inconsistent.* ### Step 2 — Compute the indirect heat transfer factor q_i Apply the split-flux formula for absorbed radiation re-radiated inward: $$q_i = \alpha_{\text{sol}} \times \frac{h_i}{h_i + h_o}$$ Substitute α_sol = 0.13, h_i = 8.0, h_o = 23.0: $$q_i = 0.13 \times \frac{8.0}{8.0 + 23.0}$$ $$q_i = 0.13 \times \frac{8.0}{31.0}$$ $$q_i = 0.13 \times 0.25806$$ $$q_i = 0.03355 \;\; (\text{rounded to } 0.034)$$ > *Interpretation: Of the 13% of solar energy absorbed by the glass, only 33.5% of that 13% (~3.4% of total incident) re-enters the room. The rest (~9.6%) re-radiates/convects back outside.* ### Step 3 — Compute the SHGC Add the direct transmittance and indirect inward-released fraction: $$\text{SHGC} = \tau_{\text{sol}} + q_i$$ $$\text{SHGC} = 0.83 + 0.03355$$ $$\boxed{\text{SHGC} = 0.8636 \approx 0.86}$$ ### Step 4 — Cross-validate against the SC reference The reference 1/8-inch (3 mm) clear float glass has SHGC = 0.87 (ASHRAE standard). Our computed 0.864 is within 0.7% of this — the small difference is due to rounding in the optical properties. ✓ ### Step 5 — Convert to Shading Coefficient (if needed) $$\text{SC} = \frac{\text{SHGC}}{0.87} = \frac{0.8636}{0.87} = 0.9926 \approx 1.0$$ A clear float glass with no external shading has SC ≈ 1.0 by definition, again validating the calculation. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: SHGC for Common Glazing Types | Glazing Type | τ_sol | α_sol | ρ_sol | q_i (with h_i=8, h_o=23) | **SHGC** | **SC** | |--------------|-------|-------|-------|--------------------------|----------|--------| | 3 mm clear float | 0.83 | 0.13 | 0.04 | 0.0335 | **0.864** | 0.99 | | 6 mm clear float | 0.77 | 0.17 | 0.06 | 0.0439 | **0.814** | 0.94 | | 6 mm tinted (grey) | 0.43 | 0.43 | 0.14 | 0.1110 | **0.541** | 0.62 | | Reflective (sputtered) | 0.21 | 0.21 | 0.58 | 0.0542 | **0.264** | 0.30 | | Double Low-E (e=0.1, air fill) | 0.39 | 0.27 | 0.34 | 0.0697 | **0.460** | 0.53 | | Triple Low-E (e=0.05, argon) | 0.27 | 0.30 | 0.43 | 0.0774 | **0.347** | 0.40 | | Spectrally selective (modern) | 0.20 | 0.27 | 0.53 | 0.0697 | **0.270** | 0.31 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the table:** Notice how **tinted glass** has a low SHGC mostly by *absorbing* solar radiation (high α_sol). This absorbed energy re-radiates from **both sides**, heating the room. In contrast, **reflective and spectrally selective** glass achieves low SHGC by *reflecting* (high ρ_sol) — the energy simply never enters the building. **Reflective glass is therefore more effective in hot climates like Kerala.** ## 3.3 Work Calculation: Total Solar Heat Gain into a Room **Problem:** A south-facing office window in Kochi has an area A = 6 m², an SHGC = 0.40, and is exposed to peak solar irradiance I = 700 W/m² at noon. Compute the peak solar heat gain through the window. ### Step 1 — State the heat gain equation $$Q_{\text{solar}} = \text{SHGC} \times A \times I_{\text{solar}}$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute values $$Q_{\text{solar}} = 0.40 \times 6 \; \text{m}^2 \times 700 \; \text{W/m}^2$$ $$Q_{\text{solar}} = 0.40 \times 4200 \; \text{W}$$ $$Q_{\text{solar}} = 1680 \; \text{W} = 1.68 \; \text{kW}$$ ### Step 3 — Convert to cooling load (TR — Ton of Refrigeration) 1 TR = 3.517 kW. Cooling Load = (SHGC × A × I) × CLF / 3.517. Using CLF = 1.0 (peak, unshaded): $$Q_{\text{cooling}} = \frac{1.68 \; \text{kW}}{3.517 \; \text{kW/TR}} = 0.478 \; \text{TR}$$ > *Interpretation: A 6 m² window in peak Kochi sun dumps nearly **half a ton of AC load** into the room — equivalent to running a 1.5 HP split AC just to fight the sun. Halving the SHGC (from 0.40 to 0.20) would halve this load to 0.24 TR — a tangible reduction in electricity bills.* ## 3.4 KTU-Standard Stepwise Solution Format (Template) > [!NOTE] > For full-mark answers, KTU examiners expect this exact 4-step pattern: > 1. **Statement of formula / principle** > 2. **Identification of given data with units** > 3. **Substitution and numerical evaluation** > 4. **Final boxed answer with units + engineering interpretation** # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Energy Flow Schematic — How Solar Heat Enters Through a Window The following block diagram traces the energy balance of a glazing element, showing exactly how incident solar radiation is partitioned into transmitted, absorbed, and reflected portions, and how the absorbed portion re-partitions into inward and outward heat flows. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["INCIDENT SOLAR RADIATION
I_solar = 100 percent"]:::inNode nodeB["REFLECTED OUTWARD
rho_sol approx 0.04 to 0.53
depending on glazing type"]:::refNode nodeC["ABSORBED BY GLASS
alpha_sol = 0.13 to 0.30
heating the glass body"]:::absNode nodeD["TRANSMITTED DIRECTLY
tau_sol = 0.20 to 0.83
passes through glass"]:::transNode nodeE["INWARD-RELEASED HEAT
q_i part of alpha_sol
h_i / h_i + h_o"]:::inRelNode nodeF["OUTWARD-RELEASED HEAT
1 minus q_i part of alpha_sol
h_o / h_i + h_o"]:::outRelNode nodeG["TOTAL HEAT GAIN INTO ROOM
SHGC = tau_sol + q_i
equals 0.20 to 0.87"]:::finalNode nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeD nodeC --> nodeE nodeC --> nodeF nodeD --> nodeG nodeE --> nodeG classDef inNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef refNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef absNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#CD5C5C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef transNode fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef inRelNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outRelNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef finalNode fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Decision Tree — Selecting the Right SHGC for Kerala Buildings ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeS["SELECT SHGC FOR GLAZING"]:::startNode nodeO1["ORIENTATION OF WINDOW"]:::decNode nodeO2["NORTH FACING"]:::branchNode nodeO3["SOUTH FACING"]:::branchNode nodeO4["EAST / WEST FACING"]:::branchNode nodeR1["SHGC 0.40 to 0.55
modest solar gain
use double low-E clear"]:::resNode nodeR2["SHGC 0.25 to 0.40
low gain
use spectrally selective"]:::resNode nodeR3["SHGC 0.20 to 0.30
very low gain
use reflective or high-performance low-E"]:::resNode nodeEND["SPECIFY IN WINDOW SCHEDULE
WITH U-factor and VT"]:::endNode nodeS --> nodeO1 nodeO1 --> nodeO2 nodeO1 --> nodeO3 nodeO1 --> nodeO4 nodeO2 --> nodeR1 nodeO3 --> nodeR2 nodeO4 --> nodeR3 nodeR1 --> nodeEND nodeR2 --> nodeEND nodeR3 --> nodeEND classDef startNode fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decNode fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef branchNode fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef resNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef endNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Schematic of the Resistance Network for Indirect Heat Flow (q_i) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeT_in["Indoor air
T_in"]:::airNode nodeR_conv_in["Film resistance
1 over h_i"]:::resNode nodeG_surf["Glass surface
absorbs alpha_sol fraction"]:::absNode nodeR_conv_out["Film resistance
1 over h_o"]:::resNode nodeT_out["Outdoor air
T_out"]:::airNode nodeT_in --- nodeR_conv_in --- nodeG_surf --- nodeR_conv_out --- nodeT_out classDef airNode fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef resNode fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B7E1A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef absNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#CD5C5C,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` > *Interpretation: This illustrates the parallel-path split for absorbed solar heat. Because indoor resistance (1/h_i = 0.125) is **larger** than outdoor resistance (1/h_o = 0.043), only a *fraction* of the absorbed heat flows inward — which is why the q_i factor is typically 0.03 to 0.08 even for absorptive tinted glasses.* # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]` **Define Solar Heat Gain Coefficient (SHGC) of a glazing material. State the relationship between SHGC and Shading Coefficient (SC).** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Solar Heat Gain Coefficient (SHGC) is the ratio of the total solar heat gain through a fenestration element to the total solar radiation incident upon it. It includes both the **directly transmitted** solar radiation (τ_sol) and the **indirectly re-radiated/convected** portion (q_i) of the absorbed radiation. $$\text{SHGC} = \tau_{\text{sol}} + q_i \quad ; \quad 0 \leq \text{SHGC} \leq 1$$ The relationship with Shading Coefficient (SC) is: $$\text{SC} = \frac{\text{SHGC}}{0.87} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \text{SHGC} = 0.87 \times \text{SC}$$ The factor **0.87** represents the SHGC of the reference 1/8-inch (3 mm) clear double-strength float glass. **(3 marks)** --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]` **A double-glazed unit has direct solar transmittance τ_sol = 0.45 and solar absorptance α_sol = 0.25. Using h_i = 8 W/m²K and h_o = 23 W/m²K, compute the SHGC.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Step 1:** Compute indirect factor q_i: $$q_i = \alpha_{\text{sol}} \times \frac{h_i}{h_i + h_o} = 0.25 \times \frac{8}{8 + 23} = 0.25 \times 0.2581 = 0.0645$$ **Step 2:** Compute SHGC: $$\text{SHGC} = \tau_{\text{sol}} + q_i = 0.45 + 0.0645 = 0.5145 \approx 0.51$$ $$\boxed{\text{SHGC} = 0.51}$$ **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Define SHGC and SC. Explain the components of SHGC with the help of an energy balance equation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a 6 mm tinted glass, the optical properties are τ_sol = 0.43, α_sol = 0.43, and ρ_sol = 0.14. Calculate the SHGC and SC. Compare its effectiveness with a reflective glass (τ_sol = 0.21, α_sol = 0.21, ρ_sol = 0.58) for tropical climate application. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Definition and Components (7 marks):** - **SHGC Definition (2 marks):** SHGC is the ratio of total solar heat gain through a fenestration to the total solar radiation incident on it. It is dimensionless, ranging from 0 (no transmission) to 1 (full transmission). It is the modern replacement for Shading Coefficient. - **SC Definition (1 mark):** Shading Coefficient (SC) is the ratio of SHGC of a given glazing to the SHGC of 3 mm clear float glass (reference = 0.87). - **Components and Energy Balance (4 marks):** Incident solar radiation on a glazing is split into three parts: 1. **Reflected (ρ_sol)** — sent back to outside. 2. **Absorbed (α_sol)** — trapped in glass body, then re-released partially inward and partially outward. 3. **Transmitted (τ_sol)** — passes directly into the room. The energy balance is: $$\tau_{\text{sol}} + \alpha_{\text{sol}} + \rho_{\text{sol}} = 1$$ Of the absorbed portion, the inward-released fraction (q_i) contributes to indoor heat gain. Therefore: $$\text{SHGC} = \tau_{\text{sol}} + q_i \quad ; \quad q_i = \alpha_{\text{sol}} \times \frac{h_i}{h_i + h_o}$$ With h_i = 8 W/m²K and h_o = 23 W/m²K, the ratio h_i/(h_i+h_o) ≈ 0.258. Thus ~26% of absorbed solar energy is released indoors. **(b) Numerical Comparison (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Tinted Glass (3 marks):** $$q_i = 0.43 \times \frac{8}{31} = 0.43 \times 0.2581 = 0.1110$$ $$\text{SHGC}_{\text{tinted}} = 0.43 + 0.1110 = 0.541$$ $$\text{SC}_{\text{tinted}} = \frac{0.541}{0.87} = 0.622$$ **Step 2 — Reflective Glass (3 marks):** $$q_i = 0.21 \times 0.2581 = 0.0542$$ $$\text{SHGC}_{\text{reflective}} = 0.21 + 0.0542 = 0.264$$ $$\text{SC}_{\text{reflective}} = \frac{0.264}{0.87} = 0.303$$ **Step 3 — Comparative Analysis (1 mark):** The reflective glass has a **much lower SHGC (0.264 vs 0.541)** because it rejects solar energy by *reflection* (ρ_sol = 0.58), whereas tinted glass rejects it by *absorption* (α_sol = 0.43). Absorbed energy raises the glass temperature and is partially re-radiated indoors (q_i = 0.111). For tropical climates like Kerala, **reflective glass is significantly more effective** at reducing cooling loads because the energy never enters the building envelope at all. **(Total 7 marks for part b)** --- ### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **(a)** Discuss the importance of SHGC in the context of sustainable building design in tropical climates like Kerala. Recommend suitable SHGC values for different orientations as per ECBC guidelines. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A west-facing window of a commercial building in Thiruvananthapuram has an area of 8 m² and uses double-glazed low-E glass with SHGC = 0.25. The peak afternoon solar irradiance on the west façade is 650 W/m². Calculate the peak solar heat gain and equivalent cooling load in TR. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Importance & ECBC Recommendations (7 marks):** - **Significance in Tropical Climate (3 marks):** In hot-humid climates like Kerala, solar heat gain through fenestration is the dominant contributor to cooling load (often 25–35% of total AC load). Reducing SHGC directly cuts refrigeration tonnage, peak electricity demand, and operating cost. ECBC and SuperECBC prescribe maximum allowable SHGC for compliance. - **Orientation-Based Recommendations (4 marks):** | Orientation | Recommended SHGC | Reasoning | |-------------|------------------|-----------| | North | 0.40 – 0.55 | Low-angle diffuse radiation; some gain acceptable for daylighting. | | South | 0.25 – 0.40 | High summer sun; use spectrally selective low-E. | | East | 0.20 – 0.30 | Low-angle morning sun penetrates deeply; need low SHGC. | | West | 0.20 – 0.30 | Hottest exposure; afternoon load coincides with peak AC use. | ECBC 2017 (India) for *Warm-Humid* climate: SHGC ≤ 0.25 for west/east and ≤ 0.40 for north/south, with WWR limits. **(Total 7 marks)** **(b) Peak Heat Gain Calculation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — State formula (1 mark):** $$Q_{\text{solar}} = \text{SHGC} \times A \times I_{\text{solar}}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute (1 mark):** $$Q_{\text{solar}} = 0.25 \times 8 \; \text{m}^2 \times 650 \; \text{W/m}^2 = 1300 \; \text{W} = 1.30 \; \text{kW}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to TR (3 marks):** $$1 \; \text{TR} = 3.517 \; \text{kW}$$ $$Q_{\text{cooling}} = \frac{1.30}{3.517} = 0.370 \; \text{TR}$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation (2 marks):** A single 8 m² west window contributes ~0.37 TR of cooling load at peak afternoon. For a building with 10 such windows, the cumulative load is ~3.7 TR — equivalent to running a 5 HP outdoor AC unit **just to fight solar heat through glass**. If a cheaper glass with SHGC = 0.50 were used instead, the load would double to 0.74 TR per window — highlighting the strong ROI of high-performance glazing in Kerala commercial buildings. **(Total 7 marks)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Forgetting the 0.87 conversion factor** when asked about Shading Coefficient. Many students write SC = SHGC directly. ❌ The correct relation is SHGC = 0.87 × SC. (Loss: 1 mark) > 2. **Using only τ_sol as SHGC.** This is the most common conceptual error. SHGC = τ_sol + q_i, NOT just τ_sol. Always compute and add the indirect factor. (Loss: 2 marks) > 3. **Mixing SHGC with U-factor.** SHGC is dimensionless and relates to *solar* heat; U-factor has units W/m²K and relates to *temperature-driven conductive* heat. Examiners specifically test this distinction. (Loss: 1–2 marks) > 4. **Wrong h_i and h_o values.** Use h_i = 8 W/m²K and h_o = 23 W/m²K unless otherwise specified. Using reversed values gives q_i > 0.5, which is physically impossible for any reasonable glass. (Loss: 1 mark) > 5. **Skipping units in final answer.** Always write **W or kW or TR** explicitly. (Loss: 0.5 mark) > 6. **No engineering interpretation.** A full-mark answer for a 14-mark numerical problem requires a concluding sentence on *practical significance* (e.g., "this reduces AC tonnage by X%"). (Loss: 1–2 marks) --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Solar Heat Gain Coefficient** - ✅ **SHGC** = fraction of incident solar radiation admitted indoors through glazing (0 to 1). - ✅ Two components: **Direct transmittance (τ_sol)** + **Indirect inward-released absorbed heat (q_i)**. - ✅ **q_i = α_sol × h_i / (h_i + h_o)**, with **h_i = 8** and **h_o = 23 W/m²K**. - ✅ **SHGC = 0.87 × SC**; reference glass is 3 mm clear float with SHGC = 0.87. - ✅ **Low SHGC = Low solar heat gain = lower AC load** → critical for Kerala's hot-humid climate. - ✅ **SHGC ≠ U-factor**: SHGC controls solar/radiant heat; U-factor controls conductive heat. - ✅ **Tinted glass** absorbs (low SHGC but hot glass body); **reflective glass** reflects (low SHGC, cooler glass body) — **reflective is better in tropics**. - ✅ **ECBC (India) recommendations for warm-humid climate**: SHGC ≤ 0.25 for east/west, ≤ 0.40 for north/south. - ✅ **Energy balance on glass**: τ_sol + α_sol + ρ_sol = 1.0 (verify before calculation). - ✅ **Q_solar (W) = SHGC × A × I_solar** — peak instantaneous heat gain through window. - ✅ **1 TR = 3.517 kW** — convert heat gain to AC tonnage. - ✅ Kerala's solar irradiance peaks at ~700–900 W/m²; a 6 m² clear-glass window can dump **1+ TR** of unwanted heat. - ✅ **Specifying glass for KTU projects**: always state SHGC, U-factor, and Visible Transmittance (VT) together — they form the "**energy triplet**" of fenestration performance. - ✅ GRIHA / IGBC credit points awarded for SHGC values below ECBC baseline — direct link to sustainable design.

topics.title: U-Values for Facade Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET752 - SUSTAINABLE CONSTRUCTION PRACTICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** U-Values for Facade Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the domain of sustainable building design and thermal performance of building envelopes, the **U-Value** (also known as the **Thermal Transmittance** or **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient**) is defined as the steady-state rate of heat transfer through a unit area of a building element (such as a wall, roof, floor, or window) per unit temperature difference between the warm side and the cold side, measured in **watts per square metre per kelvin (W/m²K)**.

The U-value is the **reciprocal of the total thermal resistance (R_total)** of the construction assembly, including surface air films, structural layers, insulation, cavities, and any thermal bridges.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECET752 Module 2):**
> The U-value governs the heating/cooling load of a building, directly influencing operational energy consumption, ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code) compliance, IGBC/GRIHA ratings, and life-cycle thermal comfort. Lower U-values indicate better insulating performance.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are holding a cup of hot coffee on a winter morning. A **thin steel cup** (high U-value, low resistance) lets heat escape quickly — the coffee turns cold in minutes. A **double-walled vacuum flask** (low U-value, high resistance) keeps coffee hot for hours because heat struggles to pass through it.

In a building facade, the U-value works exactly the same way:
- A **single-pane glass window** acts like the steel cup — heat rushes out (high U ≈ 5.7 W/m²K).
- A **double-glazed low-E insulated wall** acts like the vacuum flask — heat barely moves (low U ≈ 0.35 W/m²K).

## 1.3 Governing Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thermal Transmittance | **U** | **W/m²K** | 0.10 – 5.80 |
| Thermal Resistance | **R** | **m²K/W** | 0.17 – 10.00 |
| Thermal Conductivity | **k** or **λ** | **W/mK** | 0.02 – 2.50 |
| Material Thickness | **d** | **m** | 0.005 – 0.300 |
| Surface Film Resistance (Internal) | **R_si** | **m²K/W** | **0.13** (high emissivity) |
| Surface Film Resistance (External) | **R_se** | **m²K/W** | **0.04** (standard) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Definition Box:**
> The fundamental governing relationship taught across all KTU sustainable-construction modules is:
>
> $$U = \frac{1}{R_{total}}$$
>
> where $R_{total}$ is the algebraic sum of every individual thermal resistance in the heat-flow path.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logic Breakdown of U-Value Computation

Heat flowing from a warm interior to a cold exterior must cross the facade by **conduction, convection, and radiation**. Each of these mechanisms contributes a "resistance" to the heat flow. KTU examinations require students to identify and sum these resistances correctly. The logical steps are:

1. **Identify the construction assembly** (e.g., brick – air gap – insulation – block – plaster).
2. **Catalogue each homogeneous layer** and obtain its thickness ($d_i$) and thermal conductivity ($k_i$).
3. **Compute the conductive resistance** of each solid layer using $R_i = d_i / k_i$.
4. **Account for non-homogeneous layers** such as air cavities, which have a fixed *cavity resistance* (R_cavity) supplied by standards (IS 3792, ISO 6946, ASHRAE Fundamentals Handbook).
5. **Apply fixed surface film resistances** (R_si for the warm side, R_se for the cold side) to capture convective and radiative transfer at the air-film interfaces.
6. **Sum all resistances** in series to obtain $R_{total}$.
7. **Invert** $R_{total}$ to obtain U.
8. **Apply a correction factor** ($\Delta U$) for thermal bridges, fixings, and air gaps in insulation (per ISO 6946).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **must-memorize cheat sheet** for the KTU End Semester Examination. Memorize the equations, units, and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Standard / Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_i = \dfrac{d_i}{k_i}$ | Conductive resistance of a homogeneous solid layer | m²K/W | Fourier's Law |
| 2 | $R_{si} = 0.13$ | Internal surface film resistance (high emissivity, still air) | m²K/W | ISO 6946, IS 3792 |
| 3 | $R_{se} = 0.04$ | External surface film resistance (standard exposure) | m²K/W | ISO 6946, IS 3792 |
| 4 | $R_{cavity} = 0.18$ | Unventilated air cavity (≈ 20 mm) | m²K/W | ISO 6946 Table 1 |
| 5 | $R_{total} = R_{si} + \sum \dfrac{d_i}{k_i} + R_{cavity} + R_{se}$ | Total resistance of layered facade | m²K/W | ISO 6946 |
| 6 | $U = \dfrac{1}{R_{total}}$ | Steady-state thermal transmittance | W/m²K | Reciprocal identity |
| 7 | $U_c = U + \Delta U$ | Corrected U-value (accounting for thermal bridges/air voids) | W/m²K | ISO 6946 §6 |
| 8 | $q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat loss through element (Q) | W (J/s) | KTU heat-load module |
| 9 | $U_{R-value} = \dfrac{1}{R_{Imperial}} \times 5.678$ | Conversion: Imperial R (ft²·°F·h/BTU) → SI U | — | Unit conversion |
| 10 | $U_{window} = \dfrac{1}{R_{si} + R_{glass} + R_{gap} + R_{glass} + R_{se}}$ | U-value for double/triple-glazed units | W/m²K | IS 3792 |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In practice, KTU graduates working as **energy auditors, green-building consultants, ECBC compliance officers, or BIM coordinators** will use U-value calculations to:

- **Size HVAC equipment**: Heat loss $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ directly determines chiller/heater tonnage.
- **Certify green ratings**: IGBC & GRIHA award credits for U-values below 0.45 W/m²K in walls.
- **Validate ECBC compliance**: ECBC 2017 (India) prescribes maximum U-values of **0.40 W/m²K** for opaque walls in the Composite Climate Zone.
- **Optimize wall section thickness**: Iterative U-value calculations help choose the right insulation thickness to meet a target U-value (e.g., 50 mm vs. 75 mm XPS).
- **Mitigate thermal bridges**: Columns, slab edges, and window jambs act as thermal bridges; their effect is captured using the $\Delta U$ correction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Master Problem: U-Value of a Multi-Layered Facade Wall

### 3.1.1 Given Data (Typical Cavity Wall)

Consider an exterior cavity-wall assembly (warm interior → cold exterior) composed of the following layers, as per IS 3792:1978 (Reaffirmed 2016) and ISO 6946:2017:

| Layer (from inside → outside) | Thickness $d$ (m) | Thermal Conductivity $k$ (W/mK) | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Internal plaster (Gypsum) | 0.013 | 0.45 | IS 3792 |
| Concrete block (Hollow dense) | 0.100 | 0.51 | IS 3792 |
| EPS insulation board | 0.050 | 0.040 | Manufacturer datasheet |
| Ventilated air cavity (20 mm) | 0.020 | — (use R = 0.18) | ISO 6946 |
| Facing brick (clay) | 0.102 | 0.77 | IS 3792 |

Fixed constants: $R_{si} = 0.13 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$, $R_{se} = 0.04 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$.

### 3.1.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the fundamental U-value identity:**

The total thermal resistance of a layered element is the sum of all individual resistances in series, since the same heat flux $q$ passes through each layer (1-D steady-state conduction).

$$
R_{total} = R_{si} + R_{plaster} + R_{block} + R_{EPS} + R_{cavity} + R_{brick} + R_{se}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the conductive resistance of each solid layer using $R = d/k$:**

For internal plaster:
$$
R_{plaster} = \frac{d_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.013}{0.45} = 0.02889 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

For concrete block:
$$
R_{block} = \frac{d_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.100}{0.51} = 0.19608 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

For EPS insulation:
$$
R_{EPS} = \frac{d_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.050}{0.040} = 1.25000 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

For facing brick:
$$
R_{brick} = \frac{d_4}{k_4} = \frac{0.102}{0.77} = 0.13247 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

**Step 3 — Insert the standard cavity and surface resistances:**

For the 20 mm ventilated air cavity (from ISO 6946 Table 1):
$$
R_{cavity} = 0.18 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

For the internal air film:
$$
R_{si} = 0.13 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

For the external air film:
$$
R_{se} = 0.04 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum all seven resistance components in series:**

$$
R_{total} = 0.13 + 0.02889 + 0.19608 + 1.25000 + 0.18 + 0.13247 + 0.04
$$

$$
R_{total} = 1.95744 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

**Step 5 — Invert $R_{total}$ to obtain the U-value:**

$$
U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{1.95744} = 0.5109 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the $\Delta U$ correction for thermal bridges (assume $\Delta U = 0.05$ W/m²K from ISO 6946 §6 for standard steel ties):**

$$
U_c = U + \Delta U = 0.5109 + 0.05 = 0.5609 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

**Step 7 — Verification against ECBC envelope limit:**

ECBC 2017 (Composite Climate) prescribes $U \le 0.40 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$ for opaque walls. The current value (0.5609) **fails** the criterion. To meet ECBC, increase EPS thickness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:** Notice that the 50 mm EPS layer alone contributes **63.9 %** of the total thermal resistance. This dominance is why insulation thickness is the most cost-effective lever for lowering U-values in facade design.

### 3.1.3 Reverse Engineering — Find Required Insulation Thickness for ECBC Compliance

Target: $U_{target} = 0.40 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$, so $R_{required} = 1/0.40 = 2.50 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$.

All resistances except EPS are fixed at:
$$
R_{fixed} = 0.13 + 0.02889 + 0.19608 + 0.18 + 0.13247 + 0.04 = 0.70744 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

Therefore, the required EPS resistance is:
$$
R_{EPS,req} = 2.50 - 0.70744 = 1.79256 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

Solving for thickness $d_{EPS}$:
$$
d_{EPS} = R_{EPS,req} \times k_{EPS} = 1.79256 \times 0.040 = 0.0717 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{d_{EPS} \approx 72 \text{ mm} \text{ of EPS required to achieve } U = 0.40 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Heat-Flow Path Through a Multi-Layered Facade

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **block-level functional architecture** of heat flow through a layered facade, showing each resistance component as a node and the heat flux as the connecting flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Warm Interior Air T_in] -->|Convective Film| B[R_si = 0.13]
    B --> C[R_plaster = 0.013 / 0.45]
    C --> D[R_block = 0.100 / 0.51]
    D --> E[R_EPS = 0.050 / 0.04]
    E --> F[R_cavity = 0.18]
    F --> G[R_brick = 0.102 / 0.77]
    G -->|Convective Film| H[R_se = 0.04]
    H --> I[Cold Exterior Air T_out]
    I -->|q = U x A x dT| J[Heat Loss Q watts]
    J --> K[HVAC Sizing]
    J --> L[ECBC Compliance Check]
    J --> M[IGBC / GRIHA Credits]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — U-Value Calculation Procedure (KTU Examination Workflow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Read Facade Section] --> S2[Identify Each Layer and Thickness d_i]
    S2 --> S3[Obtain k_i from IS 3792 or Datasheet]
    S3 --> S4{Layer is Solid or Cavity}
    S4 -->|Solid| S5[Compute R = d / k]
    S4 -->|Cavity| S6[Use Standard R_cavity from ISO 6946]
    S5 --> S7[Add R_si and R_se]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[Sum All R in Series = R_total]
    S8 --> S9[U = 1 / R_total]
    S9 --> S10{Include Thermal Bridges}
    S10 -->|Yes| S11[U_c = U + delta U]
    S10 -->|No| S12[Report U as Final]
    S11 --> S12
    S12 --> S13[Compare with ECBC Limit]
    S13 --> S14{Compliant}
    S14 -->|Yes| S15[Design Approved]
    S14 -->|No| S16[Increase Insulation Thickness]
    S16 --> S3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Block — Typical U-Value Ranges for Common Facade Materials

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Facade Material] --> B[Single Glass Pane]
    A --> C[Uninsulated Brick 230 mm]
    A --> D[Cavity Wall with 50 mm EPS]
    A --> E[Insulated Sandwich Panel]
    A --> F[Triple Glazed Low E Window]
    B --> B1[U approx 5.7 W per m2K]
    C --> C1[U approx 1.7 W per m2K]
    D --> D1[U approx 0.55 W per m2K]
    E --> E1[U approx 0.25 W per m2K]
    F --> F1[U approx 1.1 W per m2K]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PECET752]`

**Define the term U-value. State its SI unit and explain how it relates to thermal resistance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The U-value, also called **thermal transmittance**, is the steady-state rate of heat flow (in watts) through one square metre of a building element for a temperature difference of one kelvin between the warm and cold faces. Its SI unit is **W/m²K**.

It is related to total thermal resistance $R_{total}$ by the reciprocal relationship:

$$
U = \frac{1}{R_{total}}
$$

A higher U-value indicates **poorer insulation**, while a lower U-value indicates **better thermal performance**. (3 marks)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PECET752]`

**List the standard values of internal and external surface film resistances as per ISO 6946. Why are these values added to the conductive resistance of the solid layers?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- $R_{si} = 0.13 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$ (internal surface, high emissivity, still air)
- $R_{se} = 0.04 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$ (external surface, standard exposure)

These air-film resistances are added because heat transfer at the air–surface interface is not purely conductive — it combines **convection** (air movement along the surface) and **radiation** (emission from the surface to its surroundings). ISO 6946 fixes their values so that the U-value is calculated consistently across all elements. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Students commonly lose marks on this question type by: (1) **forgetting to include $R_{si}$ and $R_{se}$** when summing resistances, (2) using the **wrong standard values** for air-cavity resistance, (3) failing to **invert $R_{total}$** at the end and reporting $R$ as U, and (4) **omitting units** in the final answer. The four-step "identify → compute → sum → invert" pattern is mandatory; examiners award step-marks for each stage.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, PECET752]`

A 240 mm solid brick wall is plastered on the inside with 15 mm cement plaster. The thermal conductivities are: brick $k = 0.77$ W/mK and plaster $k = 0.45$ W/mK.

#### (a) Calculate the U-value of the wall using ISO 6946 surface resistances. (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify all layers:**

| Layer | $d$ (m) | $k$ (W/mK) |
|---|---|---|
| Internal plaster | 0.015 | 0.45 |
| Solid brick | 0.240 | 0.77 |

**Step 2 — Compute conductive resistances:**

$$
R_{plaster} = \frac{0.015}{0.45} = 0.03333 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

$$
R_{brick} = \frac{0.240}{0.77} = 0.31169 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

**Step 3 — Add standard air-film resistances:**

$$
R_{si} = 0.13, \quad R_{se} = 0.04
$$

**Step 4 — Sum and invert:**

$$
R_{total} = 0.13 + 0.03333 + 0.31169 + 0.04 = 0.51502 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

$$
U = \frac{1}{0.51502} = 1.9417 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

**Result:** $U \approx 1.94 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$. (7 marks)

#### (b) If a 50 mm expanded polystyrene (EPS) insulation board ($k = 0.040$ W/mK) is added on the external face of the same wall, find the new U-value. (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Add the new layer's resistance:**

$$
R_{EPS} = \frac{0.050}{0.040} = 1.25000 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

**Step 2 — Recompute total resistance:**

$$
R_{total,new} = 0.13 + 0.03333 + 0.31169 + 1.25000 + 0.04 = 1.76502 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

**Step 3 — Invert:**

$$
U_{new} = \frac{1}{1.76502} = 0.5666 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

**Result:** The U-value drops from **1.94 to 0.57 W/m²K** — a **70.8 % reduction** in heat loss, demonstrating the disproportionate benefit of external insulation. (7 marks)

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, PECET752]`

Explain the concept of thermal transmittance (U-value) and its significance in sustainable building design. With a neat sketch, illustrate the thermal resistance network of a cavity wall and determine the U-value of a wall composed of the following layers (from inside to outside):

- 13 mm gypsum plaster ($k = 0.45$ W/mK)
- 100 mm concrete block ($k = 0.51$ W/mK)
- 50 mm EPS insulation ($k = 0.040$ W/mK)
- 20 mm air cavity ($R = 0.18$ m²K/W)
- 102 mm facing brick ($k = 0.77$ W/mK)

Use $R_{si} = 0.13$ m²K/W and $R_{se} = 0.04$ m²K/W.

#### (a) Concept, Significance, and Resistance Network Sketch (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Concept:** U-value (thermal transmittance) is the rate of steady-state heat transfer per unit area per unit temperature difference across a building assembly, expressed in **W/m²K**. It is the reciprocal of the total thermal resistance $R_{total}$ of the assembly, which itself is the algebraic sum of all individual layer resistances traversed by heat in series.

**Significance in Sustainable Construction:**

1. **Energy efficiency:** Lower U-values reduce heating and cooling loads, cutting operational carbon.
2. **ECBC / IGBC compliance:** Codes prescribe maximum U-values for walls, roofs, and glazing.
3. **Thermal comfort:** Reduces radiant asymmetry and cold-wall condensation risks.
4. **HVAC sizing:** Heat-loss $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ directly determines mechanical equipment capacity.
5. **Life-cycle cost:** Lower U-values reduce lifetime operational energy bills, offsetting initial insulation cost.

**Resistance-Network Sketch (conceptual — see SECTION 4 Mermaid Diagram 4.1):**

$$
q \rightarrow [R_{si}] \rightarrow [R_{plaster}] \rightarrow [R_{block}] \rightarrow [R_{EPS}] \rightarrow [R_{cavity}] \rightarrow [R_{brick}] \rightarrow [R_{se}] \rightarrow q
$$

(7 marks)

#### (b) Numerical Determination of U-Value (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute each conductive resistance:**

$$
R_{plaster} = \frac{0.013}{0.45} = 0.02889 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

$$
R_{block} = \frac{0.100}{0.51} = 0.19608 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

$$
R_{EPS} = \frac{0.050}{0.040} = 1.25000 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

$$
R_{brick} = \frac{0.102}{0.77} = 0.13247 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum all resistances including surface films and cavity:**

$$
R_{total} = 0.13 + 0.02889 + 0.19608 + 1.25 + 0.18 + 0.13247 + 0.04
$$

$$
R_{total} = 1.95744 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute U-value:**

$$
U = \frac{1}{1.95744} = 0.5109 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{U \approx 0.51 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}}$. The dominant resistance is from the 50 mm EPS, contributing 63.9 % of the total. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- **U-value definition:** Steady-state heat transfer per unit area per unit temperature difference; SI unit is **W/m²K**.
- **Reciprocal identity (the MOST important equation):** $U = 1 / R_{total}$ — memorize this first.
- **Conductive resistance of a layer:** $R_i = d_i / k_i$ with **d in metres** and **k in W/mK**; never use mm for d without converting.
- **Standard surface films (ISO 6946):** $R_{si} = 0.13$ and $R_{se} = 0.04$ m²K/W — examiners **always** expect these added to the solid-layer sum.
- **Air-cavity resistance:** A 20 mm unventilated cavity has $R \approx 0.18$ m²K/W; cavities **do not** use $d/k$ because heat transfer is by radiation + convection in air, not pure conduction.
- **Insulation dominates:** A 50 mm EPS layer can contribute **60–70 %** of the total wall resistance, so it is the most cost-effective lever for lowering U.
- **Thermal bridges** (columns, slab edges, window jambs) are captured using $\Delta U$; corrected $U_c = U + \Delta U$ per ISO 6946.
- **ECBC 2017 limit** for opaque walls in the Composite Climate Zone: $U \le 0.40$ W/m²K.
- **Heat-loss formula:** $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ — used in HVAC sizing and energy-auditing problems.
- **Imperial–SI conversion:** A U-value expressed in **Btu/(hr·ft²·°F)** must be multiplied by **5.678** to obtain **W/m²K**.
- **Step-marks pattern in KTU valuation:** Examiners award 2 marks for layer identification + 3 marks for resistance computation + 2 marks for summation + 2 marks for inversion + final unit consistency — match this structure in your answer sheet.
- **Common mistake to avoid:** Reporting $R_{total}$ as the U-value without inverting — this costs the final 2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efficient Lighting Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET752 - SUSTAINABLE CONSTRUCTION PRACTICES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Efficient Lighting Technologies ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Efficient Lighting Technologies** refer to illumination systems and luminaires that deliver the required visual task illuminance (lux) using the **minimum possible electrical input (watts)**, while preserving acceptable visual comfort parameters such as **Color Rendering Index (CRI)**, **Correlated Color Temperature (CCT)**, and **Unified Glare Rating (UGR)**. The efficiency of a lighting source is quantified by its **Luminous Efficacy** ($\eta$), measured in **lumens per watt (lm/W)**. Mathematically, the fundamental relationship that defines efficient lighting is: $$\eta = \frac{\Phi_v}{P_{in}}$$ where: - $\Phi_v$ = Luminous flux emitted by the source (in lumens, **lm**) - $P_{in}$ = Electrical power input to the source (in watts, **W**) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Efficient lighting in sustainable construction is governed by the principles laid out in the **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC) 2017 / ECO Niwas Samhita** and the **Bureau of Indian Standards (IS 3646)** for interior illumination. A "green" luminaire is one that maximises $\eta$ while minimising embodied carbon and operating at a high **Power Factor ($\cos\phi$) ≥ 0.9**. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Water-Tap" Model Imagine you are watering a garden with two taps: - **Tap A** (Incandescent lamp): You turn the handle a lot, but very little water reaches the plant — most of the energy is wasted as heat (infra-red radiation). - **Tap B** (LED lamp): You barely turn the handle, and almost all the water reaches the plant in the form of useful photons in the visible spectrum. In this analogy: - **Water flow to the plant** = Visible light (lumens, useful output) - **Wasted steam from Tap A** = Heat energy (wasted IR radiation) - **Handle rotation** = Electrical watts consumed A student instantly sees that **more lumens per watt = less wastage = more sustainable lighting**. ### 1.3 Key Quality Metrics of "Good" Light | Parameter | Symbol | Ideal Range for Indoor Workspaces | Importance | |---|---|---|---| | Luminous Efficacy | $\eta$ | **80 – 150 lm/W** (modern LEDs) | Energy efficiency | | Color Rendering Index | CRI ($R_a$) | **≥ 80** for offices, ≥ 90 for studios | True colour perception | | Correlated Color Temperature | CCT (K) | **4000 K – 5000 K** (cool white) | Visual comfort & alertness | | Power Factor | pf | **≥ 0.90** | Reduces line losses | | Unified Glare Rating | UGR | **≤ 19** (offices) | Visual comfort | | Lamp Life | $L_{70}$ | **≥ 50,000 hours** (LED) | Maintenance sustainability | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:** Do **not** confuse **luminous efficacy** (lm/W) with **luminous efficiency** (a dimensionless ratio, 0 to 1). Efficacy has units; efficiency is unitless. Examiners frequently test this distinction. ### 1.4 Classification of Light Sources (Foundation Layer) 1. **Incandescent / Tungsten-Halogen** — Resistive heating of a tungsten filament. 2. **Fluorescent Lamps (FL / CFL)** — Low-pressure mercury vapour discharge + phosphor coating. 3. **High-Intensity Discharge (HID)** — Metal Halide (MH), High-Pressure Sodium (HPS), Low-Pressure Sodium (LPS). 4. **Light Emitting Diodes (LEDs)** — Semiconductor electroluminescence (p-n junction photon emission). 5. **Induction Lamps** — Electrodeless fluorescent excitation via RF magnetic field. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Photometric Foundations — The Logic Chain To evaluate "efficient" lighting, one must understand the **chain of photometric quantities**, from the source to the work-plane: 1. **Luminous Flux ($\Phi_v$)** — Total visible light energy emitted per second by a source. $$\Phi_v = K_m \int_{380}^{780} V(\lambda) \cdot \Phi_{e,\lambda} \, d\lambda$$ where $K_m = 683 \text{ lm/W}$ is the **maximum spectral luminous efficacy** of monochromatic radiation at 555 nm, and $V(\lambda)$ is the photopic luminosity function. 2. **Luminous Intensity ($I_v$)** — Flux per unit solid angle in a given direction. $$I_v = \frac{d\Phi_v}{d\Omega} \quad [\text{candela, cd}]$$ 3. **Illuminance ($E_v$)** — Flux incident on a unit area of surface. $$E_v = \frac{\Phi_v}{A} = \frac{I_v}{d^2} \quad [\text{lux, lx}]$$ The $1/d^2$ relationship is the **Inverse Square Law** — halving the distance quadruples the illuminance. 4. **Luminance ($L_v$)** — Light intensity per unit projected area of a surface (controls perceived brightness). $$L_v = \frac{I_v}{A \cos\theta} \quad [\text{cd/m}^2]$$ 5. **Luminous Efficacy ($\eta$)** — Visible light output per unit electrical input. $$\eta = \frac{\Phi_v}{P_{in}} \quad [\text{lm/W}]$$ ### 2.2 Why These Matter in Sustainable Construction - **Flux & Efficacy** decide the *number of fixtures* and *energy consumed*. - **Illuminance ($E_v$)** decides whether the **National Building Code (NBC 2016) / IS 3646** minimum standards are met (e.g., 300 lx for offices, 500 lx for reading, 150 lx for corridors). - **Luminance & UGR** control visual ergonomics and occupant well-being (a key **WELL v2 / IGBC** criterion). - **CRI & CCT** affect circadian rhythm, productivity, and retail sales performance. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — 80% of numerical questions in Module-2 are solved using these equations.** | # | Formula | Meaning / Application | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\eta = \Phi_v / P$ | Luminous efficacy of a source | **lm/W** | | 2 | $E = \Phi / A$ | Average illuminance on a surface | **lux (lx)** | | 3 | $E = I / d^2$ | Point-source illuminance (Inverse Square Law) | **lux** | | 4 | $E_1 / E_2 = (d_2 / d_1)^2$ | Illuminance variation with distance | dimensionless | | 5 | $N = (E \cdot A) / (\eta_{lamp} \cdot n \cdot UF \cdot MF)$ | Number of lamps / luminaires required | integer | | 6 | $LPD = P_{total} / A_{floor}$ | Lighting Power Density (ECBC metric) | **W/m²** | | 7 | $MF = LLD \times LDD \times BF$ | Maintenance factor components | dimensionless | | 8 | $\Delta E\% = \frac{\Phi_{old} - \Phi_{new}}{\Phi_{old}} \times 100$ | Energy / lumen reduction percentage | % | | 9 | $S = kW \times h \times R_s$ | Annual energy savings (kWh → ₹) | **kWh, ₹** | | 10 | $PB = \frac{C_{new} - C_{old}}{S_{annual}}$ | Simple payback period | **years** | | 11 | $\% \text{ Savings} = \frac{P_{old} - P_{new}}{P_{old}} \times 100$ | Wattage reduction in retrofit | % | | 12 | $PF = P_{real} / S_{apparent}$ | Power factor (line-loss indicator) | dimensionless | **Legend:** $N$ = number of luminaires, $n$ = lamps per luminaire, $UF$ = Utilization Factor, $MF$ = Maintenance Factor, $LLD$ = Lamp Lumen Depreciation, $LDD$ = Luminaire Dirt Depreciation, $BF$ = Ballast Factor, $R_s$ = electricity tariff (₹/kWh). ### 2.4 Comparative Analysis of Lighting Technologies (THE most expected table in KTU) > [!NOTE] > **Memorise this table verbatim** — the 14-mark question frequently asks for a comparative essay or tabulation on lamp technologies. | Lamp Type | Efficacy (lm/W) | CRI ($R_a$) | CCT Range (K) | Life (hrs) | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Incandescent (GLS) | 10 – 17 | 100 | 2700 | 1 000 | Decorative (banned in many states) | | Tungsten Halogen | 15 – 25 | 100 | 2900 – 3200 | 2 000 – 4 000 | Spot, accent lighting | | Linear Fluorescent (T5/T8) | 60 – 100 | 60 – 85 | 3000 – 6500 | 15 000 – 20 000 | Offices, schools | | Compact Fluorescent (CFL) | 50 – 70 | 80 – 90 | 2700 – 6500 | 8 000 – 10 000 | Residential retrofit | | Metal Halide (MH) | 70 – 115 | 65 – 90 | 3000 – 5000 | 10 000 – 20 000 | Industrial, stadium | | High-Pressure Sodium (HPS) | 80 – 150 | 22 – 80 | 1900 – 2200 | 20 000 – 30 000 | Street lighting | | Low-Pressure Sodium (LPS) | 100 – 200 | 0 (mono-chromatic) | 1800 | 15 000 | Tunnels, security | | **LED (Modern)** | **80 – 200** | **70 – 95+** | **2200 – 6500** | **50 000 – 100 000** | **All applications** | | Induction Lamp | 70 – 85 | 80 | 2700 – 5000 | 60 000 – 100 000 | Hard-to-reach fixtures | ### 2.5 Core Engineering Realisations - A **100 W incandescent** is replaced by a **18 W LED** for the same ~1500 lm output → **82 % energy reduction**. - A **40 W fluorescent tube** (≈ 88 W with magnetic ballast, ≈ 46 W with electronic ballast) is replaced by a **18–20 W LED tube**. - **Magnetic ballasts** waste 15–20 W as heat; **Electronic ballasts (HF)** operate at 25–50 kHz, improving efficacy by ~12 % and eliminating flicker. - LEDs achieve **Directional Light** (no need for reflectors) → higher system efficacy. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples ### 3.1 Derivation: Inverse Square Law for a Point Source Consider a point source emitting a total luminous flux $\Phi$ uniformly over a spherical solid angle $\Omega = 4\pi$ steradians. The luminous intensity in any radial direction is: $$I = \frac{\Phi}{\Omega} = \frac{\Phi}{4\pi}$$ For a small surface patch of area $A$ lying perpendicular to the radial direction at distance $d$ from the source, the subtended solid angle is: $$d\Omega = \frac{A}{d^2}$$ The flux intercepted by this patch is: $$d\Phi = I \cdot d\Omega = \frac{\Phi}{4\pi} \cdot \frac{A}{d^2}$$ The illuminance (flux per unit area) is: $$E = \frac{d\Phi}{A} = \frac{\Phi}{4\pi \cdot d^2} = \frac{I}{d^2}$$ This proves the **Inverse Square Law**: $$\boxed{E \propto \frac{1}{d^2}}$$ > **Physical Insight:** Doubling the mounting height of a luminaire from 3 m to 6 m reduces illuminance on the work-plane to **one-fourth (25 %)**. Designers therefore use wide-beam luminaires for high-bay installations. ### 3.2 Derivation: Number of Luminaires Formula (Lumen Method) The lumen method (also called the **"Useful-Lumen Method"** or **"zonal-cavity method's simpler form"**) is the standard design procedure in IS 3646. **Step 1 — Total lumens required to be emitted by all lamps:** $$\Phi_{total} = \frac{E \cdot A}{UF \cdot MF}$$ **Step 2 — Total lumens emitted by ONE luminaire (with $n$ lamps per luminaire, each of $\eta_{lamp}$ lumens):** $$\Phi_{luminaire} = n \cdot \eta_{lamp}$$ **Step 3 — Number of luminaires required (must be an integer, rounded UP):** $$N = \frac{\Phi_{total}}{\Phi_{luminaire}} = \frac{E \cdot A}{n \cdot \eta_{lamp} \cdot UF \cdot MF}$$ > **Validity Check:** $N$ must be a positive integer. If $N = 7.4$, the designer must use **8 luminaires** and re-verify $E$ on the work-plane. ### 3.3 Worked Numerical — Classic KTU 14-Mark Format **Problem [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]:** > A seminar hall of dimensions **20 m × 15 m × 4 m (height)** is to be illuminated to an average illuminance of **400 lux** on the working plane. The proposed design uses **LED down-lights**, each luminaire containing **2 lamps of 36 W** with a luminous flux of **3600 lumens per lamp**. Take **Utilization Factor (UF) = 0.6** and **Maintenance Factor (MF) = 0.8**. Determine the number of luminaires required. Also calculate the **Lighting Power Density (LPD)** in W/m² and verify against the **ECBC 2017 limit of 8.5 W/m²** for assembly occupancies. **Solution — Step 1: Identify given data** $$E = 400 \text{ lx}, \quad A = 20 \times 15 = 300 \text{ m}^2, \quad n = 2, \quad \eta_{lamp} = 3600 \text{ lm}$$ $$UF = 0.6, \quad MF = 0.8$$ **Step 2: Apply the lumen-method formula** $$N = \frac{E \cdot A}{n \cdot \eta_{lamp} \cdot UF \cdot MF}$$ $$N = \frac{400 \times 300}{2 \times 3600 \times 0.6 \times 0.8}$$ $$N = \frac{120{,}000}{3456}$$ $$N = 34.72 \text{ luminaires}$$ **Step 3: Round up to integer** $$N = 35 \text{ luminaires}$$ **Step 4: Calculate total connected electrical load** $$P_{total} = 35 \text{ luminaires} \times 2 \text{ lamps} \times 36 \text{ W} = 2520 \text{ W} = 2.52 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 5: Calculate Lighting Power Density (LPD)** $$LPD = \frac{P_{total}}{A} = \frac{2520}{300} = 8.4 \text{ W/m}^2$$ **Step 6: Verify against ECBC 2017** $$LPD_{actual} = 8.4 \text{ W/m}^2 \leq LPD_{ECBC} = 8.5 \text{ W/m}^2 \;\; \checkmark \text{ COMPLIANT}$$ **Step 7: Recompute the actual illuminance achieved with 35 luminaires** $$\Phi_{supplied} = 35 \times 2 \times 3600 \times 0.6 \times 0.8 = 120{,}960 \text{ lm}$$ $$E_{actual} = \frac{\Phi_{supplied}}{A} = \frac{120{,}960}{300} = 403.2 \text{ lx} \;\; \text{(slight over-design, acceptable)}$$ > **Final Answer:** 35 LED luminaires, LPD = 8.4 W/m², ECBC compliant. ### 3.4 Worked Numerical — Retrofit Payback Analysis **Problem [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]:** > An office floor (12 m × 10 m) currently uses **20 fluorescent fixtures**, each consuming **88 W (incl. ballast)**, operating **10 hours/day, 300 days/year**. The proposal is to retrofit with **LED panels of 36 W each** delivering equivalent illumination. Electricity tariff is **₹9.50 / kWh**. LED panel cost = **₹1800**, fluorescent fixture cost = **₹700**. Calculate: (i) annual energy savings in kWh and ₹, (ii) simple payback period, (iii) CO₂ avoided per year (assume 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh grid emission factor). **Solution — Step 1: Old system annual energy** $$E_{old} = 20 \times 88 \times 10 \times 300 = 5{,}280{,}000 \text{ Wh} = 5280 \text{ kWh/yr}$$ **Step 2: New system annual energy** $$E_{new} = 20 \times 36 \times 10 \times 300 = 2{,}160{,}000 \text{ Wh} = 2160 \text{ kWh/yr}$$ **Step 3: Energy and cost savings** $$\Delta E = 5280 - 2160 = 3120 \text{ kWh/yr}$$ $$\text{₹ Savings} = 3120 \times 9.50 = \text{₹ } 29{,}640 \text{ / yr}$$ **Step 4: Incremental capital cost** $$\Delta C = 20 \times (1800 - 700) = \text{₹ } 22{,}000$$ **Step 5: Simple Payback Period** $$PB = \frac{\Delta C}{\text{₹ Savings}} = \frac{22{,}000}{29{,}640} = 0.742 \text{ years} \approx 8.9 \text{ months}$$ **Step 6: CO₂ emission avoided** $$\text{CO}_2 \text{ avoided} = 3120 \times 0.82 = 2558.4 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{yr} \approx 2.56 \text{ t CO}_2/\text{yr}$$ > **Conclusion:** A 36 W LED retrofit pays back in under **9 months** and avoids **2.56 tonnes of CO₂ annually** — a textbook sustainable-construction win. ## 4.
