KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define **Luminous Efficacy** of a lamp. An LED bulb delivers **1800 lumens** at a power input of **15 W**. Calculate its luminous efficacy. State why this metric is the **single most important** parameter in sustainable lighting design. > **Model Answer (10 lines):** > Luminous efficacy ($\eta$) is the ratio of luminous flux ($\Phi_v$, in lumens) emitted by a source to the electrical power ($P$, in watts) consumed by it. > > $$\eta = \frac{\Phi_v}{P} = \frac{1800 \text{ lm}}{15 \text{ W}} = 120 \text{ lm/W}$$ > > It is the single most important metric in sustainable lighting because it directly quantifies the **"light per watt"** — a higher efficacy means fewer fixtures, lower connected load, lower electricity bills, and lower **embodied carbon over the operational life-cycle**. The ECBC 2017 prescribes efficacy-based lamp selection, and BEE 5-star ratings are awarded primarily on $\eta$. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **Color Rendering Index (CRI)** and **Correlated Color Temperature (CCT)**. Why is a **CRI ≥ 80 and CCT of 4000 K** recommended for office interiors? > **Model Answer (10 lines):** > **CRI ($R_a$)** is a dimensionless index (0–100) that measures how accurately a light source renders the colours of objects compared to a reference source. **CCT (Kelvin, K)** describes the *appearance* (warmth/coolness) of the emitted light, indicating the temperature of a black-body radiator of similar hue. > > - High CRI → better colour fidelity (essential in studios, retail, hospitals). > - CCT 4000 K → "cool white" → neutral, alertness-promoting, preferred for offices per **IS 3646 / NBC 2016**. > - For office interiors, **CRI ≥ 80** ensures printouts, fabric samples, and skin tones appear natural; **4000 K CCT** reduces eye-strain, supports circadian rhythm, and is glare-balanced with UGR ≤ 19. --- ### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]* **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **working principle of an LED**. With a neat sketch, describe the **p-n junction electroluminescence** mechanism. List **six advantages** of LED lighting over conventional fluorescent lamps in the context of sustainable construction. > **Model Answer:** > > **Working Principle:** A Light Emitting Diode is a **semiconductor p-n junction** device. When a forward bias is applied, electrons from the **n-region** recombine with holes in the **p-region** at the **depletion / active region**. The band-gap energy $E_g$ released during recombination is emitted as a **photon** of wavelength: > > $$\lambda = \frac{hc}{E_g} = \frac{1240}{E_g (\text{eV})} \text{ nm}$$ > > For visible light, $E_g$ lies in **1.8 eV (red) to 3.1 eV (violet)**. White LEDs are made by coating a **blue (InGaN) chip** with a yellow **YAG:Ce³⁺ phosphor** — part of the blue light is converted to yellow, and the mixture produces white light (this is the **Phosphor-Converted LED, or PC-LED**). > > **Six Advantages of LEDs over Fluorescent Lamps:** > > 1. **Higher Efficacy:** 80–200 lm/W vs 60–100 lm/W (linear fluorescent). > 2. **Longer Life:** 50 000 – 100 000 hrs vs 15 000 – 20 000 hrs. > 3. **No Mercury:** LEDs are RoHS-compliant and 100 % recyclable; CFLs contain 4–5 mg Hg. > 4. **Directional Emission:** Better optical control → less luminaire loss. > 5. **Robust Construction:** Solid-state → no filament / no glass / shock-resistant. > 6. **Instant Start & Dimmable:** No warm-up; compatible with 0–10 V / DALI dimming. > 7. *Bonus:* Lower HVAC load (less heat → smaller AC tonnage). **(b) [7 Marks]** A **drawing office** of size **18 m × 12 m** requires an average illuminance of **750 lux** on the work-plane. The proposed design uses **40 W LED panels**, each providing **4400 lumens**. Take **UF = 0.65** and **MF = 0.75**. Determine: (i) the number of LED panels, (ii) the **Lighting Power Density (LPD)**, (iii) the percentage energy saving if the existing system used 4 × 36 W (actual draw 160 W) fluorescent fixtures delivering the same illuminance, 18 such fixtures. (Assume 8 hrs/day, 280 days/year, ₹9/kWh.) > **Model Answer:** > > **Step 1 — Data:** $E = 750$ lx, $A = 18 \times 12 = 216 \text{ m}^2$, $n = 1$ lamp/panel, $\eta = 4400$ lm, $UF = 0.65$, $MF = 0.75$. > > **Step 2 — Number of LED panels (Lumen Method):** > > $$N_{LED} = \frac{E \cdot A}{n \cdot \eta \cdot UF \cdot MF} = \frac{750 \times 216}{1 \times 4400 \times 0.65 \times 0.75}$$ > > $$N_{LED} = \frac{162{,}000}{2145} = 75.52 \rightarrow 76 \text{ panels (round up)}$$ > > **Step 3 — LPD for LED design:** > > $$P_{LED} = 76 \times 40 = 3040 \text{ W}$$ > > $$LPD_{LED} = \frac{3040}{216} = 14.07 \text{ W/m}^2$$ > > **Step 4 — Old fluorescent system power:** > > $$P_{FL} = 18 \times 160 = 2880 \text{ W}$$ > > **Step 5 — Annual energy and savings:** > > $$E_{LED} = 3040 \times 8 \times 280 = 6{,}809{,}600 \text{ Wh} = 6809.6 \text{ kWh}$$ > > $$E_{FL} = 2880 \times 8 \times 280 = 6{,}451{,}200 \text{ Wh} = 6451.2 \text{ kWh}$$ > > $$\Delta E = 6451.2 - 6809.6 = -358.4 \text{ kWh}$$ > > (Negative because LEDs deliver higher illuminance at comparable wattage — the *real* saving is in **maintenance, replacement, mercury-free disposal**.) > > **Step 6 — Equivalised comparison (scale LEDs to same 750 lx):** > The 75.52 panels were theoretical; 76 panels deliver **6 % more light**, so for a like-for-like comparison the LED system could be scaled down. With only **72 LED panels** the same 750 lx is delivered: > > $$P_{LED,equiv} = 72 \times 40 = 2880 \text{ W (coincidentally equal)}$$ > > In this case the wattage is similar but the LED lasts **3× longer** and is **mercury-free** — the *true* sustainability gain is qualitative (life-cycle, recyclability, no UV). > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often forget to **round UP** the value of $N$ — if 75.52 is taken as 75, the design fails the 750 lx requirement. **Always round up.** > **Examiner's Pitfall #2:** In Part (b), students forget to **convert Wh to kWh** before applying the tariff. ₹9/kWh means **divide Wh by 1000 first**. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]* **(a) [7 Marks]** Classify and discuss the **major families of energy-efficient lamps** used in sustainable construction. For each, state one **typical application**. Highlight the **limitations of incandescent lamps** that led to their phase-out under the **BEE Star Labelling Programme** and the **ECBC 2017**. > **Model Answer:** > > **Classification of Energy-Efficient Lamps:** > > **1. Fluorescent Family (Low-pressure Mercury Discharge)** > - **Linear (T5, T8):** 60–100 lm/W, 15 000–20 000 hrs. Used in offices, schools, parking. > - **Compact Fluorescent Lamps (CFL):** 50–70 lm/W, 8 000–10 000 hrs. Used in residential retrofits. > - **Induction Lamps:** 70–85 lm/W, 60 000–100 000 hrs (no electrodes). Used in high-bay industrial and hard-to-access fixtures. > > **2. HID Family (High-Intensity Discharge)** > - **Metal Halide (MH):** 70–115 lm/W, good CRI (65–90). Used in stadiums, retail atriums. > - **High-Pressure Sodium (HPS):** 80–150 lm/W, low CRI (22–80). Used in street lighting and warehouses. > - **Low-Pressure Sodium (LPS):** 100–200 lm/W, CRI ≈ 0 (mono-yellow). Used in tunnels and security. > > **3. Solid-State Family (LEDs)** > - **PC-LED (Phosphor-Converted):** Most common, 80–150 lm/W. > - **RGB / Tuneable White:** Used in human-centric lighting. > - **OLED (Organic LED):** Future, used in diffused panels. > > **4. Plasma / Induction (Hybrid)** > - Sulfur plasma lamp: very high efficacy, niche use in horticultural lighting. > > **Why Incandescents Were Phased-Out:** > - **Very low efficacy (10–17 lm/W)** → 90 % energy wasted as IR heat. > - **Short life (1 000 hrs)** → frequent replacement, high maintenance. > - **High fire hazard** (hot filament). > - The **BEE Star Labelling Programme (2009 onwards)** and the **ECBC 2017** banned inefficient GLS lamps in commercial buildings; the global **Minamata Convention on Mercury** (2013) further accelerated the move to mercury-free LEDs. > - A 100 W GLS can be replaced by a 12–18 W LED delivering equivalent 1500+ lumens — an **82 % reduction** in lighting energy. **(b) [7 Marks]** A **factory floor** is being converted from **400 W metal-halide high-bay lamps** to **LED high-bays**. There are **30 luminaires** in operation, currently drawing **440 W each (incl. ballast)**. The LED replacement draws **120 W** with equivalent light output. The factory operates **two shifts (16 hours/day), 330 days/year**. Tariff = **₹8.20 / kWh**. Calculate: (i) the connected-load reduction in **kW**, (ii) annual energy savings in **kWh and ₹**, (iii) the simple **payback period** if each LED luminaire costs **₹6500** and the metal-halide luminaire costs **₹3500** (assume identical installation cost). Also compute the **annual CO₂ reduction** using India's grid emission factor of **0.79 kg CO₂/kWh**. > **Model Answer:** > > **Step 1 — Old connected load:** > > $$P_{old} = 30 \times 440 = 13{,}200 \text{ W} = 13.2 \text{ kW}$$ > > **Step 2 — New connected load:** > > $$P_{new} = 30 \times 120 = 3600 \text{ W} = 3.6 \text{ kW}$$ > > **Step 3 — Load reduction:** > > $$\Delta P = 13.2 - 3.6 = 9.6 \text{ kW}$$ > > **Step 4 — Annual energy savings:** > > $$\Delta E = 9.6 \text{ kW} \times 16 \text{ h/day} \times 330 \text{ days/yr} = 50{,}688 \text{ kWh/yr}$$ > > **Step 5 — Monetary savings:** > > $$S = 50{,}688 \times 8.20 = \text{₹ } 4{,}15{,}641.6 \approx \text{₹ } 4.16 \text{ lakh/yr}$$ > > **Step 6 — Incremental capital cost:** > > $$\Delta C = 30 \times (6500 - 3500) = 30 \times 3000 = \text{₹ } 90{,}000$$ > > **Step 7 — Payback period:** > > $$PB = \frac{90{,}000}{4{,}15{,}642} = 0.2165 \text{ years} \approx 2.6 \text{ months}$$ > > **Step 8 — CO₂ reduction:** > > $$\Delta \text{CO}_2 = 50{,}688 \times 0.79 = 40{,}043.5 \text{ kg/yr} \approx 40.04 \text{ t CO}_2/\text{yr}$$ > > **Interpretation:** The retrofit pays for itself in **under 3 months** and offsets **40 tonnes of CO₂** annually — equivalent to planting ~**660 mature trees per year**. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall #3:** When using tariff in ₹/kWh, students often multiply by **Watts × hours** directly without dividing by 1000. Always convert **W → kW** first (here 9.6 kW, not 9600 W). > **Examiner's Pitfall #4:** In payback problems, the **incremental cost** = (new − old) is what is recovered from savings — students erroneously divide the *full* new cost by savings and get a falsely long payback. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Luminous efficacy ($\eta = \Phi/P$) is the master metric** for efficient lighting — measured in **lm/W**. - The **higher the CRI (closer to 100)**, the more accurate the colour rendering; offices need **CRI ≥ 80**. - **CCT (Kelvin)** describes appearance: **< 3000 K warm**, **3000–4000 K neutral**, **> 4000 K cool**. - The **Lumen Method formula** $N = (E \cdot A) / (n \cdot \eta \cdot UF \cdot MF)$ is the bedrock of every design calculation — **always round $N$ up** to the next integer. - **Maintenance Factor** $MF = LLD \times LDD \times BF$ is typically **0.6–0.8** for indoor designs and **0.5–0.7** for industrial/outdoor dirty environments. - **LPD (W/m²)** is the ECBC's compliance metric — common residential limit: **5 W/m²**, office: **8.5 W/m²**, assembly: **10 W/m²** (verify with the latest ECBC amendment). - **LED is the present and near-future default** (80–200 lm/W, 50 000 hrs, mercury-free, dimmable, instant-on). - **Fluorescent & HID are "interim" sustainable options** — still acceptable under ECBC when LEDs are cost-prohibitive. - **Incandescent is essentially obsolete** in commercial construction — BEE / ECBC phase-out. - **Payback Period = Incremental Cost / Annual Savings** — typical LED retrofits pay back in **6 months to 3 years**. - **CO₂ avoided (kg/yr) = $\Delta E$ (kWh/yr) × 0.79 kg/kWh** (CEA / CEA-grid factor for India). - Always cross-check the chosen design against **IS 3646 (illuminance)** and **ECBC 2017 (LPD)** — both are routinely referenced in KTU question papers. - **Power Factor ≥ 0.9** is mandatory for compliance — choose electronic ballasts / high-pf LED drivers.

topics.title: Energy Efficient and BEE Rated Appliances for Heating and Air-Conditioning Systems in Buildings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET752 - SUSTAINABLE CONSTRUCTION PRACTICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy Efficient and BEE Rated Appliances for Heating and Air-Conditioning Systems in Buildings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)

The **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** is the statutory body constituted by the Government of India under the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001**, headquartered in New Delhi. Its primary mandate is to develop policies and strategies for reducing energy consumption, with a self-imposed goal of achieving a **reduction in energy intensity** of the Indian economy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BEE Star Labeling Program** is a voluntary (and in some cases mandatory) energy efficiency rating scheme that rates appliances on a scale of **1 to 5 stars** (recently extended up to **10 stars for certain inverter ACs**), where **higher stars = lower energy consumption**.

### 1.2 Air-Conditioning and Heating Systems in Buildings

Heating, Ventilation, and Air-Conditioning (HVAC) systems are responsible for approximately **40% to 60%** of total electricity consumption in commercial buildings, making them the single largest energy end-use in most building typologies. Sustainable HVAC design therefore constitutes the cornerstone of green building practice.

**Key Terminology:**

- **COP (Coefficient of Performance):** The ratio of useful heating or cooling provided to the work (electricity) consumed. Higher COP = more efficient.
- **EER (Energy Efficiency Ratio):** Ratio of cooling capacity (in BTU/hr) to electrical power input (in Watts) under a single standard test condition.
- **ISEER (Indian Seasonal Energy Efficiency Ratio):** The Indian equivalent of SEER, defined by BEE for variable-speed (inverter) air-conditioners tested at ambient temperatures representative of Indian climatic zones.
- **SCOP (Seasonal Coefficient of Performance):** European standard for seasonal heating efficiency, especially for heat pumps.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of BEE Star Rating like the mileage (km/L) shown on a car's dashboard.** A car that gives 25 km/L is "5-star efficient," while one giving 10 km/L is "1-star efficient." Similarly, a 5-star AC consumes far less electricity than a 1-star AC to deliver the same cooling comfort. The "fuel" in buildings is **electricity (kWh)**, and we want to maximize "comfort per unit of fuel" — exactly what COP and ISEER measure.

For an immediate grasp:

- A **1-Star Split AC** might consume around **1.5 units of electricity per hour**.
- A **5-Star Inverter AC** might consume only **0.6 to 0.7 units per hour** for the same tonnage and cooling load.

This is the single largest operating-cost reduction a homeowner or building manager can engineer simply by **appliance selection**.

---

### 1.4 Scope of This Module

This topic covers:

1. Energy-efficient **air-conditioning systems** (Window, Split, Inverter Split, VRF/VRV, Central Plant).
2. Energy-efficient **heating systems** (Heat Pumps, Solar Water Heaters, BEE-rated Storage Water Heaters).
3. The **BEE star rating framework** and the metrics that drive it.
4. **Selection criteria, payback economics, and ECBC compliance** for sustainable HVAC design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Energy Efficiency Metrics — The Theory Behind the Rating

Every star on a BEE label is backed by a measurable energy efficiency metric. The two most important metrics for HVAC are **EER (for fixed-speed units)** and **ISEER (for inverter units)**.

#### 2.1.1 EER (Energy Efficiency Ratio)

The EER is measured under a **single steady-state test condition**:

- **Test Condition:** Outdoor dry-bulb temperature of **35 °C**; Indoor at **27 °C DB / 19 °C WB**.

$$
\text{EER} = \frac{Q_{\text{cooling}} \text{ (W)}}{W_{\text{input}} \text{ (W)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In India, BEE commonly expresses EER as a "**Wh/Wh**" ratio in tabular form on the energy label. A higher EER means the AC delivers more cooling watts per electrical watt consumed.

#### 2.1.2 ISEER (Indian Seasonal Energy Efficiency Ratio)

Inverter ACs vary their compressor speed. A fixed-condition EER understates their real-world efficiency. The **ISEER** weights efficiency across four standard test points (T1, T2, T3, T4) corresponding to ambient loads of **20%, 50%, 75%, and 100%**.

$$
\text{ISEER} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{4} \left( Q_{c,i} \times h_{i} \right)}{\sum_{i=1}^{4} \left( W_{i} \times h_{i} \right)}
$$

Where:
- $Q_{c,i}$ = cooling capacity at test point $i$ (W)
- $W_{i}$ = power input at test point $i$ (W)
- $h_{i}$ = hours of operation at that test point (typically weighted by Indian climatic profile)

#### 2.1.3 COP (Coefficient of Performance)

Used primarily for **heat pumps and heating-mode ACs**:

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{heating}} = \frac{Q_{\text{heat delivered}}}{W_{\text{electrical input}}}
$$

For reversible heat pumps, **COP can be 3.0 to 5.0** for air-source units, meaning 1 kW of electricity delivers 3–5 kW of heat.

#### 2.1.4 SCOP (Seasonal Coefficient of Performance)

The seasonal average of COP across a heating season, defined per **EN 14825** and adopted by BEE for heat pump water heaters:

$$
\text{SCOP} = \frac{\sum Q_{h,j}}{\sum W_{j}}
$$

### 2.2 BEE Star Rating — The Bucketing Algorithm

The BEE assigns stars in **discrete buckets** of the ISEER/EER value. The thresholds are revised periodically. As per the **latest BEE notification for Room Air Conditioners (2024 schedule)**:

| Star Rating | ISEER Range (Fixed Speed) | ISEER Range (Inverter) |
|-------------|---------------------------|------------------------|
| 1 Star      | 2.30 – 2.49              | 3.30 – 3.59            |
| 2 Star      | 2.50 – 2.69              | 3.60 – 3.89            |
| 3 Star      | 2.70 – 2.89              | 3.90 – 4.19            |
| 4 Star      | 2.90 – 3.09              | 4.20 – 4.49            |
| 5 Star      | $\geq$ 3.10              | $\geq$ 4.50            |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inverter technology is the single most influential design choice** for energy efficiency. A 5-star inverter AC can be **30–40% more efficient** than a 5-star fixed-speed AC in real-world Indian conditions.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | EER | $\dfrac{Q_c}{W_{in}}$ | Wh/Wh (dimensionless) | Fixed-speed AC rating |
| 2 | COP (Cooling) | $\dfrac{Q_c}{W_{in}}$ | Wh/Wh | Chillers, heat pumps |
| 3 | COP (Heating) | $\dfrac{Q_h}{W_{in}}$ | Wh/Wh | Heat pump heaters |
| 4 | ISEER | $\dfrac{\sum Q_{c,i} h_i}{\sum W_i h_i}$ | Wh/Wh | Inverter AC rating |
| 5 | SCOP | $\dfrac{\sum Q_{h,j}}{\sum W_j}$ | Wh/Wh | Heat pump water heater |
| 6 | Annual Energy Consumption | $\dfrac{\text{Capacity} \times \text{Hours}}{\text{Efficiency}}$ | kWh | Operating cost |
| 7 | Annual Cost | $\text{kWh} \times \text{Tariff}$ | ₹/year | Economic analysis |
| 8 | Payback Period | $\dfrac{\Delta \text{Cost}}{\Delta \text{Annual Saving}}$ | years | Investment decision |
| 9 | Energy Savings % | $\dfrac{E_1 - E_2}{E_1} \times 100$ | % | Comparative rating |
| 10 | Tons of Refrigeration (TR) | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$ cooling | kW | Capacity conversion |
| 11 | BTU/hr to TR | $1 \text{ TR} = 12{,}000 \text{ BTU/hr}$ | BTU/hr | Capacity conversion |
| 12 | Heat Pump SPF | $\dfrac{Q_{\text{useful heat}}}{\sum W_{\text{electric}}}$ | Wh/Wh | Performance factor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion to remember forever:** $1 \text{ TR} \approx 3.517 \text{ kW} \approx 12{,}000 \text{ BTU/hr}$. A "1.5-ton split AC" thus delivers about **5.27 kW** of cooling at design conditions.

### 2.4 Heating Systems — Why Heat Pumps Win

For a sustainable building, the heating side is equally critical. The hierarchy from worst to best:

1. **Electric resistance heaters** (geysers, room heaters): COP = **1.0** (every kW of electricity gives 1 kW of heat).
2. **Fuel-based heaters** (LPG, kerosene): COPs in real-world combustion ≈ **0.5–0.7** (energy losses through flue).
3. **Air-source heat pumps (ASHP)**: COP = **2.5–4.0** in Indian winter conditions.
4. **Ground-source heat pumps (GSHP)**: COP = **4.0–5.5** (uses constant ground temperature of ~25 °C in India).
5. **Solar thermal + heat pump hybrid**: Apparent COP can exceed **6.0** when solar contribution is credited.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every 1 kW of electric resistance heating replaced by an ASHP at COP 3.5 saves ~2.5 kW of energy** — directly translating to **~70% electricity cost reduction** for the same indoor comfort.

### 2.5 Air-Conditioning System Selection Heuristics

| Building Type | Recommended System | Typical Capacity Range | Key Reason |
|---------------|--------------------|-----------------------|-----------|
| Small residence ($\leq$ 60 m²) | Split / Window AC | 1.0 – 1.5 TR | Low capital, ease of installation |
| Medium residence (60–150 m²) | Multi-split inverter | 2.0 – 3.0 TR | Zonal control, high ISEER |
| Large residence / Villa | VRF system | 4.0 – 8.0 TR | Multiple indoor units, one outdoor |
| Small office ($\leq$ 500 m²) | Ductable split / VRF | 5.0 – 15.0 TR | Flexible zoning |
| Large commercial / IT | Chilled Water Plant with VFD chillers | 50 – 500+ TR | High efficiency, central plant logic |
| Hotels | 4-pipe chilled water + heat recovery | As per load | Simultaneous heating and cooling |
| Hospitals | Dedicated AHUs + HEPA + heat recovery | Per floor zoning | Infection control + efficiency |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

These concepts are not academic — they drive the design of:

- **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code) compliant buildings** — mandatory for commercial buildings $\geq$ 250 kW connected load.
- **Eco-Niwas Samhita (ENS)** for residential buildings.
- **GRIHA and IGBC green building ratings** — HVAC efficiency earns 8–15 credit points.
- **Net-Zero Energy Buildings** — where HVAC efficiency is the first step before renewable integration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Annual Energy Consumption of an Air-Conditioner

**Statement:** Derive an expression for the annual electrical energy consumed by a Split AC of given tonnage and ISEER.

**Given:**

- Rated cooling capacity: $C$ in TR (tons of refrigeration)
- BEE star rating gives ISEER value: $\eta$ in Wh/Wh
- Annual operating hours: $H$ hours
- Note: $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$ of cooling

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Convert capacity to kW:**

$$
P_{\text{cooling}} = C \times 3.517 \quad \text{[kW]}
$$

This is the cooling output the AC must deliver when running at full load.

**Step 2 — Express electrical input as a function of ISEER:**

By definition, $\eta$ = (Cooling output in Wh) / (Electrical input in Wh). Therefore the electrical input power at full load is:

$$
P_{\text{input}} = \frac{P_{\text{cooling}}}{\eta} \quad \text{[kW]}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the energy consumed over the operating period:**

Energy is power multiplied by time. Therefore:

$$
E_{\text{annual}} = P_{\text{input}} \times H \quad \text{[kWh]}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 3:**

$$
E_{\text{annual}} = \frac{C \times 3.517}{\eta} \times H \quad \text{[kWh]}
$$

**Final Boxed Result:**

$$
\boxed{E_{\text{annual}} = \dfrac{3.517 \times C \times H}{\eta} \text{ kWh}}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Annual Operating Cost and Savings

**Step 5 — Multiply by electricity tariff $T$ (₹/kWh):**

$$
\text{Annual Cost} = E_{\text{annual}} \times T = \frac{3.517 \times C \times H \times T}{\eta} \quad \text{[₹/yr]}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute savings between an old (1-star) and a new (5-star) appliance:**

$$
\text{Savings} = E_{1} \cdot T - E_{2} \cdot T = T \times 3.517 \times C \times H \left( \frac{1}{\eta_{1}} - \frac{1}{\eta_{2}} \right)
$$

**Final Boxed Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Annual Savings} = 3.517 \times C \times H \times T \times \left( \frac{1}{\eta_{1}} - \frac{1}{\eta_{2}} \right) \text{ ₹}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Simple Payback Period

Let $\Delta C_{\text{cap}}$ be the additional capital cost of the more efficient appliance (in ₹). The simple payback is:

$$
\text{Payback} = \frac{\Delta C_{\text{cap}}}{\text{Annual Savings}} \quad \text{[years]}
$$

**Final Boxed Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Payback Period} = \dfrac{\Delta C_{\text{cap}}}{3.517 \times C \times H \times T \times \left( \frac{1}{\eta_{1}} - \frac{1}{\eta_{2}} \right)} \text{ years}}
$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

> **Problem:** A homeowner plans to replace an old 1.5 TR, 3-star fixed-speed Split AC (ISEER = 2.80) with a new 1.5 TR, 5-star Inverter Split AC (ISEER = 4.60). The new AC costs ₹8,000 more. AC operates ~1,200 hours/year. Electricity tariff is ₹8.00/kWh. Compute:
> (a) Annual energy consumption of each AC.
> (b) Annual electricity cost saving.
> (c) Simple payback period.

**Solution:**

**(a) Annual energy consumption:**

For the 3-star AC:

$$
E_{1} = \frac{3.517 \times 1.5 \times 1200}{2.80}
$$

$$
E_{1} = \frac{6330.6}{2.80} = 2260.93 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

For the 5-star AC:

$$
E_{2} = \frac{3.517 \times 1.5 \times 1200}{4.60}
$$

$$
E_{2} = \frac{6330.6}{4.60} = 1376.22 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**(b) Annual cost saving:**

$$
\text{Saving} = (2260.93 - 1376.22) \times 8.00
$$

$$
\text{Saving} = 884.71 \times 8.00 = \text{₹} 7077.68 \text{ per year}
$$

**(c) Payback period:**

$$
\text{Payback} = \frac{8000}{7077.68} = 1.13 \text{ years} \approx 13.6 \text{ months}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A ₹8,000 premium is recovered in **just over a year** — thereafter, every year delivers nearly ₹7,000 of net savings. Over a 10-year AC life, this represents **~₹63,000** of cumulative savings on a single appliance. This is why BEE star-rated appliances are central to sustainable construction.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — BEE Appliance Categories for Buildings

| Appliance Category | BEE Standard | Key Efficiency Metric | Mandatory / Voluntary | Typical 5-Star Performance |
|--------------------|--------------|-----------------------|----------------------|-----------------------------|
| Room Air Conditioners (Fixed Speed) | IS 1391 (Part 1) | EER | Mandatory | $\geq$ 3.30 Wh/Wh |
| Room Air Conditioners (Inverter) | IS 1391 (Part 2) | ISEER | Mandatory | $\geq$ 4.50 Wh/Wh |
| Central Air Conditioners (Chillers) | IS 8148 | COP / IPLV | Voluntary | $\geq$ 3.10 W/W |
| Storage Water Heaters (Electric) | IS 2082 | Standing Loss (kWh/24h) | Mandatory | $\leq$ 0.415 kWh/24h (50 L) |
| Heat Pump Water Heaters | EN 16147 / IS aligned | SCOP | Voluntary | $\geq$ 3.0 |
| Room Air Conditioner (Cassette / Ductable) | IS 1391 (Part 3) | ISEER | Mandatory | $\geq$ 4.40 Wh/Wh |
| Variable Refrigerant Flow (VRF) | IS 16590 | COP (heating + cooling) | Voluntary | $\geq$ 3.80 W/W |
| Air Coolers (Desert / Personal) | IS 3315 | Evaporative Efficiency | Voluntary | $\geq$ 80% |
| Ceiling Fans | IS 374 | Service Value (m³/min/W) | Mandatory | $\geq$ 5.0 m³/min/W |
| LED Luminaires | IS 16102 | Lumens per Watt | Mandatory | $\geq$ 90 lm/W |

> [!NOTE]
> **Service Value (SV)** for fans = Air delivery (m³/min) ÷ Power input (W). A 5-star BLDC fan at 28 W can match the air delivery of a 75 W induction fan, giving an SV of ~5+ vs ~3 for old fans.

### 3.6 Derivation 4: Heat Pump Savings vs Electric Geyser

A 2 kW electric geyser delivers 2 kW of heat. An ASHP of the same 2 kW electrical input at COP 3.0 delivers 6 kW of heat.

**Step 1 — Heat delivered by electric geyser in $H$ hours:**

$$
Q_{\text{elec}} = 2 \times H \quad \text{kWh (heat)}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat delivered by heat pump in $H$ hours:**

$$
Q_{\text{HP}} = 2 \times \text{COP} \times H = 2 \times 3.0 \times H = 6H \text{ kWh (heat)}
$$

**Step 3 — To deliver the same heat ($Q = 6H$), the geyser would need to run $3H$ hours:**

$$
\text{Run-hours}_{\text{geyser}} = 3H
$$

**Step 4 — Geyser energy consumed:**

$$
E_{\text{geyser}} = 2 \times 3H = 6H \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 5 — Heat pump energy consumed:**

$$
E_{\text{HP}} = 2H \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 6 — Savings ratio:**

$$
\frac{E_{\text{geyser}} - E_{\text{HP}}}{E_{\text{geyser}}} = \frac{6H - 2H}{6H} = \frac{4}{6} = 66.7\%
$$

**Final Boxed Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Energy Savings} = \left( 1 - \frac{1}{\text{COP}} \right) \times 100\% = 66.7\% \text{ at COP} = 3.0}
$$

This is why **BEE actively promotes heat pump water heaters** under the Super-Efficient Equipment Programme (SEEP).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 BEE Star Rating Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Manufacturer Submits Appliance for Testing] --> B[NABL Accredited Lab Conducts Test per IS Standard]
    B --> C[Cooling / Heating Capacity Measured in W]
    C --> D[Power Input Measured in W]
    D --> E[Compute EER or ISEER or COP]
    E --> F{BEE Threshold Lookup}
    F -->|1.0 to 2.29| G[1 Star Label]
    F -->|2.30 to 2.49| H[2 Star Label]
    F -->|2.50 to 2.69| I[3 Star Label]
    F -->|2.70 to 2.89| J[4 Star Label]
    F -->|Greater than equal to 2.90| K[5 Star Label]
    G --> L[Energy Label Printed and Affixed]
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Market Surveillance and Verification by BEE]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of an Inverter Air-Conditioner

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Indoor Unit - Evaporator Coil] -->|Cooled Air| B[Conditioned Room Space]
    B -->|Return Air| A
    A -->|Low Pressure Refrigerant Vapor| C[Compressor - Variable Speed Inverter Driven]
    C -->|High Pressure Hot Vapor| D[Outdoor Unit - Condenser Coil]
    D -->|Heat Rejected to Ambient| E[Outdoor Air]
    D -->|High Pressure Liquid| F[Expansion Valve / Capillary Tube]
    F -->|Low Pressure Liquid Vapor Mix| A
    C -->|Electrical Input| G[Power Supply 230V 50Hz]
    D --> H[Outdoor Fan for Heat Rejection]
    G --> I[Inverter PCB - Variable Frequency Drive]
    I --> C
```

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Selecting an Energy-Efficient AC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: AC Selection] --> B{Building Type?}
    B -->|Residential Single Room| C{Single or Multiple Rooms?}
    B -->|Residential Multi Room| D{Independent Zoning Needed?}
    B -->|Commercial Office| E{Floor Area?}
    B -->|Hotel or Hospital| F[4-Pipe Chilled Water Plant with Heat Recovery]
    C -->|Single| G[Window AC or Split AC]
    C -->|Multiple| H[Multi-Split Inverter]
    D -->|Yes| I[VRF or VRV System]
    D -->|No| J[Multiple Split Units]
    E -->|Less than 500 m2| K[Ductable Split or VRF]
    E -->|Greater than 500 m2| L[Chilled Water Plant with VFD Chillers and Cooling Tower]
    G --> M{Star Rating Check}
    H --> M
    I --> M
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M -->|Compare ISEER and Payback| N[Final Selection with 5 Star BEE Label]
```

### 4.4 Heat Pump Operating Cycle — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Evaporator - Absorbs Heat from Air or Water at Low Temperature] -->|Refrigerant Vapor| B[Compressor - Raises Pressure and Temperature]
    B -->|Hot High Pressure Vapor| C[Condenser - Releases Heat to Hot Water or Air]
    C -->|High Pressure Liquid| D[Expansion Device - Pressure Drop]
    D -->|Low Pressure Mix| A
    E[Electrical Power Input] --> B
    C -->|Useful Heat Output for Building or Water| F[Building Heating Loop or Storage Tank]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In a heat pump, the **useful output is the heat rejected at the condenser**, not the heat absorbed at the evaporator. The evaporator pulls "free" ambient heat from air/ground, and the compressor only supplies the small amount of work needed to "pump" that heat to a higher temperature.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Define ISEER and explain how it differs from EER in evaluating air-conditioner efficiency. Why is ISEER more representative of real-world performance?**

**Model Answer:**

**ISEER (Indian Seasonal Energy Efficiency Ratio)** is the weighted average of the Energy Efficiency Ratio of an air-conditioner measured at four standard test conditions corresponding to **20%, 50%, 75%, and 100% of the rated cooling load**, with the weighting reflecting typical Indian usage patterns and climatic load distribution.

**EER** is a single-point measurement at full-load condition (35 °C OD, 27 °C DB / 19 °C WB indoor). It does not capture part-load performance.

**Why ISEER is more representative:**

- Inverter ACs vary their compressor speed, operating **most of the time at 30–60% part load**.
- EER measures only the full-load point, overstating energy use.
- ISEER weights efficiency at multiple load points, reflecting the **true annual energy consumption**.
- In Indian conditions, where ambient temperatures fluctuate from ~25 °C to 45 °C, ISEER gives a more realistic picture of yearly operating cost.

**Mark Split:** Definition 1M | Difference table 1M | Real-world relevance 1M.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**List any six appliances covered under the mandatory BEE star labeling program in India. For each, state the governing IS standard and the key efficiency metric used for rating.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Appliance | IS Standard | Efficiency Metric |
|---|-----------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Room Air Conditioner (Fixed Speed) | IS 1391 (Part 1) | EER |
| 2 | Room Air Conditioner (Inverter) | IS 1391 (Part 2) | ISEER |
| 3 | Frost-Free Refrigerator | IS 15750 | kWh/year |
| 4 | LED Lamps | IS 16102 | Lumens per Watt |
| 5 | Ceiling Fans | IS 374 | Service Value (m³/min/W) |
| 6 | Storage Water Heaters (Electric) | IS 2082 | Standing Loss (kWh/24h) |
| 7 | Distribution Transformers | IS 1180 | Max Losses at 50% & 100% load |

**Mark Split:** Six appliances with metrics = 3M (½ M each).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, the student answers **one full question** (with sub-parts) of 14 marks. **Two choices are provided below; the student may attempt EITHER.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Dec 2023 pattern]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the BEE star rating methodology for room air conditioners in India. Discuss the metrics EER and ISEER, and the test conditions specified. State the latest ISEER thresholds for 5-star inverter and fixed-speed split ACs. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** runs the **Standard and Labeling Program** under the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001**. For room air conditioners, the methodology involves:

**1. Test Conditions (per IS 1391):**

- **T1 condition (full load):** Outdoor 35 °C DB, Indoor 27 °C DB / 19 °C WB.
- **T2 condition (75% load):** Outdoor 30 °C DB, Indoor 27 °C DB / 19 °C WB.
- **T3 condition (50% load):** Outdoor 25 °C DB, Indoor 27 °C DB / 19 °C WB.
- **T4 condition (20% load):** Outdoor 20 °C DB, Indoor 27 °C DB / 19 °C WB.

**2. EER Calculation (Fixed Speed):**

$$
\text{EER} = \frac{Q_{c,\text{T1}}}{W_{\text{T1}}}
$$

**3. ISEER Calculation (Inverter):**

$$
\text{ISEER} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{4} Q_{c,i} \cdot h_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{4} W_i \cdot h_i}
$$

The weight hours are: T1 = 37 h, T2 = 196 h, T3 = 323 h, T4 = 444 h (per BEE schedule).

**4. Latest ISEER Thresholds:**

| Star | Fixed Speed ISEER | Inverter ISEER |
|------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1    | 2.30 – 2.49       | 3.30 – 3.59    |
| 3    | 2.70 – 2.89       | 3.90 – 4.19    |
| 5    | $\geq$ 3.10       | $\geq$ 4.50    |

**5. Why ISEER is mandatory for inverters:** Inverter ACs modulate compressor speed; their **part-load efficiency is significantly higher** than full-load, so EER would understate their benefit. ISEER captures this correctly.

**Mark Split:** Test conditions 2M | EER vs ISEER distinction 2M | Threshold table 2M | Why ISEER for inverters 1M.

---

**(b)** A 2 TR, 3-star inverter split AC (ISEER = 3.90) currently runs 1500 hours/year in an office. The owner is considering a 5-star inverter unit (ISEER = 4.70) costing ₹12,000 more. Compute: (i) annual energy consumption of each unit, (ii) annual cost saving at ₹9/kWh tariff, and (iii) simple payback period. Conclude with a recommendation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Capacity $C = 2$ TR
- Hours $H = 1500$ h/yr
- Tariff $T =$ ₹9/kWh
- $\eta_1 = 3.90$, $\eta_2 = 4.70$
- $\Delta C =$ ₹12,000

**(i) Annual energy:**

For 3-star:
$$
E_1 = \frac{3.517 \times 2 \times 1500}{3.90} = \frac{10551}{3.90} = 2705.38 \text{ kWh}
$$

For 5-star:
$$
E_2 = \frac{3.517 \times 2 \times 1500}{4.70} = \frac{10551}{4.70} = 2244.89 \text{ kWh}
$$

**(ii) Annual cost saving:**

$$
\text{Saving} = (2705.38 - 2244.89) \times 9 = 460.49 \times 9 = \text{₹} 4144.41 \text{ per year}
$$

**(iii) Payback period:**

$$
\text{Payback} = \frac{12000}{4144.41} = 2.90 \text{ years}
$$

**Recommendation:** The payback is **2.9 years**. Over the typical 10–12 year AC life, total savings ≈ **₹41,000 – ₹50,000**, far exceeding the ₹12,000 premium. **The 5-star inverter AC is strongly recommended** on both economic and sustainability grounds.

**Mark Split:** Each sub-calculation 2M | Recommendation 1M.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Choice]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an **Air-Source Heat Pump (ASHP)** for water heating. Show how the COP determines the annual energy savings over a conventional 2 kW electric geyser for a household using 1500 litres of hot water per day, heated from 25 °C to 60 °C. Assume COP of 3.5 and specific heat of water = 4.186 kJ/kg·K. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle (Block Diagram):**

```
[Evaporator] → [Compressor] → [Condenser (around water tank)] → [Expansion Valve] → back to Evaporator
   ↑                                                                                       ↑
   Ambient air heat (T_amb)                                                          Cool refrigerant (T_evap)
```

**Cycle Description:**

1. **Evaporator:** Refrigerant absorbs heat from ambient air (even cold air has usable heat) and evaporates.
2. **Compressor:** Compresses vapor, raising its temperature above the required water temperature.
3. **Condenser:** Hot vapor condenses, releasing heat to the water in the storage tank.
4. **Expansion Valve:** Pressure drops; refrigerant becomes cold liquid-vapor mix; returns to evaporator.

**Energy Saving Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Daily hot water energy demand:**

$$
Q_{\text{daily}} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
Q_{\text{daily}} = 1500 \times 4.186 \times (60 - 25) = 1500 \times 4.186 \times 35
$$

$$
Q_{\text{daily}} = 219{,}765 \text{ kJ/day} = \frac{219765}{3600} = 61.05 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Electric geyser energy required (COP = 1):**

$$
E_{\text{geyser}} = 61.05 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Heat pump energy required (COP = 3.5):**

$$
E_{\text{HP}} = \frac{61.05}{3.5} = 17.44 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Daily energy saving:**

$$
\text{Saving}_{\text{daily}} = 61.05 - 17.44 = 43.61 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Annual saving (365 days):**

$$
\text{Saving}_{\text{annual}} = 43.61 \times 365 = 15{,}917.65 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**Step 6 — Annual monetary saving (at ₹8/kWh):**

$$
\text{Rupee saving} = 15917.65 \times 8 = \text{₹} 1{,}27{,}341 \text{ per year}
$$

**Mark Split:** Block diagram + description 3M | Energy demand 1.5M | HP vs geyser savings 2.5M.

---

**(b)** Compare **VRF/VRV** systems with **central chilled-water plants** for a 2000 m² office building. Discuss energy efficiency, partial-load behavior, capital cost, and recommunication [recommended] applicability. Also explain the role of **heat recovery VRF** in hotel applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Matrix:**

| Parameter | VRF System | Chilled Water Plant |
|-----------|-----------|---------------------|
| Capacity Range | 5 – 100 TR | 50 – 5000 TR |
| Energy Efficiency (Full Load COP) | 3.5 – 4.5 | 3.0 – 6.5 (with magnetic bearing) |
| Part-Load Efficiency | Excellent (inverter) | Good (with VFD) |
| Capital Cost (per TR) | ₹90,000 – ₹1,30,000 | ₹1,20,000 – ₹2,00,000 |
| Installation Time | 2 – 4 weeks | 8 – 16 weeks |
| Space Required | Outdoor units + refrigerant piping | Plant room + chiller + cooling tower + pumps |
| Zonal Control | Excellent (each indoor unit) | Moderate (zone valves / VAV) |
| Best Application | 200 – 5000 m² commercial | $>$ 5000 m² or high-rise |
| Refrigerant | R-410A / R-32 | R-134a / R-1234ze (in chillers) |

**Recommendation for 2000 m² office:** **VRF is the clear winner** — lower capital, faster installation, excellent zoning, comparable efficiency.

**Heat Recovery VRF in Hotels:**

In hotels, guest rooms on the **sunny side require cooling** while **conference halls or laundry areas may require heating simultaneously**. A standard VRF would extract heat from the cooling side and reject it to ambient — wasting it. A **heat-recovery VRF** captures this waste heat and **redirects it to zones that need heating**, achieving an effective **system-wide COP of 5.0 to 6.0** and eliminating the need for separate boilers.

**Mark Split:** Comparison table 4M | Recommendation 1M | Heat recovery VRF 2M.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make — and lose marks on:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Forgetting to convert TR to kW (1 TR = 3.517 kW). Marks are deducted heavily.
> 2. **EER vs ISEER mix-up:** Writing ISEER formula for a fixed-speed AC. Inverter and fixed-speed are rated differently.
> 3. **Tariff assumption:** Some students write tariff as ₹7 but the problem may specify ₹9 — re-read carefully.
> 4. **Forgetting the 50%/20% load point explanation:** When asked "why ISEER," many write only the formula. Explain the **load distribution rationale** (Indian climate profile).
> 5. **Heat pump problem skipping the energy-demand step:** Always compute $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ first before applying COP.
> 6. **Block diagrams without labels:** A bare flowchart earns partial marks; **label every component and flow direction**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- [x] BEE stands for **Bureau of Energy Efficiency**, established under **Energy Conservation Act, 2001**.
- [x] Star rating scale: **1 (least efficient) to 5 (most efficient)**; new ACs may go up to **10 stars**.
- [x] **EER** = single-point full-load efficiency metric, used for **fixed-speed** units.
- [x] **ISEER** = weighted seasonal efficiency, used for **inverter** units.
- [x] **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12,000 BTU/hr** — memorize this.
- [x] Annual energy formula: $E = \dfrac{3.517 \times C \times H}{\eta}$.
- [x] Heat pump **COP = 3.0–4.0** for air-source; **4.0–5.5** for ground-source.
- [x] Energy saving of a heat pump over resistance heating: $\left( 1 - \frac{1}{\text{COP}} \right) \times 100\%$.
- [x] **VRF** is preferred for **200–5000 m²** commercial buildings; **chilled water** for larger plants.
- [x] **ECBC compliance** is mandatory for commercial buildings $\geq$ 250 kW connected load.
- [x] **Heat recovery VRF** is ideal for hotels — simultaneous heating and cooling.
- [x] **Mandatory BEE labels** exist for ACs, refrigerators, fans, LED lamps, water heaters.
- [x] **Voluntary labels** exist for chillers, VRFs, air coolers, washing machines.
- [x] **Inverter technology saves 30–40% energy** over fixed-speed ACs of the same star rating.
- [x] Always **show units** in every line of calculation — examiners reward it.
- [x] **Payback < 3 years** is typical for 5-star appliance upgrade — almost always economically justified.

**End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Energy Efficient and BEE Rated Appliances.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Zero Ozone Depleting Potential (ODP) Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET752 - SUSTAINABLE CONSTRUCTION PRACTICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Zero Ozone Depleting Potential (ODP) Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP)** is a dimensionless relative index that quantifies the destructive capability of a chemical substance on the stratospheric ozone layer, measured relative to **CFC-11 (Trichlorofluoromethane, CCl₃F)**, which is assigned a baseline ODP value of **1.0**.

$$ODP_{x} = \frac{\text{Steady-state ozone column depletion caused by mass of substance } x}{\text{Steady-state ozone column depletion caused by equivalent mass of CFC-11}}$$

A **Zero ODP Material** is any substance, compound, or product whose ODP value equals **0.00** (i.e., the substance contains no chlorine or bromine atoms that can participate in the catalytic ozone destruction cycle in the stratosphere). The most common families of zero-ODP substances used in sustainable construction are **Hydrocarbons (HCs), Hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs), Hydrofluoroolefins (HFOs), Carbon Dioxide (CO₂), Ammonia (NH₃), Water (H₂O), and inert gases** such as nitrogen and argon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"Zero ODP materials are substances that have no chlorine or bromine content and therefore cause no measurable destruction of the stratospheric ozone layer, making them compliant with the Montreal Protocol (1987) and its subsequent Kigali Amendment (2016)."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sunburn of the Earth" Intuition

Imagine the Earth's **ozone layer** as a giant **transparent umbrella** made of millions of tiny glass beads (ozone molecules, O₃), protecting everything underneath from the Sun's harmful **ultraviolet (UV-B) rays**.

Now think of **CFCs and HCFCs** as tiny invisible **bullets** shot upward by air conditioners and old refrigerators. When these bullets reach the umbrella, they **shatter the glass beads**, creating a *hole* in the protective shield. The bigger the bullet (more chlorine atoms), the more beads it breaks.

**Zero ODP materials** are essentially **soft foam balls** — they may rise into the sky, but they cannot shatter a single glass bead. They pass through the ozone layer without damaging a single ozone molecule. Even though some (like HFCs) may still cause **global warming (GWP)**, they leave the **ozone umbrella completely intact**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Board Exams:**
> - **ODP = 0** means safe for the **ozone layer**
> - **GWP = 0** means safe for the **climate (global warming)**
> - The "ideal" sustainable refrigerant must score **zero on BOTH** scales (e.g., CO₂, Ammonia, Water).

## 1.3 Historical Context & Regulatory Framework

| Year | Event | Significance for Zero ODP Materials |
|------|-------|--------------------------------------|
| **1974** | Molina & Rowland publish CFC–ozone theory | First scientific proof of ODP |
| **1985** | Vienna Convention for Protection of the Ozone Layer | Diplomatic recognition |
| **1987** | **Montreal Protocol** signed | Mandatory phase-out of CFCs |
| **1990** | London Amendment | Added HCFCs to phase-out list |
| **1992** | Copenhagen Amendment | Accelerated CFC phase-out (developed nations: 1996) |
| **1997** | Kyoto Protocol | Introduced GWP reduction targets |
| **2016** | **Kigali Amendment** | HFC phase-down (HFCs are zero-ODP but high-GWP) |
| **2024** | KTU Module 2 (Current) | Promoting HFOs, Natural Refrigerants |

## 1.4 Why Zero ODP Materials Matter in Construction

In the built environment, the four major applications of substances that historically had ODP > 0 are:

1. **Refrigeration & Air-Conditioning** (chillers, VRF systems) — using CFCs (R-12), HCFCs (R-22)
2. **Thermal Insulation Foams** — using HCFC-141b as a blowing agent
3. **Fire Suppression Systems** — using Halons (brominated, very high ODP)
4. **Cleaning Solvents & Adhesives** — using CFC-113, methyl chloroform

Sustainable construction demands substitution of all these with **zero-ODP alternatives** to comply with **IGBC, GRIHA, LEED v4.1, and BREEAM** rating systems.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always remember the **Mnemonic — "HCF-NAW"** for Zero ODP families:
> **H** = Hydrocarbons & HFOs
> **C** = CO₂ (R-744)
> **F** = Fluorocarbons without Cl/Br (HFCs, HFO-1234yf)
> **N** = NH₃ (Ammonia, R-717)
> **A** = Air & Argon, Nitrogen
> **W** = Water (R-718)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Stratospheric Ozone Destruction Mechanism (Catalytic Cycle)

For an exam, you must understand **why** chlorine-containing substances destroy ozone. The classic **Chapman Cycle + Catalytic Destruction Cycle** is described below:

### Step-by-Step Catalytic Destruction Mechanism

1. **UV-C Photolysis in the stratosphere (λ < 220 nm)** breaks the C–Cl bond of CFC molecules:
   
   $$\text{CCl}_{2}\text{F}_{2} + h\nu \longrightarrow \text{CClF}_{2}^{\bullet} + \text{Cl}^{\bullet}$$

2. **Free chlorine radical attacks ozone:**
   
   $$\text{Cl}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_{3} \longrightarrow \text{ClO}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_{2}$$

3. **Chlorine monoxide reacts with atomic oxygen (reforming Cl radical):**
   
   $$\text{ClO}^{\bullet} + \text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Cl}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_{2}$$

4. **Net reaction (one Cl atom destroys ~100,000 O₃ molecules):**
   
   $$2\text{O}_{3} \longrightarrow 3\text{O}_{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The chlorine radical is a **catalyst** — it is **regenerated** in Step 3, meaning a **single chlorine atom can destroy up to 100,000 ozone molecules** before being deactivated. This is why ODP is non-linear.

## 2.2 Why Zero ODP Materials are Inert in the Ozone Layer

Zero-ODP substances either:
- **Contain no Cl or Br** (e.g., HFCs, HCs, CO₂) — they cannot release catalytic radicals
- **Break down in the troposphere** (e.g., hydrocarbons) — they never reach the stratosphere
- **Are naturally occurring** (e.g., CO₂, NH₃, H₂O) — already part of atmospheric chemistry

## 2.3 Zero ODP Substance Families — Comparative Analysis

| Family | Example Refrigerant | ODP | GWP (100-yr) | Atmospheric Lifetime | Use in Construction |
|--------|---------------------|-----|--------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **CFC** (Baseline) | R-12 (CCl₂F₂) | **1.0** | 10,900 | 100 years | **BANNED** |
| **HCFC** | R-22 (CHClF₂) | **0.055** | 1,810 | 12 years | Phased out 2020 (dev.), 2030 (dev.) |
| **HFC** | R-134a (CH₂FCF₃) | **0** | 1,430 | 14 years | AC chillers (transition) |
| **HFC** | R-410A | **0** | 2,088 | 15 years | VRF/AC systems |
| **HFO** | R-1234yf (CF₃CF=CH₂) | **0** | **<1** | 11 days | Next-gen chillers |
| **HFO Blend** | R-1234ze | **0** | 7 | 18 days | High-temp chillers |
| **Natural — HC** | R-290 (Propane) | **0** | 3 | Days | Domestic refrigeration |
| **Natural — CO₂** | R-744 (CO₂) | **0** | 1 | Variable | Transcritical AC systems |
| **Natural — NH₃** | R-717 (Ammonia) | **0** | 0 | Days | Industrial cold storage |
| **Natural — H₂O** | R-718 (Water) | **0** | 0 | — | Absorption chillers |
| **Inert Gas** | Nitrogen, Argon | **0** | 0 | — | Insulation blowing agent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Highlight:** HFCs have **ODP = 0** but **high GWP**. They are a *transitional* solution. The Kigali Amendment (2016) mandates their phase-down by **85%** by 2036.

## 2.4 Zero ODP Insulation Blowing Agents (Construction Application)

In rigid polyurethane (PUR) and polyisocyanurate (PIR) foams used for building insulation, the **blowing agent** historically had high ODP:

| Era | Blowing Agent | ODP | Status |
|-----|---------------|-----|--------|
| 1st Gen (1980s) | CFC-11 | 1.0 | **Banned** |
| 2nd Gen (1990s) | HCFC-141b | 0.11 | **Phased out** |
| 3rd Gen (2000s) | HFC-245fa | 0 | Transitional |
| 4th Gen (Current) | **HFO-1233zd(E)** | **0** | **Zero-ODP, Low-GWP** |
| 5th Gen (Future) | **Water/CO₂ blown** | **0** | Net-zero future |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Question Pattern:** Examiners often ask: *"Differentiate between ODP and GWP with examples."* Always quote numerical values from the table above.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | ODP Definition | $ODP_{x} = \dfrac{\Delta O_{3,x}}{\Delta O_{3,CFC11}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | GWP Definition (100-yr) | $GWP_{x} = \dfrac{AGWP_{x}}{AGWP_{CO_2}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Catalytic Cycle Multiplication | 1 Cl atom destroys ~10⁵ O₃ molecules | Order of magnitude |
| 4 | Half-Life (Atmospheric Lifetime) | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$ | Years |
| 5 | Equivalent Warming Impact | $CO_{2,eq} = m \times GWP$ | kg CO₂ equivalent |
| 6 | Energy Efficiency Ratio (EER) | $EER = \dfrac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{input}}$ | BTU/Wh or kW/kW |
| 7 | Coefficient of Performance (COP) | $COP = \dfrac{Q_{L}}{W_{compressor}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Montreal Protocol Phase-out Factor | $\% \text{ Compliance} = \left(1 - \dfrac{\text{ODP substance used}}{\text{Total refrigerant mass}}\right) \times 100$ | % |
| 9 | Insulation R-value | $R = \dfrac{L}{k}$ where L = thickness, k = thermal conductivity | m²·K/W |
| 10 | Carbon Dioxide Equivalent (CDE) | $CDE = \sum (m_i \times GWP_i)$ | kg CO₂ eq |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Table Rule:** The pipe symbol `\vert` has been used for absolute value to prevent markdown table breakage. Do not use the bare pipe `\|` symbol.

## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Green Building Certification:** Zero-ODP refrigerants & insulation foams earn 2–4 points under **LEED v4.1 — EA Credit: Fundamental Refrigerant Management**.
- **Cost Savings:** HFO-based systems (R-1234yf) have 8–12% lower lifecycle cost than HFC-134a systems due to carbon-tax avoidance.
- **Compliance:** Indian Ozone Depleting Substances (Regulation) Rules, 2000 (amended 2014) — mandatory under the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**.
- **Occupant Health:** Zero-ODP insulation foams (water-blown) eliminate off-gassing of halogenated compounds, improving **Indoor Air Quality (IAQ)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Calculation of ODP for a Hypothetical Refrigerant

**Problem Statement:** A refrigerant molecule R-XYZ contains **2 chlorine atoms** and has a stratospheric lifetime of 15 years. Using the standard semi-empirical correlation, calculate its approximate ODP relative to CFC-11 (lifetime = 60 years).

### Step 1 — Identify the ODP formula in semi-empirical form

The simplified ODP correlation used in atmospheric chemistry textbooks is:

$$ODP_x = (n_{Cl}) \times \left( \dfrac{\tau_x}{\tau_{CFC11}} \right) \times \eta_{photolysis}$$

Where:
- $n_{Cl}$ = number of chlorine atoms per molecule
- $\tau_x$ = stratospheric lifetime of substance *x* (years)
- $\tau_{CFC11}$ = stratospheric lifetime of CFC-11 = 60 years
- $\eta_{photolysis}$ = photolysis efficiency factor (≈ 1.0 for Cl-bearing halocarbons)

### Step 2 — Substitute the given values

We are given:
- $n_{Cl} = 2$ chlorine atoms
- $\tau_x = 15$ years
- $\tau_{CFC11} = 60$ years
- $\eta_{photolysis} = 1.0$

$$ODP_x = 2 \times \left( \dfrac{15}{60} \right) \times 1.0$$

### Step 3 — Perform the arithmetic

$$\dfrac{15}{60} = \dfrac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

$$ODP_x = 2 \times 0.25 = 0.50$$

### Step 4 — Interpret the result

$$\boxed{ODP_x = 0.50}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A refrigerant with 2 chlorine atoms and a 15-year lifetime has **half the ozone-depleting potential of CFC-11**. It is still NOT a zero-ODP material — it is a transitional HCFC-class substance (similar to R-22 which has ODP = 0.055 with 1 Cl atom and 12-yr lifetime).

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Conversion: GWP → CO₂ Equivalent (Construction-Scale Problem)

**Problem Statement:** An HVAC system in a 10-storey commercial building uses **500 kg** of HFC-410A (GWP = 2,088) as its refrigerant. Calculate the total CO₂-equivalent emissions and determine the percentage reduction if it is retrofitted to HFO-1234yf (GWP = 4).

### Step 1 — Write the CO₂-equivalent formula

$$CO_{2,eq} = m_{refrigerant} \times GWP$$

### Step 2 — Compute for HFC-410A

$$CO_{2,eq, HFC} = 500 \text{ kg} \times 2{,}088$$

$$CO_{2,eq, HFC} = 1{,}044{,}000 \text{ kg CO}_2 \text{ eq}$$

### Step 3 — Compute for HFO-1234yf

$$CO_{2,eq, HFO} = 500 \text{ kg} \times 4$$

$$CO_{2,eq, HFO} = 2{,}000 \text{ kg CO}_2 \text{ eq}$$

### Step 4 — Calculate percentage reduction

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \left( \dfrac{CO_{2,eq,HFC} - CO_{2,eq,HFO}}{CO_{2,eq,HFC}} \right) \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \left( \dfrac{1{,}044{,}000 - 2{,}000}{1{,}044{,}000} \right) \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \left( \dfrac{1{,}042{,}000}{1{,}044{,}000} \right) \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = 99.81\%$$

### Step 5 — Final interpretation

$$\boxed{CO_{2,eq, HFC} = 1{,}044{,}000 \text{ kg}; \quad CO_{2,eq, HFO} = 2{,}000 \text{ kg}; \quad \text{Reduction} = 99.81\%}$$

> [!TIP]
> This is a classic **14-mark Part B problem** that KTU examiners love. Always show all five steps for full marks.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Zero ODP vs Low ODP vs High ODP Materials

| Parameter | Zero ODP (HFO/CO₂/NH₃) | Low ODP (HCFC-22 = 0.055) | High ODP (CFC-12 = 1.0) |
|-----------|------------------------|--------------------------|-------------------------|
| **ODP Value** | 0.00 | 0.055 | 1.0 |
| **GWP (100-yr)** | 1 to 7 | 1,810 | 10,900 |
| **Atmospheric Lifetime** | Days to weeks | 12 years | 100 years |
| **Cl atoms/molecule** | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| **Montreal Protocol Status** | Encouraged | Phased out (2020) | Banned (1996) |
| **Kigali Amendment** | Endorsed | Not applicable | Not applicable |
| **LEED Points (EA Credit)** | 4 points | 0–1 point | 0 points |
| **GRIHA Points** | Full credit | Partial | No credit |
| **Cost (relative)** | 1.1× – 1.4× | 1.0× (legacy) | Not manufactured |
| **Energy Efficiency** | High (HFOs) / Moderate (NH₃) | Moderate | High but obsolete |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Phase-Out Schedule (KTU Board Favorite)

The Montreal Protocol phase-out timeline is a guaranteed 3-mark question:

| Substance | Developed Countries | Developing Countries (India) |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------------------|
| CFCs (R-11, R-12) | **Banned 1996** | Banned 2010 |
| Halons (R-1301) | **Banned 1994** | Banned 2010 |
| Carbon Tetrachloride | **Banned 1996** | Banned 2010 |
| Methyl Chloroform | **Banned 1996** | Banned 2015 |
| HCFCs (R-22) | **Banned 2020** | Banned 2030 (freeze by 2013) |
| HFCs (transitional) | Phase-down 85% by 2036 | Phase-down 80% by 2045 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> India is classified as a **Developing Country (Article 5)** under the Montreal Protocol, hence the **10-year grace period** for HCFC phase-out.

## 3.5 Worked Example: Insulating Foam Selection for a Green Building

**Scenario:** You are a sustainable construction consultant for a 5-star GRIHA-rated hospital. Choose the **most sustainable** insulation foam blowing agent from: (a) CFC-11, (b) HCFC-141b, (c) HFC-245fa, (d) HFO-1233zd(E), (e) Water.

### Decision Framework:

$$\text{Sustainability Score} = \dfrac{100}{(1 + ODP) \times (1 + GWP/100)}$$

| Option | ODP | GWP | Score Calculation | Score |
|--------|-----|-----|-------------------|-------|
| (a) CFC-11 | 1.00 | 4,750 | 100 ÷ (2 × 48.5) | 1.03 |
| (b) HCFC-141b | 0.11 | 725 | 100 ÷ (1.11 × 8.25) | 10.92 |
| (c) HFC-245fa | 0.00 | 1,030 | 100 ÷ (1 × 11.3) | 8.85 |
| (d) HFO-1233zd(E) | 0.00 | 1.00 | 100 ÷ (1 × 1.01) | **99.01** |
| (e) Water | 0.00 | 0.00 | 100 ÷ (1 × 1.00) | **100.00** |

### Conclusion:

$$\boxed{\text{Water-blown foam} \rightarrow \text{Best} \mid \text{HFO-1233zd(E)} \rightarrow \text{Second Best (commercially practical)}}$$

---

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Define Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP) and identify two construction materials that historically had high ODP values.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP) is a dimensionless index that expresses the destructive capability of a substance on the stratospheric ozone layer relative to **CFC-11 (CCl₃F)**, which is assigned a baseline ODP value of **1.0**.

$$ODP_x = \frac{\text{Ozone destroyed by substance } x}{\text{Ozone destroyed by CFC-11}}$$

**Two construction materials with historically high ODP:**
1. **CFC-11 (R-11)** — used as a blowing agent in rigid polyurethane insulation foams; **ODP = 1.0**
2. **HCFC-141b** — used as a blowing agent in spray-foam insulation; **ODP = 0.11**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse ODP with GWP. ODP measures **ozone destruction**; GWP measures **global warming**. They are entirely different scales.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**List any three zero-ODP refrigerants suitable for sustainable buildings and state their ODP values.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| S.No. | Refrigerant | Family | ODP Value |
|-------|-------------|--------|-----------|
| 1 | **R-1234yf (HFO)** | Hydrofluoroolefin | **0.00** |
| 2 | **R-744 (CO₂)** | Natural | **0.00** |
| 3 | **R-717 (Ammonia, NH₃)** | Natural | **0.00** |

These refrigerants are promoted under the **Kigali Amendment (2016)** and are the preferred choice for **green-rated buildings** (GRIHA, LEED) due to their zero-ODP and ultra-low GWP characteristics.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

#### **Part (a)** [7 Marks]
**Explain with neat sketches the catalytic mechanism by which chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) deplete the stratospheric ozone layer. Why are hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs) classified as zero-ODP substances?**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — UV Photolysis in Stratosphere (1.5 Marks):**
CFC molecules (e.g., CCl₂F₂) absorb high-energy UV-C radiation (λ < 220 nm) in the stratosphere, causing homolytic cleavage of the C–Cl bond:

$$CCl_2F_2 + h\nu \longrightarrow CClF_2^{\bullet} + Cl^{\bullet}$$

**Step 2 — Free Radical Attack on Ozone (1.5 Marks):**
The liberated chlorine radical reacts with ozone (O₃) to form chlorine monoxide:

$$Cl^{\bullet} + O_3 \longrightarrow ClO^{\bullet} + O_2$$

**Step 3 — Regeneration of Cl Radical (1.5 Marks):**
Chlorine monoxide reacts with free atomic oxygen (O), regenerating the chlorine radical and producing molecular oxygen:

$$ClO^{\bullet} + O \longrightarrow Cl^{\bullet} + O_2$$

**Step 4 — Net Reaction and Multiplication (1.5 Marks):**
The net reaction is the destruction of two ozone molecules:

$$2 O_3 \longrightarrow 3 O_2$$

Since chlorine is a **catalyst** and is **regenerated**, a single Cl atom can destroy **~10⁵ ozone molecules** before being sequestered.

**Step 5 — Why HFCs are Zero-ODP (1 Mark):**
HFCs (e.g., CH₂FCF₃ — R-134a) contain **only C, H, and F atoms** — **no chlorine or bromine**. Therefore, no catalytic Cl radicals are produced, and no ozone destruction occurs. Hence ODP = 0.

---

#### **Part (b)** [7 Marks]
**A commercial complex uses 800 kg of HFC-410A in its VRF system. Calculate the CO₂-equivalent emissions. If the system is retrofitted with HFO-R1234yf (GWP = 4), determine the percentage reduction in global warming impact.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — State the formula (1 Mark):**
$$CO_{2,eq} = m \times GWP$$

**Step 2 — Calculate for HFC-410A (2 Marks):**
Given: m = 800 kg, GWP (R-410A) = 2,088
$$CO_{2,eq, HFC} = 800 \times 2{,}088 = 1{,}670{,}400 \text{ kg CO}_2 \text{ eq}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate for HFO-1234yf (2 Marks):**
Given: m = 800 kg, GWP (R-1234yf) = 4
$$CO_{2,eq, HFO} = 800 \times 4 = 3{,}200 \text{ kg CO}_2 \text{ eq}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate percentage reduction (2 Marks):**
$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \left(\frac{1{,}670{,}400 - 3{,}200}{1{,}670{,}400}\right) \times 100$$
$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \left(\frac{1{,}667{,}200}{1{,}670{,}400}\right) \times 100 = 99.81\%$$

$$\boxed{CO_{2,eq, HFC} = 1{,}670{,}400 \text{ kg}; \quad \text{Reduction} = 99.81\%}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024]

#### **Part (a)** [7 Marks]
**Discuss the role of the Montreal Protocol and the Kigali Amendment in phasing out ozone-depleting substances. List four zero-ODP insulation blowing agents with their applications.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Montreal Protocol (1987) (2 Marks):**
The **Montreal Protocol on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer** is a landmark international treaty signed in 1987 under the Vienna Convention. It mandated the **phase-out of CFCs, halons, carbon tetrachloride, methyl chloroform, and HCFCs** in a phased manner, with developed countries taking the lead (CFC phase-out: 1996). It is the **only UN treaty ratified by every country (Universal Ratification)**.

**Step 2 — Kigali Amendment (2016) (2 Marks):**
The **Kigali Amendment** extended the Montreal Protocol to include **HFCs** (which are zero-ODP but high-GWP). It commits to an **80–85% phasedown of HFCs by 2045** for developing countries (India included) and **by 2036** for developed countries, potentially avoiding **0.5°C of global warming by 2100**.

**Step 3 — Four Zero-ODP Insulation Blowing Agents (3 Marks):**

| S.No. | Blowing Agent | ODP | GWP | Application |
|-------|---------------|-----|-----|-------------|
| 1 | **HFO-1233zd(E)** | 0 | 1 | Spray-foam & rigid PUR/PIR |
| 2 | **Water/CO₂ blown foam** | 0 | 0 | Phenolic & PUR foams |
| 3 | **HFC-245fa** | 0 | 1,030 | Rigid insulation (transitional) |
| 4 | **Cyclopentane** | 0 | ~5 | Polyurethane freezer insulation |

---

#### **Part (b)** [7 Marks]
**"Zero ODP is necessary but not sufficient for sustainable construction." Critically analyse this statement with reference to GWP, energy efficiency, and lifecycle cost.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Statement Clarification (1 Mark):**
The statement asserts that simply having ODP = 0 does not guarantee sustainability; other factors like GWP, energy efficiency, and lifecycle cost must also be considered.

**Step 2 — GWP Argument (2 Marks):**
HFC-134a has **ODP = 0** but **GWP = 1,430** — i.e., 1 kg of HFC-134a traps 1,430 times more heat than 1 kg of CO₂ over 100 years. HFO-1234yf has **ODP = 0 AND GWP < 1**, making it **truly sustainable**. Therefore, **ODP = 0 ≠ climate-friendly**.

**Step 3 — Energy Efficiency Argument (2 Marks):**
Ammonia (R-717) has excellent thermodynamic properties and a **COP of 4.5+** in industrial chillers, whereas some HFC blends operate at COP 3.2. **Natural refrigerants often outperform synthetic zero-ODP HFCs in energy efficiency**, translating to lower operational carbon emissions.

**Step 4 — Lifecycle Cost Argument (2 Marks):**
HFO systems cost **15–25% more upfront** than legacy HCFC systems, but deliver **30–40% lower operating cost** over 20 years. Furthermore, **carbon taxes (₹500–₹2,000 per tCO₂eq)** are avoided. The **payback period is typically 4–7 years**.

**Conclusion:** Zero ODP is a **necessary first filter** for sustainability, but the final material must be selected using a **multi-criteria decision matrix** that includes ODP, GWP, COP/EER, toxicity, flammability, and lifecycle cost.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse ODP and GWP** — this is the #1 reason for losing 2–3 marks per answer. ODP = ozone, GWP = climate.
> 2. **Don't write "CFC = HCFC"** — HCFCs have hydrogen atoms; CFCs do not.
> 3. **Don't forget India's Article-5 status** — India is a *developing country* and gets a 10-year phase-out grace period.
> 4. **Don't skip units** in numerical answers — write "kg CO₂ eq" and "% reduction".
> 5. **Don't write vague "natural is best"** — always back up with COP, GWP, and cost figures.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Fire Revision — Pin This on Your Wall**

- ✅ **ODP** is measured relative to **CFC-11 (ODP = 1.0)**.
- ✅ **Zero ODP** = substance contains **no Cl or Br atoms**.
- ✅ **CFC-12, R-11, R-22, Halon-1301** → all have **ODP > 0** → being phased out.
- ✅ **HFC, HFO, CO₂, NH₃, Water, Hydrocarbons** → all have **ODP = 0** → preferred.
- ✅ **HFC ≠ Zero GWP** — HFC-134a has GWP = 1,430 (still harmful to climate).
- ✅ **HFO-1234yf** is the **gold standard**: ODP = 0, GWP < 1.
- ✅ **Montreal Protocol (1987)** → phase-out of CFCs/HCFCs.
- ✅ **Kigali Amendment (2016)** → phase-down of HFCs.
- ✅ **India is Article-5 (developing country)** → HCFC ban by 2030; HFC phase-down 80% by 2045.
- ✅ **Catalytic Cycle:** 1 Cl atom destroys **~10⁵ O₃ molecules**.
- ✅ **Net Reaction:** 2O₃ → 3O₂ (driven by Cl radical regeneration).
- ✅ **CO₂ equivalent formula:** CO₂eq = mass × GWP (always in kg CO₂eq).
- ✅ **LEED v4.1 — EA Credit** awards **2–4 points** for zero-ODP refrigerant management.
- ✅ **GRIHA Criterion 16** mandates HCFC-free / CFC-free HVAC systems.
- ✅ **Insulation foams**: HCFC-141b (ODP 0.11) → replaced by **HFO-1233zd(E)** (ODP 0, GWP 1).
- ✅ **Fire suppression**: Halons (ODP up to 10) → replaced by **FK-5-1-12 (Novec 1230)**, water mist, inert gases.
- ✅ **Best zero-ODP alternatives ranking**: Water > NH₃ > CO₂ > HFO > HC > HFC.
- ✅ **Mnemonic HCF-NAW**: Hydrocarbons, CO₂, Fluorocarbons (HFC/HFO), NH₃, Air/Argon, Water.

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
