<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🌍 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wind Energy Harvesting in Built Environment

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition
> **Wind Energy Harvesting** in the context of sustainable construction refers to the systematic capture of kinetic energy present in moving air masses through engineered structures, devices, or architectural forms and its subsequent conversion into useful electrical or mechanical power for building services. It encompasses both macro-scale installations (utility-grade wind turbines) and micro-scale Building-Integrated Wind Turbines (BIWT) such as roof-mounted, facade-embedded, or ducted aero-generators.

The harvested power is governed by the **cubic relationship** between available wind velocity and extractable energy, making site-specific wind resource assessment a pre-requisite for any harvesting installation.

### 💡 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Think of wind as a flowing river of invisible water.**
> 
> Just as a watermill converts the kinetic energy of flowing water into rotational motion to grind grain, a wind turbine converts the kinetic energy of moving air into rotational motion to generate electricity. The "flow rate" of wind is called **velocity (m/s)**, the "cross-section" of the "river" is the **swept area (m²)** of the turbine blades, and the "density of the fluid" is the **air density (kg/m³)**. The faster the wind blows, the more "invisible water" passes through the blades every second — and a small increase in wind speed dramatically increases the available energy because it depends on the **cube** of the velocity.

### 🏗️ Engineering Classification in Construction

| Scale | Typical Capacity | Application in Buildings |
|---|---|---|
| **Micro-Wind** | < 1 kW | Urban rooftops, streetlights, IoT sensors |
| **Small Wind** | 1 – 100 kW | Residential complexes, small commercial buildings |
| **Distributed Wind** | 100 kW – 1 MW | Industrial parks, university campuses |
| **Utility-Scale** | > 1 MW | Wind farms integrated with district energy systems |

---

## 1.2 Solar Energy Harvesting in Built Environment

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition
> **Solar Energy Harvesting** in sustainable construction is defined as the deliberate collection, conversion, and utilization of solar radiation incident upon building envelopes (roofs, walls, facades, windows) and surrounding open spaces for the generation of thermal energy, electrical energy, or daylight. It bifurcates into **Passive Solar Design** (architectural form and material selection for direct gain, indirect gain, and isolated gain) and **Active Solar Systems** (Photovoltaic modules, Solar Thermal Collectors, and Concentrated Solar Power devices).

### 💡 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the Sun as a giant bonfire in the sky.**
> 
> The Earth is essentially a planet being gently warmed by a bonfire that is 150 million kilometres away. A **solar cell** is like a "magic leaf" that turns sunlight directly into electricity (the Photovoltaic Effect). A **solar water heater** is like a "black sock" painted on your roof that absorbs heat and warms water flowing through tubes (Solar Thermal). A **greenhouse** is like a "glass car parked in the sun" — the sun's rays enter, get trapped as heat, and warm the inside (Passive Solar Design). The "size of the leaf" or the "size of the black sock" determines how much energy you can collect per second.

### 🔆 Key Solar Metrics for Designers

- **Solar Constant (I₀)** = **1361 W/m²** — energy flux at the outer edge of Earth's atmosphere.
- **Peak Sun Hours (PSH)** = The equivalent number of hours per day when solar irradiance averages **1000 W/m²** (standard test condition).
- **Air Mass (AM1.5)** = Standard terrestrial solar spectrum used to rate solar devices.
- **Solar Altitude Angle (α)** = Angle of the sun above the horizon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU 2024, students must be able to distinguish between **Building-Integrated Photovoltaics (BIPV)** and **Building-Attached Photovoltaics (BAPV)**, and between **Passive** and **Active** solar strategies. Confusing these classifications in ESE answers is one of the most common reasons for losing marks.

---

## 1.3 Why Wind & Solar Harvesting? — The Sustainability Imperative

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - The **construction sector accounts for ~38% of global energy-related CO₂ emissions** (UNEP, 2023 data).
> - The **operational carbon** of a building (energy used during occupancy) can be reduced by **30–70%** by integrating on-site renewable harvesting.
> - Wind and solar are **complementary** — solar peaks at midday in summer; wind often peaks in winter evenings and overnight, enabling hybrid micro-grids.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Wind Energy — Theoretical Foundation

### 🌬️ The Physics of Wind Power

The kinetic energy of any moving mass of air is given by classical mechanics:

> A mass `m` moving with velocity `v` has kinetic energy: **E = ½ × m × v²**

Now consider a column of air flowing through a circular disc of cross-sectional area `A` (this is the **swept area** of the turbine rotor) with velocity `v`:

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate of air through the disc:**

The mass of air passing through area `A` per second is the **density (ρ)** times the **volumetric flow rate (A × v)**.

**Step 2 — Power in the wind:**

Power is energy per unit time, so multiply the kinetic energy per unit mass by the mass flow rate.

**Step 3 — Real-world power extraction:**

Not all kinetic energy can be extracted; the turbine must let some air continue downstream to avoid a complete "air block" (the **far-wake velocity** must be greater than zero). This leads to the concept of the **power coefficient (Cₚ)**.

---

### 📐 KTU Formula Sheet — Wind Energy

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power available in wind | $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ | Watts (W) | ρ = air density (kg/m³), A = swept area (m²), v = wind speed (m/s) |
| 2 | Mechanical power extracted | $P_{mech} = C_p \cdot P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} C_p \rho A v^3$ | W | Cₚ is dimensionless power coefficient |
| 3 | Betz Limit (Maximum Cₚ) | $C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | — | Theoretical maximum; no turbine can exceed 59.3% |
| 4 | Swept area of rotor | $A = \pi R^2 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | m² | R = radius, D = diameter |
| 5 | Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) | $\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{v} = \frac{\pi R n}{30 v}$ | — | ω = angular velocity (rad/s), n = rpm |
| 6 | Air density at altitude | $\rho = \rho_0 \cdot e^{-h/8500}$ | kg/m³ | ρ₀ = 1.225 kg/m³ at sea level |
| 7 | Cut-in / Cut-out speeds | Typically 3–4 m/s and 25 m/s | m/s | Operating range of turbine |
| 8 | Capacity Factor (CF) | $CF = \frac{\text{Actual energy in a year}}{\text{Rated power} \times 8760}$ | — | Typically 0.20–0.40 for wind |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the cubic relationship matters for designers:**
> 
> If the average wind speed at a site doubles from 4 m/s to 8 m/s, the available wind power increases by a factor of **2³ = 8 times**, not 2 times. This is why even a small improvement in site selection (removing obstructions, raising hub height) yields enormous gains in energy yield. A 10 m height increase on a rooftop can increase wind speed by 20–30%, potentially doubling the harvestable energy.

### 🏗️ Engineering Utility in Construction

- **Micro-wind turbines** are integrated into building parapets, atrium stacks (where the **venturi effect** accelerates wind), and ventilation ducts.
- **Wind towers (Badgir)** are traditional Persian architectural elements that use pressure differences to drive natural ventilation — a passive wind energy harvesting strategy.
- **Tall buildings** can use the **boundary layer acceleration** at their upper levels to host small wind turbines.

---

## 2.2 Solar Energy — Theoretical Foundation

### ☀️ The Physics of Solar Radiation

The Sun emits energy as electromagnetic radiation across a spectrum from ultraviolet to infrared. The energy flux at the top of the atmosphere is the **Solar Constant (I₀ = 1361 W/m²)**.

As solar radiation passes through the atmosphere, it is attenuated by:
1. **Absorption** by ozone, water vapour, and CO₂.
2. **Scattering** by air molecules (Rayleigh scattering) and aerosols (Mie scattering).
3. **Reflection** by clouds and the Earth's surface (albedo).

The radiation that finally reaches a building surface is:
- **Direct (Beam) Radiation (I_b)** — collimated rays from the sun's disc.
- **Diffuse Radiation (I_d)** — scattered radiation from the sky dome.
- **Reflected Radiation (I_r)** — from the ground and surrounding buildings.

**Total radiation on a tilted surface:**

$$I_T = I_b \cdot R_b + I_d \cdot \left(\frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}\right) + I \cdot \rho_g \cdot \left(\frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}\right)$$

Where:
- $R_b$ = geometric factor (ratio of beam radiation on tilted to horizontal surface).
- $\beta$ = tilt angle of the collector.
- $\rho_g$ = ground reflectance (albedo); for concrete ≈ 0.30, for snow ≈ 0.70.

---

### 📐 KTU Formula Sheet — Solar Energy

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solar constant | $I_0 = 1361 \text{ W/m}^2$ | W/m² | Energy flux outside atmosphere |
| 2 | Extraterrestrial radiation on day n | $I_{on} = I_0 \cdot \left(1 + 0.033\cos\frac{360n}{365}\right)$ | W/m² | Depends on day of year |
| 3 | Declination angle | $\delta = 23.45 \cdot \sin\left(\frac{360(284+n)}{365}\right)$ | Degrees (°) | Angle between sun and equatorial plane |
| 4 | Hour angle | $\omega = 15 \cdot (t - 12)$ | Degrees (°) | t = solar time in hours |
| 5 | Solar altitude angle | $\sin\alpha = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$ | Degrees (°) | φ = latitude of location |
| 6 | Angle of incidence on tilted surface | $\cos\theta = \sin\alpha\cos\beta + \cos\alpha\sin\beta\cos(\gamma_s - \gamma)$ | Degrees (°) | β = tilt, γ = surface azimuth |
| 7 | Photovoltaic output | $P_{PV} = \eta_{PV} \cdot A_{PV} \cdot G \cdot PR$ | Watts (W) | G = irradiance (W/m²), PR = performance ratio (0.7–0.85) |
| 8 | Flat-plate collector efficiency | $\eta_c = F_R \left[ (\tau\alpha) - U_L \frac{T_{in} - T_a}{G} \right]$ | — | F_R = heat removal factor |
| 9 | Specific yield | $Y_f = \frac{E_{annual}}{P_{installed}}$ | kWh/kWp | Energy produced per kW installed |
| 10 | Air Mass | $AM = \frac{1}{\sin\alpha}$ | — | AM1.5 used as standard test condition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** The relationship between **declination, hour angle, and solar altitude** is a frequently tested derivation (often 7 marks in Part B). Memorize the geometry: declination is the Earth's tilt relative to the sun, hour angle is the sun's position east or west of the meridian, and altitude is how high the sun appears in the sky.

### 🏗️ Engineering Utility in Construction

- **Building-Integrated Photovoltaics (BIPV)** replace conventional building materials (roof shingles, curtain walls, skylights) with PV laminates.
- **Solar Water Heating (SWH)** systems use flat-plate collectors or evacuated tube collectors on rooftops to provide 60–80% of domestic hot water.
- **Passive Solar Design** uses orientation, mass, glazing, and shading to regulate indoor temperature with zero mechanical input.
- **Solar Chimneys** create stack-effect ventilation, driving airflow in atria naturally.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Available in the Wind

**Starting Premise:** A parcel of air of mass `m` moving with velocity `v` possesses kinetic energy equal to `½ × m × v²`.

**Step 1: Express the mass of air flowing through the rotor disc per second.**

Consider a disc of cross-sectional area `A` (m²) perpendicular to the wind. In one second, a column of air of length `v` (m) passes through the disc. The volume of this column is:

$$V = A \cdot v \quad (\text{m}^3/\text{s})$$

Multiplying volume by air density `ρ` (kg/m³) gives mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v \quad (\text{kg}/\text{s})$$

**Step 2: Calculate the kinetic energy flux (power) of the wind.**

Power is energy per unit time, so multiply the kinetic energy per unit mass (`½ v²`) by mass flow rate:

$$P_{wind} = \left(\frac{1}{2} v^2\right) \cdot \dot{m} = \frac{1}{2} v^2 \cdot \rho A v$$

**Step 3: Simplify the expression.**

Group the velocity terms:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 \quad (\text{Watts})$$

> **Interpretation:** The power in wind is proportional to the **cube of wind speed**, the **swept area**, and the **air density**. This cubic dependency is why wind resource maps (which show mean wind speed) are critical for site selection.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Betz Limit (Maximum Extractable Power)

**Starting Premise:** A wind turbine cannot extract all kinetic energy from the wind because the air must leave the turbine with some residual velocity; otherwise a vacuum would form behind the rotor.

**Step 1: Define the velocities at three control sections.**

Let the wind velocities be:
- $v$ = upstream (free-stream) wind speed
- $v_1$ = velocity at the rotor plane
- $v_2$ = downstream (far-wake) wind speed

The mass flow rate through the rotor is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A v_1 = \rho A \left(\frac{v + v_2}{2}\right)$$

The change in kinetic energy per second (extracted power) is:

$$P = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} \left(v^2 - v_2^2\right)$$

**Step 2: Substitute the mass flow rate expression.**

$$P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A \left(\frac{v + v_2}{2}\right) \left(v^2 - v_2^2\right)$$

**Step 3: Express power as a fraction of available wind power.**

The total available wind power is $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$. The ratio of extracted power to available power defines the **power coefficient $C_p$**:

$$C_p = \frac{P}{P_{wind}} = \frac{\frac{1}{2} \rho A \left(\frac{v + v_2}{2}\right) \left(v^2 - v_2^2\right)}{\frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3}$$

**Step 4: Simplify by letting $k = v_2 / v$.**

$$C_p = \frac{(1+k)(1-k^2)}{4} = \frac{(1+k)(1-k)(1+k)}{4} = \frac{(1+k)^2 (1-k)}{4}$$

**Step 5: Maximize $C_p$ with respect to $k$.**

Differentiate $C_p$ with respect to $k$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dC_p}{dk} = \frac{1}{4} \left[ 2(1+k)(1-k) - (1+k)^2 \right] = 0$$

$$(1+k)\left[ 2(1-k) - (1+k) \right] = 0$$

$$(1+k)(1 - 3k) = 0$$

Since $k \neq -1$ (physical constraint), we get $k = 1/3$.

**Step 6: Substitute $k = 1/3$ back into the $C_p$ expression.**

$$C_{p,max} = \frac{(1 + 1/3)^2 (1 - 1/3)}{4} = \frac{(4/3)^2 (2/3)}{4} = \frac{16/9 \cdot 2/3}{4} = \frac{32/27}{4} = \frac{16}{27}$$

$$\boxed{C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \quad \text{or} \quad 59.3\%}$$

> **Interpretation:** No wind turbine — regardless of design — can extract more than **59.3%** of the kinetic energy in the wind. This is the **Betz limit**, derived by Albert Betz in 1919. Real-world turbines achieve $C_p$ values of 0.35–0.45.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Solar Altitude Angle at Solar Noon

**Starting Premise:** The solar altitude angle `α` is the angle of the sun above the horizon. At solar noon, the hour angle `ω = 0`.

**Step 1: Start with the generic solar geometry equation.**

$$\sin\alpha = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$$

**Step 2: Apply the condition ω = 0 (cos 0 = 1).**

$$\sin\alpha_{noon} = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta$$

**Step 3: Use the trigonometric identity for cosine of difference.**

Recall: $\cos(A - B) = \cos A \cos B + \sin A \sin B$.

Therefore:

$$\sin\alpha_{noon} = \cos(\phi - \delta)$$

$$\alpha_{noon} = 90° - \vert \phi - \delta \vert$$

> **Interpretation:** At solar noon, the sun is highest when the observer's latitude `φ` equals the sun's declination `δ` (this happens around March 21 and September 23 — the equinoxes). For a site in Kerala (φ ≈ 10° N), the sun is nearly overhead for much of the year — which is why Kerala has some of the best solar energy potential in India.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Size of a Standalone PV System

**Step 1: Estimate the daily energy demand of the building (kWh/day).**

Sum the wattage of all electrical loads multiplied by hours of use per day:

$$E_{load} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot t_i \quad (\text{Wh/day})$$

**Step 2: Determine Peak Sun Hours (PSH) at the site.**

From NASA SSE or local meteorological data, e.g., PSH = 5.0 h/day for Kerala.

**Step 3: Compute the required PV array size in kWp.**

$$P_{PV} = \frac{E_{load}}{PSH \cdot \eta_{system} \cdot PR}$$

Where:
- $\eta_{system}$ = overall system efficiency (charge controller + inverter + wiring ≈ 0.85).
- $PR$ = Performance Ratio (accounts for temperature, dust, soiling losses ≈ 0.75).

**Step 4: Compute battery bank size (if off-grid).**

$$\text{Battery capacity (Ah)} = \frac{E_{load} \times \text{Autonomy days}}{V_{battery} \times DoD \times \eta_{battery}}$$

Where:
- $DoD$ = Depth of Discharge (typically 0.5–0.8 for lead-acid).
- $\eta_{battery}$ = battery round-trip efficiency (≈ 0.85).

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

A house in Kochi has a daily electrical load of 10 kWh. PSH = 5 h/day. Compute the required PV size.

$$P_{PV} = \frac{10{,}000}{5 \times 0.85 \times 0.75} = \frac{10{,}000}{3.1875} \approx 3137 \text{ Wp} \approx 3.2 \text{ kWp}$$

The system would need approximately **10 panels** of 330 Wp each (assuming $10 \text{ m}^2$ roof area).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Wind vs Solar in Construction

| Parameter | Wind Energy Harvesting | Solar Energy Harvesting |
|---|---|---|
| **Source variability** | Highly variable, diurnal & seasonal | Predictable, follows sun's path |
| **Power density** | 200–500 W/m² (swept area) | 150–200 W/m² (PV module area) |
| **Theoretical max efficiency** | 59.3% (Betz) | ~33% (Shockley-Queisser for single-junction Si) |
| **Practical efficiency** | 35–45% (Cp) | 18–22% (commercial Si PV) |
| **Best site in building** | Roof, tall towers, exposed ridges | South-facing roof (in N. hemisphere), facades |
| **Major losses** | Turbulence, mechanical, wake | Heat, dust, shading, spectral mismatch |
| **Aesthetic concerns** | Moderate (vibration, noise) | Low (BIPV can replace cladding) |
| **Levelized cost (LCOE)** | ₹3–5/kWh | ₹2–4/kWh |
| **Storage requirement** | Moderate (complementary to solar) | High (overnight use) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Direct Gain:** Sunlight enters the space directly and is absorbed by thermal mass (concrete floor, masonry walls). **Indirect Gain:** A thermal mass (Trombe wall) is placed between the sun and the space, absorbing heat and transferring it slowly. **Isolated Gain:** A separate sunspace is used as a buffer zone that can be isolated from the main living area.

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📌 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]`
**Define the term "Betz Limit" and state its numerical value. Why is it important in wind energy harvesting?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Betz Limit** is the theoretical maximum fraction of kinetic energy in wind that an ideal wind turbine can extract. It was derived by German physicist Albert Betz in 1919.

$$C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593 \quad \text{or} \quad 59.3\%$$

**Importance (1 mark):**
- It establishes that **no wind turbine, regardless of design, can convert more than 59.3%** of the wind's kinetic energy into useful mechanical work.
- It serves as a **benchmark for evaluating turbine efficiency**; real-world turbines typically achieve $C_p$ values of 0.35–0.45.
- The remaining ~40% of energy must be left in the wind to avoid stagnation and allow downstream flow.
- In sustainable construction, this limit informs the maximum number of turbines that can be economically installed on a site without diminishing returns.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]`
**Distinguish between Building-Integrated Photovoltaics (BIPV) and Building-Attached Photovoltaics (BAPV). Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | **BIPV** | **BAPV** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | PV modules that **replace conventional building materials** | PV modules that are **mounted on top of** existing building surfaces |
| Function | Serves **dual purpose** — building envelope + power generation | Serves **single purpose** — power generation only |
| Aesthetic | **Seamlessly integrated** with architecture | Appears as an **add-on** system |
| Example | PV curtain wall replacing glass facade; PV roof shingles | Rooftop solar panels mounted on rails over an existing RCC roof |

> **Example of BIPV:** Solar skylights or PV glazing that generates electricity while letting diffused light pass through.
> **Example of BAPV:** Standard crystalline-Si panels clamped onto a metal frame on top of an existing terrace.

---

## 📌 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **omit units** in formula substitutions — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** per missing unit.
> - Writing the **Betz derivation** without showing the differentiation step $dC_p/dk = 0$ leads to **loss of 3 marks**.
> - **Confusing "power coefficient" $C_p$ with "performance ratio" $PR$** is a common error in PV problems.
> - Always state **assumptions clearly** at the start of derivations (e.g., "assuming incompressible, steady, one-dimensional flow").

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — Wind Energy Focus

#### **Q.A.(a)** `[7 Marks]`
**Derive the expression for the power available in the wind. A site in Kerala has a mean wind speed of 6 m/s at 10 m height. The air density is 1.20 kg/m³. Calculate the power available in the wind for a horizontal-axis turbine with rotor diameter of 10 m. If the turbine operates at a power coefficient of 0.40, what is the actual electrical power output?**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Derivation of wind power formula (3 marks):**

> As derived in Section 3.1, the power in wind is:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the swept area (1 mark):**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (10)^2}{4} = \frac{100\pi}{4} = 25\pi \approx 78.54 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Calculate available power in the wind (1 mark):**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.20 \times 78.54 \times (6)^3$$

$$P_{wind} = 0.6 \times 78.54 \times 216 = 10{,}178.8 \text{ W} \approx 10.18 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the actual electrical output (2 marks):**

$$P_{mech} = C_p \times P_{wind} = 0.40 \times 10{,}178.8 = 4{,}071.5 \text{ W} \approx 4.07 \text{ kW}$$

> **Final Answer:** The turbine can produce approximately **4.07 kW** of mechanical power under the given conditions. This is **40% of the 10.18 kW** theoretically available in the wind — a reasonable operating point well within the Betz limit of 59.3%.

---

#### **Q.A.(b)** `[7 Marks]`
**With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a Vertical Axis Wind Turbine (VAWT). Compare it with a Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT) for building integration. State the Betz limit and explain its significance.**

**Model Solution Outline (7 marks):**

**Working Principle of VAWT (3 marks):**
- A VAWT has its **rotor shaft oriented perpendicular (vertical) to the ground**.
- Blades rotate around a vertical axis regardless of wind direction.
- Wind striking the curved blades creates **aerodynamic lift and drag** forces, causing rotation.
- Common types: **Savonius** (drag-based) and **Darrieus** (lift-based, egg-beater shape).
- **Savonius:** Two or three half-cylinders catch wind from any direction. High starting torque but low efficiency ($C_p \approx 0.15$).
- **Darrieus:** Uses airfoil-shaped blades in a "troposkein" curve. Higher efficiency ($C_p \approx 0.35$) but poor self-starting.

**VAWT vs HAWT Comparison (3 marks):**

| Parameter | **VAWT** | **HAWT** |
|---|---|---|
| Wind direction sensitivity | **Omni-directional** — no yaw mechanism needed | Requires **yaw control** to face the wind |
| Generator location | **At ground level** (easy maintenance) | **At top of tower** (difficult maintenance) |
| Noise level | **Low** (lower tip speeds) | Higher |
| Suitability for buildings | **Excellent** — roof edges, facades, between buildings | Limited — needs tall mast |
| Efficiency | Lower (0.15–0.35) | Higher (0.35–0.45) |
| Starting torque | **High** (Savonius) | **Low** — needs auxiliary motor |

**Betz Limit and Significance (1 mark):**
- $C_{p,max} = 16/27 \approx 59.3\%$
- This is the **theoretical maximum** for any wind turbine. It arises because a turbine must let the wind exit with some residual velocity, otherwise a vacuum forms behind it.
- Real turbines achieve 35–45% due to mechanical losses, turbulence, and non-ideal blade aerodynamics.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Solar Energy Focus

#### **Q.B.(a)** `[7 Marks]`
**Define the following terms with respect to solar geometry: (i) Declination angle, (ii) Hour angle, (iii) Solar altitude angle, (iv) Azimuth angle. For a site at latitude 10°N on March 21 (declination = 0°), calculate the solar altitude angle at solar noon.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions (4 marks):**

1. **Declination Angle (δ):** The angle between the line joining the centres of the Sun and Earth and the equatorial plane. It varies from −23.45° (winter solstice) to +23.45° (summer solstice), being 0° at equinoxes.

$$\delta = 23.45 \cdot \sin\left(\frac{360(284+n)}{365}\right)$$

2. **Hour Angle (ω):** The angular displacement of the Sun east or west of the local meridian. It is zero at solar noon, negative in the morning, positive in the afternoon. $\omega = 15° \times (t - 12)$ where `t` is solar time in hours.

3. **Solar Altitude Angle (α):** The angle of the Sun above the horizon (measured vertically).

$$\sin\alpha = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$$

4. **Azimuth Angle (γ):** The horizontal angle of the Sun measured clockwise from true North.

**Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: φ = 10°N, δ = 0° (March 21), ω = 0° (solar noon).

Apply the identity $\sin\alpha = \cos(\phi - \delta)$ at ω = 0:

$$\sin\alpha = \cos(10° - 0°) = \cos(10°)$$

$$\sin\alpha = 0.9848$$

$$\alpha = \sin^{-1}(0.9848) = 80°$$

> **Final Answer:** At solar noon on March 21, the Sun is at an altitude of **80°** above the horizon at latitude 10°N (Kerala). This is nearly overhead, which is why Kerala has excellent solar energy potential.

---

#### **Q.B.(b)** `[7 Marks]`
**Explain the working principle of a flat-plate solar collector with a neat diagram. A flat-plate collector has an area of 2.5 m² and receives solar radiation of 800 W/m². If the collector efficiency is 55%, calculate the useful heat gain per hour. State the factors affecting the efficiency of a flat-plate collector.**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
- A flat-plate solar collector consists of a **flat absorber plate** (usually copper or aluminium, painted black) with a network of tubes or fins attached.
- The plate is covered by one or more layers of **transmissive glass** to trap heat (greenhouse effect).
- The underside is insulated with **thermal insulation** (glass wool / rock wool) to minimize heat loss.
- A working fluid (water or air) flows through the tubes, absorbing heat from the absorber plate.
- The fluid is then circulated to a **storage tank** for domestic or industrial use.

**Calculation of useful heat gain (2 marks):**

$$Q_{useful} = \eta \cdot A \cdot G \cdot t$$

$$Q_{useful} = 0.55 \times 2.5 \times 800 \times 3600 \text{ s (1 hour)}$$

$$Q_{useful} = 0.55 \times 2.5 \times 800 \times 3600 = 3{,}960{,}000 \text{ J} = 3.96 \text{ MJ}$$

> **Final Answer:** The useful heat gain is **3.96 MJ/hour** (or equivalently, 3.96 MJ ≈ **1.1 kWh** of thermal energy per hour).

**Factors affecting flat-plate collector efficiency (2 marks):**

1. **Transmittance-Absorptance product (τα):** Higher τ (glass) and α (absorber coating) → higher efficiency.
2. **Overall heat loss coefficient ($U_L$):** Lower $U_L$ (better insulation, fewer glass losses) → higher efficiency.
3. **Fluid inlet temperature:** Higher inlet temperature reduces efficiency (second law of thermodynamics).
4. **Ambient temperature:** Colder ambient conditions improve efficiency.
5. **Solar irradiance:** Higher $G$ typically improves efficiency (the fixed loss terms become smaller fraction).
6. **Dust and soiling:** Reduce transmittance of the glass cover.
7. **Glazing thickness and number of covers:** More covers reduce losses but also reduce transmittance.
8. **Collector tilt and orientation:** Sub-optimal tilt reduces intercepted radiation.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- ✅ **Wind Power Formula:** $P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ — **cubic dependence on velocity** is the single most important fact.
- ✅ **Betz Limit:** $C_{p,max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ — derivable by maximizing $C_p$ as a function of downstream velocity ratio.
- ✅ **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR):** $\lambda = \omega R / v$ — optimal TSR is ~7–8 for modern three-blade HAWTs.
- ✅ **Air density at sea level:** $\rho_0 = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ — use $\rho = 1.225 \cdot e^{-h/8500}$ for altitude correction.
- ✅ **Solar Constant:** $I_0 = 1361 \text{ W/m}^2$ — applies outside the atmosphere; surface values are ~70% of this.
- ✅ **Declination Angle:** $\delta = 23.45 \sin\left(\frac{360(284+n)}{365}\right)$ — varies ±23.45° through the year.
- ✅ **At solar noon:** $\alpha_{noon} = 90° - \vert \phi - \delta \vert$ — useful shortcut for exam problems.
- ✅ **PV output:** $P_{PV} = \eta_{PV} \cdot A_{PV} \cdot G \cdot PR$ where PR = 0.7–0.85.
- ✅ **Flat-plate collector efficiency:** $\eta_c = F_R \left[ (\tau\alpha) - U_L \frac{T_{in} - T_a}{G} \right]$.
- ✅ **BIPV vs BAPV:** BIPV **replaces** building material; BAPV is **mounted on** existing surfaces.
- ✅ **Passive vs Active Solar:** Passive uses **architectural form** (no mechanical/electrical equipment); Active uses **PV/Thermal collectors**.
- ✅ **HAWT vs VAWT for buildings:** VAWT is omni-directional and ground-level generator; HAWT needs yaw control.
- ✅ **Hybrid systems** (wind + solar + battery) are preferred in tropical climates like Kerala for 24×7 reliability.
- ✅ **Kerala specific data:** Latitude ~10°N, mean wind speed 4–6 m/s, PSH ~5.0 h/day, biomass + solar + small-hydro are abundant.

> **Last-Minute Memory Aid:** If you remember only **three** things for this topic, remember:
> 1. $P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ (Wind Power)
> 2. Betz Limit = 59.3% (Maximum extraction)
> 3. Solar noon altitude = $90° - \vert \phi - \delta \vert$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Metering and Monitoring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET752 - SUSTAINABLE CONSTRUCTION PRACTICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy Metering and Monitoring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Energy Metering** is the systematic process of quantifying, recording, and reporting the consumption of various forms of energy (electrical, thermal, gas, and renewable) at defined boundary points within a building, campus, or construction site using calibrated measurement devices. **Energy Monitoring** is the continuous (or periodic) collection, transmission, processing, and visualization of this metered data to enable informed operational, maintenance, and capital investment decisions aimed at optimizing energy performance, ensuring occupant comfort, and validating compliance with green-building rating systems (e.g., GRIHA, LEED, IGBC, BEE-ECBC).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Energy Metering & Monitoring (EM&M) is the foundational **"Measure"** step of the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA)** cycle mandated under **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code) 2017/2024** and the **ISO 50001:2018 Energy Management System** standard. Without sub-metering, no energy-efficiency intervention can be technically verified or financially justified.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a building's energy system like a **household kitchen budget**:

- **Energy Meter** = The grocery bill. It tells you *exactly* how much you spent.
- **Energy Monitor** = The expense-tracking app on your phone. It tells you *where* the money is going (which appliance, at what time, and why).
- **Sub-meter** = Individual receipts for vegetables, milk, and electricity — you can see *which category* is bleeding your wallet.
- **BEMS (Building Energy Management System)** = Your financial advisor who suggests cutting down on non-essentials and tracks your savings.

**Without metering → you are guessing.**
**Without monitoring → you are flying blind.**
**Together → they form the "eyes and ears" of a sustainable building.**

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

- **1 kWh** = 3.6 MJ (Mega-Joules) — *standard energy unit for billing*.
- **1 TOE (Tonne of Oil Equivalent)** = **11,630 kWh** — *macro-level energy benchmark*.
- **1 tCO₂e** (tonne of CO₂ equivalent) — *carbon emission per 1 MWh ≈ 0.7–0.9 tCO₂e* (grid emission factor, India CEA: **0.72 kg CO₂/kWh** as of 2024).
- **Power factor (cos φ)** of an ideal inductive load ranges from **0.85 to 0.95** in commercial buildings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under **ECBC+ and SuperECBC** buildings, **sub-metering is mandatory** for:
> 1. HVAC systems (≥ 20% of total connected load)
> 2. Interior lighting (≥ 10% of total connected load)
> 3. Plug loads and equipment
> 4. Renewable energy systems (for net-metering verification)

### 1.4 Classification of Energy Meters

| Class | Type | Application | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Class 0.2 / 0.5 | High-precision static | Reference / tariff meters | ± 0.2% to 0.5% |
| Class 1.0 | Static (electronic) | Commercial & industrial | ± 1.0% |
| Class 2.0 | Electromechanical | Residential | ± 2.0% |
| Smart Meter | IoT-enabled bi-directional | Net-metering / AMI | ± 0.5% to 1.0% |

> [!TIP]
> **Green-Building Credit Tip:** GRIHA & LEED v4.1 (EA prerequisite) require **permanent metering infrastructure** for all major end-use categories. A project without sub-meters typically **loses 5–8 points** on the rating.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of an Energy Metering & Monitoring System

The system operates on a **4-tier hierarchical architecture**:

1. **Tier 1 — Sensing Layer (Field Devices):**
   - Current Transformers (CTs), Potential Transformers (PTs), temperature sensors (RTDs/thermocouples), flow meters (ultrasonic/electromagnetic), and gas meters (diaphragm/turbine).
   - Function: Convert physical quantities into standardized **4–20 mA** or **0–10 V** analog signals, or digital pulses.

2. **Tier 2 — Data Acquisition Layer (Data Loggers / Concentrators):**
   - Devices like **ELSPEC, Schneider PowerLogic, Honeywell E-Mon, Kamstrup**, or **Siemens SENTRON**.
   - Function: Aggregate signals, perform **A/D conversion**, timestamp, and store data locally (typically 5–60 second intervals).

3. **Tier 3 — Communication Layer (Protocols):**
   - **Modbus RTU/TCP** (industrial standard)
   - **BACnet** (ASHRAE standard for buildings)
   - **LONWORKS** (legacy building automation)
   - **M-Bus** (European meter bus for utilities)
   - **LoRaWAN / NB-IoT** (wireless for retrofit)

4. **Tier 4 — Analytics & Visualization Layer (Software):**
   - Cloud platforms (AWS IoT, Azure Digital Twins), BEMS dashboards, and KPI reporting tools.
   - Function: Convert raw data into **EUI, cost, demand, peak-load, and carbon-footprint** reports.

### 2.2 Why & How of Each Tier — Engineering Reasoning

- **Why CTs instead of direct connection?** At high currents (>100 A), direct connection is unsafe and impractical. CTs step down current proportionally (e.g., **200:5 A** ratio) for safe measurement.
- **Why 15-minute intervals?** This is the **standard billing block** used by utilities (TOD — Time of Day) globally and matches ASHRAE Guideline 14 minimum.
- **Why BACnet over Modbus?** BACnet is **object-oriented** and supports HVAC-specific semantics (setpoints, schedules), while Modbus is **register-based** and limited to generic data exchange.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All vertical bars replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table breakage.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = P \times t$ | Energy = Power × Time | kWh | Basic consumption |
| 2 | $P = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L \times \cos\phi$ | Three-phase active power | kW | Industrial loads |
| 3 | $S = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L$ | Three-phase apparent power | kVA | Transformer sizing |
| 4 | $Q = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L \times \sin\phi$ | Reactive power | kVAr | PF correction |
| 5 | $PF = \cos\phi = \dfrac{P}{S}$ | Power factor (cos φ) | dimensionless | Billing penalty |
| 6 | $DF = \dfrac{\text{Maximum Demand}}{\text{Connected Load}}$ | Demand factor | dimensionless | Load planning |
| 7 | $LF = \dfrac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Peak Load}}$ | Load factor | dimensionless | Efficiency |
| 8 | $\text{EUI} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{A_{floor}}$ | Energy Use Intensity | kWh/m²/yr | Green building KPI |
| 9 | $\text{Carbon} = E \times EF_{grid}$ | CO₂ emissions | kgCO₂ | Carbon audit |
| 10 | $\text{Cost Savings} = \Delta E \times R$ | Annual savings | ₹ / $ | Payback analysis |
| 11 | $\text{Payback} = \dfrac{C_{capex}}{\text{Cost Savings}}$ | Simple payback | years | Investment appraisal |
| 12 | $CFM = \dfrac{V \times ACH}{60}$ | Air flow for HVAC | CFM | Ventilation metering |
| 13 | $\text{kVAh} = \dfrac{\text{kWh}}{PF}$ | Apparent energy for billing | kVAh | Utility tariff |
| 14 | $\text{Peak Demand} = \max_{t \in T} P(t)$ | Maximum 15-min average | kW | Demand charges |
| 15 | $\text{M\&V Savings} = E_{baseline} - E_{reporting} \pm \Delta_{adj}$ | IPMVP equation | kWh | M&V verification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Benchmark Values for Indian Commercial Buildings (ECBC 2017):**
> - Office Building EUI: **100 – 200 kWh/m²/year**
> - Mall EUI: **250 – 400 kWh/m²/year**
> - Hospital EUI: **300 – 500 kWh/m²/year**
> - **ECBC-compliant building: ≤ 90 kWh/m²/year (5-Star)**
> - **SuperECBC: ≤ 50 kWh/m²/year**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Grids:** Bi-directional metering enables net-metering for rooftop solar PV (PM-Surya Ghar Yojana, India).
- **ESCO (Energy Service Company) Models:** Guaranteed savings contracts depend entirely on **IPMVP Option B (Retrofit Isolation)** or **Option D (Calibrated Simulation)** — both require robust M&M infrastructure.
- **Net-Zero Buildings:** Achieving net-zero is impossible without a **dedicated renewable energy sub-meter** and a **carbon tracker dashboard**.
- **Green-B rating compliance** under **ISO 50001** mandates PDCA cycles driven by monitored data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Use Intensity (EUI) from Raw Meter Data

**Problem Statement:** A commercial office building has the following monthly sub-meter readings for FY 2023-24. Calculate the annual EUI, the total carbon footprint, and the percentage share of HVAC in total energy. Total conditioned floor area = **2,500 m²**.

**Sub-meter Data (kWh):**

| Month | HVAC (Chiller + AHU) | Lighting | Plug Loads | Lifts & Pumps | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Apr | 18,400 | 4,200 | 3,800 | 2,100 | 28,500 |
| May | 22,100 | 4,400 | 3,900 | 2,200 | 32,600 |
| Jun | 24,800 | 4,600 | 3,950 | 2,300 | 35,650 |
| Jul | 26,500 | 4,700 | 4,000 | 2,400 | 37,600 |
| Aug | 25,900 | 4,650 | 3,980 | 2,350 | 36,880 |
| Sep | 23,400 | 4,500 | 3,920 | 2,250 | 34,070 |
| Oct | 20,100 | 4,300 | 3,880 | 2,150 | 30,430 |
| Nov | 16,800 | 4,100 | 3,820 | 2,050 | 26,770 |
| Dec | 14,200 | 3,950 | 3,780 | 2,000 | 23,930 |
| Jan | 13,500 | 3,900 | 3,760 | 1,980 | 23,140 |
| Feb | 15,600 | 4,000 | 3,800 | 2,020 | 25,420 |
| Mar | 17,300 | 4,150 | 3,830 | 2,080 | 27,360 |

**Step 1 — Sum each column to get annual energy by end-use:**

For HVAC:

$$E_{HVAC} = \sum_{m=1}^{12} E_{HVAC,m}$$

$$E_{HVAC} = 18400 + 22100 + 24800 + 26500 + 25900 + 23400 + 20100 + 16800 + 14200 + 13500 + 15600 + 17300$$

Adding systematically:
- 18400 + 22100 = 40500
- 40500 + 24800 = 65300
- 65300 + 26500 = 91800
- 91800 + 25900 = 117700
- 117700 + 23400 = 141100
- 141100 + 20100 = 161200
- 161200 + 16800 = 178000
- 178000 + 14200 = 192200
- 192200 + 13500 = 205700
- 205700 + 15600 = 221300
- 221300 + 17300 = 238600

$$\therefore E_{HVAC} = 238{,}600 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

For Lighting:

$$E_{LTG} = 4200 + 4400 + 4600 + 4700 + 4650 + 4500 + 4300 + 4100 + 3950 + 3900 + 4000 + 4150$$

Sequential sum:
- 4200 + 4400 = 8600
- 8600 + 4600 = 13200
- 13200 + 4700 = 17900
- 17900 + 4650 = 22550
- 22550 + 4500 = 27050
- 27050 + 4300 = 31350
- 31350 + 4100 = 35450
- 35450 + 3950 = 39400
- 39400 + 3900 = 43300
- 43300 + 4000 = 47300
- 47300 + 4150 = 51450

$$\therefore E_{LTG} = 51{,}450 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

For Plug Loads:

$$E_{PL} = 3800 + 3900 + 3950 + 4000 + 3980 + 3920 + 3880 + 3820 + 3780 + 3760 + 3800 + 3830$$

Sequential sum:
- 3800 + 3900 = 7700
- 7700 + 3950 = 11650
- 11650 + 4000 = 15650
- 15650 + 3980 = 19630
- 19630 + 3920 = 23550
- 23550 + 3880 = 27430
- 27430 + 3820 = 31250
- 31250 + 3780 = 35030
- 35030 + 3760 = 38790
- 38790 + 3800 = 42590
- 42590 + 3830 = 46420

$$\therefore E_{PL} = 46{,}420 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

For Lifts & Pumps:

$$E_{LP} = 2100 + 2200 + 2300 + 2400 + 2350 + 2250 + 2150 + 2050 + 2000 + 1980 + 2020 + 2080$$

Sequential sum:
- 2100 + 2200 = 4300
- 4300 + 2300 = 6600
- 6600 + 2400 = 9000
- 9000 + 2350 = 11350
- 11350 + 2250 = 13600
- 13600 + 2150 = 15750
- 15750 + 2050 = 17800
- 17800 + 2000 = 19800
- 19800 + 1980 = 21780
- 21780 + 2020 = 23800
- 23800 + 2080 = 25880

$$\therefore E_{LP} = 25{,}880 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

**Step 2 — Total annual energy:**

$$E_{Total} = E_{HVAC} + E_{LTG} + E_{PL} + E_{LP}$$

$$E_{Total} = 238600 + 51450 + 46420 + 25880$$

Adding:
- 238600 + 51450 = 290050
- 290050 + 46420 = 336470
- 336470 + 25880 = 362350

$$\therefore E_{Total} = 362{,}350 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

**Step 3 — Energy Use Intensity (EUI):**

$$\text{EUI} = \frac{E_{Total}}{A_{floor}} = \frac{362350}{2500}$$

$$\therefore \text{EUI} = 144.94 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{yr}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since 144.94 kWh/m²/yr lies between **100 and 200 kWh/m²/yr**, the building is in the **typical Indian office** range — **not yet ECBC-compliant** (which requires ≤ 90 kWh/m²/yr for 5-Star). Energy retrofit is recommended.

**Step 4 — HVAC Energy Share:**

$$\% \text{ HVAC} = \frac{E_{HVAC}}{E_{Total}} \times 100 = \frac{238600}{362350} \times 100$$

$$\therefore \% \text{ HVAC} = 65.85\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> HVAC dominates at **~66%** — a classic signature of an Indian commercial building. This validates the **"Trias Energetica"** principle: focus first on the **biggest energy user**.

**Step 5 — Annual Carbon Footprint (using CEA grid emission factor $EF_{grid} = 0.72$ kg CO₂/kWh):**

$$C_{annual} = E_{Total} \times EF_{grid} = 362350 \times 0.72$$

$$\therefore C_{annual} = 260{,}892 \text{ kg CO}_2 = 260.89 \text{ tCO}_2\text{e/yr}$$

**Step 6 — Per-capita carbon footprint (assume 200 occupants):**

$$C_{per\,person} = \frac{260.89 \times 1000}{200} = 1304.46 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{person/yr}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Demand & Load Factor Calculation

**Problem:** A factory has a connected load of 1,200 kW. The following 15-minute interval readings (in kW) were recorded by a digital maximum-demand meter over a day: **780, 815, 902, 950, 1,105, 1,180, 1,150, 980, 870, 820, 760, 720, 690, 670, 650, 670, 720, 840, 1,020, 1,140, 1,180, 1,090, 950, 870**.

**Step 1 — Identify the Peak Demand:**

$$\text{Maximum Demand} = \max(P_t) = 1{,}180 \text{ kW}$$

(Note: 1,180 appears twice — typically the *first occurrence* is recorded by the meter.)

**Step 2 — Sum all 24 readings to get total energy over 15-min intervals:**

$$E_{15min\,total} = \sum P_t = 780 + 815 + 902 + 950 + 1105 + 1180 + 1150 + 980 + 870 + 820 + 760 + 720 + 690 + 670 + 650 + 670 + 720 + 840 + 1020 + 1140 + 1180 + 1090 + 950 + 870$$

Sequential summation:
- 780 + 815 = 1595
- 1595 + 902 = 2497
- 2497 + 950 = 3447
- 3447 + 1105 = 4552
- 4552 + 1180 = 5732
- 5732 + 1150 = 6882
- 6882 + 980 = 7862
- 7862 + 870 = 8732
- 8732 + 820 = 9552
- 9552 + 760 = 10312
- 10312 + 720 = 11032
- 11032 + 690 = 11722
- 11722 + 670 = 12392
- 12392 + 650 = 13042
- 13042 + 670 = 13712
- 13712 + 720 = 14432
- 14432 + 840 = 15272
- 15272 + 1020 = 16292
- 16292 + 1140 = 17432
- 17432 + 1180 = 18612
- 18612 + 1090 = 19702
- 19702 + 950 = 20652
- 20652 + 870 = 21522

$$\therefore E_{15min\,total} = 21{,}522 \text{ kW} \cdot 15\text{min}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to daily energy in kWh:**

Since each interval is 15 min = 0.25 hr:

$$E_{day} = 21522 \times 0.25 = 5{,}380.5 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 4 — Average Load over 24 hours:**

$$\bar{P} = \frac{E_{day}}{24} = \frac{5380.5}{24} = 224.19 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Load Factor:**

$$LF = \frac{\bar{P}}{P_{max}} = \frac{224.19}{1180} = 0.1900 \text{ or } 19.00\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Low Load Factor (19%)** indicates massive under-utilization. Strategies: **load shifting, peak-shaving via BESS, demand-side management**.

**Step 6 — Demand Factor:**

$$DF = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{connected}} = \frac{1180}{1200} = 0.9833 \text{ or } 98.33\%$$

**Step 7 — Diversity Factor (assumed 2.0):**

$$DivF = \frac{\sum \text{Individual Max Demands}}{\text{Group Max Demand}} = 2.0$$

This implies that simultaneous operation is unlikely; the **group max demand is halved** compared to individual peaks.

**Step 8 — Energy Cost (assume ₹9.50/kWh + ₹450/kW/month demand charge):**

$$C_{energy} = 5380.5 \times 9.50 = \text{₹ } 51{,}114.75/\text{day}$$

$$C_{demand} = 1180 \times 450 = \text{₹ } 5{,}31{,}000/\text{month}$$

$$\text{Annual energy cost} = 51114.75 \times 365 = \text{₹ } 1,86,56,883.75 \approx \text{₹ } 1.87 \text{ Crore/yr}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Measurement & Verification (M&V) Savings via IPMVP Option B

**Problem:** A building underwent a chiller retrofit. Baseline (pre-retrofit) energy = **485,000 kWh/yr** (from 3-year sub-meter history, weather-normalized). Reporting period (post-retrofit) energy = **372,000 kWh/yr**. Adjustments for unusual occupancy = **+8,200 kWh** (baseline equivalent). Routine & non-routine adjustments = **+3,400 kWh**. Calculate the M&V-verified savings.

**Step 1 — Apply IPMVP Option B formula:**

$$\text{Savings} = (E_{baseline} - E_{reporting}) \pm \Delta_{adjustments}$$

**Step 2 — Plug in values:**

$$\text{Savings} = (485000 - 372000) + 8200 + 3400$$

- 485000 - 372000 = 113000
- 113000 + 8200 = 121200
- 121200 + 3400 = 124600

$$\therefore \text{Savings} = 124{,}600 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage savings:**

$$\% \text{ Savings} = \frac{124600}{485000} \times 100 = 25.69\%$$

**Step 4 — Monetary savings (at ₹9.50/kWh):**

$$\text{Rs. Savings} = 124600 \times 9.50 = \text{₹ } 11{,}83{,}700/\text{yr}$$

**Step 5 — Carbon savings:**

$$\text{CO}_2 \text{ avoided} = 124600 \times 0.72 = 89{,}712 \text{ kg} = 89.71 \text{ tCO}_2\text{e/yr}$$

> [!TIP]
> **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement & Verification Protocol)** has **4 options (A, B, C, D)**. Option B is the **most common for retrofit isolation** projects. Option A is for partial measurement with stipulated parameters. Option C uses the whole facility. Option D uses calibrated simulation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: Hierarchical Architecture of a Building Energy Monitoring System (BEMS)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Field Sensors Layer\nCTs, PTs, RTDs, Flow Meters, Gas Meters]
    nodeB[Data Acquisition Layer\nEnergy Meters, Data Loggers, Pulse Counters]
    nodeC[Communication Layer\nModbus, BACnet, M-Bus, LoRaWAN]
    nodeD[Gateway and Edge Computing\nProtocol Converters, Local Storage]
    nodeE[Cloud and Analytics Layer\nTime-Series DB, KPI Engine, AI ML Models]
    nodeF[Visualization Layer\nDashboards, Mobile Apps, Reports]
    nodeG[Action and Control Layer\nAlarms, Automated Setpoints, BEMS Actuators]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeG -.feedback.-> nodeB
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2: IPMVP Measurement & Verification (M&V) Process Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    step1[Step 1 Define Project Boundary\nand M&V Option A B C D]
    step2[Step 2 Install Sub-meters\nClass 1.0 accuracy minimum]
    step3[Step 3 Collect Baseline Data\nMinimum 12 months]
    step4[Step 4 Develop Baseline Model\nRegression Analysis or Simulation]
    step5[Step 5 Implement ECM\nEnergy Conservation Measure]
    step6[Step 6 Collect Reporting Period Data\nPost-retrofit]
    step7[Step 7 Apply Adjustments\nWeather, Occupancy, Production]
    step8[Step 8 Calculate Savings\nE_baseline minus E_reporting plus adjustments]
    step9[Step 9 Verify and Report\nQA QC, Uncertainty Analysis]

    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
    step3 --> step4
    step4 --> step5
    step5 --> step6
    step6 --> step7
    step7 --> step8
    step8 --> step9
    step9 -.feedback loop continuous M&V.-> step6
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Smart Meter Data Flow (AMI – Advanced Metering Infrastructure)

```mermaid
graph LR
    meterA[Smart Meter 1\nHVAC Sub-meter]
    meterB[Smart Meter 2\nLighting Sub-meter]
    meterC[Smart Meter 3\nPlug Load Meter]
    meterD[Smart Meter 4\nRenewable Net Meter]

    concentrator[Data Concentrator\nAMI Gateway]

    cloud[Cloud Platform\nTime Series Database]

    dashboard[Analytics Dashboard\nEUI Cost Carbon KPIs]

    alarm[Alarm and Notification Engine]

    meterA --> concentrator
    meterB --> concentrator
    meterC --> concentrator
    meterD --> concentrator
    concentrator --> cloud
    cloud --> dashboard
    cloud --> alarm
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4: Energy Use Intensity (EUI) Benchmarking Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Start: Measure EUI kWh per m2 per year]
    level1{Is EUI less than 50?}
    level2{Is EUI between 50 and 90?}
    level3{Is EUI between 90 and 200?}
    level4{Is EUI greater than 200?}

    a1[Result: SuperECBC Compliant\nNet Zero Ready]
    a2[Result: ECBC Plus Compliant\n5 Star Rating]
    a3[Result: ECBC Compliant\n3 Star Rating]
    a4[Result: Non-Compliant\nRetrofit Required]

    start --> level1
    level1 -->|Yes| a1
    level1 -->|No| level2
    level2 -->|Yes| a2
    level2 -->|No| level3
    level3 -->|Yes| a3
    level3 -->|No| level4
    level4 -->|Yes| a4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid block diagrams and not CAD drawings?** Mermaid renders natively in modern markdown viewers, including GitHub, GitLab, and most LMS platforms. For complex physical schematics (e.g., electrical single-line diagrams with CT polarity, metering cubicle layouts), refer to **IS 14697 (A.C. Static Watt-hour Meters)** and **CBIP Manual on Power System Metering**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Define **Energy Metering** and **Energy Monitoring**. How are they different from each other in the context of a sustainable building?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Energy Metering (1 mark):** It is the physical process of measuring the quantity of energy (electrical, thermal, gas) consumed at a defined point using calibrated devices such as watt-hour meters, BTU meters, and gas meters. It provides a *quantitative snapshot* (e.g., "This chiller consumed 4,500 kWh last month").
- **Energy Monitoring (1 mark):** It is the *continuous collection, transmission, and analysis* of metered data over time to identify patterns, wastage, and opportunities for optimization. It includes dashboards, alarms, and trend analysis.
- **Key Difference (1 mark):** Metering is the *act of measurement*; Monitoring is the *act of analysis and response*. **Metering without monitoring = data waste. Monitoring without metering = guesswork.** Together, they form the **"Measure"** step of the PDCA cycle under ISO 50001 and the foundation of M&V under IPMVP.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Exam]**

**Q2.** List any **three differences between a conventional electromechanical energy meter and a modern smart meter**. Why are smart meters preferred for net-metering applications?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Electromechanical Meter | Smart Meter |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Measurement | Induction-disc, mechanical | Solid-state, digital DSP |
| 2. Accuracy | Class 2.0 (±2%) | Class 0.5 to 1.0 (±0.5–1%) |
| 3. Communication | None (manual reading) | Bi-directional (RF/PLC/IoT) |
| 4. Net-metering | Not possible | Possible (import + export) |
| 5. Data resolution | Monthly cumulative | 15-min interval or real-time |

**Net-metering preference (1 mark):** Smart meters can record **bi-directional energy flow** (grid import AND solar PV export) with time-stamping, enabling accurate **net energy accounting** and **time-of-day (ToD) tariff** calculations mandated by the **MNRE Net-Metering Regulations 2023**.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, End Semester Evaluation]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A.(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **four-tier architecture** of a Building Energy Management System (BEMS). List the key components, communication protocols, and data flow at each tier with a suitable block diagram.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

A BEMS follows a **four-tier hierarchical architecture** that ensures data integrity, scalability, and actionable intelligence:

**Tier 1 — Field Sensing Layer (2 marks):**
- **Components:** Current Transformers (CTs), Potential Transformers (PTs), Resistance Temperature Detectors (RTDs), Thermocouples, Ultrasonic/electromagnetic flow meters, gas meters, lux sensors, and occupancy sensors.
- **Function:** Convert physical quantities (kW, °C, l/s, ppm) into standardized **4–20 mA / 0–10 V analog signals** or **digital pulses (kWh pulses)**.

**Tier 2 — Data Acquisition Layer (2 marks):**
- **Components:** Energy meters (Schneider PM5560, Siemens SENTRON PAC4200, ABB M4M 30), data loggers (ELSPEC G4500, Onset HOBO), and pulse concentrators.
- **Function:** Aggregate signals, perform **A/D conversion**, store data locally with timestamps, and push to higher tiers via standard protocols. Sampling interval is typically **1 to 15 minutes**.

**Tier 3 — Communication Layer (1.5 marks):**
- **Protocols:** 
  - **Modbus RTU/TCP** (industrial, register-based)
  - **BACnet/IP** (ASHRAE 135, object-based, HVAC-specific)
  - **M-Bus** (European meter bus, low-power)
  - **LoRaWAN / NB-IoT** (wireless for retrofits)
  - **DNP3** (substation automation)
- **Function:** Ensure interoperability between heterogeneous devices and enable bidirectional data exchange.

**Tier 4 — Analytics & Visualization Layer (1.5 marks):**
- **Components:** Cloud platforms (AWS IoT Core, Azure Digital Twins, Siemens Navigator), local servers, and SCADA systems.
- **Function:** Compute **KPIs (EUI, kWh/m², cost, carbon)**, generate **trend charts, alarms, and reports**, and feed back **control signals** to HVAC actuators, VAV boxes, and chiller plant controllers.
- **Standards:** ASHRAE Guideline 14, IPMVP, ISO 50001.

> **[Reference Block Diagram – see SECTION 4, Diagram 1]**

---

**Q.A.(b) [7 Marks]** A **5-storey commercial office building** has the following monthly energy consumption data from sub-meters for one year. Calculate:
1. The **annual Energy Use Intensity (EUI)** in kWh/m²/yr
2. The **percentage share of HVAC** in total energy
3. The **annual carbon emissions** in tCO₂e, assuming the CEA grid emission factor = **0.72 kg CO₂/kWh**
4. The **annual energy cost** at ₹9.50/kWh

**Data:** Total conditioned area = **4,000 m²**
**HVAC sub-meter total = 285,000 kWh/yr**
**Lighting sub-meter total = 64,000 kWh/yr**
**Plug loads sub-meter total = 58,000 kWh/yr**
**Lifts and pumps sub-meter total = 33,000 kWh/yr**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total annual energy (1 mark):**

$$E_{Total} = 285000 + 64000 + 58000 + 33000 = 440{,}000 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

**Step 2 — EUI calculation (2 marks):**

$$\text{EUI} = \frac{E_{Total}}{A} = \frac{440000}{4000} = 110 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{yr}$$

> **Interpretation:** Since EUI = 110 kWh/m²/yr, the building is in the **ECBC-Compliant (3-Star)** band (90–200 range) but **not 5-Star** (which requires ≤ 90 kWh/m²/yr).

**Step 3 — HVAC share (1.5 marks):**

$$\% \text{ HVAC} = \frac{285000}{440000} \times 100 = 64.77\%$$

> This is a typical Indian commercial-building signature; HVAC is the **dominant end-use** and the prime target for retrofits (chiller replacement, VFD installation, free cooling, ECBC-compliant glazing).

**Step 4 — Annual carbon emissions (1.5 marks):**

$$C_{annual} = 440000 \times 0.72 = 316{,}800 \text{ kg CO}_2 = 316.80 \text{ tCO}_2\text{e/yr}$$

**Step 5 — Annual energy cost (1 mark):**

$$\text{Cost} = 440000 \times 9.50 = \text{₹ } 41{,}80{,}000 = \text{₹ } 41.80 \text{ Lakh/yr}$$

**Final Tabulated Result:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Total Energy | 4,40,000 kWh/yr |
| EUI | 110 kWh/m²/yr |
| HVAC Share | 64.77% |
| Carbon Footprint | 316.80 tCO₂e/yr |
| Annual Cost | ₹ 41.80 Lakh |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B.(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement & Verification Protocol)** framework in detail. Explain its **four options (A, B, C, D)** with suitable examples. Why is M&V critical for **ESCO (Energy Service Company) guaranteed-savings contracts**?

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** IPMVP, published by **EVO (Efficiency Valuation Organization)**, is the global standard for **Measurement & Verification (M&V)** of energy-efficiency projects. It provides a structured methodology to **quantify savings** achieved by Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs) in a **transparent, repeatable, and defensible** manner.

**The Four IPMVP Options (4 marks):**

| Option | Method | Typical Use Case | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A — Retrofit Isolation (Key Parameter)** | Measure one or more key parameters; others are stipulated | Lighting retrofit, VFD on pumps | Measure kW of new lights; assume stipulated operating hours |
| **B — Retrofit Isolation (All Parameters)** | Measure ALL parameters of the ECM | Chiller replacement, boiler retrofit | Sub-meter both baseline and post-retrofit chiller energy |
| **C — Whole Facility** | Measure total facility energy; use regression for normalization | Multi-ECM project at building level | Use 3-year utility bill history + weather regression |
| **D — Calibrated Simulation** | Use calibrated energy model | New construction, major HVAC redesign | eQUEST/EnergyPlus model calibrated to actual sub-meter data |

**Key Equation (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\text{Savings} = (E_{baseline} - E_{reporting}) \pm \text{Adjustments}}$$

Where adjustments include **weather (CDD/HDD), occupancy, production volume, and non-routine events**.

**Why M&V is critical for ESCOs (1 mark):**
- ESCO contracts guarantee a fixed saving (e.g., "30% reduction or your money back").
- **Without IPMVP-compliant M&V**, savings cannot be **verified objectively**, leading to disputes.
- IPMVP provides the **legal and financial foundation** for **Energy Performance Contracts (EPCs)**.
- It enables **Measurement-Based Verification (MBV)** and protects both client and ESCO interests.

---

**Q.B.(b) [7 Marks]** A factory installed a **smart energy monitoring system** for a capex of **₹ 18,00,000**. Post-installation, the sub-meters revealed that the plant was wasting energy equivalent to **₹ 4,50,000 per year** through idle equipment, poor PF, and unnecessary lighting. Calculate:
1. The **Simple Payback Period** in years
2. The **Return on Investment (ROI)** over 5 years
3. The **Net Present Value (NPV)** at a discount rate of 10% per year over 5 years
4. Comment on the **financial viability** of the investment.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Simple Payback (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{Payback} = \frac{C_{capex}}{\text{Annual Savings}} = \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{4{,}50{,}000} = 4.0 \text{ years}$$

**Step 2 — 5-Year ROI (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{Total 5-yr Savings} = 450000 \times 5 = \text{₹ } 22{,}50{,}000$$

$$\text{ROI} = \frac{\text{Net Savings}}{\text{Capex}} \times 100 = \frac{2250000 - 1800000}{1800000} \times 100 = 25\%$$

**Step 3 — NPV at 10% discount rate (3 marks):**

NPV formula:

$$\text{NPV} = -C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

Where $C_0 = 18,00,000$, $CF_t = 4,50,000$, $r = 0.10$, $n = 5$.

$$\text{NPV} = -1800000 + 450000 \left[ \frac{1}{1.1} + \frac{1}{1.1^2} + \frac{1}{1.1^3} + \frac{1}{1.1^4} + \frac{1}{1.1^5} \right]$$

Computing each discount factor:
- $\frac{1}{1.1} = 0.9091$
- $\frac{1}{1.1^2} = 0.8264$
- $\frac{1}{1.1^3} = 0.7513$
- $\frac{1}{1.1^4} = 0.6830$
- $\frac{1}{1.1^5} = 0.6209$

Sum of discount factors:

$$0.9091 + 0.8264 + 0.7513 + 0.6830 + 0.6209 = 3.7908$$

**Present Value of Cash Inflows:**

$$PV = 450000 \times 3.7908 = 17{,}05{,}860$$

**Net Present Value:**

$$\text{NPV} = -1800000 + 1705860 = -94{,}140$$

**Step 4 — Financial Viability Comment (1 mark):**

> Since NPV is **negative (–₹ 94,140)** at a 10% discount rate over 5 years, the project is **marginally unviable** at the stipulated discount rate. However, the **payback is 4 years** (acceptable for sustainable infrastructure) and the **lifetime of the system is typically 10–15 years**, yielding positive returns from year 6 onwards. **Recommendation: APPROVE** the investment, but negotiate a **lower capex** or pursue **subsidy from BEE/PCRA** to improve NPV.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (Lose 1–3 marks each):**
> 1. **Confusing "metering" with "monitoring":** Metering = measurement device; Monitoring = data analytics + visualization. Examiners *specifically* test this distinction for 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor $\sqrt{3}$ in 3-phase power:** A 3-phase power formula missing $\sqrt{3}$ is a guaranteed 0.5-mark deduction. Always check if the load is 3-phase.
> 3. **Using the wrong EUI denominator:** EUI uses **conditioned/total floor area (m²)**, not plot area or built-up area. Mixing units (m² vs ft²) is a common error.
> 4. **Missing the carbon emission factor:** Examiners often give 1 mark for applying the **0.72 kg CO₂/kWh (India CEA)** factor; students who skip this lose easy marks.
> 5. **Not drawing/mentioning the block diagram:** For Tier-architecture questions, **always include a labelled block diagram** (mermaid, ASCII, or hand-drawn). A text-only answer loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Using ₹ without commas or units:** Always write "₹ 4,50,000/year" not "450000 rs". Professional notation earns goodwill marks.
> 7. **Forgetting to interpret the EUI result:** Don't just calculate — **state the rating band** (e.g., "EUI = 110 → ECBC 3-Star, not 5-Star"). Interpretation is the "Apply" level of Bloom's Taxonomy and carries bonus marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Metering & Monitoring — Last-Minute Revision (Read in 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Definition (1-liner):** Metering = Measure; Monitoring = Analyze + Act. Both = ISO 50001 PDCA.
- **EUI Formula:** $\text{EUI} = E_{total} / A_{floor}$ in **kWh/m²/yr**.
- **3-Phase Power:** $P = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L \times \cos\phi$ — **don't forget the √3**!
- **India Grid EF:** $0.72$ kg CO₂/kWh (CEA 2024) — *memorize this constant*.
- **ECBC EUI Benchmarks:** ECBC = 90, ECBC+ = 50, SuperECBC < 50 kWh/m²/yr.
- **HVAC Share:** Typically **60–70%** in Indian commercial buildings — biggest retrofit target.
- **IPMVP Equation:** $\text{Savings} = E_{baseline} - E_{reporting} \pm \text{Adjustments}$.
- **4 IPMVP Options:** A (Key Parameter) → B (All Parameters) → C (Whole Facility) → D (Calibrated Simulation).
- **4 BEMS Tiers:** Sensors → Data Acquisition → Communication → Analytics/Visualization.
- **Key Protocols:** Modbus (industrial), BACnet (buildings), M-Bus (meters), LoRaWAN (wireless).
- **Smart Meter Advantage:** Bi-directional, 15-min interval, ToD tariff, net-metering ready.
- **Payback Formula:** $\text{Payback} = C_{capex} / \text{Annual Savings}$ (in years).
- **NPV Formula:** $\text{NPV} = -C_0 + \sum \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$.
- **Carbon:** 1 tCO₂e ≈ **1,389 kWh** of grid electricity (at 0.72 kg/kWh).
- **Green Building Credits:** GRIHA/LEED EA prerequisite — permanent sub-metering is **mandatory**.
- **Common Rating Bands:** <50 (SuperECBC), 50–90 (ECBC+), 90–200 (ECBC), >200 (Non-compliant).
- **Power Factor Penalty:** Utilities in India penalize **PF < 0.90** — *always check this in billing problems*.
- **TOD Tariff:** Peak (6 PM–10 PM) costs 1.5× normal rate — energy monitoring enables load shifting.
- **Standards to Cite:** IS 14697, ECBC 2017, ISO 50001:2018, IPMVP, ASHRAE Guideline 14, BEE Star Labeling.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When a question gives you monthly sub-meter data, *always* sum to annual, compute EUI, compute carbon, and **end with an interpretation sentence**. This single sentence often earns you the **"Apply/Analyze" mark** in Bloom's Taxonomy and pushes your answer from "Good" to "Excellent".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Net Zero Buildings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET752 - SUSTAINABLE CONSTRUCTION PRACTICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept of Net Zero Buildings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Net Zero Building (NZB):** A highly energy-efficient building that, through the integrated application of on-site renewable energy systems and/or off-site renewable energy procurement, achieves a net balance between the **energy consumed** by the building and the **energy generated/exported** by renewable sources, measured over a defined boundary period (typically **12 months / 1 calendar year**).

A Net Zero Building is **not** a building that consumes *zero* energy. It is a building engineered to consume such a small quantity of energy (via passive design, high-performance envelopes, and efficient HVAC/lighting systems) that the balance of its annual energy demand can be offset by clean, renewable generation.

### Sub-Categories of Net Zero (Per KTU/IGBC/NABH Frameworks)

| Sub-Type | What is Balanced to Zero? | Typical Time Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| **Net Zero Energy (NZE)** | Source Energy / Site Energy | 12 months |
| **Net Zero Carbon (NZC)** | Operational + Embodied CO₂ | 50–60 year lifecycle |
| **Net Zero Water (NZW)** | Water consumed = Water harvested/recycled | 12 months |
| **Net Zero Waste** | Waste sent to landfill | Project life |
| **Net Zero Ready** | Designed to NZB specs but renewable systems not yet installed | — |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Bank Account" Analogy:**
> Think of a Net Zero Building like a person living entirely on their annual salary — **without ever going into debt**.
>
> - The **building's annual energy bill** is like your yearly expenses (rent, food, electricity).
> - **Rooftop solar PV** is your "side income" from a small home business.
> - To achieve **Net Zero**, you must do two things: **(a) drastically cut your expenses** (super-insulated walls, LED lights, passive cooling) so that the bill is small, and **(b) earn at least that much** from the side income (solar panels sized correctly).
>
> If you earn ₹8,000 from solar but spend ₹10,000 in electricity — you have a **deficit (Net Positive Energy building)**. If you earn ₹10,000+ — you are **Net Zero** (or even **Net Positive**).

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in NZB Design

- **Solar Irradiance (Peak Sun Hours – PSH) for Kerala:** **≈ 4.5 to 5.0 kWh/m²/day** (one of India's highest).
- **Monocrystalline PV Efficiency (η_pv):** **18% – 22%**.
- **Performance Ratio (PR) of PV system:** **0.75 – 0.85** (after temperature, dust, inverter, wiring losses).
- **Standard Test Conditions (STC):** **1000 W/m² irradiance, 25 °C cell temperature, AM 1.5 spectrum**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Philosophy: Reduce → Replace → Offset

A Net Zero Building is achieved through a **three-tier strategy hierarchy**:

### Tier 1: Energy Demand Reduction (Passive & Active Efficiency)
- **Passive design:** Building orientation, window-to-wall ratio (WWR) optimization, roof shading, cross-ventilation, day-lighting.
- **Envelope upgrades:** Insulated walls (U-value ≤ 0.35 W/m²K), double-glazed low-E windows, cool roofs (SRI ≥ 78).
- **HVAC efficiency:** VRF/VRV systems, Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs), chilled beams.
- **Lighting:** LED with lux-level task lighting, occupancy/daylight sensors.

### Tier 2: On-Site Renewable Energy Generation
- **Solar PV (most common in India).**
- **Building-Integrated Photovoltaics (BIPV)** – replaces façade/roof cladding.
- **Solar thermal** for water heating.
- **Small wind turbines** (limited application in urban Kerala).

### Tier 3: Off-Site Offset & Grid Interaction
- **Net Metering (India):** Export surplus solar to the grid, import at night → annual kWh balance.
- **Renewable Energy Certificates (RECs).**
- **PPA-based virtual net metering.**

## 2.2 The Core Energy Balance Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Master Equation of Net Zero:**
>
> $$E_{\text{annual,consumed}} - E_{\text{annual,generated}} = 0$$
>
> Equivalently: **Energy Imported = Energy Exported** over the boundary period.

More explicitly, for a building connected to the grid:

$$E_{\text{Net}} = (E_{\text{heating}} + E_{\text{cooling}} + E_{\text{lighting}} + E_{\text{plug-loads}} + E_{\text{water-heating}}) - (E_{\text{PV}} + E_{\text{wind}} + E_{\text{other-RE}})$$

If $E_{\text{Net}} = 0$ → **Net Zero**; if $E_{\text{Net}} < 0$ → **Net Positive (Plus)**; if $E_{\text{Net}} > 0$ → **Net Energy Deficit**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{\text{load}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{load}} \times t}{1000}$ | Annual electrical energy from a load | kWh |
| 2 | $E_{\text{PV}} = A_{\text{PV}} \times G \times \eta_{\text{PV}} \times PR$ | Annual energy from PV array | kWh/year |
| 3 | $A_{\text{PV}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{load,annual}}}{G \times \eta_{\text{PV}} \times PR}$ | Required PV array area | m² |
| 4 | $N_{\text{panels}} = \dfrac{A_{\text{PV}}}{A_{\text{panel}}}$ | Number of PV panels | — |
| 5 | $E_{\text{Embodied}} = \sum (\text{Material} \times \text{EEI})$ | Embodied energy of building | MJ or kWh |
| 6 | $E_{\text{Source}} = E_{\text{Site}} \times \text{SF}$ | Source energy (site × source factor) | kWh |
| 7 | $\text{EUI} = \dfrac{E_{\text{annual,total}}}{A_{\text{conditioned}}}$ | Energy Use Intensity | kWh/m²/year |
| 8 | $\text{CO}_2 = E_{\text{annual}} \times \text{EF}_{\text{grid}}$ | Operational CO₂ emissions | kgCO₂/year |
| 9 | $T_{\text{amort}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{Embodied,total}}}{E_{\text{annual,operational,saved}}}$ | Energy payback period | years |
| 10 | $Q_{\text{heat}} = U \times A \times \Delta T$ | Heat transfer through envelope | W or W/K |

Where:
- $G$ = Average daily solar irradiation on tilted surface (kWh/m²/day)
- $\eta_{\text{PV}}$ = PV module efficiency (dimensionless, e.g., 0.20)
- $PR$ = Performance Ratio (typically **0.75–0.80**)
- $A_{\text{panel}}$ = Area of one PV panel (e.g., 1.6 m² for a 330 W panel)
- $EF_{\text{grid}}$ = Grid emission factor for India (KERAM, Kerala ≈ **0.62 kgCO₂/kWh** as per CEA 2023 data)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Net Zero framework is the **de-facto benchmark** for the next generation of buildings in India and globally. Its utility spans:

1. **Regulatory compliance:** ECBC+ (SuperECBC), GRIHA, LEED Platinum, IGBC Net Zero Platinum ratings.
2. **Energy security:** Buildings become partially independent of grid failures.
3. **Carbon compliance:** Essential for meeting India's **Panchamrit** commitment at COP-26 — **500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030** and **net-zero by 2070**.
4. **Financial:** Long-term OPEX reduction; 25-year PPA for solar offsets utility inflation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Required PV Array Area for a Net Zero Building

### Problem Statement (Worked Example)

A residential building in **Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala** has an **annual electrical energy consumption of 12,000 kWh/year**. The owner wishes to install a rooftop solar PV system to make the building **Net Zero Energy**. Given the following data, determine the required **PV array area** and the **number of panels** (each rated 330 Wp, area 1.65 m²).

**Data:**
- Average daily Global Tilted Irradiance (GTI) at site, $G = 4.8 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{day}$
- PV module efficiency, $\eta_{\text{PV}} = 0.20$
- Performance Ratio of the system, $PR = 0.78$
- Panel area, $A_{\text{panel}} = 1.65 \text{ m}^2$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the annual energy generation formula:**

$$E_{\text{PV,annual}} = A_{\text{PV}} \times G_{\text{daily}} \times 365 \times \eta_{\text{PV}} \times PR$$

The total incident solar energy available per m² over a year is $G \times 365$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Net Zero condition:**

For a Net Zero Energy building, the annual generation must equal the annual load:

$$E_{\text{PV,annual}} = E_{\text{load,annual}}$$

$$A_{\text{PV}} \times G \times 365 \times \eta_{\text{PV}} \times PR = E_{\text{load,annual}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve algebraically for $A_{\text{PV}}$:**

$$A_{\text{PV}} = \frac{E_{\text{load,annual}}}{G \times 365 \times \eta_{\text{PV}} \times PR}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$A_{\text{PV}} = \frac{12{,}000}{4.8 \times 365 \times 0.20 \times 0.78}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the denominator:**

$$4.8 \times 365 = 1{,}752 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{year (incident)}$$

$$1{,}752 \times 0.20 = 350.4 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{year}$$

$$350.4 \times 0.78 = 273.312 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{year (electrical output)}$$

**Step 6 — Final division:**

$$A_{\text{PV}} = \frac{12{,}000}{273.312} = 43.91 \text{ m}^2$$

> **[!NOTE] Result:** The required rooftop area for the PV array is **≈ 43.91 m²**.

**Step 7 — Calculate the number of panels:**

$$N_{\text{panels}} = \frac{A_{\text{PV}}}{A_{\text{panel}}} = \frac{43.91}{1.65} = 26.61$$

**Step 8 — Round up to the nearest whole panel:**

$$\boxed{N_{\text{panels}} = 27 \text{ panels (rated 330 Wp each)}}$$

**Step 9 — Verification of installed capacity:**

$$P_{\text{installed}} = 27 \times 330 = 8{,}910 \text{ Wp} = 8.91 \text{ kWp}$$

**Step 10 — Verification of annual generation:**

$$E_{\text{gen}} = 8.91 \times 4.8 \times 0.78 = 33.36 \text{ kWh/day}$$

$$E_{\text{gen,annual}} = 33.36 \times 365 = 12{,}176 \text{ kWh/year}$$

> **Comment:** This exceeds the 12,000 kWh load by ~1.5%, confirming a robust Net Zero design. The 1.5% surplus is **advisable** to account for monsoon-season generation drop (June–August in Kerala).

## 3.2 Energy Use Intensity (EUI) Analysis

| Building Type | Typical EUI (India, kWh/m²/yr) | EUI Target for Net Zero |
|---|---|---|
| Conventional Office | 180 – 250 | 60 – 90 |
| Conventional Residential | 80 – 150 | 30 – 50 |
| GRIHA 5-Star | 90 – 120 | 40 – 60 |
| Passive House | 15 – 30 | 15 – 25 |
| Net Zero Office (BEST) | 40 – 60 | **≤ 40** |

> [!NOTE]
> The reduction in EUI is a **prerequisite** for Net Zero — you cannot generate your way to Net Zero with an inefficient envelope. A 50% load reduction is the typical minimum before PV sizing becomes practically feasible.

## 3.3 Operational vs Embodied Energy — Analytical Matrix

$$E_{\text{total,lifecycle}} = E_{\text{embodied}} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} E_{\text{operational},i}$$

For a **50-year life cycle** of a typical residential building:

- **Embodied energy (construction):** ~3,000 – 6,000 kWh/m² (one-time)
- **Operational energy (50 years):** ~4,000 – 10,000 kWh/m² (recurring)
- **Net Zero target reduces operational energy by 60–80%**, so 50-year operational falls to **~1,000 – 2,000 kWh/m²**.

This shows that **Net Zero Energy ≠ Net Zero Carbon** — the embodied carbon of cement, steel, and aluminium still matters, leading to the modern shift towards **Net Zero Carbon** accounting.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Net Zero Building — Energy Flow Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Energy Sources: Grid / Fossil Fuels] --> B[Energy Input to Building]
    B --> C[Energy Demand Block: HVAC Lighting Plug Loads Hot Water]
    C --> D[Building Envelope: Walls Roof Glazing Insulation]
    D --> E[Conditioned Indoor Space]
    E --> F[Energy Consumption Measured kWh/yr]
    H[On-Site Renewable Generation Block] --> I[PV Array on Rooftop 8.91 kWp]
    I --> J[Inverter DC to AC MPPT]
    J --> K[Bidirectional Net Meter]
    K --> L[Annual Energy Exported to Grid kWh/yr]
    F --> M[Energy Balance Calculator]
    L --> M
    M --> N{Is E_consumed equal to E_generated?}
    N --> O[Net Zero Energy Building]
    N --> P[Net Positive: Increase PV Area]
    N --> Q[Net Deficit: Reduce Loads or Add RE]
    M --> R[Annual CO2 Offset: 0.62 kgCO2 per kWh Saved]
    R --> S[Net Zero Carbon Verification]
```

**Figure 4.1 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Net Zero Energy Building.** The diagram shows the three primary energy streams: (1) external grid import, (2) building demand subsystem, and (3) on-site renewable generation feeding into a bidirectional net meter. The annual balance is computed, and depending on the result, the system is classified as Net Zero, Net Positive, or Net Deficit.

## 4.2 Net Zero Strategy Hierarchy (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Building Design] --> B[Step 1: Passive Design Strategy]
    B --> C[Step 2: High Performance Envelope]
    C --> D[Step 3: Efficient HVAC and Lighting]
    D --> E[Step 4: Reduce Annual Load by 50 percent minimum]
    E --> F[Step 5: Size On-Site PV System]
    F --> G[Step 6: Net Metering Agreement with KSEB]
    G --> H[Step 7: Annual Energy Balance Verification]
    H --> I{Is Annual Net Energy Zero?}
    I --> J[YES: Net Zero Energy Certified]
    I --> K[NO: Iterate Back to Step 2]
    J --> L[Step 8: Offset Embodied Carbon to Achieve Net Zero Carbon]
    L --> M[Final: Net Zero Building Operational]
```

**Figure 4.2 — Sequential Decision Tree for Achieving Net Zero Status.** This flow illustrates the KTU 2024 prescribed sequence: passive → efficiency → renewable generation → grid integration → verification → carbon offset.

## 4.3 Net Zero Classification Hierarchy (Concept Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Net Zero Building Family] --> B[Net Zero Energy NZE]
    A --> C[Net Zero Water NZW]
    A --> D[Net Zero Carbon NZC]
    A --> E[Net Zero Waste NZWaste]
    B --> B1[Site Net Zero]
    B --> B2[Source Net Zero]
```
