```mermaid
    B --> B3[Cost Net Zero]
    D --> D1[Operational Carbon]
    D --> D2[Embodied Carbon]
    D --> D3[Lifecycle Carbon]
    C --> C1[Rainwater Harvesting]
    C --> C2[Wastewater Recycling]
    C --> C3[Total Water Balance equals Zero]
```

**Figure 4.3 — Concept Map of the Net Zero Family.** A Net Zero Building is a superset that may include NZE, NZC, NZW, and Net Zero Waste simultaneously — true sustainability spans all four dimensions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Similar Pattern]**
**Define the term "Net Zero Energy Building". Differentiate it from a "Zero Energy Building".**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Net Zero Energy Building (NZEB)** is one that, over a defined boundary period (typically one year), generates an amount of renewable energy equal to the total amount of energy it consumes from external sources, resulting in a **net energy import of zero**.

A **Zero Energy Building** (theoretically, in the strictest sense) is a building that consumes **absolutely no external energy** — implying 100% on-site generation with **no grid connection at all**. In practice, this is rarely feasible, and the term is often used synonymously with NZEB. The key distinction is that NZEB allows **grid interaction (net metering)**, whereas a true Zero Energy Building does not.

> **Marking Tip (1 mark each):** Definition of NZEB • Boundary period mention • Difference from Zero Energy Building.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]**
**List any six (6) design strategies used to achieve Net Zero status in a building.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — list any 6):**
1. Passive solar building orientation and planning.
2. High-performance insulated building envelope (low U-value walls, double-glazed low-E windows).
3. Cool/green roofing with high Solar Reflectance Index (SRI ≥ 78).
4. High-efficiency HVAC systems (VRF, chillers with COP ≥ 4.5, free cooling).
5. LED lighting with daylight and occupancy sensors.
6. Rooftop solar PV system with net metering.
7. Rainwater harvesting and wastewater recycling.
8. Building Energy Management System (BEMS).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> When answering Part B questions on Net Zero Buildings, students **frequently lose 2–3 marks** by:
> (i) Writing the energy balance equation but **forgetting to multiply daily irradiance by 365 days** (a silly unit-conversion error).
> (ii) Ignoring the **Performance Ratio (PR)** in PV calculations — assuming 100% efficiency.
> (iii) Not specifying whether the building is **Site Net Zero, Source Net Zero, or Cost Net Zero** — always state the boundary explicitly.
> (iv) Confusing **Net Zero Energy** with **Net Zero Carbon** — these are different accounting frameworks.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **concept of Net Zero Buildings**. Discuss the various sub-categories (NZE, NZC, NZW) and the key design strategies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 120 m² residential building in **Kochi, Kerala** consumes **9,500 kWh/year** of electricity. The owner wants to install a rooftop solar PV system to make the building **Net Zero Energy**. Using the following data, determine the required **PV array area** and the **number of 330 Wp panels** (each of area 1.65 m²) needed:
Average daily GTI = **4.7 kWh/m²/day**, $\eta_{\text{PV}} = 0.19$, Performance Ratio = **0.76**.
Also calculate the **annual operational CO₂ emissions avoided**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Concept Explanation (7 marks):**

1. **Definition (2 marks):** A Net Zero Energy Building is a building that, over an annual cycle, balances the energy it consumes from external sources with the equivalent amount of renewable energy generated on-site or procured off-site. The key concept is **balance, not zero consumption**.

2. **Sub-categories (2 marks):**
   - **Net Zero Energy (NZE):** Total source/site energy consumed = total renewable energy generated.
   - **Net Zero Carbon (NZC):** Sum of operational + embodied CO₂ emissions is fully offset by renewable generation and/or carbon credits.
   - **Net Zero Water (NZW):** Total water consumed = total water harvested + recycled.

3. **Design Strategies (3 marks):**
   - **Passive design:** Orientation, WWR, shading, natural ventilation, day-lighting.
   - **Envelope efficiency:** U-value ≤ 0.35 W/m²K walls, double-glazed low-E glazing, cool roofs.
   - **System efficiency:** VRF HVAC, LED + sensor lighting, BEMS controls.
   - **On-site RE:** Rooftop/BIPV solar PV, solar thermal water heating.
   - **Grid integration:** Net metering (KSEB), bidirectional meter.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Write the formula for required PV area.

$$A_{\text{PV}} = \frac{E_{\text{load,annual}}}{G \times 365 \times \eta_{\text{PV}} \times PR}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$A_{\text{PV}} = \frac{9{,}500}{4.7 \times 365 \times 0.19 \times 0.76}$$

**Step 3:** Compute denominator.

$$4.7 \times 365 = 1{,}715.5 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{yr (incident)}$$

$$1{,}715.5 \times 0.19 = 325.945 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{yr}$$

$$325.945 \times 0.76 = 247.7182 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{yr (electrical output)}$$

**Step 4:** Final division.

$$A_{\text{PV}} = \frac{9{,}500}{247.7182} = 38.35 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5:** Number of panels.

$$N_{\text{panels}} = \frac{38.35}{1.65} = 23.24 \approx 24 \text{ panels (round up)}$$

**Step 6:** Annual CO₂ emissions avoided (using Kerala grid emission factor ≈ 0.62 kgCO₂/kWh).

$$\text{CO}_2\text{ avoided} = 9{,}500 \times 0.62 = 5{,}890 \text{ kgCO}_2/\text{year} = 5.89 \text{ tCO}_2/\text{year}$$

> **Final Answer:** $A_{\text{PV}} = 38.35 \text{ m}^2$, $N_{\text{panels}} = 24$, CO₂ avoided = **5.89 tCO₂/year**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **Net Zero Energy balance equation** in detail. Discuss the differences between **Site Net Zero, Source Net Zero, and Cost Net Zero**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A commercial office building in **Kozhikode, Kerala** has the following annual loads: Lighting = 8,000 kWh, HVAC = 22,000 kWh, Plug loads = 6,000 kWh, and Hot water heating (electric) = 4,000 kWh. Calculate the **total annual load** and the **Energy Use Intensity (EUI)** given the conditioned floor area is **300 m²**. If the building is to achieve Net Zero with PV panels of efficiency 0.20 and PR = 0.80, find the **required PV area** at GTI = **4.6 kWh/m²/day**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Energy Balance Equation (7 marks):**

**The Net Zero Energy Balance Equation:**

$$E_{\text{imported}} = E_{\text{exported}}$$

Or in expanded form:

$$\sum E_{\text{loads}} - \sum E_{\text{RE,generated}} = 0$$

Where $\sum E_{\text{loads}} = E_{\text{HVAC}} + E_{\text{lighting}} + E_{\text{plug}} + E_{\text{water heat}} + E_{\text{others}}$ and $\sum E_{\text{RE}} = E_{\text{PV}} + E_{\text{wind}} + E_{\text{solar-thermal-eq}}$.

**Three Definitions of Net Zero (3 marks):**

| Type | Definition | Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| **Site Net Zero** | Energy at building site-meter balance = 0 | Site boundary |
| **Source Net Zero** | Energy at primary source (before transmission losses) balance = 0 | National grid |
| **Cost Net Zero** | Rupee value of energy imported = rupee value of energy exported | Economic |

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Total annual load.

$$E_{\text{load,total}} = 8{,}000 + 22{,}000 + 6{,}000 + 4{,}000 = 40{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 2:** Energy Use Intensity (EUI).

$$\text{EUI} = \frac{E_{\text{load,total}}}{A_{\text{floor}}} = \frac{40{,}000}{300} = 133.33 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{year}$$

**Step 3:** Required PV area.

$$A_{\text{PV}} = \frac{40{,}000}{4.6 \times 365 \times 0.20 \times 0.80} = \frac{40{,}000}{268.64} = 148.90 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4:** Number of 330 Wp panels (1.65 m² each).

$$N = \frac{148.90}{1.65} \approx 91 \text{ panels (rounded up from 90.24)}$$

**Step 5:** Installed capacity.

$$P_{\text{inst}} = 91 \times 0.330 = 30.03 \text{ kWp}$$

> **Final Answer:** $E_{\text{load}} = 40{,}000 \text{ kWh/yr}$, $\text{EUI} = 133.33 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{yr}$, $A_{\text{PV}} = 148.90 \text{ m}^2$, $N = 91$ panels.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU Board Exam:**

- ✅ Net Zero = **Balance ≠ Zero consumption**; always evaluated over a **12-month boundary**.
- ✅ The master equation: **$E_{\text{consumed}} = E_{\text{generated}}$** (annual basis).
- ✅ Three types of NZ energy definitions: **Site, Source, Cost** — always specify the type in answers.
- ✅ Net Zero **Energy ≠ Net Zero Carbon** — embodied carbon must be separately accounted for.
- ✅ The **PV sizing formula** is the most-tested numerical in this topic:
  $$A_{\text{PV}} = \frac{E_{\text{load}}}{G \times 365 \times \eta_{\text{PV}} \times PR}$$
- ✅ Always use **Performance Ratio (PR = 0.75–0.80)** — never assume 100% system efficiency.
- ✅ Kerala's **daily GTI ≈ 4.5–5.0 kWh/m²/day** (one of the highest in India).
- ✅ Standard **emission factor** for Indian grid ≈ **0.62 kgCO₂/kWh** (use 0.70–0.75 if a generic value is given).
- ✅ **Energy Use Intensity (EUI)** is a key performance indicator; target for NZB: **≤ 40–60 kWh/m²/yr** for offices.
- ✅ **Three-step strategy:** Reduce demand → Replace with RE → Offset residual via grid/net metering.
- ✅ Kerala/KSEB has a robust **Net Metering policy (2018, amended 2023)** — the grid legally accepts surplus export.
- ✅ Net Zero Building is a **family**: NZE + NZC + NZW + Zero Waste can co-exist.
- ✅ **Embodied energy** of concrete (~1.0 MJ/kg) and steel (~25 MJ/kg) matters for lifecycle NZC accounting.
- ✅ Common **losses** in PV: temperature (~5–8%), dust (~2–5%), inverter (~3–4%), wiring (~1–2%) — all bundled in PR.

> **Golden Rule for KTU Board Answer:** *State the boundary, write the balance equation, show the PR, and convert daily to annual — these are the four most common markers' checks.* ✍️
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rainwater Harvesting Methods for Roof & Non-Roof
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET752 - SUSTAINABLE CONSTRUCTION PRACTICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rainwater Harvesting Methods for Roof & Non-Roof

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Rainwater Harvesting (RWH)** is the systematic collection, conveyance, storage, treatment, and utilization of rainwater that falls on a catchment surface (such as rooftops, paved areas, or open land) for the purpose of augmenting groundwater recharge, supplementing domestic/industrial water demand, and reducing stormwater runoff in urban and rural catchments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (PECET752 Module 3):**
> "Rainwater harvesting is a sustainable water management technique that intercepts and stores precipitation from roof and non-roof catchments, employing either **storage-type systems** (direct use) or **recharge-type systems** (groundwater augmentation), thereby reducing pressure on conventional freshwater sources and mitigating urban flooding."

The two principal classifications under the KTU 2024 PECET752 syllabus are:

| Classification | Catchment Type | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Roof-top Rainwater Harvesting (RRH)** | Building roofs, sheds, garages | Domestic use, groundwater recharge |
| **Non-Roof / Surface Runoff Harvesting** | Open land, pavements, roads, parks | Recharge pits, percolation tanks |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Bank Account of Water"**
>
> Imagine rainwater is **salary credited to a bank account** every month. A city without RWH is like someone who **spends every rupee the moment it arrives** and then borrows at high interest during dry months. Rainwater harvesting is like **opening a savings account**: the roof of your house is the *catchment surface* (your "earnings zone"), the gutters and downpipes are the *conveyor belts* carrying the money to the bank, the storage tank or recharge well is the *locker* where it is safely deposited, and the dry summer is the *rainy day* (literally!) when you withdraw from your saved reserves.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics in Rainwater Harvesting

The following quantitative metrics are mandated in KTU 2024 evaluation:

- **Rainfall Intensity (I)** — measured in **mm/hr** or **mm/day**
- **Catchment Area (A)** — measured in **m²**
- **Runoff Coefficient (C)** — dimensionless fraction (0.6–0.9 for roofs; 0.5–0.7 for paved surfaces)
- **Coefficient of Evaporation (K)** — typically **0.85–0.9** for hot climates
- **Annual Rainfall (P)** — measured in **mm/year**
- **Storage Capacity (V)** — measured in **litres** or **m³**

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Exams:** *"CATCH the water — C × A × P × K = Volume"*. Remember this 4-letter acronym (CATCH) for any numerical RWH problem.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Roof-Top Rainwater Harvesting (RRH)

A typical **Roof-Top RWH System** operates through the following structured sequence:

1. **Catchment Surface (Roof Slab):** The sloped RCC/asbestos/galvanized iron roof acts as the primary collection surface. The roof slope is kept between **1:100 to 1:50** for optimal drainage.
2. **Gutters (Conveyance Channel 1):** Horizontal channels fixed along the roof eave collect sheet flow. Typical gutter material: **PVC, GI sheet, or RCC**. Cross-section: rectangular (100 mm × 75 mm) or semi-circular (150 mm diameter).
3. **Downpipes (Vertical Conveyance):** Vertical pipes (75–100 mm diameter) transport water from the gutter to ground level. Spacing: every 6–8 m of gutter length.
4. **First-Flush Diverter (FFD):** A critical device that **diverts the first 2–5 mm of rainfall** to waste, removing dust, bird droppings, leaf debris, and atmospheric pollutants that accumulate on the roof during dry periods.
5. **Filtration Unit:** Comprises **sand-gravel-charcoal filter** (slow sand filter) or **mesh filter** to remove suspended impurities.
6. **Storage Tank / Recharge Structure:** Either an overhead/underground storage tank (storage system) OR a recharge pit/recharge well/recharge trench (recharge system).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the First-Flush Diverter Important?**
> The first few minutes of rain wash off contaminants accumulated on the roof surface during the dry spell. Skipping the FFD results in **BOD load and microbial contamination** of the stored water, making it unsuitable even for non-potable use without expensive treatment.

## 2.2 Operational Logic of Non-Roof / Surface Runoff Harvesting

Non-roof harvesting deals with runoff from **larger catchment areas** such as:

- Open grounds and parks
- Roads, parking lots, and pavements
- Agricultural fields
- Hill slopes and catchment watersheds

The systems employed are:

| Structure Type | Function | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Recharge Pit** | Directs runoff to a pit filled with boulders, gravel, sand | Recharging shallow aquifers |
| **Recharge Trench** | Linear trench (1–2 m deep, 1–1.5 m wide) along roadside | Capturing road runoff |
| **Recharge Well / Injection Well** | 6–10 m deep bore well back-filled with gravel pack | Recharging deeper aquifers |
| **Percolation Tank** | Small earthen dam across a stream | Recharging through stream bed |
| **Check Dam / Gabion Structure** | Small barrier across gullies | Reducing runoff velocity, increasing infiltration |
| **Injection Well with Recharge Shaft** | Vertical shaft connected to a borewell | Direct aquifer recharge in urban areas |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are the EXACT equations tested in KTU ESE and Module Internal assessments. Memorize all.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = C \times I \times A$ | **Rational Method** — Peak runoff from any catchment | m³/s, m³/hr, or L/s |
| 2 | $V = C \times A \times P \times K$ | **Volume of Water Harvested** annually | Litres (L) |
| 3 | $V_{tank} = \dfrac{D \times N \times T}{1000}$ | **Storage Tank Volume** for domestic use | m³ |
| 4 | $A_{c} = \dfrac{\text{Built-up Area}}{\text{Total Plot Area}}$ | **Catchment Efficiency Ratio** | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $T_{f} = \dfrac{V_{first}}{A \times 2\,\text{mm}}$ | **First-Flush Volume** diverted | mm of rainfall |
| 6 | $A_{filter} = \dfrac{Q_{design}}{v_{filtration}}$ | **Required Filter Area** | m² |
| 7 | $h_{loss} = \dfrac{1.5 \times v^2}{2g}$ | **Head loss through gutter** (Darcy-Weisbach simplified) | m |
| 8 | $Q_{gutter} = \dfrac{1}{n} \times A_{cs} \times R^{2/3} \times S^{1/2}$ | **Manning's Equation** for gutter flow capacity | m³/s |
| 9 | $A_{recharge\text{-}pit} = \dfrac{V_{annual}}{N_{rainy\text{-}days} \times k_{soil}}$ | **Recharge Pit Surface Area** | m² |
| 10 | $\text{Yield \%} = \dfrac{V_{collected}}{V_{rainfall\text{-}potential}} \times 100$ | **System Efficiency** | % |

Where the symbols denote:
- $C$ = Runoff coefficient (dimensionless)
- $I$ = Rainfall intensity
- $A$ = Catchment area in m²
- $P$ = Annual rainfall in mm
- $K$ = Evaporation/conveyance loss factor (**0.85** standard)
- $D$ = Daily per-capita demand in litres
- $N$ = Number of occupants
- $T$ = Dry days between consecutive rains
- $v_{filtration}$ = Sand filter velocity (**0.1–0.3 m/hr**)
- $k_{soil}$ = Soil infiltration capacity (**mm/hr**)
- $n$ = Manning's roughness coefficient
- $R$ = Hydraulic radius
- $S$ = Bed slope

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall #1:** Students often forget to **convert mm to litres**. Remember: 1 mm of rainfall over 1 m² = **1 litre of water**. So always write $P$ in mm and $A$ in m² to get $V$ directly in litres.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Roof-top RWH systems in **Kerala (KTU region)** are particularly valuable because:

- Kerala receives an annual rainfall of **~3000 mm** (one of the highest in India)
- Post-flood (2018, 2019) guidelines by **Kerala Suchitwa Mission** and **LSGD** mandate RWH for buildings exceeding **1500 m²** built-up area
- **CGWB (Central Ground Water Board)** has published district-wise groundwater recharge manuals
- The **Smart City Mission** in Kochi and Trivandrum deploys RWH in all public infrastructure

Non-roof harvesting structures are predominantly used in **Kerala's laterite terrain** to address the steep gradient runoff and rapid soil erosion issues.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Volume of Harvested Water

The **fundamental RWH equation** is derived from first principles in the steps below.

### Derivation Objective
To derive the expression for the **annual volume of rainwater** that can be harvested from a given catchment.

### Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1 — Rainfall Depth to Volume Conversion**

Consider a rectangular catchment of dimensions $L \times B$ metres. Let the annual rainfall depth be $P$ millimetres.

The volume of water falling on this catchment in one year is given by the volume of a rectangular slab of water of thickness $P$ mm spread over area $A$ m².

First, we convert $P$ from millimetres to metres:
$$P_{(m)} = \dfrac{P_{(mm)}}{1000}$$

Now the rainfall volume over area $A$ m² is:
$$V_{rain} = A \times P_{(m)} = A \times \dfrac{P}{1000} \quad \text{(in m³)}$$

To convert m³ to litres, we use the conversion: $1 \text{ m}^3 = 1000 \text{ L}$.
$$V_{rain} = A \times \dfrac{P}{1000} \times 1000 = A \times P \quad \text{(in Litres)}$$

This is why **1 mm of rain on 1 m² = 1 litre** — a fact the KTU examiner loves to test.

**Step 2 — Introducing the Runoff Coefficient (C)**

In practice, **not all rain that falls can be collected**. A fraction is lost to:

- **Evaporation** from the wet roof surface during rainfall
- **Wetting losses** — initial absorption by dry roof materials
- **Wind drift** — fine droplets blown off the catchment edge
- **Conveyance losses** — leakage and overflow in gutters

This net collectable fraction is denoted by the **runoff coefficient $C$**, defined as:
$$C = \dfrac{\text{Volume of water actually reaching the storage}}{\text{Volume of rainfall on catchment}}$$

The volume reaching the storage system is therefore:
$$V_{collected} = C \times A \times P \quad \text{(Litres)}$$

**Step 3 — Introducing the Conveyance/Efficiency Factor (K)**

Beyond the roof, additional losses occur due to:
- Leakage in pipes
- Spillage in first-flush diversion
- Evaporation from open storage tanks
- Filter backwash losses

A final efficiency factor $K$ (typically **0.85** for tropical Kerala climate) is applied:
$$V_{net} = C \times A \times P \times K \quad \text{(Litres)}$$

This is the **Master RWH Equation** demanded in KTU exams.

**Step 4 — Expanded Form with Substitutions**

Substituting $K = 0.85$ explicitly:
$$V_{net} = 0.85 \times C \times A \times P \quad \text{(Litres/year)}$$

For a roof, the standard runoff coefficient is $C = 0.8$ (impermeable surface, smooth slope, no cracks):
$$V_{net,\,roof} = 0.85 \times 0.8 \times A \times P = 0.68 \times A \times P$$

For a paved/non-roof surface, $C = 0.6$ (more texture, joints, partial permeability):
$$V_{net,\,non\text{-}roof} = 0.85 \times 0.6 \times A \times P = 0.51 \times A \times P$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Format)

**Problem Statement:**
A residential building in Thrissur (Kerala) has a roof area of $A = 120 \text{ m}^2$. The annual rainfall is $P = 2800 \text{ mm}$. The runoff coefficient for the RCC roof is $C = 0.8$ and the conveyance efficiency factor is $K = 0.85$.

**Calculate:**
(a) The total annual volume of rainwater that can be harvested in litres and in cubic metres.
(b) The required storage tank volume if there are **5 occupants**, daily per-capita demand is **135 L**, and the longest dry period is **90 days**.

### Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1 — Apply the master RWH equation:**
$$V_{net} = C \times A \times P \times K$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**
$$V_{net} = 0.8 \times 120 \times 2800 \times 0.85$$

**Step 3 — Perform the multiplication progressively:**
$$0.8 \times 120 = 96$$
$$96 \times 2800 = 268{,}800$$
$$268{,}800 \times 0.85 = 228{,}480 \text{ L/year}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to cubic metres (divide by 1000):**
$$V_{net} = \dfrac{228{,}480}{1000} = 228.48 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}$$

> **Answer (a):** The total annual harvestable volume is **228,480 litres** or **228.48 m³**.

### Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Apply the storage tank volume formula:**
$$V_{tank} = \dfrac{D \times N \times T}{1000}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the parameters:**
- $D = 135$ L/day (per capita demand)
- $N = 5$ occupants
- $T = 90$ days (longest dry period)

**Step 3 — Substitute and compute:**
$$D \times N = 135 \times 5 = 675 \text{ L/day (household demand)}$$
$$D \times N \times T = 675 \times 90 = 60{,}750 \text{ L}$$
$$V_{tank} = \dfrac{60{,}750}{1000} = 60.75 \text{ m}^3$$

> **Answer (b):** The required storage tank capacity is **60.75 m³** (≈ 60,750 litres).

### Analytical Verification

We must confirm that **available water ≥ required storage**:
$$228{,}480 \text{ L (available)} \gg 60{,}750 \text{ L (required)}$$

The annual harvest **fully covers** the 90-day storage demand with surplus of **167,730 L** to spare for other uses (e.g., gardening, vehicle washing, recharge).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Marking Insight:** When this type of numerical appears, marks are awarded for: (i) the **correct formula** (3 marks), (ii) **correct substitution** (2 marks), (iii) **unit consistency** (1 mark), (iv) **correct arithmetic** (1 mark). Forgetting to write the formula explicitly costs you 3 marks even if the final answer is right.

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Roof vs. Non-Roof Harvesting

| Parameter | Roof-Top RWH | Non-Roof / Surface RWH |
|---|---|---|
| **Catchment Area** | Limited to roof projection | Large (parks, roads, open land) |
| **Runoff Coefficient (C)** | 0.8–0.9 (impervious) | 0.5–0.7 (semi-pervious) |
| **Water Quality** | Relatively clean | Heavily contaminated (oil, silt) |
| **First-Flush Treatment** | Mandatory | Strongly recommended |
| **Primary Use** | Potable (with treatment) / non-potable | Recharge only, rarely direct use |
| **Storage Type** | Tanks (overhead/underground) | Recharge pits, wells, percolation tanks |
| **Cost (per m³ stored)** | High | Low |
| **Maintenance Frequency** | Quarterly cleaning | Biannual desilting |
| **KTU Exam Weightage** | High (70%) | Moderate (30%) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Complete Roof-Top RWH System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[R01 Roof Catchment Surface] --> B[R02 Gutter Horizontal Conveyance]
    B --> C[R03 Downpipe Vertical Drop]
    C --> D[R04 First Flush Diverter FFD]
    D --> E[R05 Mesh Filter 5mm Screen]
    E --> F[R06 Sand Gravel Charcoal Slow Filter]
    F --> G{R07 Decision Storage or Recharge}
    G --> H[R08A Overhead Tank Domestic Use]
    G --> I[R08B Underground Tank Bulk Storage]
    G --> J[R08C Recharge Pit Shallow Aquifer]
    G --> K[R08D Recharge Well Deep Aquifer]
    H --> L[R09 End Use Potable and Non Potable]
    I --> L
    J --> M[R10 Aquifer Recharge Groundwater]
    K --> M
```

**Explanation of Node Sequence:**
The rainwater undergoes a linear pipeline of treatment stages, with a critical decision point at **R07** determining whether the water is destined for **direct consumption** (storage branches) or **groundwater augmentation** (recharge branches).

## 4.2 System Architecture — Non-Roof Surface Runoff Harvesting

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[NR01 Open Land Catchment] --> B[NR02 Surface Runoff Flow]
    C[NR03 Road Pavement] --> B
    D[NR04 Park and Garden] --> B
    B --> E[NR05 Catch Drain or Desilting Basin]
    E --> F[NR06 Trash and Debris Screen]
    F --> G[NR07 Settling Chamber Silt Removal]
    G --> H[NR08 Recharge Selection]
    H --> I[NR09A Recharge Trench along Road]
    H --> J[NR09B Percolation Tank in Stream Bed]
    H --> K[NR09C Check Dam across Gully]
    H --> L[NR09D Injection Well with Recharge Shaft]
    I --> M[NR10 Shallow Aquifer Recharge]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> N[NR11 Deep Aquifer Recharge]
```

**Explanation:**
The non-roof architecture must incorporate a **desilting basin (NR07)** as a priority, because surface runoff carries significantly higher particulate load than roof runoff. The four recharge structures at NR09 represent the principal **structural alternatives** listed in the KTU syllabus.

## 4.3 Cross-Section Schematic — Recharge Pit Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[L01 Top Layer Turf Grass Surface]
    L2[L02 Second Layer Coarse Sand 300mm]
    L3[L03 Third Layer Gravel 20mm 400mm]
    L4[L04 Fourth Layer Boulders 50mm 500mm]
    L5[L05 Bottom Recharge Pit Floor Soil Infiltration Zone]
    S1[S01 Inlet Pipe 100mm dia from Collection Tank]
    S2[S02 Overflow Pipe 50mm dia to Next Recharge Structure]
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
    S1 -.-> L2
    L4 --> S2
```

**Layer-by-Layer Specification (KTU Standard):**

| Layer (top → bottom) | Material | Thickness | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| Top | Turf / grass | 50–100 mm | Erosion control, UV protection |
| Second | Coarse sand | 300 mm | Slow filtration, fine particle capture |
| Third | Gravel (20 mm) | 400 mm | Medium filtration, aeration |
| Fourth | Boulders (50 mm) | 500 mm | Structural support, primary filtration |
| Bottom | Native soil | Variable | Final infiltration into aquifer |

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Selecting Roof vs. Non-Roof Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Q1 What is the primary water demand]
    Q --> R{Is demand for direct domestic use}
    R -->|Yes| S{Small or large plot}
    S -->|Small plot less than 500 sqm| T[Choose Roof Top RWH with Storage Tank]
    S -->|Large plot more than 500 sqm| U[Choose Roof Top RWH plus Recharge Well]
    R -->|No recharge only| V{Is catchment open land or paved}
    V -->|Open land with slope less than 5 percent| W[Use Recharge Trench and Percolation Tank]
    V -->|Paved urban area high runoff| X[Use Recharge Pit and Injection Well]
    V -->|Stream or gully with seasonal flow| Y[Use Check Dam and Gabion Structure]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Reference Rule:**
> - **Roof RWH** → Water is **clean enough** to use directly.
> - **Non-Roof RWH** → Water is **too dirty** to use; therefore **recharge only**.
> - **In urban Kerala** with limited open space, roof-top with recharge well is the **default KTU-recommended** configuration.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define rainwater harvesting. List any four advantages of roof-top rainwater harvesting.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Rainwater harvesting is the technique of collecting, storing, and utilizing rainwater that falls on roof and non-roof surfaces for domestic use or groundwater recharge, thereby reducing dependency on conventional water sources.

**Four Advantages (0.375 each = 1.5 marks):**
1. **Reduces water bills** and dependency on municipal supply.
2. **Recharges groundwater** and raises the local water table.
3. **Reduces urban flooding** by intercepting stormwater runoff.
4. **Provides high-quality water** with low TDS, ideal for washing, flushing, and gardening.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Explain the function of a First-Flush Diverter (FFD) in a roof-top rainwater harvesting system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **First-Flush Diverter (FFD)** is a device installed at the entry of the downpipe that automatically **diverts the initial 2–5 mm of rainfall** to a drainage system, bypassing the storage tank. Its functions are:

1. **Removes contaminants** like dust, bird droppings, leaves, and atmospheric pollutants accumulated on the roof during the dry period. (1.5 marks)
2. **Improves water quality** of stored water by reducing BOD, turbidity, and microbial load. (0.75 marks)
3. **Reduces filter maintenance frequency** by preventing rapid clogging of the sand filter. (0.75 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> A simple FFD can be made using a **ball-and-chain mechanism** or a **manual valve** at the bottom of a vertical standing pipe of length 1–1.5 m.

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice]`
**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the components of a **roof-top rainwater harvesting system**. Explain the role of each component in the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A residential building in Kochi has a roof area of **150 m²**. The annual rainfall is **3200 mm**. The runoff coefficient is **0.85** and the conveyance factor is **0.80**. Calculate:
- (i) The annual volume of water harvested in **litres**.
- (ii) The annual volume in **cubic metres**.
- (iii) The **storage tank capacity** required for 4 occupants with a per-capita demand of 150 L/day and a dry period of 75 days. **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL ANSWER — Question A

**Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The components of a roof-top RWH system (with reference to the diagram in Section 4.1) are:

| Component | Function | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| **Roof Catchment (R01)** | Sloped impermeable surface (RCC/Metal) that intercepts rain; provides the primary collection zone | 1.0 |
| **Gutters (R02)** | Horizontal channels at roof edge that collect sheet flow from the roof; guide water to downpipes | 0.5 |
| **Downpipes (R03)** | Vertical pipes (75–100 mm) that convey water from gutter to ground; 6–8 m spacing | 0.5 |
| **First-Flush Diverter (R04)** | Diverts the first 2–5 mm of rainfall to remove dust, debris, and contaminants | 1.0 |
| **Mesh Filter (R05)** | Removes leaves and large particles (>5 mm) | 0.5 |
| **Sand-Gravel Filter (R06)** | Slow sand filter (0.1–0.3 m/hr filtration velocity) for fine suspended particles | 1.0 |
| **Storage/Recharge (R08)** | Overhead tank, underground tank, recharge pit, or recharge well | 1.5 |
| **Distribution (R09)** | Plumbing network for end-use to taps, washing machines, gardens | 1.0 |

**Total: 7 marks**

**Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Part (b)(i) — Annual Volume in Litres**

Apply the master equation:
$$V_{net} = C \times A \times P \times K$$

Substitute the values ($C = 0.85$, $A = 150 \text{ m}^2$, $P = 3200 \text{ mm}$, $K = 0.80$):
$$V_{net} = 0.85 \times 150 \times 3200 \times 0.80$$

Compute step by step:
$$0.85 \times 150 = 127.5$$
$$127.5 \times 3200 = 408{,}000$$
$$408{,}000 \times 0.80 = 326{,}400 \text{ L}$$

> **Answer (b)(i):** **3,26,400 litres/year** (1 mark for formula + 1 mark for calculation + 0.5 for unit)

**Part (b)(ii) — Annual Volume in m³**

Convert litres to m³ (divide by 1000):
$$V_{net} = \dfrac{3{,}26{,}400}{1000} = 326.4 \text{ m}^3$$

> **Answer (b)(ii):** **326.4 m³/year** (0.5 mark for conversion + 0.5 for answer)

**Part (b)(iii) — Storage Tank Capacity**

Apply the storage formula:
$$V_{tank} = \dfrac{D \times N \times T}{1000}$$

Substitute ($D = 150$ L/day, $N = 4$ occupants, $T = 75$ days):
$$D \times N = 150 \times 4 = 600 \text{ L/day}$$
$$D \times N \times T = 600 \times 75 = 45{,}000 \text{ L}$$
$$V_{tank} = \dfrac{45{,}000}{1000} = 45 \text{ m}^3$$

> **Answer (b)(iii):** **45 m³** (1 mark for formula + 1 mark for calculation)

**Total Part (b): 7 marks**

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3 Internal Choice]`
**(a)** What are the different **non-roof rainwater harvesting structures**? Describe any **three** of them with neat sketches, indicating their typical dimensions and applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **storage-type** and **recharge-type** RWH systems. Under what site conditions is each preferred? **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL ANSWER — Question B

**Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The non-roof rainwater harvesting structures are: (1) Recharge Pit, (2) Recharge Trench, (3) Recharge Well / Injection Well, (4) Percolation Tank, (5) Check Dam, and (6) Recharge Shaft.

**Structure 1: Recharge Pit (2.5 marks)**

A recharge pit is a vertical excavation (typically **1.5–3.0 m deep** and **1.0–2.0 m wide**) filled with layers of boulders, gravel, and sand. The runoff water is diverted from the catchment through a pipe into the pit, where it infiltrates downward to recharge the shallow aquifer.

- **Dimensions:** 1.5–3.0 m depth, 1.0–2.0 m diameter
- **Application:** Urban areas with limited open space; recharge of shallow aquifer
- **Layered cross-section (top to bottom):** Turf → Sand (300 mm) → Gravel (400 mm) → Boulders (500 mm)

**Structure 2: Recharge Trench (2.5 marks)**

A linear trench constructed along the side of a road or pavement, typically **0.5–1.5 m deep** and **0.5–1.0 m wide**, filled with boulders and gravel, and covered with a perforated cover.

- **Dimensions:** Length 5–15 m, depth 0.5–1.5 m, width 0.5–1.0 m
- **Application:** Capturing road/street runoff in urban layouts
- **Mark 1 for dimensions + 0.5 for layer description + 1 for application**

**Structure 3: Percolation Tank (2 marks)**

A small earthen embankment constructed across a stream or nallah bed to hold back water and allow it to percolate through the stream bed into the ground.

- **Dimensions:** 3–6 m high embankment, 50–500 m length
- **Application:** Rural catchments, degraded watersheds
- **Mark 1 for description + 0.5 for dimensions + 0.5 for application**

**Total Part (a): 7 marks**

**Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks:**

| Parameter | Storage-Type RWH | Recharge-Type RWH |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Function** | Store rainwater in tanks for direct use | Recharge groundwater through pits/wells |
| **Water Destination** | Storage tank → household plumbing | Reaches aquifer → pumped later via borewell |
| **End-Use** | Direct domestic, gardening, washing | Augments existing borewell/municipal supply |
| **Capital Cost** | Higher (tank + plumbing) | Lower (pit + pipe) |
| **Maintenance** | Frequent tank cleaning | Periodic desilting of recharge structure |
| **Water Quality** | Treatment required for potable use | No treatment needed; natural filtration by soil |
| **Suitable Site** | Urban plots with rooftop, low groundwater table | Areas with declining water table, large open land |
| **Recovery Time** | Immediate (after installation) | Delayed (reaches aquifer over weeks/months) |

**Marking Distribution (b):**
- 1 mark for clear definition of both types
- 2 marks for comparative table (8 rows × 0.25 mark)
- 2 marks for site condition discussion of storage-type
- 2 marks for site condition discussion of recharge-type

**Site Conditions:**

**Storage-type preferred when:** (1) groundwater table is **deep** (no immediate recharge benefit), (2) the building is **urban** with limited open space for recharge structures, (3) **immediate non-potable water demand** exists for flushing, gardening, and washing.

**Recharge-type preferred when:** (1) the groundwater table is **declining rapidly** (e.g., Kochi, Palakkad districts), (2) **large open space** is available in the plot, (3) the soil has **good permeability** (sandy/laterite formations typical of Kerala), (4) existing borewells are **underperforming** and need augmentation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the unit conversions** in numerical problems (mm ↔ m ↔ litres). The KTU examiner deducts **1 full mark** for each inconsistent unit.
> 2. **Using runoff coefficient = 1** instead of the realistic value (0.8–0.9). This is a common error that makes the answer **inaccurate by 10–20%**, leading to loss of 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing storage-type with recharge-type** in descriptive answers. The KTU 2024 marking scheme allocates **2 marks for clear distinction** between the two.
> 4. **Omitting the First-Flush Diverter** in roof-top component diagrams. FFD is **mandatory** in KTU-evaluated sketches and carries **1 full mark** on its own.
> 5. **Not converting mm to litres** in the annual volume formula. Many students write $A \times P$ without $C$ and $K$ and claim the answer in m³ — this costs **3 marks**.
> 6. **Writing 14-mark answers in less than 3 pages** — KTU expects a full **8–10 page answer** with diagrams, tables, and 2–3 paragraphs per sub-question.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU PECET752 Module 3**

- ✅ **Master RWH Equation:** $V = C \times A \times P \times K$ (litres) — remember the 4 variables.
- ✅ **Key Conversion:** 1 mm rain on 1 m² = **1 litre of water**.
- ✅ **Runoff Coefficient (C):** Roof = **0.8–0.9**, Paved = **0.5–0.7**, Bare soil = **0.3–0.4**.
- ✅ **Efficiency Factor (K):** **0.85** for tropical climate like Kerala.
- ✅ **First-Flush Diverter** is **mandatory** in all KTU-evaluated roof-top designs.
- ✅ **Sand filter velocity:** **0.1–0.3 m/hr** (slow sand filtration standard).
- ✅ **Storage tank formula:** $V_{tank} = D \times N \times T / 1000$ (m³).
- ✅ **Gutter spacing:** downpipes every **6–8 metres** of gutter length.
- ✅ **Gutter material:** PVC, GI sheet, RCC, or asbestos (never wooden, never plain iron).
- ✅ **Non-roof structures:** 6 types — Recharge Pit, Trench, Well, Percolation Tank, Check Dam, Shaft.
- ✅ **Recharge Pit depth:** **1.5–3.0 m**; Recharge Well depth: **6–10 m**.
- ✅ **Storage-type** is for **direct use**; **Recharge-type** is for **groundwater augmentation**.
- ✅ **Manning's roughness (n):** PVC pipe ≈ **0.009**, RCC drain ≈ **0.013**, Earthen channel ≈ **0.025**.
- ✅ **Kerala-specific:** LSGD mandates RWH for buildings > **1500 m²** built-up area.
- ✅ **CGWB** = Central Ground Water Board (issuing authority for RWH guidelines in India).
- ✅ **Kerala annual rainfall** ≈ **3000 mm** (one of the highest in India, makes RWH highly viable).
- ✅ **Three-tier filter layers** in recharge pit: Sand (300 mm) → Gravel (400 mm) → Boulders (500 mm).
- ✅ **Per-capita domestic water demand (Kerala standard):** **135–150 L/day** (uses 2024 NBC).
- ✅ **Dry period (T)** = longest rainless period in the local climatic record (typically **60–90 days** in Kerala).
- ✅ **Cost-effectiveness:** Non-roof RWH ≈ ₹500–1000 per m³ of recharge capacity; Roof RWH ≈ ₹2000–5000 per m³ of storage.
- ✅ **Critical KTU 2024 syllabus terminologies:** "Storage-type system", "Recharge-type system", "First-flush diverter", "Catchment coefficient", "Rational method".

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Rainwater Harvesting Methods for Roof & Non-Roof**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reducing Landscape Water Demand by Proper Irrigation Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET752 - SUSTAINABLE CONSTRUCTION PRACTICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reducing Landscape Water Demand by Proper Irrigation Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Landscape Water Demand** is defined as the total volume of water required to sustain healthy growth of ornamental and functional vegetation (turf, shrubs, trees, ground covers) in a built environment, quantified in terms of **Evapotranspiration (ET)** losses, expressed typically in **millimeters per day (mm/day)** or **litres per square metre per day (L/m²/day)**.

**Proper Irrigation Systems** in sustainable construction refer to engineered water-delivery networks designed to match the **Application Efficiency (Ea)** of water to the **Reference Evapotranspiration (ETo)** and **Crop Coefficient (Kc)** of the planted species, thereby minimizing conveyance, evaporation, runoff, and deep percolation losses.

The governing principle is expressed by the **Landscape Water Requirement (LWR)** formula:

$$LWR = ETo \times Kc \times A \times \frac{1}{Ea}$$

Where:
- $ETo$ = Reference Evapotranspiration (mm/day)
- $Kc$ = Crop Coefficient (dimensionless)
- $A$ = Area irrigated (m²)
- $Ea$ = Application Efficiency (fraction, 0–1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 3):** Sustainable landscape design emphasizes *reducing*—not *supplying*—water demand. The hierarchy is: **1) Design (xeriscape/hydrozone) → 2) Soil improvement → 3) Mulching → 4) Efficient delivery system → 5) Smart scheduling**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **feeding a baby with a spoon** instead of **hosing the baby with a fire hydrant**. A conventional sprinkler on a lawn is like the fire hydrant — most of the water is wasted (on the driveway, evaporating into the air, or running off). A **drip irrigation system** is the spoon — it delivers a precise, measured amount of water exactly where the plant roots need it, when they need it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of a **bank account**. If your income (rainfall + irrigation supply) equals your expenses (ET losses), your balance (plant health) stays healthy. **Proper irrigation is a budgeting tool** — it ensures water is *spent* only on what is *needed*, not wasted on over-irrigation.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU-Exam Ready)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reference Evapotranspiration | $ETo$ | 2–8 mm/day | Atmospheric demand |
| Crop Coefficient | $Kc$ | 0.3 (drought tolerant) – 1.2 (turfgrass) | Plant-specific water use |
| Application Efficiency | $Ea$ | 0.60 (sprinkler) – 0.95 (drip) | System performance |
| Distribution Uniformity | $DU$ | 0.70 – 0.95 | Evenness of water spread |
| Soil Infiltration Rate | $f$ | 1–250 mm/hr | Soil's ability to absorb water |
| Field Capacity | $FC$ | 10–35% (volumetric) | Water held after drainage |
| Permanent Wilting Point | $PWP$ | 5–20% (volumetric) | Water unavailable to plants |
| Available Water Capacity | $AWC$ | $FC - PWP$ | Total plant-usable water |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Available Water Capacity (AWC)** is the "water tank" in the soil that plants draw from. **Irrigation scheduling** is essentially the art of refilling this tank *only* when the plant is about to exhaust its share — known as the **Management Allowed Depletion (MAD)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Water-Efficient Irrigation

A sustainable landscape water-management system rests on **five interconnected pillars**. Each pillar addresses a *different* type of loss in the water balance.

### Pillar 1: Demand Reduction (Xeriscaping)
- Select **drought-tolerant native species** with low $Kc$ values.
- Group plants by water need → **Hydrozoning**.
- Replace high-water turfgrass with **decomposed granite, mulch beds, or native grass**.
- Typical reduction: **30%–75%** in irrigation demand.

### Pillar 2: Soil Improvement
- **Amend clay soils** with compost to improve infiltration (avoid runoff).
- **Amend sandy soils** with organic matter to raise $AWC$.
- Target **infiltration rate** ($f$) between **10–25 mm/hr** to prevent both runoff and deep percolation.

### Pillar 3: Mulching
- Apply **50–100 mm** of organic mulch (bark, wood chips) on shrub/tree basins.
- Reduces surface **evaporation by 25%–70%**.
- Moderates soil temperature and suppresses weed competition (which would otherwise consume water).

### Pillar 4: High-Efficiency Delivery Systems
- **Drip / Trickle irrigation**: $Ea$ = **0.90–0.95**
- **Bubbler systems**: $Ea$ = **0.85–0.90**
- **Subsurface drip irrigation (SDI)**: $Ea$ = **0.95**
- **Rotary/stream sprinklers**: $Ea$ = **0.70–0.80**
- **Spray sprinklers**: $Ea$ = **0.60–0.70**
- **Flood/furrow**: $Ea$ = **0.40–0.60** (NOT recommended for sustainable landscaping)

### Pillar 5: Smart Scheduling & Controllers
- **ET-based controllers** adjust run times using real-time weather data.
- **Soil moisture sensor (SMS) controllers** start irrigation only when volumetric water content falls below a threshold (typically **50% MAD**).
- **Rain shut-off devices** prevent irrigation after measurable rainfall.
- Achieve **20%–50%** additional water savings over timer-based systems.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield, Board-Exam Essential)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value and conditional notations in formulas use `\vert` or `\mid` to maintain markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $ETc = ETo \times Kc$ | Crop Evapotranspiration (plant water need) | mm/day | Plant demand calculation |
| 2 | $LWR = \dfrac{ETc \times A}{Ea}$ | Landscape Water Requirement | L/day | Total system capacity |
| 3 | $NIR = \dfrac{ETc}{Ea}$ | Net Irrigation Requirement | mm/day | Field-scale design |
| 4 | $GIR = \dfrac{NIR}{CU}$ | Gross Irrigation Requirement | mm/day | Pump sizing |
| 5 | $DU = \dfrac{\bar{z}_{LQ}}{\bar{z}_{mean}}$ | Distribution Uniformity (Low-Quarter) | fraction (0–1) | System performance test |
| 6 | $CU = 1 - \dfrac{1.96 \times \sigma}{\bar{z}}$ | Christiansen's Coefficient of Uniformity | fraction (0–1) | Catch-can test analysis |
| 7 | $t = \dfrac{d \times 60}{P \times Ea}$ | Run time per irrigation event | minutes | Scheduling |
| 8 | $Q = \dfrac{A \times d}{t \times Ea}$ | System flow rate required | L/min | Pipe/valve sizing |
| 9 | $MAD = p \times AWC$ | Management Allowed Depletion | mm depth | Sensor set-point |
| 10 | $SF = \dfrac{Spacing_{lateral} \times Spacing_{emitter}}{Emitter\;flow}$ | System flow per unit area | L/m²/hr | Drip design check |

Where:
- $d$ = depth of water to apply (mm)
- $P$ = precipitation rate of emitter/sprinkler (mm/hr)
- $t$ = duration of irrigation (hours)
- $\bar{z}_{LQ}$ = average depth in the lowest 25% of catch-cans
- $\bar{z}_{mean}$ = average depth across all catch-cans
- $\sigma$ = standard deviation of catch-can depths
- $p$ = allowable depletion fraction (0.3–0.5 typical)

## 2.3 Engineering Utility & Real-World Significance

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU/PEC exam context:** Sustainable irrigation is a *cross-cutting topic* linking Module 1 (sustainable materials), Module 2 (energy), and Module 3 (water). Real-world applications include:
> - **IGBC / GRIHA / LEED v4.1** certification credits for "Water Efficient Landscaping" (LEED credit *WE Landscape Water Use Reduction*).
> - Municipal bylaws in Kerala (e.g., **Kerala Municipal Building Rules 2019**) mandating rainwater harvesting and greywater reuse for landscapes.
> - **Smart City Mission** projects using sensor-based irrigation in public parks.
> - **Mahindra World City** and **Infosys Mysore campus** — case studies of campus-scale sustainable irrigation.

## 2.4 The Concept of Hydrozoning (Detailed Logic)

**Hydrozoning** is the deliberate grouping of plants with *similar* water needs into distinct irrigation zones. The logic is:

1. **Identify plant water needs** → assign $Kc$ for each species.
2. **Group by similarity** → Low, Medium, High water-use zones.
3. **Design separate irrigation valves/lines** per zone.
4. **Match emitter type** to zone (drip for low, spray for high).
5. **Schedule independently** to avoid over-watering low-need plants.

This single step often yields **40%–60%** water savings with **no plant stress**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Converting ET-Based Demand into System Run-Time

**Problem statement:** A residential landscape in Trivandrum has a turf area of **200 m²**. The reference evapotranspiration $ETo$ for the design month (March) is **6.0 mm/day**. The crop coefficient for warm-season turfgrass is **$Kc$ = 0.80**. The irrigation system is a **rotary sprinkler** with an **application efficiency $Ea$ = 0.75** and a **precipitation rate $P$ = 12 mm/hr**. The **distribution uniformity $DU$ = 0.80**.

**Required:** (a) Net Irrigation Requirement, (b) Gross Irrigation Requirement, (c) Daily run time in minutes, (d) Total daily water volume in litres, (e) Comment on hydrozoning opportunity.

---

### Step 1: Calculate Crop Evapotranspiration (ETc)

$$ETc = ETo \times Kc$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ETc &= 6.0\ \tfrac{mm}{day} \times 0.80 \\
ETc &= 4.8\ \tfrac{mm}{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The turfgrass *needs* 4.8 mm of water per day to stay healthy, replacing what it loses through transpiration and soil evaporation.

---

### Step 2: Net Irrigation Requirement (NIR)

This is the *actual* depth of water that must reach the root zone, accounting for application efficiency:

$$NIR = \frac{ETc}{Ea}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
NIR &= \frac{4.8\ \tfrac{mm}{day}}{0.75} \\
NIR &= 6.4\ \tfrac{mm}{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why divide by $Ea$?** Because a sprinkler with 75% efficiency *loses* 25% to wind drift, evaporation, and runoff. So we must *apply* more than 4.8 mm to *deliver* 4.8 mm to the roots.

---

### Step 3: Gross Irrigation Requirement (GIR)

This is the depth the system must apply, **including correction for non-uniform distribution** (using $DU$ instead of $CU$):

$$GIR = \frac{NIR}{DU}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
GIR &= \frac{6.4\ \tfrac{mm}{day}}{0.80} \\
GIR &= 8.0\ \tfrac{mm}{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this extra correction?** Even with 75% efficient sprinklers, the *driest* 25% of the area receives less water than the average. To guarantee that **all** turf receives its required 6.4 mm, we must apply 8.0 mm at the sprinkler head (so the driest quarter still gets its share).

---

### Step 4: Daily Run Time (t)

The relationship between application depth, precipitation rate, and time is:

$$GIR = P \times t \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \frac{GIR}{P}$$

But we must convert units carefully. $GIR$ is in **mm/day**, $P$ is in **mm/hr**, so the result is in **hours/day**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{8.0\ \tfrac{mm}{day}}{12\ \tfrac{mm}{hr}} \\
t &= 0.667\ \tfrac{hr}{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to **minutes per day**:

$$t = 0.667 \times 60 = 40\ \tfrac{min}{day}$$

---

### Step 5: Total Daily Water Volume

The volume is the area times the gross depth (remember: 1 mm over 1 m² = 1 litre):

$$V = GIR \times A$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 8.0\ \tfrac{mm}{day} \times 200\ m^2 \\
V &= 1600\ \tfrac{L}{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick check using efficiency:** The plant needs $ETc \times A = 4.8 \times 200 = 960$ L/day. The system delivers 1600 L/day. The "wasted" 640 L (40%) is the price of inefficiency — replaced by more drip and SDI systems in sustainable design.

---

### Step 6: Analytical Matrix — Comparing System Choices

Let us hold $ETo = 6.0$ mm/day, $Kc = 0.80$, $A = 200$ m² constant, and evaluate three system alternatives:

| Parameter | Spray Sprinkler | Rotary Sprinkler | Drip Irrigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Application Efficiency $Ea$ | 0.65 | 0.75 | 0.92 |
| Distribution Uniformity $DU$ | 0.70 | 0.80 | 0.90 |
| NIR (mm/day) | 7.38 | 6.40 | 5.22 |
| GIR (mm/day) | 10.55 | 8.00 | 5.80 |
| Daily Volume (L/day) | 2,110 | 1,600 | 1,160 |
| **Annual Volume (kL)** | **770.1** | **584.0** | **423.4** |
| **Savings vs. Spray** | Baseline | **24.2%** | **45.0%** |
| Suitable for turf? | Yes | Yes | Marginal |
| Suitable for shrubs/trees? | No (wasteful) | No (wasteful) | Ideal |

**Annual calculation (for 365 days):**
- Spray: $2110 \times 365 = 770{,}150\ L = 770.1\ kL$
- Rotary: $1600 \times 365 = 584{,}000\ L = 584.0\ kL$
- Drip: $1160 \times 365 = 423{,}400\ L = 423.4\ kL$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The annual water saving by switching from a spray sprinkler to a drip system on a 200 m² landscape is ~347 kL/year — enough to supply ~2 average Indian households for a year.** This is the kind of number KTU examiners love in a 14-mark question.

## 3.2 Derivation: Management Allowed Depletion (MAD) and Sensor Set-Point

The **Available Water Capacity (AWC)** of a soil is:

$$AWC = (FC - PWP) \times \rho_b \times d_{root}$$

Where:
- $FC$ = Field Capacity (% by volume)
- $PWP$ = Permanent Wilting Point (% by volume)
- $\rho_b$ = Bulk density of soil (g/cm³, often 1.0–1.6)
- $d_{root}$ = Effective root depth (mm)

**Example:** For a loamy soil with $FC = 28\%$, $PWP = 12\%$, $\rho_b = 1.3$, $d_{root} = 300$ mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
AWC &= (0.28 - 0.12) \times 1.3 \times 300 \\
AWC &= 0.16 \times 1.3 \times 300 \\
AWC &= 62.4\ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

For a drought-tolerant shrub with allowable depletion $p = 0.5$:

$$MAD = p \times AWC = 0.5 \times 62.4 = 31.2\ mm$$

**Sensor set-point (volumetric water content, VWC):**

$$VWC_{threshold} = PWP + (FC - PWP) \times p$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
VWC_{threshold} &= 0.12 + (0.28 - 0.12) \times 0.5 \\
VWC_{threshold} &= 0.12 + 0.08 = 0.20 = 20\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The smart controller will start irrigation only when soil VWC falls **below 20%**, and stop at 28% (field capacity). This avoids both over- and under-watering automatically.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Decision Flow — Selecting a Sustainable Irrigation System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Landscape Water Demand Analysis] --> B{Assess Site Climate and ETo Data}
    B --> C[Calculate ETc = ETo x Kc]
    C --> D{Group Plants by Water Need}
    D --> E[Create Hydrozones: Low / Medium / High]
    E --> F{Soil Type and Infiltration Rate}
    F --> G[Amend Soil: Compost / Sand / Loam]
    G --> H[Apply Mulch 50 to 100 mm]
    H --> I{Select Delivery System}
    I --> J[High Water Zone: Rotary Sprinkler]
    I --> K[Medium Water Zone: Spray Sprinkler]
    I --> L[Low Water Zone: Drip or Bubbler]
    J --> M{Add Smart Controller}
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[ET Based or Soil Moisture Sensor]
    N --> O[Install Rain Shut Off Device]
    O --> P[Commission and Audit DU and Ea]
    P --> Q[Monitor and Adjust Quarterly]
    Q --> R[Sustainable Landscape Achieved]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block-level flow maps the **decision logic** an irrigation designer follows, in alignment with **IGBC Green Landscape** guidelines and **KTU Module 3** pedagogy.

## 4.2 System Architecture — Smart Irrigation Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Water Source: Municipal / Rainwater Harvest / Recycled Greywater] --> B[Backflow Preventer]
    B --> C[Master Valve and Filter]
    C --> D[Pressure Regulator]
    D --> E[Smart Controller ET Based]
    E --> F1[Zone 1: Drip Line for Shrubs]
    E --> F2[Zone 2: Rotary Sprinkler for Turf]
    E --> F3[Zone 3: Bubbler for Trees]
    F1 --> G1[Soil Moisture Sensor 1]
    F2 --> G2[Soil Moisture Sensor 2]
    F3 --> G3[Soil Moisture Sensor 3]
    G1 --> E
    G2 --> E
    G3 --> E
    E --> H[Rain Shut Off Sensor]
    H --> E
    E --> I[Cloud Dashboard and Mobile App]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each **zone** is an independent hydrozone with its **own sensor** and **own valve**.
- The **smart controller** is the "brain" — it integrates weather data, soil moisture, and rainfall to decide *when* and *how long* each zone runs.
- The **cloud dashboard** provides water-use analytics for **LEED/IGBC documentation** and identifies leaks or malfunctioning zones in real time.

## 4.3 Comparative Performance Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Conventional Spray Sprinkler System] --> B[Application Efficiency 60 to 70 percent]
    B --> C[High Evaporation and Wind Drift Losses]
    C --> D[Over Watering and Runoff]
    D --> E[High Landscape Water Demand 100 percent Baseline]
    E --> F[Unsustainable Practice]
    G[High Efficiency Drip System] --> H[Application Efficiency 90 to 95 percent]
    H --> I[Low Evaporation and No Wind Drift]
    I --> J[Precise Root Zone Delivery]
    J --> K[Low Landscape Water Demand 40 to 55 percent of Baseline]
    K --> L[Sustainable Practice]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare" irrigation systems, always structure the answer around the four metrics: **$Ea$, $DU$, typical application, and water savings (%)**. Mention the **system life-cycle cost** too — drip is more expensive upfront but saves water and labour over 10–15 years.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define hydrozoning. Why is it considered the most cost-effective first step in reducing landscape water demand?**

**Model Answer:**

**Hydrozoning** is the practice of grouping plants with similar water requirements (and often similar sun/shade exposure and soil type) into distinct, independently controlled irrigation zones. The principle ensures that low-water-use plants (e.g., succulents, native shrubs with $Kc$ = 0.3) are **not** over-watered by the same valve circuit that supplies high-water-use turfgrass ($Kc$ = 0.8–1.0).

It is the most cost-effective first step because:
1. It requires **only design-level changes** — no new hardware.
2. Typical savings are **40%–60%** without replacing a single emitter.
3. It enables the use of appropriate emitter types per zone (drip for low, sprinkler for high).
4. It is a **prerequisite for proper scheduling** — no controller can optimize zones that are mismatched.

> [3 marks: 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for justification]

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between "Reference Evapotranspiration (ETo)" and "Crop Coefficient (Kc)". How are they used to determine actual plant water need?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | $ETo$ (Reference ET) | $Kc$ (Crop Coefficient) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | ET from a standardized reference crop (grass, 0.12 m tall) | Ratio of crop ET to $ETo$ |
| Depends on | Weather only: solar radiation, temperature, wind, humidity | Plant species, growth stage, canopy density |
| Unit | mm/day | Dimensionless (0.3 – 1.2) |
| Source | Penman-Monteith / Hargreaves equations / pan evaporation | Empirical tables (FAO-56) |
| Variability | Changes daily with weather | Changes seasonally with plant |

**Actual plant water need (Crop ET):**

$$ETc = ETo \times Kc$$

For example, a Bermuda-grass lawn with $ETo = 5.0$ mm/day and $Kc = 0.80$ requires $ETc = 4.0$ mm/day of water to remain healthy.

> [3 marks: 1.5 for distinction, 1.5 for usage]

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the working principles of **Drip Irrigation**, **Sprinkler Irrigation**, and **Subsurface Drip Irrigation (SDI)**. Compare their **application efficiencies, suitability, and typical water savings**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A commercial complex in Kochi has a landscape area of **1,500 m²** comprising **60% turfgrass** ($Kc = 0.85$) and **40% drought-tolerant shrubs** ($Kc = 0.40$). The reference ET for the design month is $ETo = 5.5$ mm/day. The system uses a rotary sprinkler for turf ($Ea = 0.75$, $DU = 0.80$) and drip for shrubs ($Ea = 0.92$, $DU = 0.90$). Calculate:
(i) Daily water requirement for the **turf zone** in litres.
(ii) Daily water requirement for the **shrub zone** in litres.
(iii) Total **annual water saving (in kL)** if the same area were irrigated by a *single* conventional spray system with $Ea = 0.65$ and $DU = 0.70$ instead.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of **smart irrigation controllers** in reducing landscape water demand. Explain the working of **ET-based controllers** and **soil moisture sensor (SMS) controllers** with suitable block diagrams. List **five best-management practices (BMPs)** for sustainable landscape irrigation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A residential park in Kozhikode has a sandy loam soil with the following properties: **Field Capacity = 24%**, **Permanent Wilting Point = 10%**, **Bulk Density = 1.4 g/cm³**, **Effective Root Depth = 400 mm**. The landscape is planted with medium-water-need shrubs (allowable depletion $p = 0.50$). Compute:
(i) Available Water Capacity (AWC) in mm of water.
(ii) Management Allowed Depletion (MAD) in mm.
(iii) The volumetric water content (VWC, in %) at which the soil-moisture-sensor controller must trigger irrigation.
(iv) Comment on how **mulching** would modify the irrigation frequency.

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions — Question A

### Solution to A(a): Working Principles & Comparison

**Drip Irrigation:**
Water is delivered through a network of **polyethylene laterals** fitted with **emitters** (pressure-compensating or non-PC) that release water at low flow rates (1–8 L/hr) directly to the root zone. Emitter spacing is typically 30–60 cm. Operates at low pressure (1–2 bar).

**Sprinkler Irrigation:**
Water is pressurized (2.5–4 bar) and sprayed through rotating or fixed nozzles, simulating rainfall. Coverage is **overhead**. Suitable for turf and ground covers. **Rotary nozzles** are more efficient than fixed spray heads because larger droplets resist wind drift.

**Subsurface Drip Irrigation (SDI):**
Laterals are buried **100–200 mm** below the soil surface. Water is delivered directly to the root zone via **geotextile-protected emitters**, eliminating all surface evaporation and virtually all wind drift. Used in high-value landscapes, sports fields, and arid regions.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Drip | Sprinkler | SDI |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Ea$ | 0.90–0.95 | 0.60–0.80 | 0.95 |
| Water savings vs. flood | 50–70% | 30–50% | 60–80% |
| Suitable for turf | Marginal | Excellent | Excellent |
| Suitable for trees/shrubs | Excellent | Wasteful | Excellent |
| Wind sensitivity | None | High | None |
| Capital cost | Moderate | Low–Moderate | High |
| Maintenance burden | Emitter clogging | Nozzle wear | Rodent/digging damage |

### Solution to A(b): Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- Total area = 1500 m²
- Turf area = 60% × 1500 = **900 m²**, $Kc_t = 0.85$, $ETo = 5.5$ mm/day
- Shrub area = 40% × 1500 = **600 m²**, $Kc_s = 0.40$, $ETo = 5.5$ mm/day
- Turf system: $Ea = 0.75$, $DU = 0.80$
- Shrub system: $Ea = 0.92$, $DU = 0.90$

**Step (i): Daily water for turf zone**

$$ETc_t = 5.5 \times 0.85 = 4.675\ \tfrac{mm}{day}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
GIR_t &= \frac{ETc_t}{Ea_t \times DU_t} = \frac{4.675}{0.75 \times 0.80} \\
GIR_t &= \frac{4.675}{0.60} = 7.79\ \tfrac{mm}{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$V_t = GIR_t \times A_t = 7.79 \times 900 = 7{,}011\ \tfrac{L}{day}$$

**Step (ii): Daily water for shrub zone**

$$ETc_s = 5.5 \times 0.40 = 2.20\ \tfrac{mm}{day}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
GIR_s &= \frac{ETc_s}{Ea_s \times DU_s} = \frac{2.20}{0.92 \times 0.90} \\
GIR_s &= \frac{2.20}{0.828} = 2.657\ \tfrac{mm}{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$V_s = GIR_s \times A_s = 2.657 \times 600 = 1{,}594\ \tfrac{L}{day}$$

**Total zoned daily volume:**

$$V_{zoned} = 7{,}011 + 1{,}594 = 8{,}605\ \tfrac{L}{day} = 8.605\ \tfrac{kL}{day}$$

**Step (iii): Comparison with single conventional spray system**

If the *entire* 1500 m² is irrigated with one conventional spray system ($Ea = 0.65$, $DU = 0.70$), we must use a *weighted* $Kc$ because the system cannot differentiate zones:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Kc_{avg} &= (0.60 \times 0.85) + (0.40 \times 0.40) \\
Kc_{avg} &= 0.51 + 0.16 = 0.67
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
ETc_{avg} &= 5.5 \times 0.67 = 3.685\ \tfrac{mm}{day} \\
GIR_{spray} &= \frac{3.685}{0.65 \times 0.70} = \frac{3.685}{0.455} = 8.099\ \tfrac{mm}{day} \\
V_{spray} &= 8.099 \times 1500 = 12{,}149\ \tfrac{L}{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Annual water saving (per year = 365 days):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V_{daily} &= 12{,}149 - 8{,}605 = 3{,}544\ \tfrac{L}{day} \\
\Delta V_{annual} &= 3{,}544 \times 365 = 1{,}293{,}560\ \tfrac{L}{year} \\
\Delta V_{annual} &\approx 1{,}293.6\ \tfrac{kL}{year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** ~**1,293.6 kL/year** saved — equivalent to **~50,000 person-days** of drinking water (at 25 L/person/day).

---

## 5.4 Model Solutions — Question B

### Solution to B(a): Smart Controllers & BMPs

**Working of ET-based controllers:**
- A **weather station** (on-site or web-based) provides daily $ETo$ values.
- The controller multiplies $ETo$ by a pre-programmed $Kc$ (per zone) and adjusts run times automatically.
- A **rain shut-off device** interrupts irrigation after measurable rainfall.
- Modern models use **forecast data** (skip irrigation if rain is predicted within 24 hours).

**Working of SMS controllers:**
- **Capacitance or TDR sensors** measure the soil's **volumetric water content (VWC)** in real time.
- The controller starts irrigation only when VWC falls **below the user-set threshold** (e.g., 20%).
- It stops irrigation when VWC reaches the upper threshold (e.g., 28% = field capacity).
- Eliminates calendar-based over-irrigation.

**Five BMPs for Sustainable Landscape Irrigation:**
1. **Hydrozoning** — group plants by water need.
2. **Soil amendment** with compost to increase AWC.
3. **Mulching** 75–100 mm depth to reduce evaporation by 30%–70%.
4. **Use drip or SDI** for shrubs/trees; rotary nozzles for turf.
5. **Install ET or SMS controllers** with rain shut-off sensors.
6. (Bonus) **Audit Distribution Uniformity** annually using catch-can tests.
7. (Bonus) **Use recycled greywater or harvested rainwater** for irrigation.

### Solution to B(b): Soil Moisture & MAD

**Given:** $FC = 24\%$, $PWP = 10\%$, $\rho_b = 1.4\ g/cm^3$, $d_{root} = 400$ mm, $p = 0.50$.

**Step (i): Available Water Capacity**

$$AWC = (FC - PWP) \times \rho_b \times d_{root}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
AWC &= (0.24 - 0.10) \times 1.4 \times 400 \\
AWC &= 0.14 \times 1.4 \times 400 \\
AWC &= 78.4\ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii): Management Allowed Depletion**

$$MAD = p \times AWC = 0.50 \times 78.4 = 39.2\ mm$$

**Step (iii): Trigger VWC threshold**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VWC_{threshold} &= PWP + (FC - PWP) \times p \\
&= 0.10 + (0.24 - 0.10) \times 0.50 \\
&= 0.10 + 0.07 \\
&= 0.17 = 17\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The SMS controller triggers irrigation when the soil moisture drops to **17% VWC** and stops at **24% VWC**.

**Step (iv): Effect of mulching**

A 75 mm organic mulch layer reduces **surface evaporation by ~50%**, slowing the rate at which VWC falls. The soil now takes **~2× longer** to deplete from 24% to 17%, so irrigation frequency is **halved**. Combined with drip irrigation beneath the mulch, water savings of **60%–75%** are typical.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Forgetting to convert mm/day × m² → litres (1 mm × 1 m² = 1 L).
> 2. **Confusing NIR and GIR:** Many students forget the *second* division by $DU$ — only ~30% of students get this right in the KTU 2023 paper.
> 3. **Mixing $Ea$ and $DU$:** $Ea$ accounts for *losses* (evap., wind, runoff); $DU$ accounts for *unevenness* of distribution. **Both** must be applied.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions:** Always write "assuming no rainfall contribution" and "design month = peak ET month".
> 5. **Skipping hydrozoning mention:** When a problem involves mixed plant types, examiners expect explicit mention of hydrozoning — it carries 1–2 free marks.
> 6. **Forgetting to annualize:** A 14-mark question almost always asks for an annual or seasonal total. Multiplying by 365 is a routine step worth 1 mark.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Frequency Recall Points for KTU Board Exam:**

- **Core demand equation:** $ETc = ETo \times Kc$
- **System delivery equation:** $GIR = \dfrac{ETc}{Ea \times DU}$
- **Volume equation:** $V\ (L) = GIR\ (mm) \times A\ (m^2)$
- **Run-time equation:** $t\ (hr) = \dfrac{GIR}{P\ (mm/hr)}$
- **Application Efficiency ranking (best to worst):** **SDI > Drip > Bubbler > Rotary > Spray > Flood**
- **Hydrozoning = single most cost-effective BMP**, savings of 40–60% achievable.
- **AWC = (FC − PWP) × ρb × d_root**; **MAD = p × AWC**; **Trigger VWC = PWP + p(FC − PWP)**
- **Mulch depth:** 50–100 mm organic; reduces evaporation by 25–70%.
- **Smart controllers** (ET or SMS-based) yield 20–50% additional savings over timers.
- **Distribution Uniformity (DU)** is measured by **catch-can test**; $DU \geq 0.80$ is the KTU/IGBC benchmark.
- **LEED v4.1 / IGBC** credit: *WE Landscape Water Use Reduction* — requires ≥ 50% reduction from baseline.
- **Kerala-specific note:** BIS/KSBC codes; use **rotary nozzles** in windy coastal zones; prefer **drip with sub-surface emitters** in red-laterite soils to avoid runoff.
- **Most common KTU pitfall:** confusing $Ea$ with $DU$ — they are *multiplied*, not added.
- **Always convert:** 1 mm depth over 1 m² area = 1 litre of water.
- **Sustainability hierarchy:** Design → Soil → Mulch → Delivery → Scheduling → Source (rainwater/recycled).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Efficient Plumbing Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET752 - SUSTAINABLE CONSTRUCTION PRACTICES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Water Efficient Plumbing Systems # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Water Efficient Plumbing System (WEPS)** is an integrated, engineered network of pipes, fixtures, fittings, valves, and water-reusing subsystems designed to deliver the required functional service to occupants while **minimizing the consumption of potable water**, reducing wastewater generation, and lowering the overall energy footprint associated with water heating and pumping. It operates on the three foundational pillars of sustainable water management: **Reduce, Reuse, and Recycle**. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** and sustainable building codes (IGBC, GRIHA, LEED v4.1, and the National Building Code of India 2016), a Water Efficient Plumbing System is engineered to achieve a reduction of **at least 30% to 50%** in baseline indoor potable water consumption compared to a conventional, code-minimum plumbing installation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (PECET752 Module 3):** > Water-efficient plumbing is **not just about low-flow taps**. It is a **holistic system-design philosophy** that combines demand-side reduction (efficient fixtures), supply-side optimization (pressure management, leak detection), and alternative water sourcing (rainwater, greywater) to achieve a net-zero water or near-zero water building. > [!NOTE] > **Key Reference Standards for KTU Examinations:** > - **EPAct 1992** (U.S. Energy Policy Act) — sets the baseline maximum flow rates for fixtures > - **EPA WaterSense® Program** — voluntary label for high-efficiency fixtures > - **IS 1172:1993** — Indian Standard for water supply requirements > - **NBC 2016 (Part 9)** — National Building Code of India, Plumbing Services > - **CPHEEO Manual** — Central Public Health and Environmental Engineering Organisation --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Bank Account" View Imagine your household water supply as a **monthly salary credited to a bank account** (the municipal pipeline). In an old, inefficient plumbing system, you essentially **leave the tap dripping, forget to turn off the shower, and pour half your drinking water onto the street** — yet still expect your "account" to last the month. A **Water Efficient Plumbing System** is like a **financially disciplined, automated personal-finance system**: | Real-World Finance Analogy | Plumbing System Equivalent | | :--- | :--- | | Auto-debit for small daily expenses | **Low-flow fixtures** (regulated flow rate) | | Two-tier credit card limits | **Dual-flush cisterns** (half-flush / full-flush) | | Voltage stabilizers on home appliances | **Pressure Reducing Valves (PRV)** — prevent pipe bursts and wastage from over-pressure | | A rainwater harvesting tank beside the well | **Rainwater harvesting for non-potable use** (toilet flushing, garden) | | Recycling dishwater to water the garden | **Greywater recycling for toilet flushing & landscaping** | | Monthly budget tracker app | **Smart water meters & leak detection sensors** | > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Takeaway:** Just as smart finance is *not* about earning more but *spending wisely*, water efficiency is **not** about finding new water sources — it is about **stopping waste and reusing what we already have**. --- ## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Water Efficiency The architecture of a water-efficient plumbing system rests on three integrated engineering strategies: ### Pillar 1: REDUCE (Demand-Side Management) - Installing **low-flow / ultra-low-flow fixtures** (taps, showers, toilets, urinals). - Using **pressure-reducing valves** to eliminate excessive pipe pressure. - Adopting **leak-detection smart meters** and **automatic shut-off valves**. - Designing **zoned distribution networks** to isolate high-usage areas. ### Pillar 2: REUSE (Greywater & Blackwater Reuse) - **Greywater** (from showers, basins, washing machines) → treated and reused for **toilet flushing, gardening, and floor cleaning**. - **Blackwater** (from toilets) → treated through **Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs)** with **Constructed Wetlands** or **Membrane Bio-Reactors (MBR)** → reused for landscape irrigation and cooling tower make-up. ### Pillar 3: RECYCLE (Rainwater Harvesting) - **Rooftop Rainwater Harvesting (RWH)** systems capture stormwater. - Treated rainwater is stored in **underground sumps / overhead tanks** and used for **non-potable applications** like toilet flushing, car washing, and landscape irrigation. - Reduces the **annual municipal water demand** of a building by **20% to 40%**. --- ## 1.4 Core Quantitative Metrics The performance of a water-efficient plumbing system is evaluated using these standard metrics (memorize these for KTU exams): - **Flow Rate ($Q$):** Volume of water delivered per unit time, measured in **Litres per Minute (LPM)** or **Litres per Second (LPS)**. - **Flush Volume ($V_f$):** Water used per toilet flush, measured in **Litres (L)**. - **Water Consumption Index (WCI):** Litres of water used per occupant per day (Lpcd). - **Percentage Water Saved (%):** $$\% \text{ Saved} = \frac{Q_{\text{conventional}} - Q_{\text{efficient}}}{Q_{\text{conventional}}} \times 100$$ - **Effective Water Use Ratio (EWUR):** Ratio of *beneficially used water* to *total water supplied*. > [!NOTE] > **Conventional vs. Efficient Fixture Benchmark (per EPAct 1992 & WaterSense):** > > | Fixture | Conventional Flow | Water-Efficient Flow | % Reduction | > | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | > | Lavatory Faucet | **8.0 LPM** (2.2 GPM) | **4.0 LPM** (1.5 GPM) | 50% | > | Kitchen Faucet | **8.0 LPM** | **5.7 LPM** (2.0 GPM) | 30% | > | Showerhead | **9.5 LPM** (2.5 GPM) | **5.7 LPM** (2.0 GPM) | 40% | > | Toilet (Single Flush) | **13.25 L** (3.5 gal) | **4.8 L** (1.28 gal) | 64% | > | Urinal | **3.8 L** (1.0 gal) | **0.5 L** (Waterless) | 87% | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Component-by-Component Theoretical Breakdown ### 2.1.1 Low-Flow Faucets and Aerators A **standard faucet** flows at 8–15 LPM. A low-flow faucet achieves **4–6 LPM** by: - **Aerator Insertion:** Mixes air with water, maintaining perceived pressure while using less water. A typical aerator reduces flow by **30–50%**. - **Flow Restrictor Discs:** Mechanical discs with calibrated orifices installed at the faucet outlet. - **Sensor-Activated (Touchless) Faucets:** Automatically shut off when hands are removed, eliminating wastage from taps left running. > **Why it works (The Physics):** The human perception of "adequate flow" depends primarily on **velocity and wetting area**, not strictly on volume. An aerated stream delivers the same sensory "wetness" with **half the water**. --- ### 2.1.2 Dual-Flush Toilet Cisterns A dual-flush cistern provides **two flush options**: - **Half-Flush:** **3 litres** (for liquid waste) - **Full-Flush:** **6 litres** (for solid waste) **Operating Logic:** 1. User selects the appropriate button based on waste type. 2. The **flush valve mechanism** opens a calibrated outlet for either the half or full syphon. 3. The cistern refills via a **ball-cock valve** with an adjustable float to limit refill volume. > **Engineering Insight:** Conventional single-flush toilets (13 L) waste water because they use the same volume for liquid and solid waste. Dual-flush is one of the **single most impactful retrofits** — replacing one toilet can save **20,000–30,000 litres per person per year**. --- ### 2.1.3 Pressure Reducing Valves (PRV) Municipal supply pressure in Indian cities ranges from **1.5 bar to 6 bar**. Plumbing fixtures are designed for **1.0 to 2.0 bar**. **The "Pressure Paradox":** - Higher pressure = **higher flow rate** (and therefore higher water consumption per use) - Higher pressure = **higher leakage rate** from joints and valves - Higher pressure = **higher pipe-burst risk** → water loss A **PRV** is installed at the building's main inlet to step down the pressure to a calibrated level (typically **2.0–2.5 bar**).
