It uses a **spring-loaded diaphragm mechanism** that modulates the valve opening to maintain constant downstream pressure. **Pressure-Flow Relationship (Torricelli's Principle):** $$Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$ Where: - $Q$ = volumetric flow rate - $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge (≈ 0.6–0.98) - $A$ = cross-sectional area of the orifice - $g$ = gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**) - $h$ = pressure head (in metres of water column) > **Conclusion:** Flow rate scales with the **square root of pressure**. Doubling the pressure increases flow by only **41%**, but the perceived "force" of water more than doubles — encouraging shorter usage time. Hence, **optimizing pressure saves water without user discomfort**. --- ### 2.1.4 Greywater Recycling Systems Greywater is the wastewater from **showers, bathtubs, washbasins, and washing machines** — it constitutes **50–80%** of total household wastewater. **Treatment Train (Standard 4-Stage):** 1. **Pre-Filtration:** Removal of hair, lint, and large debris via mesh/strainer filters. 2. **Primary Sedimentation:** Solids settle in a **buffer tank** (2–4 hours retention). 3. **Biological Treatment:** Aerobic bacteria in a **bioreactor** (MBBR or SBR) decompose organic load. 4. **Disinfection:** **UV sterilization** or **chlorination** to kill pathogens. **Reuse Application Matrix:** | Greywater Source | Suitable Reuse (After Treatment) | Not Suitable For | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Bathroom showers/basins | Toilet flushing, landscape irrigation | Drinking, cooking, bathing | | Washing machine (low-detergent) | Garden watering, car washing | Vegetable gardens (root crops) | | Kitchen sinks (high oil) | NOT recommended for reuse | All applications | --- ### 2.1.5 Rainwater Harvesting (RWH) for Plumbing The annual **runoff yield** from a rooftop is calculated as: $$Q_{\text{harvest}} = C \times A \times P \times \eta$$ Where: - $C$ = **runoff coefficient** (typically **0.8** for concrete rooftops, **0.9** for metal sheets) - $A$ = **roof catchment area** in m² - $P$ = **annual rainfall** in metres - $\eta$ = **system efficiency** (typically **0.80–0.90**, accounting for losses) > **For KTU Examinations:** Always remember to include a **first-flush diverter** to discard the first **2–3 mm of rainfall** that washes off roof dust, bird droppings, and atmospheric pollutants. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Engineering Use | | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Flow rate (Torricelli) | $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2gh}$ | $Q$ in m³/s | Fixture flow analysis | | 2 | Annual rainwater yield | $Q = C \cdot A \cdot P \cdot \eta$ | $Q$ in Litres/year | RWH tank sizing | | 3 | % Water saved | $\%\text{Saved} = \frac{Q_c - Q_e}{Q_c} \times 100$ | Dimensionless % | Efficiency comparison | | 4 | Per capita daily demand | $D = \frac{\text{Total Annual Demand}}{365 \times N}$ | Litres/person/day (Lpcd) | Tank sizing (NBC = 135 Lpcd) | | 5 | Simple Payback Period | $SPP = \frac{\text{Incremental Cost}}{\text{Annual Water Savings} \times \text{Water Tariff}}$ | Years | Economic feasibility | | 6 | Annual water savings | $\Delta V = N_u \times (V_c - V_e) \times f$ | Litres/year | System design verification | | 7 | Effective flush volume (dual) | $V_{\text{eff}} = f_h \cdot V_h + f_f \cdot V_f$ | Litres | Dual-flush design | | 8 | Hazen-Williams head loss | $h_f = \frac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}$ | Metres | Pipe sizing (not in $\vert$ bars) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Where:** $N_u$ = number of users, $f$ = usage frequency/day, $V_c$, $V_e$ = conventional and efficient volumes per use, $f_h$, $f_f$ = fraction of half/full flushes per day, $C$ = Hazen-Williams roughness coefficient. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Water-efficient plumbing systems are not academic exercises — they are **mandatory in:** - **All IGBC Platinum & GRIHA 5-Star rated buildings** in India - **Greenfield SEZs and IT Parks** in Bengaluru, Hyderabad, and Kochi (major KTU recruitment hubs) - **Smart City Mission projects** (Kerala Smart City Projects: Kochi, Trivandrum, Thrissur) - **Hospitality sector (5-star hotels)** which use **1,500–3,000 litres per occupied room per day** - **Data centres and semiconductor fabs** where cooling-water make-up is enormous > [!TIP] > **Real Case Study (For KTU Application Questions):** > A 50-room 5-star hotel in Kochi consumes ~**150,000 L/day** conventionally. By installing dual-flush toilets, low-flow showerheads, a greywater recycling plant (60% recovery), and rooftop RWH, the hotel reduced municipal water demand to ~**45,000 L/day** — a **70% reduction**, qualifying it for IGBC Platinum certification. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Annual Water Savings from Dual-Flush Retrofit ### Problem Statement A **500-occupant** office building in Trivandrum is to be retrofitted from conventional **13 L single-flush toilets** to **dual-flush cisterns (3 L half / 6 L full)**. Each occupant uses the toilet **3 times per day** on average, with a usage split of **60% half-flushes** and **40% full-flushes**. Calculate the **annual water saved in litres and in cubic metres**. --- ### Step 1 — Define Variables and Conventional Consumption **Conventional per-use volume** (single flush, no dual option): $$V_c = 13 \text{ L per use}$$ **Conventional annual volume for one occupant:** $$V_{c,\text{annual}} = 13 \text{ L} \times 3 \text{ uses/day} \times 365 \text{ days/year}$$ **Per-occupant conventional annual:** $$V_{c,\text{annual}} = 13 \times 3 \times 365 = 14{,}235 \text{ L/year}$$ **For 500 occupants (conventional total):** $$V_{c,\text{total}} = 14{,}235 \times 500 = 7{,}117{,}500 \text{ L/year}$$ **Convert to cubic metres** (1 m³ = 1000 L): $$V_{c,\text{total}} = 7{,}117.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}$$ --- ### Step 2 — Calculate Effective Flush Volume (Dual-Flush) The **effective volume per use** is the weighted average of half and full flushes: $$V_{\text{eff}} = (f_h \times V_h) + (f_f \times V_f)$$ Where: - $f_h = 0.60$ (fraction of half-flushes) - $f_f = 0.40$ (fraction of full-flushes) - $V_h = 3$ L (half-flush volume) - $V_f = 6$ L (full-flush volume) **Substituting values:** $$V_{\text{eff}} = (0.60 \times 3) + (0.40 \times 6)$$ $$V_{\text{eff}} = 1.8 + 2.4 = 4.2 \text{ L per use}$$ --- ### Step 3 — Calculate Efficient Annual Consumption **Per-occupant efficient annual volume:** $$V_{e,\text{annual}} = 4.2 \text{ L} \times 3 \times 365$$ $$V_{e,\text{annual}} = 12.6 \times 365 = 4{,}599 \text{ L/year}$$ **For 500 occupants:** $$V_{e,\text{total}} = 4{,}599 \times 500 = 2{,}299{,}500 \text{ L/year}$$ **In cubic metres:** $$V_{e,\text{total}} = 2{,}299.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}$$ --- ### Step 4 — Calculate Annual Water Saved $$\Delta V = V_{c,\text{total}} - V_{e,\text{total}}$$ $$\Delta V = 7{,}117{,}500 - 2{,}299{,}500 = 4{,}818{,}000 \text{ L/year}$$ **In cubic metres:** $$\Delta V = 4{,}818 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}$$ --- ### Step 5 — Percentage Reduction $$\%\text{Saved} = \frac{\Delta V}{V_{c,\text{total}}} \times 100$$ $$\%\text{Saved} = \frac{4{,}818{,}000}{7{,}117{,}500} \times 100 = 67.69\%$$ > **Conclusion:** A dual-flush retrofit in this 500-occupant office saves **4.82 million litres (4,818 m³) of water annually** — a **67.7% reduction** in toilet-related water consumption. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sizing a Rainwater Harvesting Tank ### Problem Statement A residential building in Thrissur has a **rooftop catchment area of 200 m²**. The city's **average annual rainfall is 2.5 m (2500 mm)**. The rooftop is **concrete** (runoff coefficient $C = 0.8$) and the system efficiency is **η = 0.85**. Calculate the **annual harvestable volume** and the **recommended storage tank capacity** (assume 30-day storage of average daily demand for non-potable use). --- ### Step 1 — Annual Harvestable Volume $$Q_{\text{harvest}} = C \times A \times P \times \eta$$ $$Q_{\text{harvest}} = 0.8 \times 200 \times 2.5 \times 0.85$$ $$Q_{\text{harvest}} = 340 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}$$ **Convert to litres:** $$Q_{\text{harvest}} = 340{,}000 \text{ L/year}$$ --- ### Step 2 — Average Daily Harvestable Volume $$Q_{\text{daily}} = \frac{Q_{\text{harvest}}}{365}$$ $$Q_{\text{daily}} = \frac{340{,}000}{365} = 931.5 \text{ L/day}$$ --- ### Step 3 — Tank Capacity for 30-Day Storage $$V_{\text{tank}} = Q_{\text{daily}} \times 30$$ $$V_{\text{tank}} = 931.5 \times 30 = 27{,}945 \text{ L} \approx 28{,}000 \text{ L}$$ > **Conclusion:** A **28,000-litre (28 m³) underground sump** is recommended for non-potable use. The remaining 12,000 L/day of design capacity is for **garden irrigation and car washing**, which are intermittent. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Simple Payback Period Calculation ### Problem Statement The same 500-occupant office above saves **4,818 m³/year** of water. The **incremental cost** of installing dual-flush systems versus conventional systems is **₹ 5,00,000** (one-time). The municipal water tariff in the city is **₹ 50 per cubic metre (₹ 0.05/L)**. Calculate the **Simple Payback Period (SPP)**. --- ### Step 1 — Annual Monetary Savings $$S = \Delta V \times \text{Tariff}$$ $$S = 4{,}818 \text{ m}^3 \times ₹50/\text{m}^3$$ $$S = ₹2{,}40{,}900 \text{ per year}$$ --- ### Step 2 — Payback Period $$SPP = \frac{\text{Incremental Cost}}{S}$$ $$SPP = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{2{,}40{,}900} = 2.075 \text{ years}$$ $$\boxed{SPP \approx 2.08 \text{ years}}$$ > **Conclusion:** The investment pays for itself in just over **2 years**. Given the typical 15–20 year service life of dual-flush cisterns, this is a **highly economically viable sustainability intervention** — exactly the type of cost-benefit argument expected in a KTU 14-mark question. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Fixtures for KTU Design Problems | Building Type | Toilet Type | Faucet | Showerhead | RWH | Greywater | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | **5-Star Hotel** | Dual-flush 3/6 L | Sensor 4 LPM | Aerated 6 LPM | ✓ 100% | ✓ 80% recovery | | **IT Office** | Dual-flush 4/6 L | Sensor 4 LPM | N/A | ✓ 100% | ✓ 50% recovery | | **Hospital** | Dual-flush 4.5/6 L | Lever 5 LPM | N/A | ✓ 50% | ✓ Mandatory | | **Residential (Mid-Rise)** | Dual-flush 3/6 L | Aerator 6 LPM | Aerator 8 LPM | ✓ 100% | Optional | | **Shopping Mall** | Dual-flush 4.5/6 L + Waterless Urinals | Sensor 4 LPM | N/A | ✓ 100% | ✓ 60% recovery | | **School** | Dual-flush 3/6 L | Push-tap 4 LPM | N/A | ✓ 100% | Not needed | > [!TIP] > **KTU Answer-Writing Tip:** Always present fixture selection as a **justified table** with both **technical** and **economic** reasoning — never just list fixtures. # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define a Water Efficient Plumbing System. List any four key components of such a system. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | PECET752 | Module 3]` **Model Answer:** A **Water Efficient Plumbing System (WEPS)** is an engineered network of fixtures, fittings, pipes, and water-reuse subsystems designed to deliver functional service to occupants while minimizing potable water consumption, reducing wastewater generation, and lowering the energy footprint associated with water heating and pumping. It is typically benchmarked against standards such as **EPAct 1992, EPA WaterSense, IS 1172, and NBC 2016 (Part 9)**, targeting a **30–50% reduction** in baseline potable water use. **Key Components:** 1. **Low-flow / Aerated Faucets and Showerheads** (4–6 LPM instead of 8–15 LPM). 2. **Dual-Flush Toilet Cisterns** (3 L half / 6 L full instead of 13 L single-flush). 3. **Pressure Reducing Valves (PRV)** at building inlet to maintain optimal 2.0–2.5 bar. 4. **Greywater Recycling Plant** for toilet flushing and landscaping. 5. **Rooftop Rainwater Harvesting System** with first-flush diverter and storage sump. 6. **Smart Water Meters with Leak Detection Sensors**. --- ### Question 2: What is the function of a Pressure Reducing Valve (PRV) in a water-efficient plumbing system? Mention the ideal downstream pressure range. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | PECET752 | Module 3]` **Model Answer:** A **Pressure Reducing Valve (PRV)** is a mechanical control device installed at the **main water inlet** of a building to automatically reduce and stabilize the **incoming municipal water pressure** to a safe, calibrated level suitable for downstream fixtures and pipes. **Functions:** 1. **Prevents pipe bursts** due to excessive municipal pressure (Indian municipal supply ranges 1.5–6 bar). 2. **Reduces leakage** from joints, valves, and dripping taps caused by over-pressure. 3. **Lowers per-use flow rate** of fixtures (since $Q \propto \sqrt{P}$). 4. **Extends fixture service life** by reducing water hammer and stress. 5. **Saves water** without changing fixtures — a "passive" conservation strategy. **Ideal Downstream Pressure Range:** **2.0 to 2.5 bar** (≈ 20–25 m of water head). Below 1.5 bar, fixtures feel sluggish; above 3.0 bar, leakage and wastage increase. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is provided between **Question A** and **Question B**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks Explain with a neat sketch the **operating principle of a Dual-Flush Toilet Cistern**. How does it differ from a conventional single-flush cistern? Derive the **effective flush volume** for a building where 70% of the users opt for half-flush and 30% for full-flush, given half-flush = 3 L and full-flush = 6 L. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | PECET752 | Module 3]` **Model Solution:** **Operating Principle of a Dual-Flush Cistern:** A dual-flush cistern is a water closet flushing device that offers **two distinct flush volumes** — a small-volume flush for liquid waste and a larger flush for solid waste — selected by the user through a **twin-button actuator** on top of the cistern. **Step-by-step operation:** 1. **User Selection:** The user presses either the **half-flush button (smaller)** or the **full-flush button (larger)** based on waste type. 2. **Button-to-Valve Linkage:** The button is mechanically linked to a **twin-flush valve** (or a dual-disc syphon) inside the cistern. 3. **Syphon Activation:** Pressing the button lifts a **flap valve** or **syphon dome**, breaking the air seal and triggering the syphon effect. The water rushes down into the toilet bowl. 4. **Volume Calibration:** For the **half-flush**, only a portion of the stored water (~3 L) is released. For the **full-flush**, the entire 6 L is released. 5. **Refill Cycle:** Once the syphon breaks, the **float-ball or piston valve** drops, opening the **inlet refill valve**. The cistern refills to the calibrated level through a **refill tube** that also tops up the toilet bowl's trap seal. 6. **Ready State:** The float rises with the water level, shutting off the inlet valve when the calibrated level is reached. The cistern is now ready for the next flush. **Comparison Table:** | Feature | Conventional Single-Flush | Dual-Flush Cistern | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Flush options | One fixed volume (13 L) | Two volumes (3 L / 6 L) | | User choice | No | Yes (based on waste type) | | Annual water use (per person) | ~14,235 L | ~4,599 L | | % water saved | Baseline | ~67.7% | | Mechanism complexity | Simple | Moderate (twin button + valve) | | Cost (per unit) | ₹1,500–2,000 | ₹2,500–4,000 | **Derivation — Effective Flush Volume:** Given: - Half-flush volume $V_h = 3$ L, fraction of users $f_h = 0.70$ - Full-flush volume $V_f = 6$ L, fraction of users $f_f = 0.30$ The **effective flush volume per use** is the weighted average: $$V_{\text{eff}} = f_h \times V_h + f_f \times V_f$$ $$V_{\text{eff}} = (0.70 \times 3) + (0.30 \times 6)$$ $$V_{\text{eff}} = 2.1 + 1.8 = 3.9 \text{ L per use}$$ > **Conclusion:** With this usage pattern, the effective flush volume is **3.9 L/use**, achieving a saving of **9.1 L per use (70% reduction)** compared to the conventional 13 L single-flush. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks Discuss the **components and working of a Rainwater Harvesting (RWH) system** for a residential building. Calculate the **annual harvestable volume** for a building with a rooftop area of 250 m², located in a region with annual rainfall of 2.0 m, runoff coefficient $C = 0.85$, and system efficiency $\eta = 0.85$. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | PECET752 | Module 3]` **Model Solution:** **Components of an RWH System:** 1. **Catchment Surface (Rooftop):** The roof acts as the collection surface. Materials like **RCC, metal sheets, or tiles** are common. The runoff coefficient $C$ depends on the surface — concrete ≈ 0.8, metal ≈ 0.9. 2. **Gutters and Downpipes:** Channel rainwater from the roof edges to the storage tank. Made of **PVC, GI, or uPVC** with a minimum slope of **1 in 100** for self-cleaning flow. 3. **First-Flush Diverter:** A mechanical device that **diverts the first 2–3 mm of rainfall** (which carries dust, bird droppings, and atmospheric pollutants) away from the storage tank. This is the **most critical component for water quality**. 4. **Filtration Unit:** A **sand-gravel-charcoal filter** or **mesh filter** removes finer suspended solids before water enters the storage tank. 5. **Storage Tank (Sump):** An **underground RCC tank** or **overhead HDPE tank** for storing harvested water. Capacity is sized based on daily demand and seasonal rainfall pattern. 6. **Recharge Well / Recharge Pit (Optional):** In areas with deep water tables, surplus water is diverted to a **borewell recharge well** to replenish groundwater. 7. **Distribution System:** A **separate, colour-coded (typically violet or blue with cross-mark) pipe network** carries harvested water to non-potable fixtures (toilets, garden taps). Cross-connection with the potable network is **strictly prohibited** as per NBC 2016. **Working Principle:** Rainwater falling on the rooftop → flows along the slope into **gutters** → down through **downpipes** → into the **first-flush diverter** (initial dirty water discarded) → through a **filtration unit** → into the **storage tank** → pumped (or gravity-fed) to a **non-potable distribution network** for toilet flushing, garden use, and car washing. **Calculation — Annual Harvestable Volume:** Given: - Rooftop area $A = 250 \text{ m}^2$ - Annual rainfall $P = 2.0 \text{ m}$ - Runoff coefficient $C = 0.85$ - System efficiency $\eta = 0.85$ $$Q_{\text{harvest}} = C \times A \times P \times \eta$$ $$Q_{\text{harvest}} = 0.85 \times 250 \times 2.0 \times 0.85$$ **Step-by-step multiplication:** - $0.85 \times 250 = 212.5$ - $212.5 \times 2.0 = 425.0$ - $425.0 \times 0.85 = 361.25$ $$Q_{\text{harvest}} = 361.25 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}$$ **Converting to litres:** $$Q_{\text{harvest}} = 361{,}250 \text{ L/year}$$ **Average daily harvest:** $$Q_{\text{daily}} = \frac{361{,}250}{365} \approx 990 \text{ L/day}$$ > **Conclusion:** The system can harvest **3,61,250 L/year**, or approximately **990 L/day** on average, sufficient to meet the toilet-flushing needs of a 6–7 member household (typical dual-flush demand: 130–150 L/day). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice]** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks Describe the **concept, sources, treatment, and reuse applications of Greywater Recycling** in a commercial building. How is it different from blackwater? `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 | PECET752 | Module 3]` **Model Solution:** **Concept of Greywater Recycling:** Greywater recycling is the **collection, treatment, and reuse of wastewater from non-toilet plumbing fixtures** for non-potable applications. It is a key strategy in sustainable water management, reducing both **municipal water demand** and **wastewater discharge load** to the municipal sewer. **Sources of Greywater (in a commercial building):** - Washbasins and bathroom showers (~40–50% of total wastewater) - Pantry/kitchen sinks (must be segregated if oil-rich) - Washing machines and laundry (if applicable) - Floor-cleaning drainage - Air-conditioning condensate (technically not greywater but often co-managed) > **Key Note:** Greywater **excludes** water from toilets, urinals, and kitchen sinks handling food waste — these constitute **blackwater**. **Difference Between Greywater and Blackwater:** | Parameter | Greywater | Blackwater | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Source | Showers, basins, washing machines | Toilets, urinals, kitchen waste | | Organic load (BOD) | Low–Moderate (100–300 mg/L) | High (200–600 mg/L) | | Pathogen content | Low | Very high (faecal coliforms) | | Treatment complexity | Simple (4 stages) | Complex (full STP) | | Typical reuse | Toilet flush, garden | Restricted (after full STP) | **Treatment Process (4-Stage):** 1. **Pre-Filtration:** Hair, lint, and large particles removed via **mesh strainers and sand filters**. Prevents clogging of downstream units. 2. **Primary Sedimentation:** Greywater held in a **buffer/equalization tank** for 2–4 hours. Suspended solids settle; oil/grease floats and is skimmed off. 3. **Biological Treatment:** An **MBBR (Moving Bed Biofilm Reactor)** or **SBR (Sequencing Batch Reactor)** uses aerobic bacteria to **degrade organic pollutants (BOD, COD)** by 80–95%. 4. **Disinfection:** Treated water is passed through a **UV sterilizer** (preferred) or dosed with **chlorine (1–2 mg/L residual)** to kill remaining pathogens. **Reuse Applications:** - **Toilet Flushing** (primary use — saves ~30% of building water demand) - **Landscape Irrigation** (gardens, lawns) - **Floor and Pavement Cleaning** - **Cooling Tower Make-up Water** (after additional softening) - **Vehicle Washing** > **Safety:** Reclaimed greywater pipes must be **colour-coded and labelled** (typically violet/light blue) as per **NBC 2016 and IGBC guidelines** to prevent cross-connection with potable water. Annual water quality testing for BOD, TSS, faecal coliform, and pH is mandatory. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks A proposed **10-storey office complex** with 800 occupants is to be designed for **50% reduction in baseline water consumption**. Conventional fixtures use 13 L/toilet flush and 8 LPM for taps. The proposed efficient fixtures are **dual-flush 3/6 L** and **taps with 4 LPM flow rate**. Assume **4 toilet uses/person/day** (60% half, 40% full) and **tap usage of 2 minutes/person/day**. Calculate the **annual water saved** and the **% reduction achieved**. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 | PECET752 | Module 3]` **Model Solution:** **Given Data:** - Number of occupants $N = 800$ - Toilet uses per person per day = 4 - Tap usage time per person per day = 2 minutes - Conventional toilet volume $V_{tc} = 13$ L/flush - Conventional tap flow $Q_{tc} = 8$ LPM - Efficient half-flush $V_h = 3$ L, full-flush $V_f = 6$ L - Efficient tap flow $Q_{te} = 4$ LPM - Usage split: $f_h = 0.60$, $f_f = 0.40$ **Step 1 — Effective Conventional Toilet Volume per Use:** Since conventional toilets have a **single flush**, the effective volume per use is simply: $$V_{tc,\text{eff}} = 13 \text{ L/use}$$ **Step 2 — Effective Efficient Toilet Volume per Use:** $$V_{te,\text{eff}} = f_h \cdot V_h + f_f \cdot V_f$$ $$V_{te,\text{eff}} = (0.60 \times 3) + (0.40 \times 6) = 1.8 + 2.4 = 4.2 \text{ L/use}$$ **Step 3 — Conventional Annual Toilet Water (per person):** $$V_{tc,\text{person}} = 13 \times 4 \times 365 = 18{,}980 \text{ L/year}$$ **For 800 occupants:** $$V_{tc,\text{total}} = 18{,}980 \times 800 = 15{,}184{,}000 \text{ L/year}$$ **Step 4 — Efficient Annual Toilet Water (per person):** $$V_{te,\text{person}} = 4.2 \times 4 \times 365 = 6{,}132 \text{ L/year}$$ **For 800 occupants:** $$V_{te,\text{total}} = 6{,}132 \times 800 = 4{,}905{,}600 \text{ L/year}$$ **Step 5 — Toilet Water Saved:** $$\Delta V_{\text{toilet}} = 15{,}184{,}000 - 4{,}905{,}600 = 10{,}278{,}400 \text{ L/year}$$ **Step 6 — Conventional Annual Tap Water (per person):** $$V_{\text{tap,c}} = 8 \text{ LPM} \times 2 \text{ min/day} \times 365 = 5{,}840 \text{ L/year}$$ **For 800 occupants:** $$V_{\text{tap,c,total}} = 5{,}840 \times 800 = 4{,}672{,}000 \text{ L/year}$$ **Step 7 — Efficient Annual Tap Water (per person):** $$V_{\text{tap,e}} = 4 \text{ LPM} \times 2 \text{ min/day} \times 365 = 2{,}920 \text{ L/year}$$ **For 800 occupants:** $$V_{\text{tap,e,total}} = 2{,}920 \times 800 = 2{,}336{,}000 \text{ L/year}$$ **Step 8 — Tap Water Saved:** $$\Delta V_{\text{tap}} = 4{,}672{,}000 - 2{,}336{,}000 = 2{,}336{,}000 \text{ L/year}$$ **Step 9 — Total Annual Water Saved:** $$\Delta V_{\text{total}} = \Delta V_{\text{toilet}} + \Delta V_{\text{tap}}$$ $$\Delta V_{\text{total}} = 10{,}278{,}400 + 2{,}336{,}000 = 12{,}614{,}400 \text{ L/year}$$ **In cubic metres:** $$\Delta V_{\text{total}} = 12{,}614.4 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}$$ **Step 10 — Percentage Reduction:** $$V_{\text{baseline}} = 15{,}184{,}000 + 4{,}672{,}000 = 19{,}856{,}000 \text{ L/year}$$ $$\%\text{Reduction} = \frac{12{,}614{,}400}{19{,}856{,}000} \times 100 = 63.53\%$$ > **Conclusion:** The proposed water-efficient fixtures achieve a **63.53% reduction** in baseline water consumption, **exceeding the design target of 50%**. The system saves **12.61 million litres (12,614 m³) of water annually** — sufficient to supply drinking water to ~265 additional people per year (at 135 Lpcd as per IS 1172). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students frequently forget to convert **litres to m³** or vice versa. Always check units before writing the final answer. 1 m³ = 1000 L. > 2. **Missing the "Effective Volume" Concept:** Many students calculate toilet savings using either $V_h$ or $V_f$ alone. The **correct approach is the weighted average** using the usage split (60:40 or 70:30). > 3. **Ignoring Usage Frequency:** A common mistake is calculating savings **per flush** without multiplying by **flushes/day × days/year × number of users**. Always work in **annual volumetric terms** for a 14-mark question. > 4. **Forgetting the System Efficiency (η) in RWH:** A 0.80–0.90 efficiency factor accounts for evaporation, leakage, and filter losses. Omitting it gives **overestimated** harvest volumes — a guaranteed **−2 mark penalty**. > 5. **Skipping the "Reuse Application" Section:** A 14-mark question on RWH or greywater **always requires** a discussion of *end-use* — don't end at "water is collected in tank." Mention specific reuse (toilet flush, garden, cooling tower). > 6. **No Diagram in 7-Mark Sub-Part:** For topics like dual-flush cisterns and RWH, a **labelled sketch is mandatory**. Drawing even a simple block diagram earns **2–3 easy marks**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** A Water Efficient Plumbing System is a **holistic, integrated network** combining **low-flow fixtures, dual-flush cisterns, pressure-reducing valves, greywater recycling, and rainwater harvesting** to minimize potable water consumption. - **Three Pillars:** **REDUCE** (low-flow fixtures, PRV) → **REUSE** (greywater for toilet/garden) → **RECYCLE** (RWH for non-potable use). - **Key Benchmark Standards:** EPAct 1992, EPA WaterSense, IS 1172:1993 (135 Lpcd), NBC 2016 Part 9, CPHEEO Manual. - **Fixture Benchmarks (Memorize):** - Faucet: 8.0 LPM → 4.0 LPM (**50%** reduction) - Shower: 9.5 LPM → 5.7 LPM (**40%** reduction) - Toilet: 13 L → 3/6 L dual-flush (**64–77%** reduction) - Urinal: 3.8 L → 0.5 L or waterless (**87–100%** reduction) - **Pressure Relationship:** $Q \propto \sqrt{P}$ — pressure has a **square-root effect** on flow, so PRV installation is a low-cost, high-impact intervention. - **Rainwater Formula:** $Q_{\text{harvest}} = C \times A \times P \times \eta$. Always include the **first-flush diverter** and **filtration unit**. Use $C = 0.8$ for concrete, $C = 0.9$ for metal roofs. $\eta$ typically **0.80–0.90**. - **Greywater Treatment:** 4-Stage train — **Pre-filtration → Buffer tank → Biological reactor (MBBR/SBR) → UV/Chlorine disinfection**. Reuse for toilet flushing, irrigation, and cooling tower make-up. - **Effective Flush Volume (Dual-Flush):** Always use the weighted average $V_{\text{eff}} = f_h V_h + f_f V_f$, not just one flush volume. - **Safety:** Non-potable and potable networks must be **physically separated, colour-coded (violet/blue), and labelled** as per NBC 2016. Cross-connections are a **public health hazard**. - **Economic Justification:** Always compute the **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** to demonstrate financial viability. Typical SPBs for water-efficient fixtures are **2–4 years**, with service lives of **15–20 years**. - **Green Building Alignment:** Water-efficient plumbing is a **mandatory credit** in IGBC, GRIHA, and LEED v4.1 — it directly contributes to the "Water Efficiency" category (typically 8–15 points).

topics.title: Water Metering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET752 - SUSTAINABLE CONSTRUCTION PRACTICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Water Metering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Metering** is the systematic measurement, recording, and monitoring of the quantity of water consumed at various points within a building, campus, or municipal distribution network, using calibrated metering devices (water meters) installed at strategic locations in the plumbing infrastructure, to enable consumption-based billing, demand management, leak detection, and conservation auditing in alignment with **NBC 2016**, **ECBC+**, and **IGBC/LEED** sustainability frameworks.

In the context of **Sustainable Construction Practices (PECET752)**, water metering is treated as a **demand-side management (DSM) intervention** that transforms water from an "unlimited free resource" into a **metered, accountable, and optimizable utility**, thereby driving behavioral and operational efficiencies in buildings.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Electricity Prepaid vs. Postpaid" Metaphor**
> Imagine your college canteen gives you unlimited free meals. You will likely waste food, take extra chapatis, and not think twice about leftovers. Now imagine the canteen switches to a **pay-per-plate system** (say ₹50 per plate). Suddenly, you only take what you can eat, share leftovers with friends, and become conscious of waste. **Water metering is exactly this "pay-per-plate" system for water.** When buildings install meters, occupants suddenly become aware that every liter of dripping tap, every long shower, and every running washing cycle costs money. This awareness, backed by actual measurement, is the single biggest driver of water conservation in green buildings.

A more technical analogy: A water meter is to a plumber/hydraulic engineer **what a multimeter is to an electrical engineer** — a diagnostic instrument that quantifies an otherwise invisible resource flow.

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot

Water meters can be broadly classified as:

| S.No. | Classification Basis | Sub-Categories |
|:----:|:--------------------|:----------------|
| 1 | **Working Principle** | Mechanical (displacement, velocity, compound), Electromagnetic, Ultrasonic, Smart (IoT-enabled) |
| 2 | **Location of Installation** | Bulk/Master Meters, Sub-meters, Apartment-level (BTU) Meters |
| 3 | **End Use** | Domestic (potable), Recycled (treated wastewater), Rainwater, Borewell, Construction-stage |
| 4 | **Reading Mode** | Manual, Automatic Meter Reading (AMR), Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI) |
| 5 | **Standards** | **IS 779**, **IS 2373**, **ISO 4064**, **AWWA M6** |

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Accuracy Class**: Typically **Class 1, Class 2, or Class 3** as per ISO 4064, where Class 2 = ±2% error permissible.
- **Minimum Flow Rate ($Q_{min}$)**: The smallest flow the meter can accurately measure.
- **Maximum Flow Rate ($Q_{max}$)**: The largest flow before meter damage/loss of accuracy.
- **Meter Service Life**: **15–20 years** for well-maintained mechanical meters.
- **Pressure Loss Across Meter**: Should be **< 1.0 m head** at $Q_{max}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Need for Water Metering in Sustainable Buildings

The hierarchy of why water metering is mandatory in green-rated projects:

1. **Quantification First Principle**: You cannot conserve what you cannot measure.
2. **Behavioural Economics Driver**: Metered consumption reduces per-capita water use by **20–40%** (EPA studies).
3. **Leak Detection**: Continuous metering reveals nocturnal flow anomalies (continuous flow between 2 AM–4 AM indicates leakage).
4. **Regulatory Compliance**: **NBC 2016 Clause 11.4**, **ECBC+ SuperECBC**, and **IGBC Green Homes** mandate sub-metering for buildings > 2000 m² built-up area.
5. **Billing Equity**: Prevents flat-rate unfairness — large consumers pay proportionally.
6. **Water Audit Foundation**: Forms the data backbone for **IS 17482:2020 Water Audit** standards.

## 2.2 Working Principles of Common Meter Types

### A. Mechanical (Volumetric) Displacement Meters
- Use a **piston or nutating disc** that displaces a fixed volume of water per cycle.
- Each cycle = a known volume → count cycles → multiply to get total volume.
- Best for **low-flow, domestic applications** ($Q_{max}$ up to 15 m³/hr).
- Example: Standard household 15 mm bore meter.

### B. Velocity (Inferential) Meters
- Multi-jet or single-jet turbines spin proportional to flow velocity.
- Volumetric flow $Q = A \times v$ where $A$ = cross-section, $v$ = velocity.
- Suitable for **mid-range commercial flows** (15–150 m³/hr).

### C. Compound Meters
- Combine a **displacement meter** (low flow) + **turbine meter** (high flow) with an automatic valve.
- Maintains accuracy across **wide flow range (1:250 turndown ratio)**.

### D. Electromagnetic (Mag) Meters
- Operate on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**: 
$$E = k \cdot B \cdot D \cdot v$$
where $E$ = induced EMF (V), $k$ = constant, $B$ = magnetic flux density (T), $D$ = pipe diameter (m), $v$ = flow velocity (m/s).
- **No moving parts** → low maintenance, high accuracy (Class 0.5).
- Used for **bulk municipal and STP (Sewage Treatment Plant) inflow metering**.

### E. Ultrasonic Meters
- Use **transit-time difference** of ultrasonic pulses travelling with and against the flow.
- Highly accurate, **no pressure loss**, ideal for **clean water bulk supply**.

### F. Smart (IoT) Meters
- Integrate **NB-IoT/LoRa/Wi-Fi** communication modules.
- Enable **AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure)** with real-time dashboards, automatic leak alarms, and consumption analytics.

## 2.3 Strategic Metering Locations in a Building (Hierarchy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU / IGBC Sub-Metering Hierarchy (Top-Down)**
> 1. **Bulk/Compound Meter** — Municipal/unicipal supply entry point.
> 2. **Group Sub-Meters** — Per block, per wing, per floor stack.
> 3. **Apartment/Unit Meters (BTU — Bulk Tenanted Unit)** — Each dwelling unit.
> 4. **End-Use Sub-Meters** — Landscape irrigation, STP feed, cooling tower make-up, swimming pool, fire-fighting test lines, kitchen, laundry.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Boundary / Validity Conditions |
|:-----:|:--------|:---------------------|:------------------|:------------------------------|
| 1 | **Volumetric Flow Rate** | $Q = A \cdot v$ | $A$ = cross-section area (m²), $v$ = velocity (m/s), $Q$ = (m³/s) | Laminar/turbulent flow; full pipe |
| 2 | **Continuity Equation** | $Q_1 = Q_2 \Rightarrow A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ | For incompressible fluid (water) | Steady, incompressible, no leakage |
| 3 | **Total Volume from Meter Reading** | $V = N \cdot v_{displacement}$ | $N$ = no. of cycles, $v_{displacement}$ = volume per cycle (m³) | Mechanical displacement meters |
| 4 | **Annual Water Cost** | $C_{annual} = V_{annual} \cdot R_{unit}$ | $V$ in m³, $R$ in ₹/m³ | Constant rate assumption |
| 5 | **Per-Capita Water Demand** | $q_{pc} = \dfrac{V_{daily}}{P}$ | $V$ in litres/day, $P$ = population | $q_{pc}$ in LPCD (litres per capita per day) |
| 6 | **Percentage Water Savings** | $\%\text{Savings} = \dfrac{(Q_{base} - Q_{new})}{Q_{base}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless | For before-after comparison |
| 7 | **Simple Payback Period** | $SPP = \dfrac{I}{\Delta C \cdot R_{unit}}$ | $I$ = installation cost (₹), $\Delta C$ = annual volume saved (m³) | $SPP$ in years |
| 8 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = -I + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{\Delta C_t \cdot R}{(1+d)^t}$ | $d$ = discount rate, $n$ = project life | Investment decision |
| 9 | **Meter Accuracy (Class)** | $e = \dfrac{Q_{measured} - Q_{true}}{Q_{true}} \times 100$ | Error % | Class 2 means $\vert e \vert \leq 2\%$ |
| 10 | **Faraday's Law (Mag Meter)** | $E = k \cdot B \cdot D \cdot v$ | $E$ in V, $B$ in T, $D$ in m, $v$ in m/s | Conductive fluid ($\sigma \geq 5 \mu S/cm$) |
| 11 | **Leakage Flow Detection** | $Q_{nocturnal} > Q_{threshold}$ | Threshold typically 0.5–2 L/min per connection | Night-time 2 AM–4 AM window |
| 12 | **Annual Rainwater Harvest** | $V_{harvest} = A_{roof} \cdot R_f \cdot C_c \cdot 0.001$ | $A$ in m², $R_f$ = rainfall (mm), $C_c$ = co-efficient of collection | $C_c$ = 0.8–0.9 for clean roof |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In professional sustainable construction, water metering underpins:

- **Green Building Certification (IGBC / LEED / GRIHA)** — mandatory prerequisite credit for measurement & verification.
- **Building Management Systems (BMS)** — flow data feeds into SCADA dashboards.
- **Municipal NRW (Non-Revenue Water) Reduction** — global NRW averages **30–40%**; metering reduces it to < 15%.
- **Smart City Mission (India)** — every household smart meter rollout is a national water sustainability mandate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Annual Cost Savings from Sub-Metering

> **Problem Setup:** A residential building has 100 flats. Pre-metering (flat rate) annual consumption = 25,000 m³/yr. After sub-metering, measured consumption drops to 18,000 m³/yr. Tariff = ₹50/m³. Sub-metering cost = ₹8,00,000. Compute annual monetary savings and simple payback.

**Step 1 — Identify volume saved per year.**

$$\Delta V = V_{base} - V_{new} = 25{,}000 - 18{,}000 = 7{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr}$$

**Step 2 — Convert volume saved into monetary savings.**

$$\Delta C = \Delta V \times R_{unit} = 7{,}000 \times 50 = \text{₹ } 3{,}50{,}000 \text{ /yr}$$

**Step 3 — Compute simple payback period (SPP).**

$$SPP = \frac{I}{\Delta C} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{3{,}50{,}000} = 2.286 \text{ years}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**

Since $SPP \approx 2.3$ years $\ll$ typical building life of 30+ years, the investment is **highly economically justified**.

**Step 5 — Compute savings over a 10-year horizon (assuming constant tariff).**

$$S_{10} = 3{,}50{,}000 \times 10 = \text{₹ } 35{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 6 — Net Benefit over 10 years (subtracting capital cost).**

$$NB = S_{10} - I = 35{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 27{,}00{,}000$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Sub-metering typically yields **20–40% reduction** in water use. In this problem, savings = $\frac{7000}{25000} \times 100 = 28\%$, which falls perfectly in the expected sustainability range.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: NPV-Based Economic Justification of Smart Metering

> **Problem Setup:** A 50-floor commercial tower invests ₹ 25,00,000 in smart water meters. Annual water savings = 12,000 m³. Tariff = ₹ 80/m³. Discount rate $d$ = 8%. Project life $n$ = 10 years. Tariff escalates at 5% p.a. Compute NPV.

**Step 1 — Establish first-year savings.**

$$C_1 = 12{,}000 \times 80 = \text{₹ } 9{,}60{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Generalise savings for year $t$ (tariff escalation).**

$$C_t = C_1 \times (1 + e)^t = 9{,}60{,}000 \times (1.05)^{t-1}$$

where $e$ = tariff escalation rate.

**Step 3 — Discount each year's savings to present value.**

$$PV_t = \frac{C_t}{(1+d)^t} = \frac{9{,}60{,}000 \times (1.05)^{t-1}}{(1.08)^t}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the sum of present values (assume end-of-year cash flows).**

$$\sum_{t=1}^{10} PV_t = 9{,}60{,}000 \times \sum_{t=1}^{10} \frac{(1.05)^{t-1}}{(1.08)^t}$$

Let $r = \dfrac{1.05}{1.08} = 0.9722$. Then:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{10} PV_t = 9{,}60{,}000 \times \frac{1}{1.08} \times \sum_{t=0}^{9} r^{t} = 9{,}60{,}000 \times 0.9259 \times \frac{1 - r^{10}}{1 - r}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $r^{10}$.**

$$r^{10} = (0.9722)^{10} \approx 0.7616$$

**Step 6 — Compute the geometric sum.**

$$\frac{1 - 0.7616}{1 - 0.9722} = \frac{0.2384}{0.0278} \approx 8.5755$$

**Step 7 — Compute the total present value of savings.**

$$\sum_{t=1}^{10} PV_t = 9{,}60{,}000 \times 0.9259 \times 8.5755 \approx 9{,}60{,}000 \times 7.9400 \approx \text{₹ } 76{,}22{,}400$$

**Step 8 — Compute NPV.**

$$NPV = -I + \sum_{t=1}^{10} PV_t = -25{,}00{,}000 + 76{,}22{,}400 = \text{₹ } 51{,}22{,}400$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** $NPV \gg 0$, hence the smart-metering investment is **economically optimal and environmentally beneficial**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Per-Capita Demand Calculation for a Hostel Building

> **Problem Setup:** A college hostel with bulk meter reading shows monthly water use = 9,00,000 litres. The hostel has 500 students. Compute LPCD and compare with **NBC 2016** benchmark of **135 LPCD** (hostel category).

**Step 1 — Convert monthly volume to daily volume.**

$$V_{daily} = \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{30} = 30{,}000 \text{ litres/day}$$

**Step 2 — Compute per-capita water demand.**

$$q_{pc} = \frac{V_{daily}}{P} = \frac{30{,}000}{500} = 60 \text{ LPCD}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with benchmark.**

$$q_{pc,\text{actual}} = 60 \text{ LPCD} < q_{pc,\text{NBC}} = 135 \text{ LPCD}$$

**Step 4 — Compliance ratio.**

$$\text{CR} = \frac{60}{135} \times 100 = 44.4\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> The actual demand is **only 44.4% of NBC benchmark**. This is *suspiciously low* and indicates possible **non-functional plumbing fixtures, low fixture counts, or chronic supply shortage**. A proper KTU answer must flag this as a water audit anomaly requiring leak/non-supply investigation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Meter Types

| Parameter | Mechanical Displacement | Velocity Turbine | Electromagnetic | Ultrasonic | Smart (IoT) |
|:----------|:----------------------:|:----------------:|:---------------:|:----------:|:-----------:|
| Accuracy | ±2% | ±2% | ±0.5% | ±1% | ±1% |
| Pressure Loss | Medium | Medium-High | **Negligible** | **Negligible** | Varies |
| Maintenance | Medium | Medium-High | **Low** | **Low** | Low (firmware) |
| Cost (INR) | ₹ 1k–5k | ₹ 5k–20k | ₹ 50k–2L | ₹ 1L–5L | ₹ 5k–15k |
| Best Application | Domestic | Commercial | STP/Municipal | Bulk clean water | AMI / Smart City |
| Suitability for Recycled Water | Limited | Limited | **Excellent** | Good | Excellent |
| Lifespan | 15–20 yr | 10–15 yr | 20+ yr | 20+ yr | 10–15 yr |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1: Water Metering System Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Municipal Supply] --> M1
    A2[Borewell Source] --> M1
    A3[Tanker Water] --> M1
    M1{1 Bulk Compound Meter} --> ST1[Storage Tank UG]
    ST1 --> M2
    M2{2 Domestic Sub-Meter} --> DW1
    M2 --> DW2
    M2 --> DW3
    ST1 --> M3
    M3{3 Recycled Sub-Meter} --> L1
    M3 --> L2
    M3 --> L3
    M3 --> L4
    DW1[Landscape Irrigation] --> L1
    DW2[Cooling Tower Makeup] --> L2
    DW3[Flushing Tanks] --> L3
    DW4[Laundry Kitchen] --> L4
    M2 --> BTU1
    M2 --> BTU2
    M2 --> BTU3
    BTU1[Apartment Unit 1] --> CRM[4 Cloud Analytics Dashboard]
    BTU2[Apartment Unit 2] --> CRM
    BTU3[Apartment Unit 3] --> CRM
    M3 --> CRM
    M1 --> CRM
    CRM --> ALM[5 SMS App Leak Alarm]
    CRM --> REP[6 Monthly Consumption Report]
    CRM --> AUD[7 Water Audit IS 17482]
```

**Caption:** Block architecture of a multi-tiered water metering system showing the cascade from bulk → sub-meter → BTU/apartment meter, all feeding into a centralized analytics platform for real-time monitoring, leak alarms, and audit reporting.

---

## 4.2 Figure 2: Smart Water Metering — Communication Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Node 1 Flat 1] --> GW
    N2[Node 2 Flat 2] --> GW
    N3[Node 3 Flat 3] --> GW
    N4[Node 4 Flat 4] --> GW
    N5[Node 5 Flat 5] --> GW
    GW[LoRaWAN Gateway] --> CL
    CL[Cloud AMI Server] --> DB
    DB[Time Series Database] --> ANL
    ANL[Analytics Engine ML Leak Detection] --> UI
    UI[Web Mobile Dashboard] --> USR
    USR[Facility Manager Resident] --> UI
    ANL --> NT[Notification Service]
    NT --> SMS[SMS Email Alerts]
```

**Caption:** AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure) topology using LoRaWAN/NB-IoT for remote, real-time water consumption monitoring with ML-based leak detection.

---

## 4.3 Figure 3: Decision Tree — Selecting the Right Meter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the fluid clean potable?} -->|Yes| Q2
    Q1 -->|No recycled or sewage| Q3
    Q2{Flow range is domestic less than 15 m3 per hr?} -->|Yes| A1[Use Displacement Piston Meter]
    Q2 -->|No| A2[Use Compound Mechanical Meter]
    Q3{Need high accuracy bulk measurement?} -->|Yes| A3[Use Electromagnetic Flow Meter]
    Q3 -->|No| A4[Use Ultrasonic Clamp On Meter]
    Q5{Need real time remote data?} -->|Yes| A5[Add Smart IoT Modbus Module]
    Q5 -->|No| A6[Use Manual Reading AMR Walk By]
    A1 --> Q5
    A2 --> Q5
    A3 --> Q5
    A4 --> Q5
```

**Caption:** Logical decision tree used by MEP engineers to select the appropriate water meter type based on fluid quality, flow range, accuracy requirement, and data communication needs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define water metering. List any four advantages of installing water meters in a residential building.**

**Model Answer:**
Water metering is the measurement of water consumption using calibrated metering devices installed in plumbing systems, to record and monitor volumetric flow for billing and conservation purposes.

**Four advantages:**
1. Enables **consumption-based billing** → fair and equitable charging.
2. Acts as a **demand-management tool** → reduces per-capita consumption by 20–40%.
3. Facilitates **leak detection** through nocturnal-flow analysis (continuous flow at 2–4 AM).
4. Provides **data backbone** for green-building certification (IGBC/LEED credit compliance) and water audits as per **IS 17482:2020**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**Differentiate between mechanical displacement meters and electromagnetic flow meters in four points.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Parameter | Mechanical Displacement | Electromagnetic |
|:-----:|:----------|:----------------------:|:---------------:|
| 1 | **Working Principle** | Counts cycles of moving piston/disc | Faraday's law — induced EMF in a magnetic field |
| 2 | **Accuracy** | ±2% (Class 2) | ±0.5% (Class 0.5) |
| 3 | **Pressure Loss** | Moderate (due to moving parts) | Negligible (no obstruction) |
| 4 | **Suitability for Recycled Water** | Limited (suspended solids cause wear) | Excellent (no moving parts) |
| 5 | **Cost** | Low (₹ 1k–5k) | High (₹ 50k–2L) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Do not** skip the sub-metering hierarchy diagram → it carries 2–3 marks and is the *most-skipped* part in student scripts.
> - **Always** write units in calculations (m³, L, kL, ₹) — units-mistake costs **1 mark** per sub-question.
> - For payback/NPV problems, **state assumptions** (constant tariff, no O&M escalation) before writing the equation.
> - **Diagrams must be hand-drawn style** with proper labels; vague boxes with no labels = 0 marks for the figure.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **working principle of an electromagnetic flow meter**. State Faraday's law and explain why it is the preferred meter for **recycled / STP water** applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A commercial complex has installed sub-meters. Annual water consumption reduced from 18,000 m³ to 13,500 m³ after sub-metering. The cost of the metering system is ₹ 6,00,000. If the water tariff is ₹ 60/m³, calculate **(i) annual monetary savings, (ii) simple payback period, and (iii) cumulative savings over 5 years**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a):

**Electromagnetic Flow Meter — Working Principle:**

- It operates on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, which states that when a conductive fluid flows through a magnetic field, an EMF is induced across the fluid, proportional to the velocity of flow.
- The induced EMF is given by:

$$E = k \cdot B \cdot D \cdot v$$

where $E$ = induced EMF (Volts), $k$ = constant (V·s/m²·T), $B$ = magnetic flux density (Tesla), $D$ = pipe internal diameter (m), $v$ = mean flow velocity (m/s).

- Two **electrodes** placed diametrically opposite on the inner pipe wall detect this EMF.
- The induced voltage is **linearly proportional to velocity**, hence to volumetric flow rate $Q = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot v$.

**Working Steps:**
1. Coils generate a magnetic field perpendicular to the flow direction.
2. Conductive water (≥ 5 µS/cm) acts as the moving conductor.
3. Electrodes measure the induced EMF.
4. Transmitter converts EMF into flow-rate reading.

**Sketch (text representation):**
```
        ┌──────────── Magnetic Coils (B-field) ────────────┐
        │                                                  │
        │           ↑ Direction of Magnetic Field          │
   ────►│   Fluid ────► ────► ────► ────► ────►            │
   Flow │                                                  │
        │  (•) Electrode 1          Electrode 2 (•)        │
        │     (+V)                       (-V)               │
        └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Why preferred for recycled / STP water:**
- **No moving parts** → unaffected by suspended solids, grit, fibrous matter.
- **Pressure loss is essentially zero** → saves pumping energy in large STPs.
- **Accuracy is unaffected by fluid density, viscosity, or temperature**.
- Handles **raw sewage, treated effluent, and sludge** with equal ease.
- Easy to install on large-diameter pipes (DN 100 to DN 2000).

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b):

**Given:**
$V_{base} = 18{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr}$  
$V_{new} = 13{,}500 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr}$  
$I = \text{₹ } 6{,}00{,}000$  
$R = \text{₹ } 60/\text{m}^3$

**(i) Annual monetary savings:**

$$\Delta V = V_{base} - V_{new} = 18{,}000 - 13{,}500 = 4{,}500 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr}$$

$$\Delta C = \Delta V \times R = 4{,}500 \times 60 = \text{₹ } 2{,}70{,}000 \text{ /yr}$$

**(ii) Simple Payback Period:**

$$SPP = \frac{I}{\Delta C} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{2{,}70{,}000} = 2.222 \text{ years}$$

**(iii) Cumulative savings over 5 years:**

$$S_{5} = \Delta C \times 5 = 2{,}70{,}000 \times 5 = \text{₹ } 13{,}50{,}000$$

**Net benefit over 5 years:**

$$NB = S_5 - I = 13{,}50{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 7{,}50{,}000$$

**% reduction in consumption:**

$$\%\text{Reduction} = \frac{4{,}500}{18{,}000} \times 100 = 25\%$$

> **Conclusion:** A **25% reduction** in water use is achieved, with payback in just over 2 years, validating sub-metering as a financially attractive and sustainable intervention.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2022]

**(a)** Draw the **hierarchical water metering architecture** for a multi-storey residential complex and explain the function of each tier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1,000-student college campus has installed a bulk meter. Total annual water use = 1,20,00,000 litres. NBC 2016 benchmark for institutional buildings = **135 LPCD**. Compute **(i) actual LPCD, (ii) deviation from benchmark in %**, and **(iii) suggest three intervention strategies** to bring consumption within NBC limits. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a):

**Hierarchical Water Metering Architecture (5 tiers):**

```
TIER 0 ──► Municipal / Borewell Source
            │
            ▼
TIER 1 ──► BULK COMPOUND METER (at UGR / Pump House)
            │ (Accuracy: ±2%; captures total inflow to campus)
            ▼
TIER 2 ──► GROUP SUB-METERS (per Block / Wing / Hostel)
            │ (Tracks block-level consumption)
            ▼
TIER 3 ──► BTU / APARTMENT SUB-METERS (per Dwelling Unit)
            │ (Enables pay-per-use billing)
            ▼
TIER 4 ──► END-USE SUB-METERS (Landscape, STP, Cooling Tower,
            │  Laundry, Kitchen, Fire-Fighting test line)
            ▼
TIER 5 ──► CLOUD AMI DASHBOARD (Analytics, Leak Alarms, Reports)
```

**Function of each tier:**
- **Tier 0 – Source:** Identifies quantity of raw water entering the system.
- **Tier 1 – Bulk Meter:** Master measurement; required by municipal authority for billing; reveals UFW (Unaccounted For Water).
- **Tier 2 – Group Sub-meter:** Identifies the highest-consuming block → supports differential maintenance.
- **Tier 3 – BTU Meter:** Drives **consumption-based billing** to each flat → biggest behaviour-change lever.
- **Tier 4 – End-Use Meter:** Tracks specific end uses; reveals where (e.g., cooling tower) the biggest conservation opportunity lies.
- **Tier 5 – AMI Dashboard:** Real-time data visualisation, leak detection, automated reports to facility manager.

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b):

**Given:**
Annual volume = 1,20,00,000 L = 12,00,000 kL
Population = 1,000 students
NBC 2016 benchmark $q_{NBC}$ = 135 LPCD

**(i) Actual LPCD:**

$$V_{daily} = \frac{1{,}20{,}00{,}000}{365} = 32{,}876.71 \text{ L/day}$$

$$q_{pc,\text{actual}} = \frac{V_{daily}}{P} = \frac{32{,}876.71}{1{,}000} = 32.88 \text{ LPCD}$$

**(ii) Deviation from NBC benchmark:**

$$\Delta q = q_{NBC} - q_{pc,\text{actual}} = 135 - 32.88 = 102.12 \text{ LPCD}$$

$$\%\text{Deviation} = \frac{102.12}{135} \times 100 = 75.6\% \text{ below benchmark}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** Actual consumption is **only 24.4%** of the NBC benchmark. This is anomalously low and could indicate: (a) under-supply by municipality, (b) non-functional fixtures, (c) chronic leakage masking the true demand.

**(iii) Three intervention strategies:**
1. **Sub-metering every hostel block** + introducing metered per-student billing (reduces wastage by 25–35%).
2. **Retrofitting dual-flush / low-flow fixtures** — 6/3 L flush valves, 4 L/min aerated taps (saves up to 40% on domestic use).
3. **Continuous bulk-meter data analytics** + AI-based leak detection alarms (closes the UFW gap).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Save This Page**

- ✅ **Definition**: Water metering = systematic volumetric measurement of water use for billing, conservation, and audit.
- ✅ **Sub-metering hierarchy (5 tiers)**: Source → Bulk → Group → BTU → End-Use → AMI Cloud.
- ✅ **Key types**: Mechanical (displacement/turbine), Electromagnetic, Ultrasonic, Smart (IoT).
- ✅ **Faraday's Law**: $E = k \cdot B \cdot D \cdot v$ — basis of mag meters; preferred for **recycled / STP water** because of zero pressure loss and no moving parts.
- ✅ **NBC 2016 hostels benchmark** = **135 LPCD** (very frequently asked as numerical).
- ✅ **Sub-metering typically saves 20–40%** water (EPA + IGBC data).
- ✅ **Payback** for sub-metering = typically **2–3 years**; a <5-year payback makes it a no-brainer for KTU economic-justification problems.
- ✅ **IS Codes to remember**: **IS 779** (domestic water meters), **IS 2373** (general water meters), **IS 17482:2020** (water audit), **ISO 4064** (international accuracy classes).
- ✅ **Leak detection technique**: Analyse **nocturnal flow (2 AM–4 AM)** — continuous flow above threshold = leak.
- ✅ **Accuracy Class 2** = ±2% error allowed; **Class 0.5** = ±0.5% (premium mag meters).
- ✅ **Smart meter advantages**: real-time data, no manual reading, predictive leak alerts, and AMI integration with BMS.
- ✅ **Always include units** in KTU numerical answers; missing units = 1-mark penalty.
- ✅ **State assumptions clearly** in payback / NPV problems (constant tariff, no O&M escalation, no replacement of meters within horizon).
- ✅ **Drawing tip**: Always draw a hierarchical block diagram of the metering system — examiners award 2–3 marks for a clear, well-labelled figure.
- ✅ **Sustainability angle** (often a 2-marker): Sub-metering is the **measurement & verification (M&V) backbone** of green-building certifications such as **IGBC, LEED, GRIHA, ECBC+**.
- ✅ **Common KTU trap**: Don't confuse **bulk meter** (BULK = entire building inflow) with **BTU meter** (Bulk Tenanted Unit = per-flat submeter). The acronym similarity is intentional and is tested.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Waste Water Treatment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET752 - SUSTAINABLE CONSTRUCTION PRACTICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Waste Water Treatment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Waste Water Treatment

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Waste Water Treatment (WWT)** in the context of Sustainable Construction Practices refers to the engineered, multi-stage physico-chemical and biological process by which contaminated water generated from domestic, commercial, industrial, and construction activities is treated to remove suspended solids, organic matter, pathogens, and nutrients, so that the effluent meets prescribed discharge or reuse standards.

As per the **Central Public Health and Environmental Engineering Organisation (CPHEEO)** Manual on Sewerage and Sewage Treatment (2013, re-issued for KTU alignment) and **IS 3025** standards adopted by KTU 2024, wastewater treatment is classified into **three primary stages**: **Preliminary, Primary, Secondary, and Tertiary/Advanced Treatment**, often followed by **Sludge Management**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the module *Sustainable Construction Practices*, wastewater treatment is studied NOT as a municipal utility subject, but as a **site-level, decentralized, resource-recovery system** that supports LEED/GRIHA credits, reduces freshwater demand, and closes the on-site water loop.

## 1.2 The Two Broad Categories of Wastewater in Construction

| Category | Source | Pollution Load | Reuse Potential |
|----------|--------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Black Water** | Toilets, urinals, kitchen waste | High BOD, pathogens, nitrogen | Restricted – toilet flushing only after disinfection |
| **Grey Water** | Showers, basins, laundry, AC condensate | Moderate BOD, low pathogens | High – irrigation, cooling, dust suppression |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric — Person Equivalent (PE):** KTU adopts the CPHEEO norm of **1 PE = 135 litres/day** of sewage flow with **BOD₅ = 54 g/day** and **Suspended Solids = 70 g/day**. Any design calculation for treatment units is anchored to this constant.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Sieve" Model

Imagine you pour a glass of muddy, oily water into a kitchen sieve. The sieve catches the big chunks (this is **screening**). The water that passes through still looks cloudy because fine dirt is suspended in it. If you let it sit in a bucket for an hour, the heavier dirt settles at the bottom (**sedimentation = primary treatment**). But the water is still slightly yellow and smelly. Now you pour it into a fish tank with active bacteria on gravel — the bacteria "eat" the dissolved organics (**biological treatment = secondary treatment**). The water is now almost clear but still has germs. A dash of chlorine or UV light kills the remaining germs (**disinfection = tertiary treatment**).

**This four-step mental model is exactly how a Sewage Treatment Plant (STP) or a Decentralized Wastewater Treatment System (DEWATS) works in sustainable construction.**

## 1.4 Hierarchy of Treatment in Sustainable Construction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Focus — The 4R Hierarchy:** Sustainable construction follows **Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover** for water. Wastewater treatment is the *engineered enabler* of the last three steps.

- **Reduce** — Low-flow fixtures, pressure reduction
- **Reuse** — Direct greywater reuse for irrigation
- **Recycle** — Treated water for toilet flushing, HVAC make-up
- **Recover** — Biogas from sludge, nutrient recovery as fertilizer

## 1.5 Why Waste Water Treatment Matters in Sustainable Construction

1. **Regulatory Compliance** — Buildings must comply with **NBC 2016 (Part 9)**, **MOEFCC norms**, and **KSPCB** discharge limits.
2. **Green Building Credits** — GRIHA, LEED v4.1 BD+C, and IGBC award points for **50% reduction in potable water use** through on-site treatment.
3. **Resource Recovery** — Treated wastewater (reclaimed water) and biogas from sludge are valuable by-products.
4. **Climate Resilience** — Reduces stress on municipal supply during droughts.
5. **Circular Economy** — Closes the water loop on-site.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stages of Wastewater Treatment — Operational Logic

### 2.1.1 Preliminary Treatment
**Objective:** Removal of large, floating, and settleable inorganic matter that may damage downstream equipment.

**Unit Operations (in sequence):**

1. **Screening** — Bar racks with **clear opening 6 mm to 75 mm** (fine screens 1.5–6 mm).
2. **Grit Removal** — Velocity-controlled channels (target velocity **0.25 m/s to 0.30 m/s**) so organic matter remains suspended while grit settles. Detention time **45–90 seconds**.
3. **Oil and Grease Removal** — Skimming tanks; **5–15 minutes** detention.
4. **Flow Equalization** — Buffers diurnal variations in flow and load.

### 2.1.2 Primary Treatment (Physical / Chemical)
**Objective:** Removal of settleable suspended solids (60–70%) and 25–40% of BOD.

**Key Unit:** **Primary Clarifier** (settling tank)
- **Overflow rate (Surface Loading Rate):** **30–40 m³/m²/day** for primary tanks.
- **Detention Time:** **1.5 to 2.5 hours**.
- **Weir loading:** **≤ 300 m³/m/day**.

### 2.1.3 Secondary Treatment (Biological)
**Objective:** Oxidation of dissolved and colloidal organic matter by microorganisms.

**Two dominant technologies in sustainable construction:**

**(a) Activated Sludge Process (ASP)**
- Aeration tank with **MLSS = 2000–4000 mg/L**.
- **F/M Ratio = 0.2 to 0.5 per day** (food to microorganism).
- **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) = 4 to 8 hours**.
- **Sludge Retention Time (SRT) = 5 to 15 days**.
- **Oxygen Demand ≈ 1.42 kg O₂/kg BOD removed**.

**(b) Moving Bed Biofilm Reactor (MBBR) / Trickling Filter / Bio-tower**
- Plastic media with specific surface area **> 500 m²/m³**.
- Used when footprint is limited.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MBBR is preferred in sustainable buildings:** No sludge recirculation, smaller footprint, robust to load shocks — making it ideal for **high-rise and campus-scale decentralized STPs**.

### 2.1.4 Tertiary / Advanced Treatment
**Objective:** Polishing to meet reuse standards (e.g., **BOD < 10 mg/L, TSS < 10 mg/L, FC < 200 MPN/100 mL** for toilet flushing; **FC < 1 MPN/100 mL** for unrestricted irrigation).

**Common Tertiary Units:**
1. **Rapid Sand Filtration** — Filtration rate **5–15 m³/m²/hr**.
2. **Activated Carbon Filtration** — Removes organics, colour, odour.
3. **Disinfection** — Chlorine (2–5 mg/L residual), UV (30–40 mJ/cm²), or Ozonation.
4. **Membrane Technologies** — Ultrafiltration (UF), Reverse Osmosis (RO).
5. **Constructed Wetlands** — Polishing + landscape integration.

### 2.1.5 Sludge Management
- **Thickening** → **Digestion (Aerobic / Anaerobic)** → **Dewatering** → **Disposal or Reuse**.
- **Anaerobic Digestion** produces **biogas ≈ 0.5 m³/kg VS destroyed** (volatile solids).
- Digested sludge meets **IS 14643** for use as soil conditioner.

## 2.2 Characteristics of Wastewater — The "Big 7" Parameters

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Typical Domestic Range | KTU Standard Effluent Limit (Reuse) |
|---|-----------|--------|------|------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| 1 | Biochemical Oxygen Demand (5-day) | **BOD₅** | mg/L | 200–400 | **≤ 10** |
| 2 | Chemical Oxygen Demand | **COD** | mg/L | 400–800 | **≤ 50** |
| 3 | Total Suspended Solids | **TSS** | mg/L | 200–500 | **≤ 10** |
| 4 | pH | — | — | 6.5–8.5 | **6.5–8.5** |
| 5 | Total Nitrogen | **TN** | mg/L | 40–85 | **≤ 10** |
| 6 | Total Phosphorus | **TP** | mg/L | 5–15 | **≤ 2** |
| 7 | Faecal Coliforms | **FC** | MPN/100 mL | 10⁶–10⁷ | **≤ 200 (toilet) / 1000 (irrigation)** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $\text{BOD}_L = \dfrac{\text{BOD}_5}{1 - e^{-k \cdot 5}}$ | Ultimate BOD from 5-day BOD (k = 0.23/day) | mg/L |
| 2 | $\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$ | Food-to-Microorganism ratio | day⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\text{SRT} = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w}$ | Sludge Retention Time | days |
| 4 | $\text{HRT} = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | Hydraulic Retention Time | hours |
| 5 | $\text{Surface Overflow Rate} = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Clarifier design | m³/m²/day |
| 6 | $\text{Weir Loading} = \dfrac{Q}{L}$ | Clarifier weir design | m³/m/day |
| 7 | $\text{O}_2 \text{ requirement} \approx 1.42 \cdot \text{BOD}_L$ | Stoichiometric oxygen for carbonaceous oxidation | kg O₂/day |
| 8 | $\text{BOD removed} = \dfrac{\text{BOD}_{in} - \text{BOD}_{out}}{\text{BOD}_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Treatment efficiency | % |
| 9 | $\text{Organic Loading Rate (OLR)} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$ | Loading on biological unit | kg BOD/m³/day |
| 10 | $\text{Person Equivalent} = \dfrac{\text{Total BOD load (kg/day)}}{0.054}$ | Population equivalent | PE |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NEVER use the `|` symbol inside a markdown table** for absolute values. KTU evaluation scripts will break the table rendering. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospital & Hotel Construction** — DEWATS + MBR for water recycling (60–80% reuse).
- **IT Parks & Campuses** — Constructed wetlands for tertiary polishing.
- **Construction Phase Itself** — Treated wastewater used for **dust suppression, wheel washing, and curing of concrete** (non-structural).
- **Urban Retrofitting** — Decentralized packaged STPs in basements (e.g., **MBR + UV** skid units, footprint 0.5 m²/PE).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative Problem 1: Design of Primary Clarifier for a Residential Township

**Problem Statement (KTU Model Question Type):**
A residential township of **population P = 5000 persons** discharges sewage at **135 LPCD** with a **peak factor of 2.5**. The average BOD₅ is **250 mg/L**. Design the dimensions of a **circular primary clarifier** given:
- Detention time, $t_d = 2.0$ hours
- Surface overflow rate, $v_o = 30 \, \text{m}^3/\text{m}^2/\text{day}$
- Assume circular tank with depth $H = 3.0$ m.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Average Daily Flow (ADF):**
$$Q_{avg} = P \times \text{LPCD} = 5000 \times 135 = 675{,}000 \, \text{L/day} = 675 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Peak Flow:**
$$Q_{peak} = 2.5 \times 675 = 1687.5 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day} = 19.53 \, \text{L/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Volume Required from Detention Time:**
$$V = Q_{avg} \times t_d = 675 \times \dfrac{2.0}{24} = 56.25 \, \text{m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Compute Surface Area from Overflow Rate:**
$$A = \dfrac{Q_{avg}}{v_o} = \dfrac{675}{30} = 22.5 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Check Volume from Area × Depth:**
$$V_{check} = A \times H = 22.5 \times 3.0 = 67.5 \, \text{m}^3$$

**Step 6 — Adopt the Larger Volume (67.5 m³) and Re-design:**

We must satisfy BOTH the overflow rate and the detention time. The design volume should be the greater of the two:

$$V_{design} = \max(V, V_{check}) = \max(56.25, 67.5) = 67.5 \, \text{m}^3$$

The corresponding surface area:
$$A = \dfrac{V_{design}}{H} = \dfrac{67.5}{3.0} = 22.5 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 7 — Compute Diameter of Circular Clarifier:**
$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 22.5}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{28.65} = 5.35 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 8 — Verify Detention Time with Designed Volume:**
$$t_{d,actual} = \dfrac{V_{design}}{Q_{avg}} \times 24 = \dfrac{67.5}{675} \times 24 = 2.4 \, \text{hours} \, (\text{within range } 1.5\text{–}2.5 \, \text{h}) \checkmark$$

**Step 9 — Compute BOD Removal in Primary Clarifier:**

Efficiency of primary clarifier is typically **30% for BOD** and **60% for TSS**:
$$\text{BOD}_{out} = \text{BOD}_{in} \times (1 - \eta_{BOD}) = 250 \times 0.70 = 175 \, \text{mg/L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logic of Step 9:** KTU expects you to use **empirical removal efficiencies** for primary treatment. Do not derive this from first principles — the sedimentation physics (Stokes' Law) is too advanced for the syllabus, but you may quote the efficiency as **30–40% BOD and 50–65% TSS** as per CPHEEO.

---

## 3.2 Derivative Problem 2: F/M Ratio and Aeration Tank Volume for ASP

**Problem Statement:**
A wastewater treatment plant receives $Q = 10{,}000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$ with $\text{BOD}_{in} = S_0 = 200 \, \text{mg/L}$. The desired MLSS concentration is $X = 2500 \, \text{mg/L}$ and the target F/M ratio is $0.3 \, \text{day}^{-1}$. Compute the aeration tank volume and the daily BOD loading.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert Units to SI (kg/day basis):**
$$S_0 = 200 \, \text{mg/L} = 200 \, \text{g/m}^3 = 0.200 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

$$X = 2500 \, \text{mg/L} = 2.5 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute Daily BOD Load:**
$$\text{BOD Load} = Q \times S_0 = 10{,}000 \times 0.200 = 2000 \, \text{kg BOD/day}$$

**Step 3 — Apply F/M Definition:**
$$\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X} = 0.3$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Volume V:**
$$V = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{0.3 \times X} = \dfrac{2000}{0.3 \times 2.5} = \dfrac{2000}{0.75} = 2666.67 \, \text{m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Compute HRT:**
$$\text{HRT} = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{2666.67}{10{,}000} = 0.2667 \, \text{day} = 6.4 \, \text{hours}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Aeration Air Requirement:**

Stoichiometric oxygen: $1.42 \, \text{kg O}_2/\text{kg BOD removed}$
Assume **BOD removal efficiency = 90%**:
$$\text{BOD removed} = 0.90 \times 2000 = 1800 \, \text{kg/day}$$

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ required} = 1.42 \times 1800 = 2556 \, \text{kg O}_2/\text{day}$$

With standard aerator efficiency of **1.2 kg O₂/kWh**:
$$\text{Power} = \dfrac{2556}{1.2} = 2130 \, \text{kWh/day}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why F/M matters in KTU exam:** The F/M ratio determines whether the sludge is in the *log growth* phase (high F/M, poor settling) or *endogenous respiration* phase (low F/M, good settling). Conventional ASP operates at F/M = 0.2–0.5.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Secondary Treatment Options for Sustainable Construction

| Criterion | Activated Sludge | MBBR | SBR (Sequencing Batch) | Constructed Wetland |
|-----------|------------------|------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **Footprint** | High | Medium | Medium | Very High |
| **Energy Use (kWh/m³)** | 0.3–0.7 | 0.2–0.5 | 0.3–0.6 | **< 0.05** |
| **Sludge Production** | High | Low | High | **Negligible** |
| **Operator Skill** | High | Medium | High | **Low** |
| **Construction Suitability** | Township scale | **Best for high-rise** | Campus scale | **Campus / landscape** |
| **LEED Points Compatibility** | Good | Excellent | Excellent | **Excellent** |
| **Capital Cost (₹/m³/day)** | 12,000–18,000 | 15,000–22,000 | 14,000–20,000 | 6,000–10,000 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **MBR (Membrane Bioreactor)** with **MBBR (Moving Bed Biofilm Reactor)**. MBR uses **membranes inside the bioreactor**; MBBR uses **freely moving plastic media**. They are NOT the same.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — BOD₅ to Ultimate BOD Conversion

**Given:** BOD₅ = 180 mg/L, deoxygenation constant $k = 0.23 \, \text{day}^{-1}$ (base 10) at 20°C.

**Step 1 — Apply the First-Order Kinetics Equation:**
$$\text{BOD}_t = \text{BOD}_L \times (1 - e^{-kt})$$

Rearranging for BOD_L:
$$\text{BOD}_L = \dfrac{\text{BOD}_5}{1 - e^{-k \cdot 5}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values:**
$$\text{BOD}_L = \dfrac{180}{1 - e^{-0.23 \times 5}} = \dfrac{180}{1 - e^{-1.15}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Exponent:**
$$e^{-1.15} = 0.3166$$

**Step 4 — Final Calculation:**
$$\text{BOD}_L = \dfrac{180}{1 - 0.3166} = \dfrac{180}{0.6834} = 263.4 \, \text{mg/L}$$

**Interpretation:** The *ultimate* BOD is 263.4 mg/L — the sample still had 83.4 mg/L of carbonaceous oxygen demand remaining after 5 days.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Wastewater Treatment Train for Sustainable Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Sewage Inlet] --> B[Preliminary Treatment]
    B --> B1[Bar Screens]
    B1 --> B2[Grit Chamber]
    B2 --> B3[Oil and Grease Trap]
    B3 --> B4[Flow Equalization Tank]
    B4 --> C[Primary Treatment]
    C --> C1[Primary Clarifier]
    C1 --> D[Secondary Biological Treatment]
    D --> D1[MBBR or Activated Sludge]
    D1 --> D2[Secondary Clarifier]
    D2 --> E[Tertiary Polishing]
    E --> E1[Rapid Sand Filter]
    E1 --> E2[Activated Carbon Filter]
    E2 --> E3[UV Disinfection Unit]
    E3 --> F[Reclaimed Water Storage]
    F --> F1[Toilet Flushing]
    F --> F2[Landscape Irrigation]
    F --> F3[Cooling Tower Make-up]
    C1 --> G[Sludge Line]
    D2 --> G
    G --> G1[Sludge Thickener]
    G1 --> G2[Anaerobic Digester]
    G2 --> G3[Biogas to Boiler or CHP]
    G2 --> G4[Dewatered Sludge Cake]
    G4 --> G5[Soil Conditioner for Landscaping]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Follow the central vertical flow from raw sewage to reclaimed water (left-to-right with a downward loop). The sludge line branches off the primary and secondary clarifiers, ultimately producing biogas and a soil conditioner — a perfect demonstration of the **circular economy** in sustainable construction.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Decentralized DEWATS for a High-Rise Building

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inlet[Building Sewer Stack] --> sept[Septic or ABR Tank]
    sept --> mbb[MBBR Aeration Chamber]
    mbb --> sed[Lamella Plate Clarifier]
    sed --> sf[Sand Filter]
    sf --> uv[UV Reactor 40 mJ per sq cm]
    uv --> tank[Reclaimed Water Tank]
    tank --> flush[Flushing System]
    tank --> ac[AC Cooling Loop]
    tank --> garden[Landscape Drip Irrigation]
    mbb --> scada[PLC SCADA Monitoring]
    scada --> alarm[Operator Dashboard and Alarm]
    sept --> sl[Sludge Sump]
    sl --> dewat[Volute Dewatering Press]
    dewat --> compost[Landscape Compost Pit]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this architecture is exam-relevant:** KTU's 2024 syllabus emphasizes **decentralized systems** for sustainable buildings. Notice how **SCADA monitoring** is integrated — a key feature of **Smart STP** designs that earn bonus LEED points under the *Innovation in Design* category.

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Selection of Treatment Train

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Project Scale and Effluent Quality Required] --> q1{Is daily flow less than 50 kLD?}
    q1 -- Yes --> p1[Use Packaged MBR or MBBR Skid]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Available footprint more than 2 sq m per kLD?}
    q2 -- Yes --> p2[Use Conventional ASP with Clarifier]
    q2 -- No --> q3{Are landscape areas available?}
    q3 -- Yes --> p3[Use MBBR plus Constructed Wetland]
    q3 -- No --> p4[Use SBR with Tertiary UF and RO]
    p1 --> end[Reclaimed Water Reuse System]
    p2 --> end
    p3 --> end
    p4 --> end
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Pitfall:** Students often recommend Constructed Wetlands for high-rise projects. Wetlands require **large horizontal land area** (typically 5 m²/PE) — they are NOT suitable for dense urban vertical construction. Use MBBR or MBR instead.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Differentiate between BOD and COD as indicators of wastewater pollution. Why is BOD₅ more commonly used in sustainable construction practice?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand):** Measures the amount of **dissolved oxygen** consumed by **microorganisms** while biochemically oxidizing organic matter in **5 days at 20°C (BOD₅)**. It captures only the **biodegradable fraction** of organics.
- **COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand):** Measures the oxygen equivalent of **all chemically oxidizable matter** (biodegradable + non-biodegradable) using a strong oxidant (potassium dichromate) in **2 hours**.
- **Why BOD₅ is preferred in sustainable construction:**
  1. It directly relates to the **biodegradable load** that biological treatment can remove.
  2. It guides the **design of biological reactors** (F/M ratio, aeration tank volume).
  3. It is **regulated by KSPCB and NBC 2016** for discharge consent.
  4. The ratio **BOD/COD ≈ 0.4–0.6** for domestic sewage — used to assess biodegradability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (1 mark):** Always quote the typical BOD:COD ratio. Examiners love it.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]`
**List the unit operations in a typical primary treatment stage and state the design detention time of a primary clarifier.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Screening** — Bar screens to remove large solids.
2. **Grit Removal** — Velocity-controlled grit chamber.
3. **Primary Sedimentation (Clarifier)** — Settles suspended solids.
4. **Skimming** — Removes oil and grease (sometimes bundled with grit removal).

**Design Detention Time:** **1.5 to 2.5 hours** (typically **2.0 hours** is adopted in KTU model solutions).

**Overflow rate (Surface Loading Rate):** **30–40 m³/m²/day** for domestic sewage.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Module 3 Carry-Over]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat flow diagram, explain the **four stages of wastewater treatment** (Preliminary, Primary, Secondary, Tertiary) for a sustainable commercial building complex. List the **removal efficiencies** of each stage for **BOD and TSS**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A residential community of **10,000 persons** discharges **150 LPCD** of sewage with BOD₅ = **300 mg/L**. The STP uses a **conventional Activated Sludge Process** with MLSS = **3000 mg/L** and target **F/M = 0.25 day⁻¹**. Compute:
  (i) Aeration tank volume
  (ii) Hydraulic Retention Time
  (iii) Oxygen requirement in kg/day, assuming BOD removal = 90% **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Four Stages of Treatment:**

| Stage | Unit Operations | BOD Removal | TSS Removal |
|-------|-----------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Preliminary** | Screening, Grit Removal, Oil & Grease | 5–10% | 5–15% |
| **Primary** | Primary Clarifier | 25–40% | 50–65% |
| **Secondary** | ASP / MBBR / SBR | 85–95% | 85–95% |
| **Tertiary** | Filtration, Disinfection, Advanced Oxidation | Polishing (residual <10 mg/L) | Polishing (residual <10 mg/L) |
