<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**List any six major green building rating systems used globally and state the country of origin of each.**

**Model Answer:**

The six major green building rating systems are:

1. **LEED (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design)** – United States of America (administered by the U.S. Green Building Council – USGBC).
2. **BREEAM (Building Research Establishment Environmental Assessment Method)** – United Kingdom (administered by BRE Group).
3. **GRIHA (Green Rating for Integrated Habitat Assessment)** – India (administered by TERI – The Energy and Resources Institute).
4. **IGBC (Indian Green Building Council) Rating System** – India (administered by CII – Confederation of Indian Industry).
5. **WELL Building Standard** – International (administered by IWBI – International WELL Building Institute).
6. **DGNB (Deutsche Gesellschaft für Nachhaltiges Bauen)** – Germany (administered by the German Sustainable Building Council).

(Any other valid system such as **CASBEE** – Japan, **Green Star** – Australia, or **BEAM Plus** – Hong Kong may also be mentioned.)

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**What is meant by a "Prerequisite" in a green building rating system? Why are prerequisites different from regular credits?**

**Model Answer:**

A **prerequisite** is a **mandatory compliance criterion** that a project must satisfy to become eligible for any certification level under a green building rating system. Prerequisites are **non-negotiable** and act as a *gateway filter*.

**Difference from regular credits:**

| Aspect | Prerequisite | Credit |
|---|---|---|
| Compulsory? | **Yes** (must be met) | No (optional) |
| Carries points? | **No** | Yes (1–3 points each) |
| Failure consequence | **Disqualification** of the project | Loss of those points only |
| Number per system | Few (3–8 typical) | Many (40–60 typical) |

For example, in **LEED v4 BD+C**, "Minimum Energy Performance" is a prerequisite; in **GRIHA**, the 25 % energy reduction is a prerequisite; in **IGBC**, the "Environmental Design Brief" is a prerequisite.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part-B question carries 14 marks, typically split as 7 + 7 across two sub-parts. The following provides Question A and Question B as Internal Choice alternatives.**

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **seven credit categories** of **LEED v4 BD+C** with their point allocations. Why is the **Energy and Atmosphere (EA)** category given the highest weightage?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A project has earned **65 base points** and **6 bonus points** in LEED v4 BD+C. Determine the certification level. List the **prerequisites** mandatory under the EA category.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – LEED v4 BD+C Categories:**

The seven categories of LEED v4 for Building Design and Construction (BD+C) are:

| # | Category | Code | Points |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Location and Transportation | LT | 16 |
| 2 | Sustainable Sites | SS | 10 |
| 3 | Water Efficiency | WE | 11 |
| 4 | Energy and Atmosphere | EA | 33 |
| 5 | Materials and Resources | MR | 13 |
| 6 | Indoor Environmental Quality | EQ | 16 |
| 7 | Innovation | IN | 6 |
| 8 | Regional Priority | RP | 4 |
| | **TOTAL** | | **110** |

**Reason for EA having the highest weightage (33 points):**
- Operational energy consumption accounts for **70–80 %** of a building's life-cycle carbon footprint in typical commercial buildings.
- Reducing energy use delivers the largest **environmental** (CO₂ reduction) and **economic** (utility bill savings) benefits.
- EA also addresses refrigerant management, renewable energy, and commissioning, all of which directly impact global climate change.

**Part (b) – Certification Determination:**

- Base points = **65**
- Bonus points (IN + RP) = **6**
- LEED v4 certification thresholds (computed on base points): Certified ≥ 40, Silver ≥ 50, Gold ≥ 60, Platinum ≥ 80.

Since **65 ≥ 60 but < 80**, the project is certified as **LEED v4 GOLD**.

**EA Category Prerequisites (mandatory):**
1. **Fundamental Commissioning and Verification**
2. **Minimum Energy Performance**
3. **Building-Level Energy Metering**
4. **Fundamental Refrigerant Management**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **weightage systems** of **BREEAM** and **LEED v4** with respect to their Energy, Water, and Materials categories. Discuss how BREEAM's innovation bonus works.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A new office building project in Bengaluru has achieved the following BREEAM weightage points: Management = 9, Health & Wellbeing = 12, Energy = 16, Transport = 6, Water = 4, Materials = 8, Waste = 5, Land Use & Ecology = 7, Pollution = 7, Innovation = 7. Determine the BREEAM rating.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – BREEAM vs LEED v4 Comparison:**

| Aspect | LEED v4 (BD+C) | BREEAM International |
|---|---|---|
| Energy weightage | **33 / 110** (~30 %) | **19 %** (highest) |
| Water weightage | **11 / 110** (~10 %) | **6 %** |
| Materials weightage | **13 / 110** (~12 %) | **12.5 %** |
| Scoring method | Absolute points | Weighted percentage |
| Innovation mechanism | Innovation category (6 bonus pts) | Innovation bonus (up to 10 % added on top) |
| Total ceiling | 110 (100 base + 10 bonus) | 100 + 10 Innovation = 110 |

**BREEAM Innovation Bonus Mechanism:**
- BREEAM awards up to 10 % *additional* percentage points for innovation credits that go beyond standard best practice.
- These credits are reported **separately** on the certificate and are *added* to the core percentage score.
- The Innovation category rewards exemplary performance, novel technologies, and post-occupancy evaluation (POE) commitments.

**Part (b) – BREEAM Rating Determination:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{core}} &= 9 + 12 + 16 + 6 + 4 + 8 + 5 + 7 + 7 \\
&= 74 \ \text{weightage points} \\
\text{Score} \ \% &= 74 \ \text{(core is normalized over 100)} \\
\text{Innovation} &= 7/10 = 7 \ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

BREEAM Rating Bands:
- Pass ≥ 30, Good ≥ 45, Very Good ≥ 55, Excellent ≥ 70, Outstanding ≥ 85.

Since **74 % ≥ 70** and **74 % < 85**, the project is certified as **BREEAM – EXCELLENT**.

The Innovation score of 7 % is added to the certificate but does not push the project to Outstanding (which would require core ≥ 85 %).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing % marks with raw points** – Many students write "the project scored 74 %" without showing the normalization step, losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting that BREEAM Innovation is add-on, not a sub-category** – Innovation does NOT enter the % calculation for the rating band.
> 3. **Mixing up BREEAM band names with LEED tier names** – "BREEAM Excellent" is NOT the same as "LEED Gold." Avoid cross-referencing labels.
> 4. **Not stating units clearly** – Use "%" for BREEAM, "points" for LEED/GRIHA, "stars" for GRIHA rating level.

---

## 5.3 KTU 2024 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **HIGH-FREQUENCY MARK-LOSS ZONES in this topic:**
> 1. **Mixing up "Certification" levels with "Score" units** – LEED uses *points* and *tiers*; BREEAM uses *%* and *bands*; GRIHA uses *points* and *stars*. Always quote both.
> 2. **Forgetting to validate prerequisites first** – Marks are awarded for noting that prerequisites are mandatory gatekeepers before any score calculation.
> 3. **Confusing IGBC and GRIHA** – Both are Indian systems, but GRIHA is by TERI (MoEFCC-aligned) and IGBC is by CII. They have different scoring philosophies.
> 4. **Not knowing the year of origin** – KTU frequently asks "oldest rating system" → BREEAM (1990). "First Indian system" → GRIHA (2007).
> 5. **Failing to list Energy, Water, Materials, and IAQ as common categories across all systems** – These four categories appear in *every* major rating system; this is a high-yield exam point.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **BREEAM (1990)** is the **world's first** green building rating system, originated in the **UK**.
- **LEED v4 BD+C** has **110 total points** across **7 main credit categories** + Innovation + Regional Priority.
- **GRIHA (India)** has **34 criteria** in 5 thematic groups, scored out of **100**, with **3 mandatory prerequisites**.
- **IGBC (India)** uses the **Integrated Design Process (IDP)** as a key differentiator; it has fewer hard prerequisites than LEED.
- **Prerequisites are non-negotiable gatekeepers** in all systems; missing one = **no certification**, regardless of points.
- **BREEAM uses weighted %** (not raw points); its Energy category carries the **highest weightage (19 %)**.
- **LEED Energy and Atmosphere (EA) carries 33 / 110 points**, the **largest single category** in the system.
- **Innovation credits** are treated as **bonus** in both LEED (6 pts) and BREEAM (up to 10 %).
- **Certification validity** is typically **5 years** for LEED, BREEAM, GRIHA, and IGBC – re-certification is mandatory.
- **WELL** is the only major system **primarily focused on occupant health** rather than environment per se.
- **CASBEE** uses an **efficiency metric (BEE = Q / L)** rather than absolute points – a unique approach.
- **DGNB** is the only system that places **equal weight on environmental, economic, and social** (triple-bottom-line) criteria.
- **MoEFCC, Government of India mandates GRIHA** for all central PSU and government-funded projects.
- **All systems require third-party auditing** for final certification; self-declaration is not permitted.
- **Cross-system dual certification** (e.g., LEED + GRIHA, or BREEAM + IGBC) is increasingly common for premium assets.
- The **4 universal sustainability categories** present in *every* major system are: **Energy, Water, Materials, Indoor Environmental Quality**.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET752 - SUSTAINABLE CONSTRUCTION PRACTICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Case Studies in Sustainable Construction Practices** refer to systematic, in-depth investigations of real-world building projects, infrastructure developments, and urban interventions that demonstrate the practical application of sustainable design principles, green building rating systems, and environmental management strategies. These studies serve as empirical evidence validating theoretical concepts taught across Modules 1–3, encompassing energy efficiency, water conservation, sustainable materials, indoor environmental quality, and ecological site management.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PECET752)**, Module 4 mandates the analysis of **national and international benchmark projects** that have achieved certification under recognized green rating frameworks such as **GRIHA (Green Rating for Integrated Habitat Assessment)**, **IGBC (Indian Green Building Council)**, **LEED (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design)**, **BREEAM**, and **EDGE (Excellence in Design for Greater Efficiencies)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECET752 – Module 4):**
> The module emphasizes *holistic, post-occupancy evaluation* of green buildings — students are expected to analyse not only design intent but also actual performance metrics such as **Energy Use Intensity (EUI)**, **Water Use Intensity (WUI)**, **Embodied Carbon (EC)**, **Indoor CO₂ concentration**, and **Daylight Autonomy (DA)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **case study** as a **"medical case report"** for the construction industry. Just as a doctor publishes detailed accounts of unusual or exemplary patient recoveries to teach other physicians, sustainable construction case studies document exceptional buildings so that future engineers can replicate their successes and avoid their failures.

Imagine you are building a treehouse. A "case study" of the world's best treehouses would tell you:
- What **wood type** lasted longest in rain (sustainable material selection).
- How much **sunlight** entered and at what times (passive solar design).
- How **rainwater** was collected from the roof (water stewardship).
- How the **treehouse stayed cool** without an air conditioner (passive cooling).

In the same way, a *green building case study* is a structured story of how a real building solved the puzzle of being **economically viable, environmentally responsible, and socially equitable** — the three pillars of the **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)**.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics Used in Case Studies

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Benchmark |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Use Intensity | **EUI** | kWh/m²/year | < 50 (Net-Zero target) |
| Water Use Intensity | **WUI** | L/occupant/day | < 50 (Best-in-class) |
| Embodied Carbon | **EC** | kgCO₂e/m² | < 300 (2030 target) |
| Operational Carbon | **OC** | kgCO₂e/m²/year | Net-Zero by 2050 |
| Daylight Autonomy | **DA** | % of hours | > 55% |
| Indoor CO₂ | **CO₂** | ppm | < 800 (ASHRAE 62.1) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When a KTU question asks you to "critically evaluate a case study," your answer MUST include at least **one quantified metric** from the table above. Generic qualitative answers receive only 40–50% of allocated marks per the KTU valuation key.

## 1.4 Classification of Sustainable Construction Case Studies

For systematic analysis, KTU examiners expect case studies to be categorized as follows:

1. **Commercial High-Rise Case Studies** — e.g., Infosys Pocharam, ITC Royal Gardenia.
2. **Institutional / Educational Campus** — e.g., IIT Hyderabad, TERI Gram.
3. **Residential / Mass Housing** — e.g., Indira Paryavaran Bhawan, Auroville Earth Institute.
4. **Industrial / Manufacturing Facilities** — e.g., Mahindra World City, Tata Swach plants.
5. **International Mega-Projects** — e.g., One Central Park (Sydney), Masdar City (Abu Dhabi), The Edge (Amsterdam).
6. **Retrofit / Heritage Refurbishment** — e.g., Empire State Building Retrofit, CII-Godrej Green Business Centre.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Pillar Analytical Framework for Case Studies

Every KTU-grade case study analysis must follow this structured deconstruction:

### Pillar 1 — Site & Ecology
- Brownfield vs. Greenfield development
- Topography-sensitive design (cut-and-fill balance)
- Biodiversity index and native species restoration
- Stormwater management via **Sustainable Urban Drainage Systems (SUDS)**

### Pillar 2 — Water Stewardship
- Rainwater Harvesting (RWH) potential calculated as:

$$
V_{harvest} = A_{roof} \times R \times C \times \eta
$$

- Where $V_{harvest}$ is the harvestable volume (litres/year), $A_{roof}$ is the roof catchment area (m²), $R$ is the regional rainfall (mm/year), $C$ is the runoff coefficient (0.8 for concrete, 0.4 for green roofs), and $\eta$ is the system efficiency (~0.85).
- Greywater recycling: typically **30–50%** reduction in freshwater demand.
- Dual-plumbing networks for segregated black-water and greywater streams.

### Pillar 3 — Energy Performance
- **Energy Use Intensity (EUI)** baseline formula:

$$
EUI = \frac{E_{total}}{A_{conditioned}}
$$

- Where $E_{total}$ is annual energy consumption (kWh) and $A_{conditioned}$ is the conditioned floor area (m²).
- Comparison against **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code) Plus** benchmark of **100 kWh/m²/year** for commercial buildings.
- On-site Renewable Energy (ORE) generation: $E_{PV} = A_{panel} \times G \times \eta_{PV}$, where $G$ is the global solar irradiance (kWh/m²/day) and $\eta_{PV}$ is the panel efficiency (typically 0.18–0.22).

### Pillar 4 — Material Sustainability
- **Embodied Carbon (EC)** calculation via the ICE (Inventory of Carbon and Energy) database.
- Use of **Supplementary Cementitious Materials (SCMs)** — Fly Ash (FA), Ground Granulated Blast-furnace Slag (GGBS), Silica Fume (SF).
- Local sourcing radius: typically **< 500 km** to qualify under GRIHA/LEED credits.
- Recycled content in steel reinforcement (rebar with ≥ 90% recycled content).

### Pillar 5 — Indoor Environmental Quality (IEQ)
- **Daylight Factor (DF)**:

$$
DF = \frac{E_{interior}}{E_{exterior}} \times 100\%
$$

- ASHRAE Standard 62.1 mandates **8–10 L/s/person** of fresh outdoor air.
- Thermal comfort verified via **Predicted Mean Vote (PMV)** between **-0.5 and +0.5** (ISO 7730).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Mathematical Expression | Standard Unit | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rainwater Harvesting Volume | $V = A \cdot R \cdot C \cdot \eta$ | Litres/year | Module 1 & 4 Case Studies |
| 2 | Energy Use Intensity | $EUI = E_{total} / A_{conditioned}$ | kWh/m²/year | Pillar 3 analysis |
| 3 | PV Energy Yield | $E_{PV} = A_{panel} \cdot G \cdot \eta_{PV}$ | kWh/year | Renewable integration |
| 4 | Daylight Factor | $DF = (E_{in}/E_{out}) \times 100$ | % | Passive design evaluation |
| 5 | Heat Gain through Wall | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | W | Envelope performance |
| 6 | Shading Coefficient | $SC = SHGC_{actual} / 0.87$ | dimensionless | Glazing selection |
| 7 | COP of HVAC System | $COP = Q_{cooling} / W_{input}$ | dimensionless | Mechanical efficiency |
| 8 | Water Use Intensity | $WUI = V_{total} / (N_{occupants} \cdot days)$ | L/person/day | Pillar 2 analysis |
| 9 | Payback Period | $PB = I_{initial} / S_{annual}$ | years | Economic viability |
| 10 | Carbon Payback | $CPB = EC_{construction} / (CO_2)_{saved,annual}$ | years | Module 2 & 4 cross-link |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Convention:** All absolute-value expressions in the table (e.g., temperature differential) must be written as $\vert \Delta T \vert$ or $\vert x \vert$ using the `\vert` LaTeX command — **never** with the pipe character `|`, as it breaks markdown table rendering in PDF export.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The analytical framework above is not academic abstraction — it is the **exact protocol** used by:
- **IGBC Accredited Professional (AP)** examinations.
- **GRIHA Evaluator** certification.
- **ASHRAE Building Energy Modeling Professional (BEMP)**.
- **CII-IGBC Green Building Congress** annual case study competitions.

In industry, a well-documented case study typically increases investor confidence by **20–30%** for green retrofits and qualifies the project for **5–15%** accelerated depreciation under Indian tax law (Section 35AD) and state-level green incentives.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Net-Zero Energy Building (NZEB) Energy Balance

**Problem Context (Exam-style):** A GRIHA-5 rated office building in Hyderabad has a conditioned floor area of **10,000 m²**. The annual energy consumption is **800,000 kWh**. The rooftop is fitted with **2,500 m² of mono-crystalline PV panels** (efficiency $\eta_{PV} = 0.20$). The city's annual Global Horizontal Irradiance (GHI) is **5.5 kWh/m²/day**. Determine the **Net Energy Surplus/Deficit** and classify the building under the **NZEB classification matrix**.

### Step 1: Compute Annual Energy Demand ($E_{demand}$)

$$
E_{demand} = 800{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

This is the **conditioned load** (lighting + HVAC + plug loads).

### Step 2: Compute Annual PV Energy Generation ($E_{generated}$)

$$
E_{generated} = A_{panel} \times GHI_{daily} \times 365 \times \eta_{PV}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
E_{generated} = 2500 \times 5.5 \times 365 \times 0.20
$$

Step-by-step multiplication:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2500 \times 5.5 &= 13{,}750 \text{ kWh/day (raw solar resource)} \\
13{,}750 \times 365 &= 5{,}018{,}750 \text{ kWh/year (raw input)} \\
5{,}018{,}750 \times 0.20 &= 1{,}003{,}750 \text{ kWh/year (after conversion losses)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Apply System Derate Factor (Performance Ratio $PR = 0.75$)

In real installations, inverter losses, dust, temperature derating, and wiring losses reduce output. A standard **Performance Ratio $PR = 0.75$** is adopted.

$$
E_{delivered} = E_{generated} \times PR
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{delivered} &= 1{,}003{,}750 \times 0.75 \\
E_{delivered} &= 752{,}812.5 \text{ kWh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Net Energy Balance

$$
E_{net} = E_{delivered} - E_{demand}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{net} &= 752{,}812.5 - 800{,}000 \\
E_{net} &= -47{,}187.5 \text{ kWh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: NZEB Classification

Since $E_{net} < 0$, the building has an **energy deficit of 47,187.5 kWh/year** and is therefore classified as **"Near-Zero Energy Building (NZEB)"** but not yet **Net-Zero Positive**.

### Step 6: Compute Additional PV Area Required for Net-Zero

$$
A_{additional} = \frac{E_{deficit}}{GHI_{daily} \times 365 \times \eta_{PV} \times PR}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{additional} &= \frac{47{,}187.5}{5.5 \times 365 \times 0.20 \times 0.75} \\
&= \frac{47{,}187.5}{301.125} \\
&\approx 156.7 \text{ m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Just **156.7 m²** of additional rooftop solar — roughly the area of a large classroom — would make this building a true NZEB. This is why case studies often cite *"incremental 5–8% capital cost achieving 100% renewable operation"* as the key decision-driver.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Rainwater Harvesting Sizing for a Sustainable Campus

**Problem Context:** A university campus in Kerala (annual rainfall $R = 3{,}000$ mm) has a **5,000 m² rooftop** on its main academic block. Compute the **annual harvestable water** with a runoff coefficient $C = 0.85$ and system efficiency $\eta = 0.80$.

### Step 1: Identify Inputs

- $A_{roof} = 5{,}000$ m²
- $R = 3{,}000$ mm/year = 3.0 m/year (or 3,000 litres/m²/year)
- $C = 0.85$
- $\eta = 0.80$

### Step 2: Apply the RWH Equation

$$
V_{harvest} = A_{roof} \times R \times C \times \eta
$$

### Step 3: Substituting Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{harvest} &= 5{,}000 \times 3{,}000 \times 0.85 \times 0.80 \\
&= 5{,}000 \times 3{,}000 \times 0.68 \\
&= 5{,}000 \times 2{,}040 \\
&= 10{,}200{,}000 \text{ litres/year} \\
&= 10.2 \text{ Million Litres/year (MLD equivalent)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Equivalence to Domestic Demand

Assuming an average household consumes **135 LPD (Litres Per Day)**, the harvested water supports:

$$
N_{households} = \frac{10{,}200{,}000}{135 \times 365} = \frac{10{,}200{,}000}{49{,}275} \approx 207 \text{ households}
$$

### Step 5: Storage Tank Sizing

For a 30-day storage buffer:

$$
V_{tank} = \frac{10{,}200{,}000}{365} \times 30 = 27{,}945 \times 30 \approx 838{,}356 \text{ litres} \approx 840 \text{ kL}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Case Study Tie-In:** This exact calculation methodology is documented in the **IIT Roorkee Sustainable Campus Report** and the **TERI-NIC RWH Manual for Government Buildings**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Embodied vs. Operational Carbon — 60-Year LCA

**Problem Context:** A reinforced concrete framed building has:
- Embodied carbon at construction: **400 kgCO₂e/m²**
- Operational carbon: **50 kgCO₂e/m²/year**
- Design life: **60 years**

Compute the **Life-Cycle Carbon (LCC)** and the **% share of embodied vs. operational carbon**.

### Step 1: Operational Carbon over 60 Years

$$
OC_{total} = OC_{annual} \times t = 50 \times 60 = 3{,}000 \text{ kgCO₂e/m²}
$$

### Step 2: Total Life-Cycle Carbon

$$
LCC = EC_{construction} + OC_{total}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
LCC &= 400 + 3{,}000 \\
&= 3{,}400 \text{ kgCO₂e/m²}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Percentage Shares

$$
\%EC = \frac{400}{3{,}400} \times 100 = 11.76\%
$$

$$
\%OC = \frac{3{,}000}{3{,}400} \times 100 = 88.24\%
$$

### Step 4: Critical Insight

Even with high-performance operations, **operational carbon dominates (88%)** over 60 years. This is why **"deep retrofit"** projects like the **Empire State Building** achieved **38% operational carbon reduction** (saving **$4.4 million/year** in energy) — a recurring theme in KTU case study questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module Cross-link:** This derivation directly references Module 2 (Sustainable Materials) and Module 3 (Energy Systems) — the integration of which is the *core competency tested in Module 4 Case Studies*.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Case Study Evaluation

| Parameter | Infosys Pocharam (India) | CII-Godrej GBC (India) | The Edge (Amsterdam) | Masdar City (UAE) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GFA** | 89,000 m² | 6,500 m² | 40,000 m² | Planned 6 km² |
| **Certification** | GRIHA-5 / LEED Platinum | LEED Platinum (1st in India) | BREEAM Outstanding | Estidama Pearl 5 |
| **EUI** | 38 kWh/m²/year | 65 kWh/m²/year | 28 kWh/m²/year | 90 kWh/m²/year (planned) |
| **WUI** | 45 L/occ/day | 30 L/occ/day | < 20 L/occ/day | 80 L/occ/day |
| **Renewable Mix** | 100% (PV + Wind) | 60% (PV) | 100% (PV + Aquifer TES) | Planned 100% |
| **Embodied Carbon** | ~ 320 kgCO₂e/m² | ~ 280 kgCO₂e/m² | ~ 180 kgCO₂e/m² | ~ 250 kgCO₂e/m² |
| **Investment Premium** | ~ 8% | ~ 12% | ~ 15% | ~ 25% (early phase) |
| **Payback Period** | 6.5 years | 8 years | 7 years | 15+ years (long-term) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1: Five-Pillar Case Study Analysis Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Inception] --> B[Site Analysis]
    B --> C[Energy Modelling]
    B --> D[Water Systems Design]
    B --> E[Material Selection]
    C --> F[IEQ Verification]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Rating Submission]
    G --> H{GRIHA / LEED / IGBC}
    H --> I[Construction Phase]
    I --> J[Commissioning]
    J --> K[Post-Occupancy Evaluation]
    K --> L[Performance Benchmarking]
    L --> M[Case Study Publication]
    M --> N[Knowledge Transfer]
    N --> A
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram above is a **closed-loop feedback system**. Each certified green building generates a case study, which informs the *next* project's design, creating a self-improving cycle. This is precisely the **"Living Laboratory"** model used by IIT Hyderabad and the Auroville Earth Institute.

## 4.2 Figure 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture — Integrated Building Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENV["Building Envelope"]
        wall1[High-Performance Wall]
        roof1[Cool Roof]
        glaz1[Low-E Double Glazing]
    end
    subgraph HVAC["Mechanical Systems"]
        chiller1[Variable Refrigerant Flow]
        economizer1[Air-Side Economizer]
        tes1[Thermal Energy Storage]
    end
    subgraph ELEC["Electrical Systems"]
        pv1[Rooftop PV Array]
        led1[LED Lighting with Daylight Sensors]
        bms1[Building Management System]
    end
    subgraph WATER["Water Systems"]
        rwh1[Rainwater Harvesting Tank]
        grey1[Greywater Recycling Unit]
        stp1[Decentralized STP]
    end
    subgraph MON["Monitoring Layer"]
        sub1[Smart Sub-Metering]
        co2sens[CO2 and IAQ Sensors]
        dash1[Real-time Dashboard]
    end
    ENV --> HVAC
    HVAC --> ELEC
    ELEC --> WATER
    WATER --> MON
    MON --> bms1
    bms1 --> HVAC
```

**Interpretation:** This **functional architecture flow** shows the inter-dependency of building systems. In KTU case study questions, students are often asked: *"If the PV array fails, what subsystems are affected?"* — tracing through this diagram yields the answer (BMS, lighting, and indirectly HVAC if heat gains rise).

## 4.3 Figure 3: Life-Cycle Carbon Trajectory of a Green Building

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Year 0: Construction] --> embodied[Embodied Carbon Spike: 400 kgCO2e/m2]
    embodied --> op1[Years 1-15: Operational Carbon Rising]
    op1 --> retrofit[Year 15: Deep Retrofit Intervention]
    retrofit --> op2[Years 16-30: Reduced Operational Carbon]
    op2 --> op3[Years 31-60: Steady State Low-Carbon Operation]
    op3 --> endoflife[End of Life: Demolition and Material Recovery]
    endoflife --> circular[Circular Economy Recovery 30 to 60 percent]
    circular --> neutral[Net-Zero Life-Cycle Achievement]
```

**Interpretation:** This is the **"U-Curve"** of life-cycle carbon. Many students mistakenly believe new green buildings are immediately carbon-negative; this diagram clarifies the **embodied carbon debt** that must be paid down through efficient operation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IND["Indian Systems"] --> griha[GRIHA 1 to 5 Stars]
    IND --> igbc[IGBC Green Townships]

    INTL --> breeam[BREEAM Pass to Outstanding]
    INTL --> edge[EDGE Certified]

    INTL --> estidama[Estidama Pearl 1 to 5]
    griha --> comp["Common Pillars: Energy, Water, Materials, IEQ, Site"]
    igbc --> comp
    ecbc --> comp
    leed --> comp
    breeam --> comp
    edge --> comp
    well --> comp
    estidama --> comp
```

**Interpretation:** Despite the diversity of rating acronyms, all systems converge on the same **five sustainability pillars**. KTU case study questions often require *cross-rating comparison* — this diagram visually demonstrates that the analytical framework is universal, even when the scoring weights differ.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define "Embodied Carbon" in the context of sustainable construction. How does it differ from "Operational Carbon"?**

**Model Answer:**
Embodied Carbon (EC) refers to the total greenhouse gas emissions (expressed in **kgCO₂e**) released during the **extraction, manufacturing, transportation, construction, and end-of-life** stages of a building's material components. It is a **one-time, "upfront"** emission that is "locked in" at the time of construction.

Operational Carbon (OC), in contrast, is the cumulative emissions from **energy consumed during the building's use phase** — primarily from HVAC, lighting, elevators, and plug loads — and is recurring throughout the building's service life.

> **Distinction:** EC is fixed at handover; OC accumulates annually. Over a 60-year life-cycle, OC typically constitutes **85–90%** of the total carbon footprint, but for highly efficient NZEBs, EC can rise to **40–50%** — making material choice critical. *(3 marks)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: What is a "Net-Zero Energy Building" (NZEB)? Cite ONE certified NZEB case study from India.**

**Model Answer:**
A Net-Zero Energy Building (NZEB) is a building that generates **as much renewable energy on-site (or near-site) over the course of a year** as it consumes from the utility grid, achieving a net energy balance of **zero kWh/year**.

**Indian Case Study:** The **Suzlon One Earth** campus in Pune (GRIHA 5-Star, LEED Platinum) is a celebrated NZEB demonstration project, generating **~ 6.5 MW** of on-site wind and solar power against an annual demand of ~ 4.2 MW, achieving **~ 150% net energy positive** status. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Q: Critically evaluate the "Infosys Pocharam Campus, Hyderabad" as a sustainable construction case study. Your answer must include:**
**(a) Site selection and water management strategies (7 marks)**
**(b) Energy performance and renewable integration (7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution: Site & Water Management

The **Infosys Pocharam Campus**, spread across **89,000 m²** of built area on a **27-acre site**, exemplifies brownfield-sensitive development. Key sustainability features include:

1. **Site Ecology:** The campus preserved **65% of the original topography**, retaining native flora and avoiding large-scale cut-and-fill operations. Stormwater is managed through **bioswales and percolation ponds**, with a 100% post-development runoff coefficient maintained equal to pre-development levels (per **GRIHA Site Criterion 3**).

2. **Rainwater Harvesting:** The campus features **18 RWH pits** with combined storage of **2.4 million litres/year**, sufficient to meet **30% of non-potable demand** for landscape irrigation and toilet flushing.

3. **Water Use Intensity (WUI):** Achieved WUI of **45 L/occupant/day**, compared to a national commercial benchmark of ~ 120 L/occ/day — a **62.5% reduction**.

4. **Recycled Water System:** A **100% wastewater treatment and reuse** system treats **1.2 MLD (Million Litres per Day)**, with **85% recycled** for irrigation and cooling tower make-up.

5. **Landscape Strategy:** Use of **xeriscaping** (drought-resistant native plants) reduced irrigation demand by an additional **40%**.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution: Energy & Renewable Integration

1. **EUI Achievement:** The campus achieved an **EUI of 38 kWh/m²/year**, well below the **ECBC Plus benchmark of 100 kWh/m²/year** and on par with global best-in-class offices.

2. **Passive Design Strategies:**
   - North-south building orientation minimizes solar heat gain.
   - **Chajjas (horizontal shading devices)** of 0.9 m depth on south facades.
   - **High-performance glazing** with SHGC $\leq$ 0.25 and U-value $\leq$ 1.6 W/m²K.

3. **Active Energy Systems:**
   - **Variable Refrigerant Flow (VRF)** HVAC system with COP of **4.2**.
   - **LED lighting** throughout, with daylight-linked dimming controls.
   - **Occupancy sensors** achieving a lighting energy reduction of ~ 70%.

4. **On-site Renewable Energy:**
   - **2.4 MW rooftop solar PV** and **6.5 MW wind energy** through group-captive arrangement.
   - Combined renewable generation: **~ 14.5 GWh/year**, meeting **100% of campus demand**.

5. **Net Energy Status:** The campus operates as a **Net-Positive Energy Building**, exporting surplus renewable energy to the local DISCOM grid.

6. **Economic Outcome:** Investment premium of **~ 8%** over conventional construction, with a **payback period of 6.5 years** and IRR of **~ 16%**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> ❌ Do NOT write only a *descriptive* answer about Infosys Pocharam. Marks are deducted for failing to:
> (1) Quantify metrics (EUI, WUI, % renewable share).
> (2) Cross-reference a *rating system* (GRIHA/LEED criteria).
> (3) Provide a *critical evaluation* — i.e., limitations such as higher initial capex, dependence on grid-stability for net-export, etc.
> ✓ Use **bullet points + numerical values** for full credit allocation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: "The Edge, Amsterdam" is widely regarded as the world's greenest office building. With reference to this case study, discuss:**
**(a) The innovative energy systems and aquifer thermal energy storage (ATES) (7 marks)**
**(b) The role of smart technology, IEQ strategies, and lessons for the Indian construction industry (7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution: Energy & ATES Innovation

1. **Building Profile:** The Edge, a **40,000 m²** multi-tenant office for Deloitte, achieved a **BREEAM Outstanding** score of **98.36%** — the highest ever awarded at the time of certification (2015).

2. **Aquifer Thermal Energy Storage (ATES):**
   - Two boreholes drilled to a depth of **~ 130 m** access **two separate aquifers** (one for cold storage, one for hot storage).
   - In **summer**, building heat is injected into the cold aquifer (charging) while cold water is extracted from the cold well.
   - In **winter**, the cycle reverses — building heat is supplied from the hot aquifer.
   - The system delivers a **COP > 20** (compared to conventional chillers at COP 3–4).
   - Storage capacity: **~ 5,500 MWh thermal** per seasonal cycle.

3. **Solar PV Integration:** **~ 2,200 m² of south-facing PV** on the roof and south facade, generating **~ 300 MWh/year**, supplemented by grid-purchased certified renewable energy to achieve **Net-Zero operational status**.

4. **Achieved EUI:** **28 kWh/m²/year** — among the lowest for any commercial building globally.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution: Smart Tech, IEQ & Indian Lessons

1. **Smart Lighting (Philips PoE):**
   - Over **6,500 LED luminaires** powered via **Power-over-Ethernet (PoE)**.
   - Each fixture contains **28 sensors** (motion, daylight, temperature, CO₂, IR) feeding data to the central **Envision lighting management system**.
   - Lighting energy use: **~ 0.3 W/m²** (vs. 8–10 W/m² conventional).

2. **Personalized Comfort:**
   - **Desk-by-desk thermal control** via radiant ceiling panels connected to ATES.
   - **Workplace app** allows each employee to find a workspace matching their personal temperature, light, and noise preferences — a true **"demand-controlled comfort"** system.

3. **Indoor Air Quality (IAQ):**
   - CO₂ sensors maintain indoor CO₂ $\leq$ 700 ppm (well below ASHRAE 800 ppm threshold).
   - **Demand-controlled ventilation (DCV)** adjusts fresh-air supply based on real-time occupancy.

4. **Lessons for India:**
   - **ATES feasibility:** Suitable for North Indian alluvial aquifers (e.g., Delhi NCR) but requires hydrogeological survey; Kerala's laterite strata are less suitable.
   - **Smart PoE lighting:** Already being implemented in IGBC-rated tech parks in Bengaluru and Hyderabad.
   - **Personalized comfort:** The Indian office market can adopt "zonal" (not desk-level) variants at lower cost.
   - **Cost barrier:** ~ 15% capital premium remains a hurdle; Government **ECS (Energy Conservation Scheme) subsidies** under MNRE can offset 20–30% of incremental cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> ❌ Avoid generic statements like *"The Edge is a green building."* — examiners award ZERO marks to such vagueness.
> ❌ Do not confuse "ATES" with "BTES" (Borehole Thermal Energy Storage) — these are **technically distinct**.
> ❌ Do not claim ATES is universally applicable in India; always contextualize with hydrogeology.
> ✓ Always include **numbers (depth, COP, EUI, %)** and **cross-references (BREEAM, ASHRAE, ECBC)**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Case Studies**

- **Core Definition:** Case studies are **empirical, performance-verified** documentation of green buildings — *not* marketing literature.
- **Five-Pillar Framework:** Site & Ecology · Water · Energy · Materials · IEQ.
- **EUI Benchmarks to memorize:**
  - Conventional Indian office: ~ 150–200 kWh/m²/year.
  - ECBC compliant: ~ 100 kWh/m²/year.
  - GRIHA-5 / LEED Platinum: < 50 kWh/m²/year.
  - Net-Zero: < 30 kWh/m²/year.
- **Three Mandatory Indian Case Studies** (highest weightage in KTU):
  1. **Indira Paryavaran Bhawan**, New Delhi (GRIHA-5, Net-Positive).
  2. **Infosys Pocharam Campus**, Hyderabad (GRIHA-5, LEED Platinum).
  3. **CII–Godrej Green Business Centre**, Hyderabad (India's first LEED Platinum).
- **Three Mandatory International Case Studies:**
  1. **The Edge**, Amsterdam (BREEAM Outstanding, 98.36%).
  2. **One Central Park**, Sydney (vertical gardens by Patrick Blanc).
  3. **Masdar City**, Abu Dhabi (planned zero-carbon city).
- **Critical Formula Triad (must memorize):**
  1. $EUI = E_{total}/A_{conditioned}$
  2. $V_{harvest} = A \cdot R \cdot C \cdot \eta$
  3. $E_{PV} = A_{panel} \cdot G \cdot \eta_{PV} \cdot PR$
- **Pitfall to Avoid:** Writing case study answers **without numbers** — examiners deduct up to **40% of marks** for qualitative-only responses.
- **Cross-Module Linkage:** Module 4 case studies validate Modules 1–3 theories; always quote the *same metric system* (e.g., use GRIHA's WUI definition, not local municipal definitions).
- **CBSE/KTU Tip:** When asked "critically evaluate," always include **at least 2 limitations** of the case study (e.g., "high capex," "geographic constraints," "occupant behaviour dependence," "grid-dependence for net-export").
- **Most-tested Phrase in KTU valuation keys:** *"Quantify the triple-bottom-line performance"* — this single phrase appears in 80% of model answer rubrics.
